nodes.institution: Mawlana Bhashani Science and Technology University
nodes.materials_raw: Ionic liquids (BMIM-TFSI, EMIM-TFSI, Pyr₁₃TFSI, EMIM-BF₄, EMIM-DCA), polymer matrices (P(VDF-HFP), PVDF, PEGDA, PVA, PMMA), silica-based sol-gel matrices, nanofillers (hBN, SiO₂, CNTs)
nodes.methods_raw: Sol-gel process, polymerization (free radical, photopolymerization, UV-curing), solution casting, electrospinning, 3D printing, Michael addition
nodes.key_findings: IL-based gels = ionogels/ionic gels formed by immobilizing ILs in polymer matrices; sol-gel derived IL gels: conductivity 1-2 mS/cm; P(VDF-HFP) gels: 8.6 mS/cm with EMIM-TFSI; applications in Li-ion batteries (gel polymer electrolytes), Na batteries, supercapacitors, fuel cells, solar cells; advantages: non-volatile, wide electrochemical window (4.87 V vs Li/Li⁺), high thermal stability; challenges: lower conductivity than pure ILs, humidity sensitivity, mechanical strength trade-offs
nodes.scientific_questions: Achieving conductivity comparable to pure ILs in gel form? Long-term cycling stability in batteries?

nodes.label: Mawlana Bhashani Science and Technology University
A review of polymer electrolytes: fundamental, approaches and applications
nodes.full_title: A review of polymer electrolytes: fundamental, approaches and applications
nodes.authors: Koh Sing Ngai, S. Ramesh, K. Ramesh, Joon Ching Juan
nodes.materials_raw: PEO, PVdF, PVdF-HFP, PMMA, PVC, PVA, PAA, PAN, PEMA, PCL, chitosan; lithium salts (LiClO₄, LiTFSI, LiPF₆, LiCF₃SO₃, LiBF₄); plasticizers (EC, PC, DMC, DBP)
nodes.methods_raw: Review of polymer electrolyte types, ionic conductivity enhancement approaches, electrochemical device applications
nodes.key_findings: Three types: gel polymer electrolytes (GPE), solid polymer electrolytes (SPE), composite polymer electrolytes (CPE); PEO: most studied but crystallizes at low T, reducing conductivity; PVdF/PVdF-HFP: high dielectric constant, good electrochemical stability; PMMA: transparent but brittle; PAN: good compatibility with Li electrodes; chitosan: biodegradable natural polymer; conductivity enhancement via blending, fillers (TiO₂, Al₂O₃, SiO₂), plasticizers, ionic liquids; applications: Li batteries, supercapacitors, fuel cells, DSSCs, electrochromic windows
nodes.scientific_questions: Achieving high room-temperature conductivity in SPEs? Balancing mechanical strength and ionic conductivity? Developing sustainable biopolymer electrolytes?

nodes.label: Extremely Tough, Stretchable Gel Electrolytes with Strong Interpolymer Hydrogen Bonding Prepared Usi
nodes.full_title: Extremely Tough, Stretchable Gel Electrolytes with Strong Interpolymer Hydrogen Bonding Prepared Using Concentrated Electrolytes to Stabilize Lithium-Metal Anodes
nodes.authors: Ryota Tamate, Yueying Peng, Yuji Kamiyama, Kei Nishikawa
nodes.materials_raw: MAAc (methacrylic acid) + NMMAm (N-methyl methacrylamide) + MMA + [Li(G4)][FSI] solvate ionic liquid electrolyte + photoinitiator
nodes.methods_raw: Photopolymerization, DSC, FTIR, MD simulations, XPS, SEM, EDS, tensile testing, Li symmetric cell cycling, full cell testing (Li||NMC622)
nodes.key_findings: Concentrated electrolytes suppress free solvent → enable strong interpolymer H-bonding → unprecedented toughness 16 MJ/m³ (comparable to tough hydrogels); Young's modulus 4.5 MPa, fracture stress 5.2 MPa, strain 770%; no sol-gel transition up to 120°C; MD confirms MAAc-NMMAm H-bonding stronger in concentrated vs dilute electrolyte; gel-coated Li anode stable >1000 h (vs 230 h for pristine Li); Li||NMC622 full cell: 87% capacity retention after 100 cycles; strategy applicable to other concentrated electrolyte systems (e.g., LiFSI/DMC)
nodes.scientific_questions: Extending to other H-bonding polymer pairs? Scaling for practical battery manufacturing? Long-term stability under high current densities?

nodes.label: Polyacrylonitrile-based gel polymer electrolytes for dye-sensitized solar cells: a review
nodes.full_title: Polyacrylonitrile-based gel polymer electrolytes for dye-sensitized solar cells: a review
nodes.authors: L.P. Teo, M.H. Buraidah, A.K. Arof
nodes.materials_raw: PAN (polyacrylonitrile), EC, PC, ACN, MPN, various iodide salts (LiI, NaI, KI, CsI, TPAI), I₂, fillers (V₂O₅, SiO₂, TiO₂, ZrO₂, Al₂O₃, BaTiO₃), PVdF, PEO, PMMA, polystyrene
nodes.methods_raw: Solution casting, electrospinning, EIS (Nyquist plots), J-V characterization, photocurrent measurements
nodes.key_findings: PAN: high ambient conductivity (>1 mS/cm), good mechanical integrity, electrochemical stability; semicrystalline with Tm=317°C, Tg~88-125°C; nitrile group (-C≡N) coordinates with Li⁺ cations; PAN GPE first reported in 1995 (4.50×10⁻³ S/cm); best DSSC efficiency ~7.75% with PAN-based GPE; electrospun PAN nanofiber membranes enhance electrolyte uptake; co-solvents (EC+PC) improve conductivity; filler addition (SiO₂, TiO₂, BaTiO₃) enhances conductivity and efficiency; I₃⁻ diffusion coefficient and ionic conductivity are key parameters for DSSC performance
nodes.scientific_questions: Optimizing PAN concentration vs salt ratio? Long-term stability of PAN GPEs in DSSCs? Scalability of electrospun membranes?

nodes.label: Overview of Ionogels in Flexible Electronics
nodes.full_title: Overview of Ionogels in Flexible Electronics
nodes.authors: Lu Zhang, Dawei Jiang, Tianhe Dong, Rajib Das, Duo Pan, Caiying Sun, Zijian Wu, Qingbo Zhang, Chuntai Liu, Zhanhu Guo
nodes.materials_raw: Ionic liquids (EMIM-TFSI, EMIM-BF₄, BMIM-TFSI, etc.), polymer networks (PMMA, P(MMA-co-MA), PEA, PDMS), nanoparticles (MXene, SiO₂, clay, cellulose nanocrystals)
nodes.methods_raw: Review of ionogel preparation methods: in-situ reactions in ILs (gelation of LMWGs, self-assembly of block copolymers, double-network formation, nanocomposites, direct polymerization), solvent exchange, direct polymerization of polymerizable ILs
nodes.key_findings: Ionogels = IL-based composites with solid 3D networks; IL properties: negligible vapor pressure, high thermal stability, wide electrochemical windows, tunable viscosity/conductivity; Walden plot evaluates ionicity; preparation methods: (1) in-situ reactions - simplest, most versatile; (2) solvent exchange - from hydrogels, maintains structure; (3) direct polymerization of polymerizable ILs - covalent integration; advantages: no evaporation, no crystallization at low T, broad application scenarios; challenges: lower conductivity than hydrogels, IL leakage compatibility issues
nodes.scientific_questions: Achieving uniform IL distribution in polymer networks? Preventing IL leakage during long-term use? Designing IL-specific polymer architectures?

nodes.label: Review of ionogel preparation methods: in-situ rea
Mechanically Adaptative and Environmentally Stable Ionogels for Energy Harvest
nodes.full_title: Mechanically Adaptative and Environmentally Stable Ionogels for Energy Harvest
nodes.authors: Wei Zhao, Zhouyue Lei, Peiyi Wu
nodes.materials_raw: P(MMA-co-MA) copolymer + EMIM-TFSI (hydrophobic IL) + MXene nanosheets (Ti₃C₂Tₓ)
nodes.methods_raw: DFT calculations, FTIR, DSC, DMA, rheology, tensile testing, EIS, thermovoltage measurements, screen printing, 3D printing
nodes.key_findings: Synergistic ionic associations among polymer, IL cations, and MXene nanosheets; MXene selectively traps EMIM⁺ cations → enhances n-type Soret effect; n-type Seebeck coefficient −8.8 mV/K; ionic conductivity >0.14 mS/cm; stretchability 1300-2100%; self-healing in 120s (including underwater); waterproof - stable under rain washing 7 days; no melting point, Tg ~ −10°C; stable performance 293-333K, 20-100% RH; open-circuit thermovoltage >2V on cloudy day (8.5×10⁻³ m² array); ionic figure of merit ZT = 0.05; applicable as paintable thermoelectric coating for building energy harvest
nodes.scientific_questions: Scaling up for real building applications? Long-term outdoor durability? Optimizing MXene loading for balanced performance?

nodes.label: Quaternary Ammonium-Functionalized Polyethylene-Based Anion Exchange Membranes: Balancing Performanc
nodes.full_title: Quaternary Ammonium-Functionalized Polyethylene-Based Anion Exchange Membranes: Balancing Performance and Stability
nodes.authors: Cheyenne R. Peltier, Wei You, Dea Fackovic Volcanjk, Qihao Li, Alexandra J. Macbeth, Héctor D. Abruña, Geoffrey W. Coates
nodes.materials_raw: Polyethylene backbone + quaternary ammonium cations (trimethylammonium, piperidinium, morpholinium, pyrrolidinium, azepanium, ether/aryl-substituted ammoniums) + cyclooctene monomers via ROMP
nodes.methods_raw: ROMP copolymerization + hydrogenation, melt-pressing, ionic conductivity measurement over 30 days, FTIR, NMR, fuel cell MEA testing
nodes.key_findings: 17 PE-based AEMs systematically compared; piperidinium with β-methyl (PE15): best balance - 41 mS/cm at 22°C, 95% conductivity retention after 30 days in 1M KOH at 80°C; cross-linked PE7: highest conductivity 56 mS/cm at 22°C, 98 mS/cm at 80°C; trimethylammonium (PE1): 44 mS/cm but only 80% retained; Hofmann elimination as degradation mechanism blocked by β-methyl; MEA peak power density 1.0 W/cm² with PE15 (IEC=1.71 mmol/g); polyethylene backbone avoids arylene ether degradation issues; cation stability in polymer differs from small molecule studies
nodes.scientific_questions: Further optimizing IEC vs swelling trade-off? Developing aromatic-free ionomers for fully PE-based MEAs? Long-term fuel cell durability testing?

nodes.label: Optically Transparent Ionic Hydrogel Electrodes for Alternating Current Electroluminescence Devices
nodes.full_title: Optically Transparent Ionic Hydrogel Electrodes for Alternating Current Electroluminescence Devices
nodes.authors: Go et al.
nodes.materials_raw: PHA (poly(2-hydroxyethyl acrylate)) + x-CS (crosslinked chitosan) double-network hydrogel + NaCl ionic conductor
nodes.methods_raw: DN hydrogel fabrication, optical transmittance measurement, EIS, stretchability testing, ACEL device integration
nodes.key_findings: DN hydrogel with PHA/x-CS achieves >99% optical transparency and 1400% stretchability; ionic conductivity 1.95×10⁻² S/m; serves as both electrode and transparent conductor for ACEL devices; replaces ITO for flexible/wearable electroluminescence
nodes.scientific_questions: Maintaining conductivity at extreme strains? Long-term optical clarity under operation? Scaling for large-area displays?

nodes.label: Molecular-Weight Dependence of Chain Diffusion in Polymerized Ionic Liquid Melts
nodes.full_title: Molecular-Weight Dependence of Chain Diffusion in Polymerized Ionic Liquid Melts
nodes.authors: Lan et al.
nodes.materials_raw: TFSI-f-PIL_N (TFSI-functionalized polymerized ionic liquid with varying N = degree of polymerization)
nodes.methods_raw: RAFT polymerization, FRAP (fluorescence recovery after photobleaching), DLS, rheology
nodes.key_findings: FRAP measurements reveal D∼N⁻² scaling for PIL chain diffusion, consistent with sticky Rouse model; counterion condensation creates transient crosslinks that slow chain dynamics; scaling differs from uncharged polymers (D∼N⁻¹) due to electrostatic associations
nodes.scientific_questions: How does counterion type affect diffusion scaling? Temperature dependence of sticky Rouse dynamics? Comparison with theoretical predictions for polyelectrolyte melts?

nodes.label: Highly Processable Ionogels with Mechanical Robustness
nodes.full_title: Highly Processable Ionogels with Mechanical Robustness
nodes.authors: Ma et al.
nodes.materials_raw: PS-PEO-PS triblock copolymer + mixed ionic liquids (EMIM-TFSI + EMIM-DCA) + UV crosslinker
nodes.methods_raw: Physical-co-chemical crosslinking, inkjet printing, spray coating, 3D printing, rheology, tensile testing, EIS
nodes.key_findings: Physical-co-chemical dual crosslinking strategy: PS domains provide physical crosslinks, UV-initiated crosslinks provide chemical network; mixed ILs (EMIM-TFSI + EMIM-DCA) optimize conductivity and processability; ionogels are inkjet-printable, spray-coatable, and 3D-printable; robust mechanical properties maintained after processing; versatile fabrication for flexible electronics
nodes.scientific_questions: Optimizing IL mixing ratio for specific applications? Print resolution limits? Long-term IL retention under mechanical cycling?

nodes.label: Ultra-Stretchable Biodegradable PLCL Elastomers for Transient Electronics
nodes.full_title: Ultra-Stretchable Biodegradable PLCL Elastomers for Transient Electronics
nodes.authors: Han et al.
nodes.materials_raw: PLCL (poly(L-lactide-co-ε-caprolactone)) biodegradable elastomer + PEDOT:PSS conductive composite
nodes.methods_raw: Melt processing, PEDOT:PSS coating/composite formation, tensile testing, biodegradation testing, in vivo implantation (cardiac jacket)
nodes.key_findings: PLCL elastomer achieves ~1600% stretchability while being fully biodegradable; PEDOT:PSS composites maintain conductivity at high strain; demonstrated as cardiac jacket implant for cardiac function monitoring; degradation products are biocompatible; bridges gap between biodegradability and extreme mechanical compliance
nodes.scientific_questions: Controlling degradation rate for specific implant durations? Maintaining electrical performance during degradation? Expanding to other biodegradable polymer systems?

nodes.label: Counterion Effects of Protic Ionic Liquids on the Conductivity and Stretchability of Free-Standing P
nodes.full_title: Counterion Effects of Protic Ionic Liquids on the Conductivity and Stretchability of Free-Standing PEDOT:PSS Films
nodes.authors: Thuy T. Le, Fatima Abbas, Shawn Bourdo, Fumiya Watanabe, Noureen Siraj
nodes.materials_raw: PEDOT:PSS + protic ILs ([p-MIM][DCA], [p-IM][DCA]) + aprotic ILs ([EMIM][BF₄], [EMIM][TCB], [EMIM][DCA])
nodes.methods_raw: HSAB theory-guided design, ion exchange synthesis, AFM, XPS, SEM-EDS, four-point probe conductivity, tensile testing
nodes.key_findings: Hard-soft acid-base (HSAB) theory applied to IL selection for PEDOT:PSS; PEDOT+ is soft cation, PSS− is hard anion → ideal IL should have hard cation + soft anion; protic [p-IM][DCA] gives 3547 S/cm conductivity (highest) with 11% strain; [p-MIM][DCA] gives 2730 S/cm with 11% strain; both surpass aprotic [EMIM][DCA] (~7% strain) and pristine PEDOT:PSS (<4%); hard cation binds to PSS− leaving PEDOT+ free → enhanced conductivity; surface roughness decreases with IL addition; AFM confirms morphological changes
nodes.scientific_questions: Extending HSAB design to other conductive polymers? Optimizing IL concentration for maximum conductivity-strain synergy? Long-term stability under cyclic strain?

nodes.label: Recent Advances in the Design of Ionenes: Toward Convergence with High-Performance Polymers
nodes.full_title: Recent Advances in the Design of Ionenes: Toward Convergence with High-Performance Polymers
nodes.authors: Jason E. Bara, Kathryn E. O'Harra
nodes.materials_raw: Ionenes (main-chain cationic polymers): ammonium, phosphonium, imidazolium, benzimidazolium types; diamine + dihalide monomers; IL monomers
nodes.methods_raw: Menshutkin reaction (A-A + B-B condensation), Debus-Radziszewski reaction, anion metathesis, melt-pressing, SEC-MALLS, TGA
nodes.key_findings: Ionenes = condensation polymers with charge in backbone (vs. pendant for poly(ILs)); phosphonium ionenes (6P,12): degradation onset ~300°C vs ~225°C for ammonium analogues; imidazolium ionenes with Tf₂N⁻ anions can stabilize free IL within matrix; benzimidazolium ionenes from Holdcroft group show alkaline stability; N-spirocyclic ammonium ionenes resist OH⁻ attack up to 80°C; trend toward HP polymer backbones (polyimides, polyamides); anionic ionenes largely unexplored; ionenes used in cosmetics, oil drilling, DNA binding, gas separation membranes, antibacterial applications
nodes.scientific_questions: Developing truly high-performance ionene backbones? Exploring anionic ionene design space? Achieving both high MW and processability?

nodes.label: Contributions to the Piezoelectric Effect in Ferroelectric Single Crystals and Ceramics
nodes.full_title: Contributions to the Piezoelectric Effect in Ferroelectric Single Crystals and Ceramics
nodes.authors: Dragan Damjanovic
nodes.materials_raw: PZT (Pb(Zr₁₋ₓTiₓ)O₃), BaTiO₃, PMN-PT, PZN-PT relaxor ferroelectric single crystals
nodes.methods_raw: Landau-Ginsburg-Devonshire (LGD) phenomenological theory, Gibbs free energy analysis, orientation-dependent piezoelectric coefficient calculations
nodes.key_findings: Enhanced piezoelectric response along nonpolar axes is consequence of anisotropic flattening of Gibbs free energy profile; flattening is common for temperature-, composition-, and field-induced enhancement; in rhombohedral PZT, largest d₃₃* along [001]c nonpolar axis, not polar [111]c; shear piezoelectric coefficient d₁₅ critical for nonpolar enhancement; dielectric susceptibility χ₁₁ increases near MPB while χ₃₃ stays constant → facilitated polarization rotation; same mechanism explains BaTiO₃ behavior near tetragonal-orthorhombic transition; extrinsic contributions from domain wall displacement also important; engineered domain structure enhances response beyond intrinsic anisotropy
nodes.scientific_questions: Quantitative prediction of optimal crystal cuts? Role of monoclinic phase in MPB enhancement? Extending to lead-free piezoelectrics?

nodes.label: Ionogels Reinforced by Ionophobic Coordination
nodes.full_title: Ionogels Reinforced by Ionophobic Coordination
nodes.authors: Hongfei Huang, Lijie Sun, Yalin Zhang, Ruohan Zhang, Chang Chen, Wei Sun
nodes.materials_raw: P(DMA-co-NIPAM-co-AATFE) copolymer + [EMIM][TFSI] + LiTFSI + photoinitiator
nodes.methods_raw: One-pot photoinitiated copolymerization, ATR-FTIR (temperature-dependent), 2D correlation spectroscopy (2DCOS), XPS, AFM, DMA, tensile testing, EIS, melt spinning, MD simulations
nodes.key_findings: "Ionophobic Coordination Reinforcement" (ICR) strategy: IL-phobic microphase separation (NIPAM shows UCST behavior in [EMIM][TFSI]) + Li+ coordination crosslinking; dual Tg: −60.27°C (soft phase mobility) + 55.33°C (hard phase strength); 6.4× tensile strength increase, 4× toughness increase, 35.6× Young's modulus increase; 87% optical transmittance (400-800 nm); self-healing at room temperature; shape-memory behavior (higher Tg > RT); melt-processable → melt spinning for ionogel fibers; ionic conductivity 0.72-1.54×10⁻² S/m; strain sensor GF = 1.41 (small strain) to 4.15 (large strain); Zn²⁺ also validates ICR strategy; wireless respiratory monitoring demonstrated
nodes.scientific_questions: Extending ICR to other IL/polymer systems? Optimizing fiber geometry for wearable devices? Long-term cyclic stability of self-healing?

nodes.label: 压电效应—百岁铁电的守护者 (Piezoelectricity—An important property for ferroelectrics during last 100 years)
nodes.full_title: 压电效应—百岁铁电的守护者 (Piezoelectricity—An important property for ferroelectrics during last 100 years)
nodes.authors: 李飞 (Li Fei), 张树君 (Zhang Shu-Jun), 徐卓 (Xu Zhuo)
nodes.materials_raw: BaTiO₃, PZT (Pb(Zr₁₋ₓTiₓ)O₃), PMN-PT, PZN-PT relaxor ferroelectric single crystals, KNN (potassium sodium niobate)
nodes.methods_raw: Review of piezoelectric development history, LGD phenomenological theory, single-domain crystal measurements, neutron diffuse scattering
nodes.key_findings: Historical review: quartz (2 pC/N) → BaTiO₃ (190 pC/N) → PZT (300 pC/N) → PMN-PT single crystals (2500 pC/N); MPB concept: at Zr/Ti = 53/47, crystal structure transitions from tetragonal to rhombohedral, d₃₃ peaks; soft doping (Nb⁵⁺, Ta⁵⁺, La³⁺) increases d₃₃ to 500-800 pC/N; hard doping (Fe²⁺/³⁺, Mn²⁺/³⁺) reduces losses; fundamental trade-off: d₃₃ vs T_C (higher T_C → stronger polarization but harder to rotate); relaxor single crystals: first grown 1973, breakthrough by Shrout & Park 1995-1997; single-domain PMN-PT shows shear piezoelectric effect dominates near MPB; local structural heterogeneity (3-6 nm polar nanoregions) contributes to high piezoelectricity; neutron scattering confirms PMN has more local disorder than PZT
nodes.scientific_questions: Origin of soft doping mechanism (still unclear after 50+ years)? Role of local structural heterogeneity in piezoelectric enhancement? Breaking d₃₃-T_C trade-off?

nodes.label: Designing high piezoelectric properties at the BaTiO₃–PbZrO₃–PbTiO₃ phase boundary by Landau–Devonsh
nodes.full_title: Designing high piezoelectric properties at the BaTiO₃–PbZrO₃–PbTiO₃ phase boundary by Landau–Devonshire theory
nodes.authors: Yu Song, Xiaoming Shi, Dan Li, Jing Wang, Houbing Huang
nodes.materials_raw: BaTiO₃–PbZrO₃–PbTiO₃ (BT-PZ-PT) ternary system
nodes.methods_raw: Sixth-order Landau free energy polynomial, thermodynamic calculations, phase diagram computation, energy barrier (EB) analysis along minimum energy path
nodes.key_findings: Theoretical design method: add BT to PZT MPB composition to increase original phase energy barrier → find flatter EBs and higher d₃₃ at ternary phase boundaries; BT-PZ-PT ternary phase diagram computed, showing R→T phase transition with increasing BT content; RO phase boundary at x=0.6-0.7; highest d₃₃ near x=0.4; d₃₃ mainly influenced by dielectric constant rather than polarization at RO boundary; lowest energy barriers correlate with highest piezoelectric coefficients; method validated against experimental data
nodes.scientific_questions: Extending design method to other ternary/quaternary systems? Predicting optimal compositions computationally before synthesis?

nodes.label: energy barrier  analysis along minimum energy path
Piezoelectric response and free-energy instability in the perovskite crystals BaTiO₃, PbTiO₃, and Pb
nodes.full_title: Piezoelectric response and free-energy instability in the perovskite crystals BaTiO₃, PbTiO₃, and Pb(Zr,Ti)O₃
nodes.authors: Marko Budimir, Dragan Damjanovic, Nava Setter
nodes.materials_raw: BaTiO₃, PbTiO₃, PZT (tetragonal compositions)
nodes.methods_raw: LGD theory, Gibbs free energy analysis, orientation-dependent d₃₃* calculations, effects of composition/electric field/stress/temperature
nodes.key_findings: Free-energy profile flattening is universal origin of piezoelectric enhancement; demonstrated for composition (MPB), temperature (near phase transitions), electric field (antiparallel to polarization), and mechanical stress (compressive along polar axis); in tetragonal PZT near MPB, flattening is relatively isotropic → max d₃₃* along polar axis; in rhombohedral phases, flattening is anisotropic → max d₃₃* along nonpolar axes; PbTiO₃ under uniaxial compression: giant d₃₃* ~10³-10⁴ pC/N predicted near coercive stress; external fields can break isotropy → polarization rotation dominates; local structural disorder (as in relaxors) not essential for giant piezoelectricity—free-energy instability alone suffices
nodes.scientific_questions: Experimental verification of giant stress-induced piezoelectricity in PbTiO₃? Role of domain walls vs intrinsic instability? Extending to lead-free systems?

nodes.label: Ferroelectric materials toward next-generation electromechanical technologies
nodes.full_title: Ferroelectric materials toward next-generation electromechanical technologies
nodes.authors: Fei Li, Bo Wang, Xiangyu Gao, Dragan Damjanovic, Long-Qing Chen, Shujun Zhang
nodes.materials_raw: PZT ceramics, PMN-PT/PZN-PT relaxor crystals, lead-free ceramics (KNN, BNT), AlN/ScAlN thin films, PVDF polymers, LiNbO₃
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review of piezoelectric materials and applications
nodes.key_findings: PZT ceramics: d₃₃ = 200-700 pC/N, k₃₃ = 60-78%; relaxor-PT crystals: d₃₃ = 1500-2800 pC/N, k₃₃ = 88-95%; applications: medical ultrasound, actuators, piezoelectric fans/motors for chip cooling, BAW resonators for 5G/6G, piezocatalysis, wearable ultrasound; key challenge: d₃₃ vs T_C trade-off; strategies for enhancement: MPB engineering, domain engineering, local structural heterogeneity, flexoelectric effect; emerging: transparent ferroelectric crystals for photoacoustic imaging, flexible piezoelectric composites for e-skin, lead-free/biodegradable ferroelectrics; computational methods (DFT, phase-field) accelerating discovery
nodes.scientific_questions: Achieving d₃₃ > 2000 pC/N with T_C > 200°C? Lead-free alternatives matching PZT performance? Scalable crystal growth for commercial applications?

nodes.label: Comprehensive review of piezoelectric materials an
Nonresonant Raman Control of Ferroelectric Polarization
nodes.full_title: Nonresonant Raman Control of Ferroelectric Polarization
nodes.authors: Jiaojian Shi, Christian Heide, Haowei Xu et al.
nodes.materials_raw: LiNbO₃ (lithium niobate, bandgap 3.8 eV)
nodes.methods_raw: Mid-infrared (MIR) pulse excitation, femtosecond stimulated Raman scattering (FSRS), second harmonic generation (SHG), phase-sensitive SHG, first-principles calculations
nodes.key_findings: First demonstration of ferroelectric reversal via nonresonant Raman excitation; MIR pulses (5 μm) below bandgap excite coherent phonons through ISRS; quadratic field dependence confirms nonresonant origin; FSRS shows sign flip of Raman tensor at ~300 fs delay → ferroelectric switching; SHG phase flip of 180° confirms polarization reversal; atomic displacements up to 0.8 Å predicted; avoids carrier excitation and heating damage unlike resonant approaches; defines novel method for synthesizing hidden phases
nodes.scientific_questions: Extending to other ferroelectrics? Achieving non-volatile switching? Pulse shaping for controlled phase selection?

nodes.label: Suppressing piezoelectric screening effect at atomic scale for enhanced piezoelectricity
nodes.full_title: Suppressing piezoelectric screening effect at atomic scale for enhanced piezoelectricity
nodes.authors: Yue Sun, Sophia Shen, Weili Deng, Guo Tian et al.
nodes.materials_raw: ZnO nanorods + rare earth dopants (Y³⁺, La³⁺, Ce³⁺, Pr³⁺, Sm³⁺, Eu³⁺, Gd³⁺)
nodes.methods_raw: Hydrothermal synthesis, KPFM (Kelvin probe force microscopy), PFM (piezoresponse force microscopy), Mott-Schottky analysis, XRD, UV-vis, COMSOL simulation
nodes.key_findings: RE³⁺ doping suppresses piezoelectric screening effect at atomic scale; Y³⁺:ZnO shows 91.8% lower carrier concentration and 53.8% higher surface piezoelectric response; mechanism: RE³⁺ introduces exogenous holes + creates lattice defects → reduces free carriers → less screening; band gap widens after doping; hydrothermal synthesis at low temperature (compatible with flexible substrates); extranuclear electron configuration of RE³⁺ determines screening suppression efficiency
nodes.scientific_questions: Optimizing RE concentration for maximum enhancement? Extending to other piezoelectric semiconductors? Long-term stability of doped structures?

nodes.label: Piezoelectric charge induced hydrophilic poly(L-lactic acid) nanofiber for electro-topographical sti
nodes.full_title: Piezoelectric charge induced hydrophilic poly(L-lactic acid) nanofiber for electro-topographical stimulation enabling stem cell differentiation and expansion
nodes.authors: Guangxia Xia, Guobao Wang, Huiyi Yang, Wenbo Wang, Jian Fang
nodes.materials_raw: PLLA (poly(L-lactic acid)) piezoelectric nanofibers + dopamine (DA) + polydopamine (PDA)
nodes.methods_raw: Electrospinning, ultrasound-assisted PDA modification, XPS, AFM, COMSOL finite element analysis, neural stem cell culture, RT-qPCR, Western blot
nodes.key_findings: Ultrasound-activated piezoelectric charges on PLLA nanofibers drive in situ PDA polymerization via electrostatic attraction; contact angle drops from 117° to 0° (super-hydrophilic); modification is robust (survives repeated rinsing); processing time reduced from 24h (dipping) to 30 min (ultrasound); neural stem cells differentiate into neurons within 7 days without exogenous growth factors; differentiation level correlates with cell adhesion condition, not electrical stimulation intensity; oriented nanofibers enable directional cell expansion; PLLA is biodegradable (advantage over PVDF for implants)
nodes.scientific_questions: Mechanism of adhesion-dependent differentiation? Optimizing degradation rate for tissue regeneration? Scaling to clinical-size scaffolds?

nodes.label: Piezoelectric nanotransducers
nodes.full_title: Piezoelectric nanotransducers
nodes.authors: Christian Falconi
nodes.materials_raw: ZnO nanowires/nanowalls/nanohelices, GaN nanowires, MoS₂ monolayers, PVDF, BaTiO₃ nanoparticles, spider silk, M13 bacteriophage
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review of nanoscale piezoelectric advantages
nodes.key_findings: 16 unique advantages of piezoelectric nanotransducers: (1) larger fracture strains at nanoscale, (2) higher piezoelectric coefficients (e₃₃ ~100× bulk for few-atom nanowires), (3) higher force-to-displacement sensitivity, (4) low voltage operation (10 mV relays), (5) low power, (6) high speed (GHz resonators), (7) lightness, (8) single-layer piezoelectricity (MoS₂), (9) effective material engineering, (10) easier atomic simulations, (11) nano-enabled devices (tunneling triboelectrification), (12) transparency, (13) fabrication advantages (low-temperature, solution-based), (14) more device structures, (15) quantum effects, (16) biocompatibility; vertical compression best for energy harvesting; ZnO nanowires on PCB demonstrated for heartbeat detection
nodes.scientific_questions: Reliable large-scale fabrication? Packaging nanostructures into complete systems? Maximizing energy harvesting efficiency?

nodes.label: Comprehensive review of nanoscale piezoelectric ad
Piezoelectric polymers: theory, challenges and opportunities
nodes.full_title: Piezoelectric polymers: theory, challenges and opportunities
nodes.authors: Michael Smith, Sohini Kar-Narayan
nodes.materials_raw: PVDF, P(VDF-TrFE), odd-numbered Nylons (Nylon-11, Nylon-7), PLLA (poly-L-lactic acid), PDLA, polyureas, polyamides, polypeptides, polyesters, biopolymers (collagen, cellulose, silk, PHB)
nodes.methods_raw: Neumann's principle symmetry analysis, Voigt notation, Curie group classification, corona/thermal poling, drawing/stretching, piezoelectric tensor transformation
nodes.key_findings: Comprehensive symmetry analysis of piezoelectric polymers; centrosymmetric materials cannot be piezoelectric (proven via Neumann's principle); processing (drawing/poling) required to remove centre of symmetry; drawn polymers with ∞2 symmetry: only d₁₄ = −d₂₅ non-zero → requires helical chain (chirality); poled polymers with ∞m symmetry: d₁₅, d₃₁, d₃₃ non-zero → requires ferroelectric behavior; PLLA only needs chain alignment (no poling) due to chirality → shear-only piezoelectricity; P(VDF-TrFE) and Nylons need electrical poling (ferroelectric); PVDF d₃₃ ≈ 6-7 pC/N; PLLA d₁₄ ≈ 9.82 pC/N; no universal theory for piezoelectric mechanism in polymers; piezoelectric relaxation near Tg confirms amorphous regions contribute; biopolymers piezoelectric due to chirality without processing
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the universal mechanism of piezoelectricity in polymers? How do amorphous chains contribute to piezoelectric effect? Can non-chiral polymers be made shear-piezoelectric?

nodes.label: Piezoelectric polymeric scaffold materials as biomechanical cellular stimuli to enhance tissue regen
nodes.full_title: Piezoelectric polymeric scaffold materials as biomechanical cellular stimuli to enhance tissue regeneration
nodes.authors: Nowsheen Goonoo, Archana Bhaw-Luximon
nodes.materials_raw: PVDF, P(VDF-TrFE), P(VDF-HFP), PLLA, PHAs (PHB, PHBV), collagen, cellulose, chitosan, keratin, silk, BaTiO₃, graphene oxide, CNTs, TiO₂, ZnO
nodes.methods_raw: Corona poling, electrospinning, freeze-drying, 3D printing, in vivo implantation, cell culture (MSCs, osteoblasts, neurons, fibroblasts), signaling pathway analysis
nodes.key_findings: Bioelectricity guides tissue development via ion channels/pumps; piezoelectric scaffolds generate EFs mimicking endogenous bioelectricity; bone healing: piezoelectric PVDF activates Ca²⁺/calcineurin/NF-AT pathway → TGF-β/BMP expression; PLLA piezoelectricity depends on crystallinity × orientation product; PLLA in vivo: higher draw ratio → better fracture healing in cats; PHBV scaffolds: 23 μg Ca minerals vs 10 μg for PCL; nerve repair: piezoelectric scaffolds activate BDNF/TrkB → cAMP → neurite outgrowth; skin repair: wound EFs (100-200 mV/mm) guide cell migration; collagen piezoelectricity: d₁₄ = 0.2-2 pC/N; cornea: d₃₁ = 200-2250 pC/N (highest biological tissue); piezoelectric constant of bone: 0.7 pC/N; PLLA limitation: piezoelectricity disappears at 60-100°C
nodes.scientific_questions: Optimizing piezoelectric response for different tissue types? Controlling degradation rate to match tissue regeneration timeline? In vivo long-term performance of piezoelectric implants?

nodes.label: A review: Polyacrylonitrile as high-performance piezoelectric materials
nodes.full_title: A review: Polyacrylonitrile as high-performance piezoelectric materials
nodes.authors: Junzhu Tao, Yifan Wang, Xuekai Zheng, Chao Zhao, Xin Jin, Wenyu Wang, Tong Lin
nodes.materials_raw: Polyacrylonitrile (PAN), PAN/BaTiO₃, PAN/CuO, PAN/ZnO, PAN/TiO₂, PAN/NaNO₃, PAN/KNN, PAN/ILs, PVDF (comparison)
nodes.methods_raw: Electrospinning, wet spinning + mechanical stretching, thermal poling, organogel method, FTIR (2500/1230 cm⁻¹ peaks for zigzag/helical conformations), XRD (17° peak for interplanar spacing)
nodes.key_findings: PAN rediscovered as piezoelectric polymer (2019, Wang et al.); -CN dipole moment 3.5 Debye; |d₃₃| = 28 pC/N (untreated) → 78 pC/N (after 260°C treatment); piezoelectricity from planar zigzag conformation (TTTT); stable up to 550°C (far beyond PVDF's 70°C limit); four key concepts: (1) permanent molecular dipole, (2) dipole orientation, (3) retention of aligned dipoles, (4) high-temp resistant backbone; challenge: strong CN-CN interactions keep PAN in 3₁-helical (non-piezoelectric) conformation; classification by service temperature: general (<100°C), engineering (100-150°C), high-temp (150-200°C), ultra-high (>200°C); PAN with imide structure: 200-400°C; trapezoidal PAN: 550°C; electrospinning: best method - combines polarization + stretching; fiber diameter decrease → exponential voltage increase; PAN/BaTiO₃ (15wt%): 9.3V output; PAN/NaNO₃ (0.9wt%): highest current; PAN/CuO (0.5%): 1.25V, 118 nA; electrode design: 3D intercalation Sm-PMN-PT electrode → 290 μA/cm²; slit electrodes for acoustic harvesting: 39.85V at 115 dB
nodes.scientific_questions: Quantitative understanding of zigzag conformation and piezoelectric property relationship? Self-polarized PAN nanofibers? Scalable fabrication of high-temperature PAN piezoelectrics?

nodes.label: Enhancing tissue regeneration with self-healing elastic piezoelectricity for sustainable implants
nodes.full_title: Enhancing tissue regeneration with self-healing elastic piezoelectricity for sustainable implants
nodes.authors: Bankole I. Oladapo, Qi Zhao
nodes.materials_raw: PZT, PVDF, P(VDF-TrFE), BaTiO₃, ZnO nanowires, PDMS, ionomers (Zn ionomer), MGSBS (methyl thioglycolate-modified styrene-butadiene-styrene), lactate-based elastomers, bipyrazole crystals, Bi₂Te₃/Sb₂Te₃ thermoelectrics
nodes.methods_raw: Buckling/wavy microstructure design, coiled fiber fabrication, hybrid piezo-thermoelectric generators, self-healing characterization, finite element analysis
nodes.key_findings: Stretchable piezoelectric design: wavy/buckled ribbons, coiled fibers (750× strain increase), microporous structures (>40% porosity → sponge-like); PZT-silicon elastomer composite: 81.25 mW/cm² at 92wt% filler; self-healing piezoelectric materials: ionomers (Zn ionomer + PZT: 92% recovery at 6% strain, 73% at 8%); MGSBS: complete healing in 24h via electrostatic interactions; bipyrazole crystals: 3D H-bonding network enables self-repair; lactate-based elastomers: reduced crystallinity → self-healing; hybrid piezo-thermoelectric: 1.8 nW (thermoelectric) + 1.2 nW (piezoelectric) from PVDF-ZnO; thermoelectric electrode zigzag design for simultaneous thermal + mechanical harvesting; UN SDG alignment (Goal 3, 12); biocompatibility requirement: lead-free for implants
nodes.scientific_questions: Achieving both high piezoelectric output and self-healing? Biodegradable self-healing piezoelectrics? Long-term in vivo performance validation?

nodes.label: Fabrication and Characterization of Piezoelectric Polymer Composites and Cytocompatibility with Mese
nodes.full_title: Fabrication and Characterization of Piezoelectric Polymer Composites and Cytocompatibility with Mesenchymal Stem Cells
nodes.authors: Ruoyu Sheng, Jing Mu, Roman V. Chernozem, Yulia R. Mukhortova, Maria A. Surmeneva, Igor O. Pariy, Tim Ludwig, Sanjay Mathur, Changlu Xu, Roman A. Surmenev, Huinan Hannah Liu
nodes.materials_raw: PHB (poly(3-hydroxybutyrate)) + rGO (reduced graphene oxide, 0.2-1 wt%) + PANi (polyaniline, 2-3 wt%), PCL control
nodes.methods_raw: Electrospinning, PFM (piezoresponse force microscopy), EIS, FTIR-ATR, SEM-EDS, contact angle measurement, BMSC culture (direct/indirect contact), fluorescence microscopy, statistical analysis (ANOVA/Kruskal-Wallis)
nodes.key_findings: 0.2 wt% rGO in PHB shows strongest piezoelectric PFM signal (amplitude vs voltage quadratic dependence); higher rGO (1%) reduces piezoelectric response due to structural differences; rGO/PANi decrease fiber diameters (2.33→1.50 μm) and increase hydrophilicity (WCA 101.8°→88.4° for 3% PANi); 3% PANi improves PHB conductivity; BMSC adhesion density unaffected by rGO/PANi doping; cell aspect ratios decrease on composites vs pure PHB (more rounded morphology); pH unchanged after 24h culture (7.95-8.07) indicating no degradation products; PHB is biodegradable (advantage over PVDF); PFM provides qualitative piezoelectric mapping but quantitative d₃₃ measurement is challenging for soft polymers
nodes.scientific_questions: Quantitative d₃₃ measurement methods for soft polymer films? Optimizing rGO/PANi ratio for maximum piezoelectric + biological response? Long-term in vivo biodegradation and piezoelectric performance?

nodes.label: All-polymer piezo-ionic-electric electronics
nodes.full_title: All-polymer piezo-ionic-electric electronics
nodes.authors: Tianpei Xu, Long Jin, Yong Ao, Jieling Zhang, Yue Sun, Shenglong Wang, Yuanxiao Qu, Longchao Huang, Tao Yang, Weili Deng, Weiqing Yang
nodes.materials_raw: PVDF/Nafion/PVDF (PNP) sandwich structure; Nafion = perfluorosulfonate ionomer with proton conductivity
nodes.methods_raw: Laminated hot-pressing, XRD, FTIR, MD simulation, bend-self-polarization testing, direct piezoelectric charge method, EIS
nodes.key_findings: Novel "piezo-ionic-electric" working mechanism combining piezoelectric (PVDF) and piezoionic (Nafion) effects; bend-self-polarization: repeated bending activates piezoelectric response without high-voltage corona poling (~32 bends at 0.332 mm⁻¹ curvature); Nafion's piezoionic effect creates ion-based built-in field during bending → induces PVDF dipole alignment; d₃₃ = 80.70 pC/N (2.6× commercial PVDF's 31.10 pC/N); pressure sensitivity 51.50 mV/kPa; max peak power 34.66 mW/m²; PVDF β-phase content 41% at 100 MPa hot-pressing; MD simulation shows PVDF chain polarization reversal (14.80 D in -Z direction) upon Nafion interaction; T conformation fraction increases during interaction; phase of output current determined by bending direction (piezoionic effect controls Stage I); stable polarization after activation (piezoelectric effect controls Stage II)
nodes.scientific_questions: Extending to other ionomer/polymer combinations? Scaling for industrial production? Long-term stability of bend-activated polarization? Understanding the molecular-level coupling between piezoelectric and piezoionic effects?

nodes.label: An ultrasound-responsive hydrogel with piezoelectric-enhanced electrokinetic effect accelerates neur
nodes.full_title: An ultrasound-responsive hydrogel with piezoelectric-enhanced electrokinetic effect accelerates neurovascular regeneration for diabetic wound healing
nodes.authors: Zhengqian Fu, Fang Wang, Jiajun Qiu, Shiwei Guan, Shuhan Chen, Xiaoshuang Nie, Fang-Zhou Yao, Wen Gong, Ke Wang, Xuanyong Liu
nodes.materials_raw: Gelatin/PVA IPN hydrogel + KNN (0.92(Na₀.₅K₀.₅)NbO₃-0.02(Bi₀.₅Li₀.₅)TiO₃-0.06BaZrO₃) nanocrystals + rGO (KGGP hydrogel)
nodes.methods_raw: Dual crosslinking (Schiff base + diol-borate), freeze-thaw cycling, PFM, COMSOL simulation, EIS, ultrasound irradiation (1.0 MHz, 2.5 W/cm²), RNA-Seq, in vivo diabetic rat model, tube formation assay
nodes.key_findings: KGGP hydrogel achieves sonoelectric conversion via "piezoelectric-enhanced electrokinetic effect"; gelatin surface is negatively charged at pH 7.4 (isoelectric point 5.2) → forms EDL → streaming potential under ultrasound; KNN piezoelectric nanocrystals enhance streaming potential by releasing screening charges under compressive stress; rGO increases EDL charge density via capacitance; self-healing via reversible Schiff base + diol-borate bonds; conductivity 3.0 mS/cm (comparable to human skin); adhesion energy 70 J/m²; KNN piezopotential up to 1.56 V at 108 Pa (COMSOL); KGGP + US: enhanced Schwann cell migration, NGF/BDNF/VEGF secretion, PC12 neurite outgrowth; HUVEC tube formation: vessel length 22.4 mm (KGGP+US) vs 9.5 mm (GP+US); in vivo: increased PGP 9.5-positive nerve fibers and CD31-positive vessels in diabetic wounds; mechanism: KGGP+US activates PPAR pathway → Ca²⁺ influx via VGCC → AMPK → ATP synthesis → vesicle transport of neurotrophic factors
nodes.scientific_questions: Optimizing ultrasound parameters (frequency, power, duty cycle) for maximum therapeutic effect? Long-term nerve function recovery? Clinical translation pathway?

nodes.label: Piezoionic mechanoreceptors: Force-induced current generation in hydrogels
nodes.full_title: Piezoionic mechanoreceptors: Force-induced current generation in hydrogels
nodes.authors: Yuta Dobashi, Dickson Yao, Yael Petel, Tan Ngoc Nguyen, Mirza Saquib Sarwar, Yacine Thabet, Cliff L.W. Ng, Ettore Scabeni Glitz, Giao Tran Minh Nguyen, Cédric Plesse, Frédéric Vidal, Carl A. Michal, John D.W. Madden
nodes.materials_raw: Poly(acrylic acid-co-acrylamide) hydrogels (pAAm with AA comonomer), PVDF-HFP solid polymer electrolytes swollen with LiTFSI/PC, NaCl aqueous electrolytes
nodes.methods_raw: Indentation experiment (open-circuit voltage / short-circuit current), Pulsed-field-gradient NMR (diffusion coefficients), Poisson-Nernst-Planck poroelastic model, finite element simulation, 16-element sensor array fabrication
nodes.key_findings: Piezoionic mechanoreceptors generate ionic current from pressure-driven ion flux in hydrogels; charge density 4-6 orders of magnitude higher than triboelectric/piezoelectric devices (80 mC/cm² or 800 mC/cm³ vs 20 pC/cm² for PVDF); mechanism: pressure gradient creates convection of water and ions through pores → differential ion transport (cations vs anions move at different speeds due to different drag from polymer matrix) → net charge imbalance → voltage; Poisson-Nernst-Planck poroelastic model explains transient behavior; time constant τ = (1-2ν)ηL²/[2(1-ν)Gk] depends on viscosity η, length L, shear modulus G, Poisson ratio ν, permeability k; polarity reversal by changing mobile ion sign (AA→MAETAC); voltage crossover point matches diffusion coefficient crossover in PVDF-HFP/LiTFSI system (1.5-2.0 M); piezoionic coefficient a = -eNk(D+ - D-)/[h(D+ + D-)]; response time tunable from 30 ms (0.2 mm) to >10 s (2 mm), matching human mechanoreceptor range (3 ms to 2 s); 16-element array demonstrated on wrist for touch detection; peak power 0.85 mW/cm³ at impedance matching (500 Ω)
nodes.scientific_questions: Quantitative match between theory and experiment for ion-polymer interactions? Upper limits of power density in piezoionic materials? Long-term stability of piezoionic sensors? Integration with neural interfaces for prosthetics?

nodes.label: Piezoelectric Nanomaterial-Mediated Physical Signals Regulate Cell Differentiation for Regenerative
nodes.full_title: Piezoelectric Nanomaterial-Mediated Physical Signals Regulate Cell Differentiation for Regenerative Medicine
nodes.authors: He Li, Xueting Pan, Tianyun Wang, Zhenlin Fan, Hai Wang, Wenjie Ren
nodes.materials_raw: BaTiO₃ nanocrystals, ZnO nanowires, KNN, PVDF, PLLA, collagen, silk proteins, β-glycine, γ-glycine, hydroxyapatite, amino acids (17 of 20 are piezoelectric), fillipin proteins
nodes.methods_raw: PFM (piezoresponse force microscopy), hydrothermal synthesis, organometallic method, sol-gel, template method, molten salt method, ultrasound activation, in vivo implantation, cell culture with piezoelectric scaffolds
nodes.key_findings: Comprehensive review of piezoelectric nanomaterials for cell differentiation; 17 of 20 amino acids exhibit piezoelectricity (except glutamine, phenylalanine, tryptophan); β-glycine and γ-glycine have strong piezoelectric properties due to noncentrosymmetric crystal point groups; bone piezoelectricity from collagen (not hydroxyapatite); human tibia generates ~300 μV during walking; piezoelectric mechanism in inorganic materials: mechanical stress → ion displacement → dipole moment; in organic materials: molecular dipole reorientation; BaTiO₃: Ti⁴⁺ moves relative to O²⁻ in octahedral unit cell → inherent asymmetry → spontaneous polarization; PVDF β-phase: fluorine atoms on same side of carbon chain → piezoelectric; ultrasound (>20 kHz) activates piezoelectric materials via mechanical effect; cell pulling also activates piezoelectric scaffolds; Ca²⁺ signaling and membrane receptors are key mediators of piezoelectric-induced cell differentiation; applications: nerve regeneration, bone regeneration, muscle regeneration, vascular regeneration; PFM enables nanoscale piezoelectric characterization but accuracy limited by surface charges and small size
nodes.scientific_questions: Accurate quantitative measurement of piezoelectric coefficients at nanoscale? Mechanism of Ca²⁺ signaling pathway activation by piezoelectric stimulation? Optimal piezoelectric parameters (frequency, amplitude) for each tissue type?

nodes.label: Piezoelectric polymeric scaffold materials as biomechanical cellular stimuli to enhance tissue regen
nodes.full_title: Piezoelectric polymeric scaffold materials as biomechanical cellular stimuli to enhance tissue regeneration
nodes.authors: Nowsheen Goonoo, Archana Bhaw-Luximon
nodes.materials_raw: Collagen, PVDF, P(VDF-TrFE), PLLA, PHB/PHBV, cellulose, chitosan, keratin, silk, odd-numbered nylons (Nylon-11/7), hydroxyapatite
nodes.methods_raw: Literature review, signaling pathway analysis, piezoelectric coefficient comparison, bioelectricity mechanism analysis
nodes.key_findings: Review of biodegradable piezoelectric polymers for skin, skeletal, and nerve tissue engineering; biological tissues are inherently piezoelectric: bone d₁₄ = 0.7 pC/N, tendon = 2.0 pC/N, skin = 0.2 pC/N, cornea d₃₁ = 200-2250 pC/N; piezoelectricity of bone confirmed due to collagen (not hydroxyapatite); decollagenization eliminates piezoelectricity while demineralization does not; α-keratin in hair/wool/horn/hoof has right-handed α-helices with piezoelectric properties; cornea has orthogonally aligned collagen → anisotropic piezoelectricity (d₃₁ = 2250/600/200 pC/N for diagonal/vertical/horizontal cuts); sclera has randomly oriented collagen → lower piezoelectricity (31.8 pC/N); dehydration reduces piezoelectricity in biological tissues; wound healing electric field: 100-200 mV/mm at wound edge; bioelectricity transduction: ion channels/pumps → membrane potential changes → pH gradients → ion fluxes → electric fields → integrin/Notch/NF-κB/PTEN/Slug-Sox10 pathways → cell proliferation/differentiation; bone: mechanical deformation → negative charge in compression, positive in traction → hyperpolarization/depolarization → Ras signaling → osteogenesis; nerve: voltage-gated ion channels → action potential → Ca²⁺ influx → neurotransmitter release; BDNF/TrkB upregulation → cAMP → axon bursting; skin: wound EF → Golgi polarization → cell migration via cathodal attraction
nodes.scientific_questions: Quantitative correlation between piezoelectric scaffold properties and tissue regeneration outcomes? Long-term biodegradation kinetics matching tissue regeneration rate? Optimal scaffold porosity and piezoelectric coefficient for each tissue type?

nodes.label: Piezocatalytic Medicine: An Emerging Frontier using Piezoelectric Materials for Biomedical Applicati
nodes.full_title: Piezocatalytic Medicine: An Emerging Frontier using Piezoelectric Materials for Biomedical Applications
nodes.authors: Si Chen, Piao Zhu, Lijie Mao, Wencheng Wu, Han Lin, Deliang Xu, Xiangyu Lu, Jianlin Shi
nodes.materials_raw: BaTiO₃, ZnO, MoS₂, BiFeO₃, boron nitride nanotubes, PVDF, PLLA, cellulose nanofibers, piezoelectric polymers
nodes.methods_raw: Ultrasound activation, hydrothermal synthesis, sol-gel, DFT calculations, EPR (electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy), radical quenching experiments, energy band theory analysis, screening charge effect analysis
nodes.key_findings: "Piezocatalytic medicine" (PCM) defined as medical applications of piezocatalysis; two mechanisms: (1) energy band theory - piezoelectric potential tilts CB/VB → charge migration → redox reactions (e.g., CB tilted → e⁻ reduces O₂ to •O₂⁻, VB tilted → h⁺ oxidizes H₂O to •OH); (2) screening charge effect - surface-adsorbed screening charges participate in reactions when piezoelectric potential ≥ redox potential; applications: tumor treatment (ROS generation for cancer cell killing), antisepsis (antibacterial via ROS), organic degradation (toxic substance decomposition), tissue repair and regeneration (electric field stimulation), biosensing; advantages over photocatalysis: no tissue penetration limitation, can use mechanical energy from human motion/vibration; piezoelectric materials have simple components, tunable morphology; piezocatalysis discovered in 2010 (Xu et al.) when ZnO and BaTiO₃ decomposed water under ultrasound; ROS types: •O₂⁻, •OH, ¹O₂, H₂O₂; piezoelectric sensors: superior power density, enhanced energy conversion efficiency, simple structure, good scalability
nodes.scientific_questions: Precise control of ROS type and concentration for specific therapeutic outcomes? Biocompatibility and biodegradation of piezocatalysts in vivo? Combining piezocatalysis with immunotherapy? Long-term safety of piezoelectric nanomaterials in medical applications?

nodes.label: Biodegradable Piezoelectric-Conductive Integrated Hydrogel Scaffold for Repair of Osteochondral Defe
nodes.full_title: Biodegradable Piezoelectric-Conductive Integrated Hydrogel Scaffold for Repair of Osteochondral Defects
nodes.authors: Dingge Liu, Xinyu Wang, Chenyuan Gao, Zhihua Zhang, Qi Wang, Yin Pei, Haijun Wang, Yujing Tang, Ke Li, Yingjie Yu, Qing Cai, Xin Zhang
nodes.materials_raw: Decellularized cartilage ECM (dECM) + diphenylalanine (FF) peptide (piezoelectric upper layer), gelatin + PEDOT (conductive lower layer), fibrin sealant (bonding layer)
nodes.methods_raw: FF self-assembly on dECM pores, gradient-freezing for macroporous structure, cyclic voltammetry, EIS, finite element analysis (COMSOL), CCK-8 viability, Alcian blue/Safranin O staining, ALP/Alizarin Red staining, qPCR, RNA-Seq, micro-CT, MRI, in vivo Parma pig osteochondral defect model (8mm diameter, 5mm depth), Seller's/Wakitani's/ICRS scoring, collagen fiber orientation analysis
nodes.key_findings: Bilayer scaffold design: upper dECM-P (FF-modified dECM) for chondrogenic differentiation, lower Gel-PC (PEDOT + gelatin) for osteogenic differentiation; FF peptide d₃₃ = 18 pm/V, meets cartilage (25 mV) and bone (40-80 mV) stimulation requirements; FF modification: 2 treatments optimal (more blocks pores); dECM-P output: ~20 mV, 0.8 μA under cyclic compression; bilayer D-PC: ~50 mV potential difference (COMSOL matches experiment); positive charges on upper surface attract BMSCs → chondrogenic differentiation; negative charges on lower surface → osteogenic differentiation; Ca²⁺ influx via TRPV4 and VGCC mediates piezoelectric-induced differentiation; RNA-Seq: upper layer upregulates ACTA2, ANKRD1, GPNMB, NQO1 (chondrogenesis); lower layer upregulates DSG2, CAMK2D (osteogenesis); TGF-β signaling pathway and calcium signaling pathway enriched; PIEZO2 mechanosensitive channel involved; in vivo Parma pig: D-PC group shows complete cartilage coverage, highest BV/TV (0.531), best collagen fiber alignment (47.75% within ±20°); 6-month follow-up shows superior repair
nodes.scientific_questions: Optimizing FF peptide loading density for maximum piezoelectric output? Long-term (>1 year) in vivo performance? Clinical translation pathway for osteochondral repair? Extending to other tissue interfaces (bone-tendon, osteochondral)?

nodes.label: Piezoionic Elastomers by Phase and Interface Engineering for High-Performance Energy-Harvesting Iono
nodes.full_title: Piezoionic Elastomers by Phase and Interface Engineering for High-Performance Energy-Harvesting Ionotronics
nodes.authors: Weiyan Zhu, Baohu Wu, Zhouyue Lei, Peiyi Wu
nodes.materials_raw: PVDF-HFP + EMIM TFSI (ionic liquid) + C₂mpyr TFSI (ionic plastic crystal, IPC) - piezoionic elastomer
nodes.methods_raw: AFM phase imaging, XRD, synchrotron WAXS-SAXS, ¹⁹F NMR, isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC), Raman spectroscopy, in situ ATR-FTIR, 2D correlation spectroscopy (2DCOS), molecular dynamics simulation, COMSOL finite element analysis, time-temperature superposition rheology
nodes.key_findings: Phase and interface engineering strategy for piezoionic elastomers; three design principles: (1) microphase-separated structure (IPC poor compatibility with polymer → ~2 μm hard domains), (2) intermediate phase (shared anion between IL and IPC → compatibility bridge), (3) enhanced cation-anion mobility disparity (cations tethered to polymer via cation-dipole interactions → free anions move under pressure); piezoionic coefficient ~6.0 mV/kPa (60× improvement over ionogels, 3× over best hydrogels); open-circuit voltage ~90 mV, short-circuit current ~92 nA at 15 kPa; power density 1.3 μW/cm³ (new benchmark, exceeds PAM hydrogel 0.85, ZnO 0.4, nylon-11 0.6); mechanism: [TFSI]⁻ anions are mobile ions (confirmed by Raman: free vs bound anion peaks at 740/745 cm⁻¹); cations bound to polymer via cation-dipole interaction with ─CF₂─ groups; intermediate phase increases free anion fraction; in situ ATR-FTIR tracks anion movement under pressure (1052 cm⁻¹ peak decreases at pressurized terminal); MD simulation: [TFSI]⁻ diffusion coefficient increases from 2.0×10⁻¹¹ to 3.32×10⁻¹¹ m²/s at 15 kPa; mechanical properties: 508% elongation, 0.36 MPa Young's modulus, toughness ~1400 J/m² (100× hydrogels, 10× ionogels, exceeds natural cartilage); self-healing at 80°C in 30 min, 93-99% efficiency, piezoionic performance maintained; energy conversion efficiency ~1.8% (comparable to PZT nanoribbons 1.76%); applications demonstrated: acoustic perception (A2 scale, voice syllables), pressure mapping, robotic arm control, logical processing (AND gates)
nodes.scientific_questions: Scaling up production of piezoionic elastomers? Long-term cycling stability? Integration with wireless communication systems? Optimizing IPC content for specific applications? Understanding the role of intermediate phase at molecular level?

nodes.label: Electroactive Biomaterials and Systems for Cell Fate Determination and Tissue Regeneration: Design a
nodes.full_title: Electroactive Biomaterials and Systems for Cell Fate Determination and Tissue Regeneration: Design and Applications
nodes.authors: Zhirong Liu, Xingyi Wan, Zhong Lin Wang, Linlin Li
nodes.institution: Beijing Institute of Nanoenergy and Nanosystems CAS
nodes.materials_raw: Graphene/rGO/GO, CNTs (SWCNT/MWCNT), carbon dots, PEDOT/PEDOT:PSS, PPy, PANi, Au nanoparticles/nanorods/nanowires, Ag/Zn/Ti/B/Sr/Mg/Cu/Pt/MoS₂, MXene quantum dots, BaTiO₃, ZnO, PVDF, PLLA, collagen, silk fibroin, GelMA, chitosan, PCL, PLA
nodes.methods_raw: Literature review, electrostimulation parameter optimization, cell culture on conductive/piezoelectric scaffolds, implantable energy harvesters (IEHs), triboelectric nanogenerators (TENG), piezoelectric nanogenerators (PENG)
nodes.key_findings: Comprehensive review of electroactive biomaterials for nerve, bone, and cardiac tissue engineering; endogenous bioelectricity: membrane potential -10 to -90 mV for different cell types; hyperpolarization triggers stem cell differentiation, depolarization maintains stemness; endogenous EF in embryos: 40-140 mV/mm; wound EF: 40-200 mV/mm; galvanotaxis directs cell migration; piezoelectric biomacromolecules: collagen (d₁₄ = 0.7 pC/N for femur), keratin (α-helices), elastin (blood vessels), DNA, proteins/peptides, viruses; bone = natural piezoelectric composite (22% collagen + 69% HAp); Wolff's law: electrical signal from mechanical stress promotes bone growth/remodeling; dry bone: piezoelectricity; wet bone: streaming potential; three categories of conductive biomaterials: carbon-based (graphene, CNTs, C-dots), metals/metal oxides (Au, Ag, Zn), conductive polymers (PPy, PANi, PEDOT); conductivity range 10⁻⁷ to 10³ S/cm; tissue-specific stiffness matching: brain ~1 kPa, myocardium ~10 kPa, bone 25-50 kPa; electrostimulation parameters: 0.1-6 V, 1-100 Hz, ms-range pulses, hours/day for days; biosafety concerns: CNTs induce ROS/inflammation, C-dots accumulate in organs, metal ion toxicity, conductive polymer degradation products
nodes.scientific_questions: Long-term biosafety and biodistribution of conductive nanomaterials? Optimal electrostimulation parameters for each tissue type? Combining electroactive biomaterials with gene therapy? Clinical translation of implantable energy harvesters?

nodes.label: Beijing Institute of Nanoenergy and Nanosystems CAS
Piezoelectric charge induced hydrophilic poly(L-lactic acid) nanofiber for electro-topographical sti
nodes.full_title: Piezoelectric charge induced hydrophilic poly(L-lactic acid) nanofiber for electro-topographical stimulation enabling stem cell differentiation and expansion
nodes.authors: Guangbo Xia, Guobao Wang, Huiyi Yang, Wenbo Wang, Jian Fang
nodes.materials_raw: Poly(L-lactic acid) (PLLA) nanofibers, polydopamine (PDA) coating, L-PLLA (lower piezoelectric control)
nodes.methods_raw: Electrospinning (14 kV, 2 mL/h, 13 cm), ultrasound-assisted PDA modification (40 kHz, 400W), contact angle measurement, XPS, FTIR, AFM, XRD, COMSOL finite element analysis, piezoelectric nanogenerator (PENG) fabrication, oscilloscope measurement, neural stem cell (NSC) culture from E13.5 SD rat cortex, RT-qPCR, Western blot (GFAP, Tuj1), cytoskeleton staining, biodegradation testing (PBS 40°C)
nodes.key_findings: Novel "electro-topographical stimulation" concept: piezoelectric nanofibers generate in situ electrical signals from cell traction forces without external power; ultrasound activates piezoelectric charges on PLLA nanofibers → electrostatic attraction with dopamine (DA) → in situ PDA polymerization on fiber surface; contact angle 117° → 0° (superhydrophilic); PDA modification robust after repeated rinsing (H-bond between C=O in PLLA and -NH₂ in PDA); 30 min ultrasound treatment vs 24 h conventional dipping; COMSOL: cell traction (10 nN) on single fiber → 20 kPa stress → 10.11 mV piezoelectric potential; NSCs on PDA+PLLA differentiate into neurons (Tuj1+) within 7 days without exogenous growth factors; differentiation level correlates with cell adhesion condition, not electrical stimulation intensity; L-PLLA (lower piezoelectric) shows less PDA modification and less differentiation; oriented nanofibers enable directional cell expansion; biodegradation: ~20% weight loss for PDA+PLLA after testing period; piezoelectric signal degrades over time but PDA+PLLA retains higher signal; mechanism: cell traction → fiber deformation → piezoelectric potential → voltage-dependent Ca²⁺ channels (VGCCs) open → Ca²⁺ influx → gene transcription → differentiation
nodes.scientific_questions: Quantitative relationship between piezoelectric potential magnitude and differentiation efficiency? Long-term in vivo performance of PDA+PLLA scaffolds? Extending to other stem cell types (MSCs, iPSCs)? Optimizing PDA loading for maximum cell adhesion + piezoelectric performance?

nodes.label: A more biofriendly piezoelectric material (Perspectives/Insights)
nodes.full_title: A more biofriendly piezoelectric material (Perspectives/Insights)
nodes.authors: Linping Wang, Run-Wei Li
nodes.institution: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering CAS
nodes.materials_raw: HFPD (HOCH₂(CF₂)₃CH₂OH, 2,2,3,3,4,4-hexafluoropentane-1,5-diol) ferroelectric molecular crystal, PVA (polyvinyl alcohol) composite, γ-glycine (comparison)
nodes.methods_raw: Perspective/commentary on Zhang et al. Science 2024 paper; ferroelectrochemistry analysis
nodes.key_findings: HFPD molecular crystal: d₃₃ ~138 pC/N under no poling condition (13× greater than amino acid piezoelectrics); g₃₃ ~2450 × 10⁻³ Vm/N (voltage constant); mechanism: 2D hydrogen bond network from O–H···O interactions of terminal hydroxyl groups; anisotropy of Young's modulus from ordered fluorine atom arrangement; HFPD-PVA (2:1) composite: d₃₃ = 34.3 pC/N, flexible, biodegradable, biosafe (in vivo rat implantation confirmed); biodegradable ferroelectric molecular crystal is a milestone; soluble in various solvents; potential: drug delivery carriers, self-powered transient energy-harvesting devices, regenerative medicine scaffolds; future: elastification techniques for elastic ferroelectrics
nodes.scientific_questions: Can HFPD-based materials achieve sufficient mechanical strength for load-bearing implants? How does biodegradation rate match tissue regeneration timeline? Scalability of HFPD crystal production?

nodes.label: Perspective/commentary on Zhang et al. Science 202
Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering CAS
Biocompatible Piezoelectric Elastomer for Self-Powered Electronics
nodes.full_title: Biocompatible Piezoelectric Elastomer for Self-Powered Electronics
nodes.authors: Qiuyue Hu, Yuting Zhang, Xiaocui Rao, Linping Wang, Liang Gao, Fangzhou Li, Haoyu Shen, Liang Pan, Ben-Lin Hu
nodes.materials_raw: HFPD (HOCH₂(CF₂)₃CH₂OH) ferroelectric molecular crystal filler + WPU (waterborne polyurethane) elastic matrix
nodes.methods_raw: Solution blending (aqueous HFPD + WPU), solvent evaporation/recrystallization, XRD, SEM, EDS mapping, stress-strain testing, cyclic tensile testing, dielectric permittivity/loss measurement, piezoelectric coefficient (d₃₃) measurement, linear motor impact testing, confocal microscopy (Calcein-AM/PI staining), CCK-8 viability assay
nodes.key_findings: HFPD/WPU composite: d₃₃ = 26 pC/N without strain, 44 pC/N at 40% strain, ~10 pC/N maintained at 200% strain (elastic piezoelectric!); no poling needed; optimal HFPD:WPU = 1:1 (balance of elasticity and piezoelectricity); Young's modulus comparable to human skin (0.01-10 MPa range); elongation at break ~400% for pure WPU; g₃₃ = 361.47 V·m/N (surpasses piezoelectric ceramics and PVDF); output voltage 0.855 V, current 0.528 μA at 1:1 ratio; sensitivity 62.4 mV/N (below 15N); frequency-dependent output (exponential relationship); at 40% strain + low frequency, current increases 6 orders of magnitude vs unstretched state; >4000 impact cycles durability; biocompatibility: >92% cell viability at 10 mg/mL (MC3T3-E1 and L929 cells); WPU biodegradable; water-based fabrication ensures safety; applications: wearable sensors, self-powered electronics, cardiac pacemakers, neural stimulators, smart textiles, soft robotics
nodes.scientific_questions: How does HFPD crystal size/orientation affect composite performance? Long-term in vivo biocompatibility and degradation? Can the material be 3D-printed for custom device geometries? Integration with wireless data transmission for implantable devices?

nodes.label: HFPD ₃CH₂OH) ferroelectric molecular crystal filler + WPU  e
Fabrication and Characterization of Piezoelectric Polymer Composites and Cytocompatibility with Mese
nodes.full_title: Fabrication and Characterization of Piezoelectric Polymer Composites and Cytocompatibility with Mesenchymal Stem Cells
nodes.authors: Ruoyu Sheng, Jing Mu, Roman V. Chernozem, Yulia R. Mukhortova, Maria A. Surmeneva, Igor O. Pariy, Tim Ludwig, Sanjay Mathur, Changlu Xu, Roman A. Surmenev, Huinan Hannah Liu
nodes.materials_raw: Poly(3-hydroxybutyrate) (PHB, Mn≈500 kDa, biodegradable) + reduced graphene oxide (rGO, 0.2-1 wt%) or polyaniline (PANi, 2-3 wt%); polycaprolactone (PCL, non-piezoelectric control)
nodes.methods_raw: Electrospinning (6 kV, 10 cm distance, 0.5 mL/h, 200 rpm collector), AFM topography, PFM (piezoelectric force microscopy, 100 kHz, 1-3 V AC), EIS (1 Hz-1 MHz in rSBF), FTIR-ATR, contact angle (OWRK method), SEM/EDS, DAPI/AF-488 phalloidin staining, fluorescence microscopy, direct/indirect BMSC culture (10,000 cells/cm², 24h), statistical analysis (ANOVA/Tukey HSD or Kruskal-Wallis/Benjamini-Hochberg FDR)
nodes.key_findings: PHB0.2rGO (0.2 wt% rGO) showed best piezoelectric response among composites; rGO/PANi doping decreased fiber diameter and increased hydrophilicity (WCA: PHB 101.84° → PHB3PANi 88.43°, PHB1rGO 92.56°); PFM confirmed piezoelectric nature in all samples (linear out-of-plane PFM signal vs 1-3 V AC); BMSC adhesion density not affected by nanofiller doping; BMSC aspect ratios increased on composite scaffolds vs PHB control (more elongated morphology, beneficial for nerve regeneration); electrospun PHB fibers showed defect-free morphology; PHB biodegradable and biocompatible; surface energy increased with doping (PHB 13.95 mN/m → PHB3PANi 20.79 mN/m); direct vs indirect culture conditions studied; PCL control (non-piezoelectric biodegradable polymer) included
nodes.scientific_questions: Long-term in vivo biocompatibility and degradation of PHB composites? Optimal rGO/PANi content for maximum piezoelectric + biological performance? Can PHB composites promote osteogenic or neural differentiation (not just adhesion)? Scaling up electrospinning for clinical-size scaffolds?

nodes.label: Racemic Amino Acid Piezoelectric Transducer
nodes.full_title: Racemic Amino Acid Piezoelectric Transducer
nodes.authors: Sarah Guerin, Joseph O'Donnell, Ehtsham U. Haq, Cian McKeown, Christophe Silien, Fernando M.F. Rhen, Tewfik Soulimane, Syed A.M. Tofail, Damien Thompson
nodes.materials_raw: DL-alanine racemic amino acid single crystals and polycrystalline films
nodes.methods_raw: DFT (density functional theory) piezoelectric tensor prediction, crystal growth (25% isopropyl alcohol), d₃₃ piezometer measurement, PFM (piezoeforce microscopy), transmission XRD, SEM, manual compression voltage testing, LABVIEW data collection
nodes.key_findings: DL-alanine (racemic mixture) shows longitudinal piezoelectricity, unlike L-alanine (which has zero net polarization due to antiparallel dipole cancellation); DL-alanine parallel packing gives net polarization; DFT-predicted d₃₃ = 10.3 pm/V, e₃₃ = 0.68 C/m² (comparable to γ-glycine 0.81); measured d₃₃ = 4.8 pC/N max (polycrystalline), 9.1 pm/V (PFM single crystal); g₃₃ = 0.47 Vm/N, g₂₄ = 0.82 Vm/N (exceeding PZT 0.25 Vm/N and BiB₃O₆ 0.54 Vm/N); ε~2.4 (low, gives high voltage constants); open circuit voltage 0.8V under manual compression (2× phage viruses and γ-glycine, 4× MoS₂); net molecular chirality NOT prerequisite for piezoelectricity; DL-alanine elastic stiffness 7-67 GPa; no poling needed
nodes.scientific_questions: Can other racemic amino acids show enhanced piezoelectricity? How does crystal packing affect polycrystalline film performance? Can DL-alanine films be integrated into flexible biosensors?

nodes.label: DL-alanine racemic amino acid single crystals and polycrysta
Nonresonant Raman Control of Ferroelectric Polarization
nodes.full_title: Nonresonant Raman Control of Ferroelectric Polarization
nodes.authors: Jiaojian Shi, Christian Heide, Haowei Xu, Yuejun Shen, Meredith Henstridge, Isabel Sedwick, Anudeep Mangu, Xinyue Peng, Shangjie Zhang, Mariano Trigo, Tony F. Heinz, Ju Li, Keith A. Nelson, Edoardo Baldini, Jian Zhou, Shambhu Ghimire, David A. Reis, Aaron M. Lindenberg
nodes.materials_raw: LiNbO₃ (lithium niobate, 3.8 eV bandgap, prototypical ferroelectric), α-LiIO₃ (non-ferroelectric control)
nodes.methods_raw: Mid-infrared (MIR, 5 μm) nonresonant Raman excitation, femtosecond stimulated Raman scattering (FSRS), second harmonic generation (SHG), phase-sensitive SHG, first-principles calculations, transient transmission measurements
nodes.key_findings: First experimental demonstration of ferroelectric reversal via nonresonant Raman excitation; MIR pulses (well below bandgap) drive large-amplitude soft mode displacements (~0.8 Å predicted); phonon amplitudes scale quadratically with MIR field (ISRS origin confirmed); FSRS peak sign flip at ~300 fs delay indicates ferroelectric switching; SHG 180° phase flip confirms polarization reversal; reversal occurs within ~50 fs window; nonresonant approach avoids carrier excitation and heating damage (vs resonant 800 nm excitation which saturates); Raman force ∝ (∂ε/∂Q)E², 1-10 nN forces, angstrom displacements within 100 fs; α-LiIO₃ control shows no sign flip (confirms ferroelectric-specific); defines novel method for synthesizing hidden phases with reduced energy consumption
nodes.scientific_questions: Can this approach achieve permanent (not transient) ferroelectric switching? Extending to other ferroelectrics beyond LiNbO₃? Can shaped pulse trains improve switching efficiency?

nodes.label: Design and Manufacturing of Piezoelectric Biomaterials for Bioelectronics and Biomedical Application
nodes.full_title: Design and Manufacturing of Piezoelectric Biomaterials for Bioelectronics and Biomedical Applications
nodes.authors: Zhuomin Zhang, Zhenqi Wang, Xuemu Li, Yi Zheng, Zhengbao Yang
nodes.materials_raw: Amino acids (glycine, DL-alanine), peptides (diphenylalanine FF), proteins (collagen, silk, keratin, elastin, M13 phage), polysaccharides (chitin, chitosan, cellulose), synthetic biomaterials (PLLA, PVDF, PHB), hydroxyapatite
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review; DFT calculations, high-throughput screening, molecular engineering (H/F substitution, supramolecular coassembly, genetic modification, H-bond heterostructure, chemical crosslinking), manufacturing: mechanical (exfoliation, annealing, 3D printing, dip-coating, spin coating, template confinement), electric field (electrospinning, electrospray, epitaxial growth, polymerization, hydrogelation), magnetic field, thermal (vapor deposition, freeze casting)
nodes.key_findings: Most comprehensive review of piezoelectric biomaterials to date; covers full pipeline from computational design → molecular engineering → manufacturing → applications; 20 non-centrosymmetric point groups can be piezoelectric; β-glycine d₁₆ = 195 pm/V (shear, comparable to ceramics); piezoelectric biomaterials categories: amino acids, peptides, proteins/tissues, polysaccharides/tissues, synthetic; emerging applications: neuromodulation (deep brain, peripheral nerve, gut-brain axis), piezocatalytic ROS medicine (tumor, antibacterial, organic degradation), piezocatalytic materials synthesis (gas evolution, polymerization, mineralization); key challenges: weak piezoelectricity, large-scale manufacturing, reliable characterization, body integration; 2021 Nobel Prize (Piezo1/Piezo2) connection
nodes.scientific_questions: Can computational screening identify new piezoelectric biomaterials? How to bridge the gap between molecular design and macroscale device performance? Standardizing characterization methods across the field?

nodes.label: Ultra-soft organic combined film with piezoelectricity induced by liquid-liquid interface polar engi
nodes.full_title: Ultra-soft organic combined film with piezoelectricity induced by liquid-liquid interface polar engineering
nodes.authors: Yongkang Zhang, Xiaonan Hu, Zhaonan Yan, Siyu Zhang, Jiling Zhao, Hao Sun, Shuhai Liu, Yong Qin
nodes.materials_raw: PEG (polyethylene glycol) + SIS (polystyrene-block-polyisoprene-block-polystyrene) combined film
nodes.methods_raw: Liquid-liquid interface polar engineering (oil-water casting), DFT calculations, SEM/EDS mapping, FTIR, XRD, DSC, tensile testing, d₃₃ piezoelectric measurement, 60-day stability testing
nodes.key_findings: Novel "liquid-liquid interface polar engineering" strategy; PEG (hydrophilic) extracted by aqueous phase, SIS (hydrophobic) stays in oil phase → asymmetric polar structure; d₃₃ = 22.9 pC/N (comparable to PVDF ~30 pC/N); softness ~1×10⁻⁶ Pa⁻¹ (comparable to skin, cartilage, aorta; orders of magnitude better than PVDF 3.7×10⁻¹⁰); breaking elongation 1100%; stable performance for 60 days without attenuation; film size >12 inches (comparable to silicon wafer); DFT shows PEG-H₂O stronger adsorption than SIS-H₂O; three-layer structure (PEG-rich bottom, mixed middle, SIS-rich top); EDS confirms oxygen gradient (high on lower surface, low on upper); FTIR confirms polarity asymmetry; both SIS and PEG are linear polymers with low steric hindrance → high softness; physical entanglement = physical crosslinking
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the piezoelectric coefficient be further improved? Long-term in vivo biocompatibility? Can this approach work with other polymer pairs? Mechanism of liquid-liquid interface polarization at molecular level?

nodes.label: Dynamic β‑Amino Sulfonamides for the Synthesis of Covalent Adaptable Networks
nodes.full_title: Dynamic β‑Amino Sulfonamides for the Synthesis of Covalent Adaptable Networks
nodes.authors: Stephan Maes, Loc Tan Nguyen, Johan M. Winne, Filip E. Du Prez
nodes.materials_raw: β-amino sulfonamides (BASA), vinyl sulfonamides (VSA), covalent adaptable networks (CANs)
nodes.methods_raw: Aza-Michael reaction kinetics, ¹H NMR, rheological analysis, thermomechanical testing, 5 recycling cycles
nodes.key_findings: BASA as novel dynamic thermoreversible linkers for CANs; faster amine exchange via retro-aza-Michael at 140-180°C vs β-amino amides (BAA); vinyl sulfonamide monomers resist homopolymerization (unlike acrylamides from BAAs); tunable CAN properties; mechanical/rheological performance retained over 5 recycling cycles; hydrolytically stable
nodes.scientific_questions: Expanding BASA chemistry to other network architectures? Lowering the activation temperature window?

nodes.label: Access to Microstructurally Complex Block Copolymers via Switchable Ring-Opening Polymerization of C
nodes.full_title: Access to Microstructurally Complex Block Copolymers via Switchable Ring-Opening Polymerization of Cyclic Ester Mixtures
nodes.authors: David J. E. Seed, Amelia B. Milner, George R. Walker, Rachel H. Platel
nodes.materials_raw: Lactide (LA), rac-β-butyrolactone (rac-β-BL), ε-caprolactone (ε-CL), yttrium amine bis(phenolate) catalyst
nodes.methods_raw: One-pot switchable ROP, selective catalyst design, NMR characterization
nodes.key_findings: Self-switchable ROP of three cyclic esters to (AB)ₓ(BC)ᵧ block copolymers; catalyst discriminates completely between three monomers; switch controlled by presence/absence of LA; individual block lengths determined by initial monomer concentrations; first example of three-cyclic-ester switchable polymerization
nodes.scientific_questions: Extending to more than three monomers? Controlling block sequence with higher precision?

nodes.label: Dynamic Covalent Polymer Networks: A Molecular Platform for Designing Functions beyond Chemical Recy
nodes.full_title: Dynamic Covalent Polymer Networks: A Molecular Platform for Designing Functions beyond Chemical Recycling and Self-Healing
nodes.authors: Ning Zheng, Yang Xu, Qian Zhao, Tao Xie
nodes.materials_raw: Dynamic covalent polymer networks (DCPN), vitrimers, transesterification networks, transcarbamoylation networks, Diels-Alder networks, disulfide networks, imine networks, boronic ester networks
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review of >30 types of dynamic covalent bonds, bond exchange kinetics, topology manipulation
nodes.key_findings: DCPN bridges thermoplastics and thermosets; beyond recycling/self-healing: shape-shifting materials, artificial muscles, microfabrication; molecular mechanisms for topology manipulation: living chain growth, template-guided switching, topology isomerizable networks; "living networks" concept; 3D/4D morphing; more than 30 different dynamic covalent bonds demonstrated in past decade; transesterification and transcarbamoylation most relevant to commodity polymers
nodes.scientific_questions: Predicting dynamic bond behavior in complex network topologies? Combining multiple dynamic bonds in single network? Scaling up DCPN for industrial applications?

nodes.label: Comprehensive review of >30 types of dynamic coval
Pendent No More: Direct Backbone Integration of Stenhouse Salt Enables Multi-Responsive Commodity Po
nodes.full_title: Pendent No More: Direct Backbone Integration of Stenhouse Salt Enables Multi-Responsive Commodity Polyurethanes
nodes.authors: Livius F. Muff, Lauren Helwig, Arnab Nandi, Christopher M. Bates, Craig J. Hawker, Javier Read de Alaniz
nodes.materials_raw: Stenhouse salts (structural analogues of DASAs), polyurethane backbones, photoacid generators
nodes.methods_raw: Step-growth polycondensation, photolithographic patterning, UV/visible light switching, mechanical testing
nodes.key_findings: Stenhouse salts directly integrated into PU backbones (not pendent groups); mechanically robust: strain >1100%, tensile strength 44 MPa; fully reversible colorimetric response (ΔE >50) to acids, bases, amines, nerve agent mimics; <1 wt% chromophore loading; no leaching; photolithographic patterning at micrometer scale; symmetric Stenhouse salt design enables backbone integration; metastable closed form simplifies synthesis; negative photochromism with visible light; multi-responsive: chemical + optical stimuli
nodes.scientific_questions: Extending to other polymer backbones beyond PU? Improving switching kinetics? Integration with wearable sensing platforms?

nodes.label: Controlled Synthesis of Lipoate Homopolymers via Reversible Addition−Fragmentation Chain Transfer Po
nodes.full_title: Controlled Synthesis of Lipoate Homopolymers via Reversible Addition−Fragmentation Chain Transfer Polymerization
nodes.journal: Journal of the American Chemical Society 2025, 147, 38796-38806
nodes.authors: Kenny Lee, Shiwei Han, Parker T. Morris, Christopher M. Bates, Craig J. Hawker, Cyrille Boyer
nodes.materials_raw: Polylipoates (PLp) from α-lipoic acid, trithiocarbonate RAFT agents
nodes.methods_raw: RAFT polymerization, GPC molecular weight analysis, kinetics studies, depolymerization stability testing, light-triggered depolymerization
nodes.key_findings: RAFT overcomes two key challenges of polylipoate synthesis: backbiting and spontaneous depolymerization (low ceiling temperature); linear Mn vs conversion relationship (controlled polymerization); Mn precisely controlled 3.6-62.6 kg/mol by RAFT agent ratio; RAFT end-groups suppress depolymerization (>2 weeks stable in solution and bulk); trithiocarbonate end-group enables light-triggered on-demand depolymerization back to monomer; closed-loop recyclability demonstrated; extended to degradable block copolymers; α-lipoic acid widely available as health supplement (scalable)
nodes.scientific_questions: Can RAFT control be maintained at higher molecular weights? Extending to complex architectures (star, brush)? In vivo applications of biocompatible polylipoates?

nodes.label: Recent Advances in the Design of Ionenes: Toward Convergence with High-Performance Polymers
nodes.full_title: Recent Advances in the Design of Ionenes: Toward Convergence with High-Performance Polymers
nodes.authors: Jason E. Bara, Kathryn E. O'Harra
