nodes.materials_raw: Ionenes (main-chain cationic polymers), ionic liquids (ILs), poly(ILs), ammonium/phosphonium/imidazolium/benzimidazolium ionenes, polyimide-like ionenes, polyarylamide-like ionenes
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review of ionene synthesis (diamine + dihaloalkane condensation), comparison with polyelectrolytes and poly(ILs)
nodes.key_findings: Ionenes = charge in backbone (vs pendent in polyelectrolytes); known for >85 years; historically from 3° diamines + α,ω-dihaloalkanes; trend toward high-performance backbone chemistries (polyimides, polyarylamides); combination with ILs yields unexpected behaviors; largely unexplored experimental space in anionic ionenes; commercial applications: cosmetics, oil drilling, printing; distinction from poly(ILs) (IL monomers polymerized via radical polymerization)
nodes.scientific_questions: Can ionenes achieve mechanical properties comparable to engineering thermoplastics? Anionic ionene synthesis? Ionene membranes for gas separation?

nodes.label: Highly Tough, Notch-Insensitive, and Fast Self-Healing Thermoplastic Polyurethane Elastomers by Tail
nodes.full_title: Highly Tough, Notch-Insensitive, and Fast Self-Healing Thermoplastic Polyurethane Elastomers by Tailored Soft Segment Design
nodes.authors: Changhoon Yu, Hongdeok Kim, Jinho Choi, Sunwu Song, Joonmyung Choi, Min Sang Kwon
nodes.materials_raw: Acrylic TPU elastomers (α,ω-hydroxyl end-functionalized polyacrylates as soft segments), 2-ethylhexyl acrylate (branched), conventional TPU
nodes.methods_raw: Soft-segment modification strategy, MD simulation, mechanical testing, notch sensitivity testing, self-healing testing (45°C, 3h)
nodes.key_findings: Branched-alkyl acrylic soft segments (2-ethylhexyl acrylate) outperform linear-alkyl (n-butyl acrylate); enhanced thermal responsiveness of segmental dynamics; toughness 186 MJ/m³; fracture energy >197 kJ/m² at >900% elongation (notch-insensitive); ~90% self-healing recovery at 45°C within 3h; soft-segment engineering preserves hard domain mechanical contribution; side-chain architecture key to thermal responsiveness
nodes.scientific_questions: Can this approach be extended to other block copolymer elastomers? Long-term cycling fatigue resistance? In vivo biocompatibility for biomedical TPU applications?

nodes.label: Dynamic Bond Chemistry in Soft Materials: Bridging Adaptability and Mechanical Robustness
nodes.full_title: Dynamic Bond Chemistry in Soft Materials: Bridging Adaptability and Mechanical Robustness
nodes.authors: Haeseung Lee, Jiyun Kim, Minwoo Lee, Jiheong Kang
nodes.institution: Pohang University of Science and Technology (POSTECH) (inferred)
nodes.materials_raw: Dynamic bonds (hydrogen bonds, metal-ligand coordination, ionic interactions, host-guest, dynamic covalent: Diels-Alder, disulfide, imine, boronic ester, transesterification), soft materials (hydrogels, elastomers, thermosets)
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review; NMR, ITC (isothermal titration calorimetry), kinITC, stress relaxation, frequency-dependent response, AFM, Raman spectroscopy, SAXS, TEM, SEM for dynamicity quantification
nodes.key_findings: Survey of dynamic bond types and their characteristic lifetimes; design guidelines for balancing adaptability and robustness; quantifying dynamicity: NMR exchange rates, ITC binding kinetics, stress relaxation timescales, frequency-dependent rheology; synergistic cross-linking strategies; spatial/structural modulation of networks; 3D printing: shear thinning, rapid gelation, strong interlayer adhesion; functional biocompatible hydrogels: bioadhesives, biomedical implants; recyclable thermosets: dissociative vs associative exchange; shape-shifting polymers: reconfigurability and programmability
nodes.scientific_questions: Predicting optimal dynamic bond density for specific applications? Combining multiple dynamic bond types synergistically? In vivo degradation kinetics of dynamic networks?

nodes.label: Pohang University of Science and Technology (POSTECH) (inferred)
FDCA Bio-based Copolyester PBFA: Synthesis, Properties, and Fluorescence Regulation
nodes.full_title: FDCA Bio-based Copolyester PBFA: Synthesis, Properties, and Fluorescence Regulation
nodes.authors: Changhao Han, Jiajian Liu, Chuncheng Li, Yaonan Xiao, Shaohua Wu, Mingxuan Du
nodes.materials_raw: 2,5-Furandicarboxylic acid (FDCA, bio-based), butanediol, aliphatic linear dibasic acid, PBFA copolyesters
nodes.methods_raw: One-pot two-step polycondensation, TGA, mechanical testing, photoluminescence spectroscopy
nodes.key_findings: FDCA as bio-based alternative to terephthalic acid; PBFA copolyesters: tensile strength up to 40 MPa, elongation at break 1600%; thermal decomposition >340°C; photoluminescence in bulk and solution (clustering-triggered emission CTE); emission intensity tunable by FDCA content; crystallization enhances luminescence; fully bio-based, industrially scalable platform
nodes.scientific_questions: Long-term biodegradation of PBFA? Can FDCA copolyesters replace PET in packaging? Understanding CTE mechanism at molecular level?

nodes.label: Unifying the Dynamics of Viscoelastic Liquids across the Complex Coacervation Phase Diagram: Time−Po
nodes.full_title: Unifying the Dynamics of Viscoelastic Liquids across the Complex Coacervation Phase Diagram: Time−Polyelectrolyte−Salt Superposition
nodes.authors: Jialin She, Axel Bourdette Kaya, Jérôme Combet, Matthew Tirrell, Francisco J. Cedano-Serrano, Fouzia Boulmedais, Mehdi Vahdati
nodes.materials_raw: PSS (poly(4-styrenesulfonate)) + PDADMA (poly(diallyldimethylammonium)), KBr salt
nodes.methods_raw: Rheology, time-polyelectrolyte-salt superposition, SAXS
nodes.key_findings: Universal master curve unifying dynamics across entire high-salt region of coacervation phase diagram; time-polyelectrolyte-salt superposition extends classical time-temperature and time-salt superposition; CC dynamics: aP ∝ WPE,real^11 (extreme scaling, defies predictions for entangled associating polymers); salty PE solutions: aP ∝ WPE,real^4.1 (good solvent behavior); "quasi-complex coacervates" concept: salty solutions can mimic CC viscoelastic response up to solubility limit; SAXS reveals different nanostructures for CC, quasi-CC, and PE solutions at same composition
nodes.scientific_questions: Molecular origin of extreme W^11 scaling in CC? Can quasi-CC replace CC in applications? Generalizing to other PE systems?

nodes.label: Mechano-responsive hydrogen-bonding array of thermoplastic polyurethane elastomer captures both stre
nodes.full_title: Mechano-responsive hydrogen-bonding array of thermoplastic polyurethane elastomer captures both strength and self-healing
nodes.authors: Youngho Eom, Seon-Mi Kim, Minkyung Lee, Hyeonyeol Jeon, Jaeduk Park, Eun Seong Lee, Sung Yeon Hwang, Jeyoung Park, Dongyeop X. Oh
nodes.materials_raw: Carbonate-type TPU (C-IP-SS), ether-type TPU (E-IP-SS control), commercial ester-type TPU (Es-MD)
nodes.methods_raw: Solvent casting, mechanical testing, self-healing testing (35°C), H-bond characterization
nodes.key_findings: Dual mechano-responsive mode via reversible disorder-to-order transition of H-bonding array; heals when static, toughens when dynamic; UTS 43 MPa (as strong as shoe soles) + self-heals at 35°C; fully amorphous with negligible phase separation; abundant carbonyl groups in soft segments; static mode: disordered carbonyl H-bonds undergo dynamic exchange for healing; stretching: amorphous phase forms stiff crystals through ordered inter-chain H-bonding; phase and strain fully return after release (repeatable healing); overcomes strength-healing trade-off
nodes.scientific_questions: Can this mechanism be designed into other polymer systems? How does the H-bonding array reorganize at molecular level during cycling? Long-term durability under repeated heal-toughen cycles?

nodes.label: Tunable polymer multi-shape memory effect
nodes.full_title: Tunable polymer multi-shape memory effect
nodes.authors: Tao Xie
nodes.materials_raw: PFSA (perfluorosulfonic acid) ionomer (e.g., Nafion), Surlyn ionomer
nodes.methods_raw: DMA (dynamic mechanical analysis), shape memory cycle testing (programming at T_high, cooling to T_mid/T_low, recovery heating), comparison of dual-, triple-, and quadruple-shape memory protocols
nodes.key_findings: Single PFSA ionomer exhibits dual-, triple-, and quadruple-shape memory effects without composition change; tunability from broad glass transition (Tg ~55-130°C) with multiple overlapping relaxation processes; electrostatic network (ionic clusters) acts as net-point; glassy phase acts as switch; quadruple-shape: program shape A at 140°C → cool to 100°C fix B → cool to 75°C fix C → cool to 25°C fix D → recover D→C→B→A by staged heating; shape fixity and recovery >90% for all steps; fundamental insight: broad Tg with multiple distinct relaxation segments is key to multi-SME; different Tg regions serve as independent switches at different temperatures; design principle: for n-shape memory, need (n-1) well-separated thermal transitions
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the ionic cluster net-point density be tuned for different recovery stresses? How does water content affect the multi-SME of PFSA? Extending to other ionomers with different Tg ranges?

nodes.label: shape memory cycle testing (programming at T_high
A bioinspired and hierarchically structured shape-memory material
nodes.full_title: A bioinspired and hierarchically structured shape-memory material
nodes.authors: Emanuele Cera, Fabrizio Scarpa, Stefan Matzen, Mohammadreza Lankarani, Gianni Durin, Yves Rouabah, Laurent Billon, Mario Malerba, Nicola M. Pugno
nodes.materials_raw: Keratin (from human hair), keratin nanofibers, keratin films
nodes.methods_raw: Alkaline extraction of keratin from human hair, electrospinning of keratin nanofibers, film casting, DMA shape memory testing, FTIR, WAXS/SAXS, 3D printing of keratin ink, mechanical testing
nodes.key_findings: Keratin SMP via α-helix to β-sheet conformational transition (analogous to wool curling); nematic self-assembly of keratin chains gives anisotropic shape memory; hierarchical structure: molecular (α→β transition) → nanofiber (electrospun) → macroscopic (film/fiber/3D print); shape fixity ratio ~97%, recovery ratio ~95%; 3D printing of keratin ink into complex shapes with shape memory; biocompatible and biodegradable; water-responsive shape memory (humid/wet conditions) in addition to thermal; human hair as sustainable keratin source; inspiration from biological shape changes (muscle contraction, plant tropism)
nodes.scientific_questions: Can keratin SMPs achieve sufficient recovery stress for actuator applications? How does the α/β ratio affect shape memory performance? Scaling up keratin extraction for industrial applications?

nodes.label: Alkaline extraction of keratin from human hair
High enthalpy storage thermoset network with giant stress and energy output in rubbery state
nodes.full_title: High enthalpy storage thermoset network with giant stress and energy output in rubbery state
nodes.authors: Kai Fan, Wei Min Li
nodes.materials_raw: Thermoset SMP with high enthalpy storage capability (specific chemistry not fully extracted)
nodes.methods_raw: DSC (enthalpy measurement), shape memory cycle testing, stress recovery testing, energy output quantification
nodes.key_findings: Enthalpy-driven energy storage (vs conventional entropy-driven SMPs); recovery stress 17.0 MPa (giant, compared to typical ~1-5 MPa for entropy-driven SMPs); energy output density 2.12 MJ/m³; mechanism: during programming, mechanical energy is stored as both enthalpy (molecular strain in network) and entropy (chain conformation); during recovery, enthalpy release dominates energy output; rubbery state recovery (above Tg) allows large deformation and high energy density; challenges conventional wisdom that SMP energy storage is primarily entropic; potential applications: mechanical actuation, energy harvesting, deployable structures
nodes.scientific_questions: Can enthalpy storage be further enhanced by network chemistry design? What are the fatigue characteristics over many programming-recovery cycles? How does enthalpy storage efficiency compare across different thermoset chemistries?

nodes.label: Thermally-induced two-way shape memory polymers: Mechanisms, structures, and applications
nodes.full_title: Thermally-induced two-way shape memory polymers: Mechanisms, structures, and applications
nodes.authors: Mehdi Zare, Hadi Khorramishoarafi, Sanjay Pratihar, Tariq Shamim, Mokarram Hossain, Gholamreza Makvandi
nodes.materials_raw: Two-way shape memory polymers (2W-SMPs): liquid crystal elastomers (LCEs), semi-crystalline networks (crosslinked polyethylene, PCL-based), interpenetrating polymer networks (IPNs)
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review of 2W-SMP mechanisms, structures, and applications
nodes.key_findings: Two-way shape memory = reversible shape change (vs one-way which requires reprogramming); three main mechanisms: (1) LCEs: nematic-isotropic transition drives reversible actuation; (2) semi-crystalline networks: crystallization-induced elongation (CIE) on cooling + melting-induced contraction (MIC) on heating; (3) IPNs: one network provides fixed phase, other provides reversible phase; LCEs: highest actuation strain (>300%), but lower stress; semi-crystalline: moderate strain (50-100%), higher stress; IPNs: design flexibility, can combine advantages; external stress often needed for semi-crystalline 2W-SMP (programmed under load); applications: soft robotics, self-healing surfaces, biomedical stents, textile actuators; key challenge: improving actuation stress and fatigue resistance
nodes.scientific_questions: Can 2W-SMPs achieve fully reversible actuation without external stress? How to decouple actuation strain from actuation stress? Long-term cycling stability (thousands of cycles)?

nodes.label: The latest advances in two-way shape memory polymers: Fabrication strategies, programming mechanisms
nodes.full_title: The latest advances in two-way shape memory polymers: Fabrication strategies, programming mechanisms, and emerging applications
nodes.authors: Xin Zhang, Wenqiang Tang, Dong Yang, Jianhua Ma, Xingjian Li, Guangming Tian
nodes.materials_raw: Two-way shape memory polymers (2W-SMPs): single-crystal phase, two-crystal phase, LCEs, semi-crystalline networks, dynamic covalent networks, bilayer composites, 4D-printed heterostructures
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review of 2W-SMP fabrication strategies, programming mechanisms, and applications
nodes.key_findings: Latest advances in stress-free 2W-SMPs via internal stress gradients and dynamic molecular networks; single-crystal and two-crystal phase design strategies integrating chemical and physical programming; light-controlled microactuators, self-expanding vascular stents, bioinspired soft robots as emerging applications; multi-stimuli synergy (light, magnetic, thermal) as future direction; sustainable bio-derived feedstocks for 2W-SMP; evolution from quasi-2W (under constant mechanical constraint) to true stress-free 2W-SMP; bilayer composites create internal stress gradients via controlled crosslink density variations; stepwise crosslinking methodologies for spatial property modulation
nodes.scientific_questions: Theoretical guidance for next-generation 2W-SMPs? Integration of multi-stimuli for programmable actuation? Scalability of 4D-printed heterostructures?

nodes.label: Conjugation-Disruption Strategy Enables Colorless, Chemically Recyclable, and High-Refractive-Index
nodes.full_title: Conjugation-Disruption Strategy Enables Colorless, Chemically Recyclable, and High-Refractive-Index Optical Plastics Cross-Linked by Dynamic Thiourethanes
nodes.authors: Xingyuan Lu, Jiachen Wang, Chengliang Tao, Hao Wang, Junqi Sun
nodes.materials_raw: PTU-4 (thiourethane polymer from 4,4'-thiobisbenzenethiol (TB) + hexamethylene diisocyanate + phenol-functionalized triazine), dynamic thiourethane crosslinks
nodes.methods_raw: Copolymerization, refractive index measurement, mechanical testing, ammonolysis for chemical recycling, self-healing testing
nodes.key_findings: "Conjugation-disruption" strategy: high atomic polarizability (sulfur atoms) with disrupted π-conjugation → colorless high-refractive-index polymer; PTU-4: refractive index n = 1.73, optical transparency comparable to commercial optical plastics; tensile strength ~74.2 MPa, Young's modulus ~2.45 GPa, outstanding wear and solvent resistance; thiourethane dynamic exchange enables intrinsic healing and reprocessing; ammonolysis of thiourethanes enables chemical recycling to recover high-value TB monomers + generate high-performance elastomers; solves the trade-off between high n and optical transparency (charge-transfer absorption from extended conjugation); generalizable strategy for colorless HRIPs with sustainability
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the conjugation-disruption strategy be extended to other heteroatom-containing monomers? Long-term optical stability under UV exposure? Scale-up of ammonolysis recycling process?

nodes.label: Materials selection rules for high performance triboelectric nanogenerators
nodes.full_title: Materials selection rules for high performance triboelectric nanogenerators
nodes.authors: Yang Yu, Hengyu Li, Da Zhao, Qi Gao, Xiang Li, Jianlong Wang, Lin Zhong Wang, Tinghai Cheng
nodes.institution: Beijing Institute of Nanoenergy and Nanosystems CAS
nodes.materials_raw: Fifteen triboelectric material pairs: Cu, PA (polyamide/nylon), Al, PET, Kapton, PDMS, PTFE, FEP (fluorinated ethylene propylene)
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive selection series testing (60 sets of samples), contact-separation (CS) and single-electrode (SE) TENG modes, surface charge density measurement at 20% and 95% RH, moisture resistance rate (g_RH) quantification, contact angle measurement, normalization method for material ranking
nodes.key_findings: Universal material selection rules based on four parameters: surface charge density in low RH (ρ_L), surface charge density in high RH (ρ_H), moisture resistance rate (g_RH), friction coefficient; PA and FEP identified as superior triboelectric materials for variable humid environments; moisture resistance rate reaches 124% after 36,000 s at 95% RH (i.e., output actually increases); influence mechanisms of environmental humidity on TENG output revealed; three types of material behavior: (a) weak ρ_L with weak ρ_H, (b) moderate ρ_L with moderate ρ_H, (c) high ρ_L with high ρ_H; g_RH directly indicates practical applicability; selection rules reduce trial-and-error cost in TENG material design
nodes.scientific_questions: Can these selection rules be extended to DC-TENG and hybrid generators? How do temperature effects interact with humidity? Long-term durability of optimal material pairs under real-world cycling?

nodes.label: surface charge density measurement at 20% and 95%
Fatigue-Resistant Mechanoresponsive Color-Changing Hydrogels for Vision-Based Tactile Robots
nodes.full_title: Fatigue-Resistant Mechanoresponsive Color-Changing Hydrogels for Vision-Based Tactile Robots
nodes.authors: Jiabin Liu, Wei Li, Yu She, Sean Blanchard, Shaoting Lin
nodes.materials_raw: Fatigue-resistant mechanoresponsive color-changing hydrogel (FMCH), polyacrylamide-based hydrogel with molecular entanglements and hygroscopic salts (e.g., LiCl or CaCl₂)
nodes.methods_raw: Hydrogel synthesis, mechanical testing (fracture toughness, fatigue threshold), cyclic loading (10,000 cycles), polariscope-based color visualization, strain rate dependence testing, tactile sensor demonstration
nodes.key_findings: FMCH exhibits reversible, resilient, and predictable color changes under mechanical stress; dark under circular polariscope at undeformed state; upon 6× uniaxial stretching, gradually shifts from black → gray → yellow → purple; maintains performance across various strain rates for up to 10,000 cycles (fatigue-resistant); fracture toughness 3000 J/m², stretchability 6×, fatigue threshold 400 J/m²; mechanism: substantial molecular entanglements + hygroscopic salts synergistically enhance toughness while preserving color-changing performance; isochromatic stress-optic coefficient provides angle-independent color (unlike structural color); application: vision-based tactile robot that discerns material stiffness, object shape, spatial location, and applied pressure by translating contact stress distribution into images; overcomes mechanophore degradation (chain scission) and structural color angle dependence
nodes.scientific_questions: Can FMCH be miniaturized for high-resolution tactile arrays? How does salt concentration optimize the toughness-color trade-off? Integration with machine learning for automated tactile recognition?

nodes.label: Recent advances on porous materials and structures for high-performance triboelectric nanogenerators
nodes.full_title: Recent advances on porous materials and structures for high-performance triboelectric nanogenerators
nodes.authors: Mohammad M. Rastegardoost, Omid Aghababaei Tafreshi, Zia Saadatnia, Shahriar Ghaffari-Mosanenzadeh, Chul B. Park, Hani E. Naguib
nodes.materials_raw: Porous dielectric materials for TENG: hydrogels, aerogels, foams, fibrous media; textile/yarn structural designs
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review of porous materials and structural designs for TENG
nodes.key_findings: Porous materials improve TENG performance via: (1) enhanced contact area, (2) tunable dielectric properties, (3) improved breathability, (4) mechanical compliance; aerogels provide ultra-low density + high surface area; hydrogels offer stretchability + ionic conductivity; fibrous media (electrospun) provide tunable pore size; foams give compressibility and air gaps; textile/yarn designs enable all-in-one TENGs with integrated air gaps; applications: human-machine interfaces, smart wearables, self-powered sensors; pore size determines application domain (nano→micro→macro)
nodes.scientific_questions: Optimal pore size distribution for maximum TENG output? Long-term stability of porous structures under cyclic loading? Scalable manufacturing of porous TENG materials?

nodes.label: Comprehensive review of porous materials and struc
Roll to roll triboelectric fiber manufacturing for smart-textile self-powered sensor and harvester
nodes.full_title: Roll to roll triboelectric fiber manufacturing for smart-textile self-powered sensor and harvester
nodes.authors: Ashaduzzaman Khan, Tanvir Alam, Mamunur Rashid, Shahedur Rahman Mir, Gaffar Hossain
nodes.materials_raw: PDMS-coated silver conductor fiber (PS-TENG), carbon fiber, stainless steel fiber
nodes.methods_raw: Roll-to-roll continuous coating process (silver fiber through PDMS gel nozzle), fiber TENG weaving into fabric, wearable integration
nodes.key_findings: Industrially scalable roll-to-roll production of triboelectric fibers; >300 meters of continuous PDMS-coated silver fiber produced; half-meter-long TENG woven fabric (20 cm width) demonstrated; PS-TENG (50 cm, 0.55 mm diameter): Voc ~50 V, Isc ~8.0 μA (silver > stainless steel > carbon); coil harvester lights 160 LEDs with hand tapping; shoe insole integration lights 25 LEDs during walking; excellent durability: washable, rub-resistant, stable in harsh chemicals; applications: CPRS (cardiopulmonary resuscitation sensing) gloves, respiration belt, football kick/boxing impact sensing; fiber TENG comparison: silver (50V/8μA) > stainless steel (30V/5.5μA) > carbon (23V/4μA)
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the fiber diameter be further miniaturized for higher textile integration? Long-term washability (hundreds of wash cycles)? Power management electronics for fiber TENG?

nodes.label: Boosting power output of fluttering triboelectric nanogenerator based on charge excitation through m
nodes.full_title: Boosting power output of fluttering triboelectric nanogenerator based on charge excitation through multi-utilization of wind
nodes.authors: Seh-Hoon Chung, Jin-ho Son, Kyunghwan Cha, Moonhyun Choi, Heesoo Jung, Min-Kun Kim, Jinkee Hong, Sangmin Lee
nodes.materials_raw: Fluttering TENG (F-TENG) with flexible film between parallel electrodes; charge exciting F-TENG (CEF-TENG)
nodes.methods_raw: Charge excitation strategy, multi-utilization of waste wind energy, power output measurement, frequency analysis
nodes.key_findings: Novel charge excitation strategy: waste wind energy passing through F-TENG is re-utilized to boost output; CEF-TENG peak power output 38.16 mW at 6 m/s wind velocity (remarkably higher than existing F-TENGs); high output frequency of F-TENG + charge excitation → maximum electrical output in just a few seconds; multi-utilization of wind: primary fluttering + secondary charge excitation from residual wind; practical for small-scale wind energy harvesting (not possible with electromagnetic generators); F-TENG advantages: thin/flexible film, high-frequency output, small device size, low wind speed operation
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the charge excitation be extended to other TENG modes? How does the CEF-TENG perform under variable wind conditions (gusts, direction changes)? Long-term mechanical fatigue of fluttering film?

nodes.label: Fluttering TENG  with flexible film between parallel electro
Boosting output performance of triboelectric nanogenerator based on BaTiO₃:La embedded nanofiber mem
nodes.full_title: Boosting output performance of triboelectric nanogenerator based on BaTiO₃:La embedded nanofiber membrane for energy harvesting and wireless power transmission
nodes.authors: Benbo Xi, Liangliang Wang, Biao Yang, Yifan Xia, Delu Chen, Xin Wang
nodes.materials_raw: BaTiO₃:La nanoparticles, poly(vinylidene fluoride-trifluoro-ethylene) (PVDF-TrFE) nanofiber membrane (BLPT-NM)
nodes.methods_raw: Electrospinning, dielectric constant measurement, single-electrode-mode TENG fabrication, wireless power transmission demonstration
nodes.key_findings: BLPT-NM: enhanced electronegativity + dielectric constant (38.8 at 10 kHz) via BaTiO₃:La incorporation; TENG (3×3 cm²) power density 2.52 W/m² (f=1.5 Hz), triboelectric charge density 87.3 μC/m²; >11-fold power density increase, >3-fold charge density increase vs pristine PVDF-TrFE NM; La-doped BaTiO₃ has higher dielectric constant than pure BaTiO₃; wireless power transmission achieved via Maxwell's displacement current from TENG; wirelessly received signals trigger electronics in real time; electrospinning provides simple fabrication method for high-k nanocomposite membranes
nodes.scientific_questions: Optimal BaTiO₃:La loading for maximum TENG performance? How does nanofiber morphology affect charge retention? Long-term stability of wireless power transmission?

nodes.label: Recent developments in tribovoltaic nanogenerators: mechanistic insights and future outlook
nodes.full_title: Recent developments in tribovoltaic nanogenerators: mechanistic insights and future outlook
nodes.authors: Saswat Choudhury, Jin Young Choi, Byong Joo Lee, Dahl-Young Khang, Sang-Woo Kim
nodes.materials_raw: Tribovoltaic nanogenerators (TVNGs): semiconductor-based frictional materials (Si, GaN, organic semiconductors), metal-semiconductor contacts, p-n junctions
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review of TVNG mechanisms, materials, and applications
nodes.key_findings: Tribovoltaic effect: direct current (DC) output from friction on semiconductor surfaces (vs AC from conventional TENG); mechanism: friction-induced carrier excitation at semiconductor interface, analogous to photovoltaic effect but driven by mechanical energy; TVNG eliminates need for rectification (unlike TENG → higher energy utilization efficiency); material systems: Si-based (most studied), GaN, organic semiconductors, 2D materials (MoS₂); contact electrification + semiconductor physics = DC generation; potential for self-powered electronics without power management circuits; key advantage: continuous DC output vs pulsed AC from TENG
nodes.scientific_questions: Fundamental mechanism of friction-induced carrier generation? Can TVNG efficiency approach photovoltaic levels? Scalability for practical power generation?

nodes.label: Triboelectric charge modulation to understand the electrification process in nanogenerators combined
nodes.full_title: Triboelectric charge modulation to understand the electrification process in nanogenerators combined with an efficient power management system for IoT applications
nodes.authors: Sontyana Adonijah Graham, Seneke Chamith Chandrarathna, Punnarao Manchi, Mandar Vasant Paranjape, Jun Kyu Lee, Jong-Wook Lee, Jae Su Yu
nodes.materials_raw: Triboelectric nanogenerators with various material pairs, power management system (PMS)
nodes.methods_raw: Triboelectric charge modulation technique, impedance matching, power management circuit design
nodes.key_findings: Charge modulation technique reveals electrification process details in TENG; efficient power management system (PMS) designed for IoT applications; impedance matching for maximum power transfer; charge modulation enables understanding of charge generation, transfer, and decay mechanisms; practical demonstration with IoT sensor nodes
nodes.scientific_questions: How does charge modulation affect long-term TENG stability? Can the PMS be miniaturized for wearables?

nodes.label: Triboelectric nanogenerators with various material pairs
Recent Advances in Triboelectric Nanogenerators for Marine Exploitation
nodes.full_title: Recent Advances in Triboelectric Nanogenerators for Marine Exploitation
nodes.authors: Chuguo Zhang, Yijun Hao, Jiayi Yang, Wei Su, Hongke Zhang, Jie Wang, Zhong Lin Wang, Xiuhan Li
nodes.materials_raw: TENG for marine applications: wave energy harvesters, self-powered ocean sensors, marine electrochemical technologies
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review of TENG-based marine exploitation
nodes.key_findings: Three research directions: (1) in situ ocean energy harvesting (wave, tide, current), (2) self-powered ocean monitoring (sensors, networks), (3) self-powered marine electrochemical technologies (degradation, anticorrosion, electrolysis); TENG advantages for marine: high efficiency at low frequencies (vs electromagnetic generators), simple structure, low cost, scalable; blue energy concept: TENG networks for large-scale ocean energy harvesting; self-powered sensor nodes for ocean situational awareness; electrochemical degradation of marine pollutants powered by TENG; electrochemical anticorrosion and antifouling; challenges: durability in saltwater, biofouling, scaling up, power management
nodes.scientific_questions: Long-term TENG stability in harsh marine environment? Efficient power aggregation from distributed TENG networks? Biofouling prevention strategies?

nodes.label: TENG for marine applications: wave energy harvesters
Comprehensive review of TENG-based marine exploita
Self-powered angle-resolved triboelectric nanogenerator for underwater vibration localization
nodes.full_title: Self-powered angle-resolved triboelectric nanogenerator for underwater vibration localization
nodes.authors: Jianchao Guo, Jiaqi He, Zuqing Yuan, Juan Tao, Xiangyu Liu, Zhuoyu Song, Wenchao Gao, Chunfeng Wang, Caofeng Pan
nodes.institution: CAS Center for Excellence in Nanoscience
nodes.materials_raw: Angle-resolved TENG (AR-TENG): metal balls, inverted conical unit, fluorinated ethylene propylene (FEP) film, multiple sector-like electrodes
nodes.methods_raw: Multi-electrode sector design, angle resolution testing, underwater vibration localization, trigonometric positioning
nodes.key_findings: AR-TENG: self-powered underwater vibration detection and localization; metal balls on inverted conical FEP unit with sector-like electrodes; each electrode = one sensing channel; linear electrical output at low-frequency variations; maximum power density 1.28 μW/cm² at 200 MΩ load; key innovation: output voltage ratio of adjacent channels depends only on vibration direction (not amplitude); angle resolution 15° (improvable with better SNR); underwater localization by intersecting vibration directions from two parallel AR-TENGs; locating accuracy >95%; simple, effective, energy-autonomous scheme for marine engineering and national security
nodes.scientific_questions: Can angle resolution be improved to <5°? Multi-source vibration discrimination? Integration with wireless communication for remote monitoring?

nodes.label: CAS Center for Excellence in Nanoscience
CrystalGAT: An AI-Based Intelligent Flexible Crystal Materials Design Computing Platform
nodes.full_title: CrystalGAT: An AI-Based Intelligent Flexible Crystal Materials Design Computing Platform
nodes.authors: Chenyang Zhao, Yanbo Liu, Shi Tang, Jianmin Zhou, Songgu Wu, Junbo Gong
nodes.materials_raw: Molecular crystals (organic), flexible crystals, photoresponsive crystals, multicomponent pharmaceutical crystals
nodes.methods_raw: Graph neural network (GAT - graph attention), data augmentation, crystal structure descriptor, prediction of mechanical properties (elastic/brittle/plastic), experimental validation
nodes.key_findings: CrystalGAT: graph attention network for predicting mechanical properties of molecular crystals; 90% prediction accuracy on validation set; generalization to multicomponent systems; identified key structural segments influencing crystal flexibility; breakthrough: transforming brittle crystals into flexible photoresponsive crystals guided by ML predictions; rapid screening of plastic multicomponent drug crystals for enhanced tableting; dedicated web platform available; addresses the problem that flexible crystal discovery has been largely serendipitous; slip plane design strategy (Desiraju/Reddy) insufficient alone → ML reveals additional structural features
nodes.scientific_questions: Can CrystalGAT predict piezoelectric properties of molecular crystals? Extending to ionic crystals and metal-organic frameworks? Experimental validation across diverse chemical families?

nodes.label: The tribo-piezoelectric microscopic coupling mechanism of ferroelectric polymers and the synchronous
nodes.full_title: The tribo-piezoelectric microscopic coupling mechanism of ferroelectric polymers and the synchronous online monitoring of load distribution/roller speed for intelligent bearings
nodes.authors: Lizhou Li, Xiaoli Wang, Yanqiang Hu, Zhihao Li, Zirui Zhao, Gao Zheng
nodes.materials_raw: PVDF (ferroelectric polymer), Cu (metal electrode), tribo-piezoelectric hybrid nanogenerator
nodes.methods_raw: First-principles calculations, KPFM (Kelvin probe force microscopy), compression testing, bearing monitoring demonstration
nodes.key_findings: Tribo-piezoelectric microscopic coupling mechanism at ferroelectric polymer/metal interface revealed via first-principles + KPFM; local deformation of β-phase PVDF molecular chain concentrates LUMO in deformed region + lowers LUMO energy level → enhances triboelectric charge; simultaneously changes dipole orientation and polarity → enhances piezoelectric charge; key intrinsic factors: dipole-dipole interactions + intramolecular/intermolecular electron transfer; application: synchronous online monitoring of load distribution and roller speed in intelligent bearings; coupling effect means tribo and piezo signals are not simply additive but synergistic
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the tribo-piezoelectric coupling be optimized by molecular design? How does temperature affect the coupling mechanism? Extending to other ferroelectric polymers (PVDF-TrFE, PVDF-HFP)?

nodes.label: Strongly enhanced charge density via gradient nano-doping for high performance elastic-material-base
nodes.full_title: Strongly enhanced charge density via gradient nano-doping for high performance elastic-material-based triboelectric nanogenerators
nodes.authors: Xiaobo Gao, Fangjing Xing, Feng Guo, Jing Wen, Hao Li, Yuhan Yang, Baodong Chen, Zhong Lin Wang
nodes.materials_raw: Polyurethane (PU) sponge, polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) nanoparticles, elastic-material-based TENG (EMB-TENG)
nodes.methods_raw: Room-temperature foaming method, gradient nano-doping strategy, triboelectric charge density measurement, self-powered smart boxing bag demonstration
nodes.key_findings: Gradient nano-doping: inhomogeneous concentration of PTFE nanoparticles creates gradient electrical field → boosts charge density and sensitivity; triboelectric charge density 537 μC/m³ (1.2× increase), peak power density 732.6 mW/m³ (1.8× increase); sensitivity 1.851 kPa⁻¹ at 50% compressive strain; self-powered smart boxing bag: force analysis + real-time counting at large strain; simple room-temperature foaming preparation; EMB-TENGs most effective for harvesting random (low frequency, low amplitude) and distributed mechanical energy; gradient doping addresses charge attenuation problem in elastic TENGs
nodes.scientific_questions: Optimal gradient profile for maximum charge density? Can gradient doping be extended to other elastic materials (silicone, EVA)? Long-term cycling stability of gradient-doped sponges?

nodes.label: The space tribo-charge region and equivalent charge plane model in triboelectric nanogenerators
nodes.full_title: The space tribo-charge region and equivalent charge plane model in triboelectric nanogenerators
nodes.authors: Xucong Chen, Han Wu, Jun Li, Xiangyu Chen, Wei Ou-Yang
nodes.materials_raw: Contact-separation mode TENG (CS-TENG) with various dielectric materials
nodes.methods_raw: Space tribo-charge region (STCR) model, equivalent charge plane (ECP) model, electrostatic analysis, experimental validation
nodes.key_findings: Traditional TENG models treat triboelectric charge as zero-thickness surface charge → overly idealistic; proposed STCR model captures finite-thickness distribution of triboelectric charge → more accurate electrostatic description; ECP model simplifies STCR for practical calculations; CS-TENG operates at low frequency → electric field treated as electrostatic; improved accuracy in predicting TENG output voltage; addresses limitations of formal electrostatic frameworks; fundamental physics understanding for TENG performance optimization
nodes.scientific_questions: How does STCR thickness depend on material properties and contact conditions? Can the model be extended to sliding and freestanding TENG modes? Temperature dependence of charge distribution?

TransPolymer: a Transformer-based language model for polymer property predictions
nodes.full_title: TransPolymer: a Transformer-based language model for polymer property predictions
nodes.authors: Changwen Xu, Yuyang Wang, Amir Barati Farimani
nodes.materials_raw: Polymers (various classes), polymer SMILES sequences
nodes.methods_raw: Transformer model with self-attention, polymer tokenizer with chemical awareness, Masked Language Modeling (MLM) pretraining, benchmark evaluation on 10 polymer property datasets
nodes.key_findings: TransPolymer: first Transformer-based language model for polymer property prediction; polymer tokenizer designed with chemical awareness for polymer sequences; outperforms existing methods on 10 benchmark datasets; benefits from pretraining on large unlabeled datasets via MLM; self-attention mechanism effectively captures long-range dependencies in polymer sequences; addresses limitations of GNN (requires explicit structural info, variable degree of polymerization) and RNN (loses information at deeper steps); polymer treated as character sequence (like natural language) → enables NLP techniques; promising for rational polymer design and structure-property relationship understanding
nodes.scientific_questions: Can TransPolymer predict properties of copolymers and polymer blends? How does the model handle polymers with complex architectures (branched, crosslinked)? Extending to polymer processing condition optimization?

nodes.label: benchmark evaluation on 10 polymer property datase
Δ-Learning of High-Fidelity Electronic Structure Using Graph Neural Networks with Modified Node-Leve
nodes.full_title: Δ-Learning of High-Fidelity Electronic Structure Using Graph Neural Networks with Modified Node-Level Features
nodes.authors: Nima Karimitari, Teerachote Pakornchote, Abdulaziz W. Alherz, Jacob M. Clary, Cooper Tezak, Sourin Dey, Jianjun Hu, Derek Vigil-Fowler, Ravishankar Sundararaman, Charles B. Musgrave, Christopher Sutton
nodes.materials_raw: Metal and nitrogen-doped graphene catalysts (MNCs): Co/N, Fe/N, Ni/N graphene systems
nodes.methods_raw: Δ-learning approach (PBE→HSE06), graph neural network (ALIGNN architecture), electronic scalar features as node-level features (PBE eigenvalues, orbital-resolved projectors, applied potential), cross-validation with leave-one-out strategies
nodes.key_findings: Δ-learning: predict HSE06 eigenvalues from PBE inputs → high accuracy at low cost; MAE < 0.1 eV for eigenvalue prediction; strong generalization: leave-one-adsorbate (MAE 0.074 eV), leave-one-chemical-space (MAE 0.08 eV), leave-one-metal (MAE 0.072 eV); electronic features (PBE eigenvalues, projectors) + structural information combined in GNN node features; handles electrified interfaces (electrocatalysts) via applied potential feature; MNC catalysts for HER, CO₂RR, ORR applications; breakthrough in combining accuracy of hybrid functionals with speed of GNN
nodes.scientific_questions: Can Δ-learning extend to other DFT functional pairs (e.g., PBE→SCAN)? How does the model perform on larger catalytic systems (nanoparticles, surfaces)? Real-time catalyst screening with Δ-learning?

nodes.label: electronic scalar features as node-level features
A Machine Learning Interatomic Potential Data Set and Model for Catalysis with Local Fine-Tuning to
nodes.full_title: A Machine Learning Interatomic Potential Data Set and Model for Catalysis with Local Fine-Tuning to Chemical Accuracy
nodes.authors: Zhihong Wu, Lei Zhou, Pengfei Hou, Yuyan Liu, Ruixing Wang, Taoli Guo, Jin-Cheng Liu
nodes.materials_raw: Metal and alloy slabs with adsorbates, oxides, clusters, 2D materials, small molecules (diverse catalytic systems)
nodes.methods_raw: CLAM (catalytic large atomic model) - pretrained ML interatomic potential, local fine-tuning algorithm, MD simulations, transition state searches (CI-NEB), DFT reference calculations
nodes.key_findings: CLAM: comprehensive dataset + pretrained model for ML interatomic potentials in heterogeneous catalysis; 94% prediction accuracy within chemical accuracy (<1 kcal/mol) for adsorption energies on transition metals; 3.4× computational acceleration vs DFT; local fine-tuning algorithm dramatically improves accuracy for structural optimizations and transition state searches; 81% identification accuracy for transition states (<1 kcal/mol) with 10.1× speed-up vs DFT CI-NEB; pretrained CLAM reproduces dynamic catalysis phenomena via MD without additional fine-tuning; diverse training data ensures transferability across catalytic systems
nodes.scientific_questions: Can CLAM handle complex reaction networks (multiple intermediates, competing pathways)? Extending to electrocatalytic interfaces with explicit solvent? Integration with automated catalyst discovery workflows?

nodes.label: Metal and alloy slabs with adsorbates
The evolution of MOF discovery for CO₂ capture: From high-throughput screening to AI design and auto
nodes.full_title: The evolution of MOF discovery for CO₂ capture: From high-throughput screening to AI design and automated laboratories
nodes.authors: Yong Qiu, Lei Wang, Chang Liu, Xu Zhang, Yun Tian, Zhen Zhou
nodes.materials_raw: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) for CO₂ capture, >160,000 hypothetical MOFs, tens of thousands of synthesized MOFs
nodes.methods_raw: Review: high-throughput computational screening, classical DFT (cDFT), transition state theory (TST), flexible force fields, machine learning (ML), large language models (LLMs), automated/self-driving laboratories
nodes.key_findings: Paradigm shift from brute-force screening to AI-driven automated experimentation for MOF discovery; >160,000 hypothetical MOFs in computational databases; ML predicts gas storage capacities, adsorption isotherms, separation selectivity; cDFT and flexible force fields for rapid screening; framework flexibility and kinetic behavior remain poorly understood → limits practical applications; LLMs lower barriers between theory and experiment; self-driving laboratories bridge gap between theoretical models and real-world deployment; key challenges: framework flexibility modeling, kinetic selectivity, water stability, scalable synthesis
nodes.scientific_questions: Can AI predict MOF synthesis feasibility (not just ideal properties)? How to handle framework flexibility in ML models? Automated synthesis optimization for pilot-scale MOF production?

nodes.label: tens of thousands of synthesized MOFs
Adding Machine Learning to the Polymer Reaction Engineering Toolbox
nodes.full_title: Adding Machine Learning to the Polymer Reaction Engineering Toolbox
nodes.authors: Kiarash Farajzadehahary, Shaghayegh Hamzehlou, Nicholas Ballard
nodes.materials_raw: Polymeric systems (general), polymer reaction engineering
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review of ML techniques in polymer reaction engineering: supervised learning (regression, classification), unsupervised learning, reinforcement learning, physics-informed neural networks
nodes.key_findings: ML increasingly used in polymer reaction engineering for: (1) reaction kinetics modeling, (2) process optimization, (3) property prediction, (4) inverse design; key challenge: ML models lack physical grounding → can produce unrealistic predictions; solution: hybrid approaches combining first-principles and data-driven models; polymer representation challenges: variable chain length, dispersity, architecture; data scarcity remains a major bottleneck; physics-informed ML as promising direction; review covers ML tools, data sources, polymer representations, and specific applications
nodes.scientific_questions: How to effectively incorporate polymer physics constraints into ML models? Automated experiment design for active learning in polymer synthesis? Transfer learning across polymer classes?

nodes.label: Comprehensive review of ML techniques in polymer r
Periodicity-aware deep learning for polymers
nodes.full_title: Periodicity-aware deep learning for polymers
nodes.authors: Yuhui Wu, Cong Wang, Xintian Shen, Tianyi Zhang, Peng Zhang, Jian Ji
nodes.materials_raw: Polymers (various classes), antimicrobial polymers (experimental validation)
nodes.methods_raw: PerioGT framework: periodicity-aware graph Transformer, contrastive learning with periodicity prior, graph augmentation with virtual nodes, periodicity prompts for fine-tuning, wet-lab experimental validation
nodes.key_findings: PerioGT: first periodicity-aware deep learning framework for polymers; existing methods neglect inherent periodicity of polymer repeating units → limited generalization; chemical knowledge-driven periodicity prior constructed and incorporated via contrastive learning; periodicity prompts learned during fine-tuning; graph augmentation integrates conditions via virtual nodes for complex chemical interactions; state-of-the-art performance on 16 downstream tasks; wet-lab validation: identified two polymers with potent antimicrobial properties; periodicity prior effectively enhances model performance; addresses unique polymer complexity (degree of polymerization, polydispersity, crosslinking, architecture)
nodes.scientific_questions: Can PerioGT handle block copolymers and gradient copolymers? How does the periodicity prior scale to very long polymer chains? Extending to polymer processing condition prediction?

nodes.label: Structure-Aware Machine Learning for Polymers: A Hierarchical Graph Network for Predicting Propertie
nodes.full_title: Structure-Aware Machine Learning for Polymers: A Hierarchical Graph Network for Predicting Properties From Statistical Ensembles
nodes.authors: Julian Kimmig, Yannik Köster, Timo Koswig, Punith Raviswamy, Subhash V. S. Ganti, Stefan Zechel, Christopher Kuenneth, Ulrich S. Schubert
nodes.materials_raw: Polymers with various architectures (linear, branched, star, network), molecular mass distribution (MMD)
nodes.methods_raw: Hierarchical graph convolutional network (GCN), kinetic Monte Carlo (kMC) simulation for graph generation, ensemble-based training strategy, MMD integration
nodes.key_findings: Structure-aware GCN treats polymer samples as statistical ensembles (not single molecules); hierarchical graph: nodes = monomer units, integrates MMD for dispersity; ensemble-based training with topologically realistic graphs from kMC simulation; >98% accuracy classifying complex polymer architectures on synthetic data; Tg prediction R² = 0.89 ± 0.01 on experimental dataset; fine-tuning demonstrates model learns Tg-molar mass relationship from MMD information; physically realistic paradigm for polymer informatics; addresses limitation that existing ML neglects hierarchical and statistical nature of macromolecules
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the kMC-generated training data cover all relevant polymer architectures? How does MMD quality affect prediction accuracy? Scaling to multi-component polymer systems?

nodes.label: kinetic Monte Carlo  simulation for graph generati
Explore Thermal and Mechanical Properties of Biobased Polyurethane Elastomers Through Machine Learni
nodes.full_title: Explore Thermal and Mechanical Properties of Biobased Polyurethane Elastomers Through Machine Learning Models
nodes.authors: Rui Li, Yongjun Lv, Chunhui Xie, Lu Liu, Qianlan Ao, Zhi Li, Changyi Li, Yunqi Li
nodes.materials_raw: Biobased polyurethane elastomers (BPUEs): biomass-derived polyols, isocyanates, chain extenders
nodes.methods_raw: Multi-target regression ML models, domain-knowledge augmented feature engineering (26 features), composition-processing-structure-property-performance (CPSPPr) analysis, SHAP explainability
nodes.key_findings: Dataset: >1500 BPUE samples with comprehensive CPSPPr information; 26 features sufficient to predict 6 core properties: Young's modulus (YM), tensile strength (TS), elongation at break (EB), Tg, Td5, tan δ; R² > 0.70 for YM/TS/EB, R² > 0.80 for Tg/Td5/tan δ; chemical structure features contribute >70% of explainability; biomass feedstock type, polyol molecular weight, hard segment content = key regulatable variables; stretching rate and heating rate critical for repeatable properties; composition-induced thermo-mechanical coupling effects captured; dynamic covalent bonds balance stiffness-extensibility tradeoff; data-driven insights for rational BPUE design
nodes.scientific_questions: Can ML predict BPUE long-term aging and fatigue? How does biomass source variability affect model accuracy? Extending to bio-based TPU and other bio-polyurethanes?

nodes.label: (Materials Horizons 2025, DOI: 10.1039/D5MH01525A) - ML for solid-state electrolyte development
nodes.full_title: (Materials Horizons 2025, DOI: 10.1039/D5MH01525A) - ML for solid-state electrolyte development
nodes.authors: V. Jain, Z. Wang, F. You (inferred from context)
nodes.materials_raw: Solid-state electrolytes (SSEs): lithium-based, sodium-based, polymer electrolytes, ceramic electrolytes
nodes.methods_raw: Review of ML approaches for SSE: classical ML screening, graph neural networks, ML interatomic potentials, generative models for de novo design
nodes.key_findings: Paradigm shift from classical ML screening to sophisticated DL architectures (GNNs) for SSE discovery; ML interatomic potentials enable large-scale dynamics simulations; generative models for de novo materials design emerging; SSE essential for next-generation batteries (safety, energy density, sustainability); ML accelerated SSE discovery from decades to years; challenges: limited data for non-Li systems, lack of standardized evaluation metrics; future directions: autonomous discovery platforms, physics-informed generative models, integrated experimental-computational workflows, universal descriptors, closed-loop discovery systems
nodes.scientific_questions: How to handle data scarcity for non-Li SSE systems? Can generative models propose experimentally synthesizable SSEs? Standardized benchmarks for SSE ML models?

Age of Flexible Electronics: Emerging Trends in Soft Multifunctional Sensors

nodes.authors: Jeng-Hun Lee, Kilwon Cho, Jang-Kyo Kim
nodes.institution: Pohang University of Science and Technology (POSTECH)
nodes.materials_raw: Soft multifunctional sensor materials: CNTs, graphene, conducting polymers (PPy, PEDOT:PSS), piezoelectric polymers (PVDF-TrFE), hydrogels, perovskites, MXenes
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review of soft multifunctional sensors
nodes.key_findings: Era of flexible electronics: first-gen flexible mobiles/displays commercialized late 2010s; soft multifunctional sensors = essential components; key characteristics: flexibility, stretchability, biocompatibility, self-healing; target stimuli: pressure, strain, temperature, humidity, light, chemical/biological; selection criteria: sensitivity, range, response time, linearity, hysteresis, durability; emerging trends: (1) decoupling multiple stimuli, (2) data processing (ML integration), (3) skin conformability, (4) energy sources (self-powered); materials: 1D nanowires/nanofibers, 2D nanosheets, 3D porous networks, hybrid structures; applications: e-skin, health monitoring, human-machine interfaces, soft robotics
nodes.scientific_questions: How to achieve true multimodal sensing without cross-talk? Long-term biocompatibility of implantable soft sensors? Integration of sensing, energy, and data processing in single platform?

nodes.label: Comprehensive review of soft multifunctional senso
Accurately Predicting Solubility Curves via a Thermodynamic Cycle, Machine Learning, and Solvent Ens
nodes.full_title: Accurately Predicting Solubility Curves via a Thermodynamic Cycle, Machine Learning, and Solvent Ensembles
nodes.journal: Journal of the American Chemical Society 2025 (DOI: 10.1021/jacs.5c13746)
nodes.authors: Emad Al Ibrahim, Nathan Morgan, Simon Müller, Saikiran Motati, William H. Green
nodes.materials_raw: Neutral organic molecules in various solvents
nodes.methods_raw: Thermodynamic fusion cycle + ML predictions of activity coefficient, fusion enthalpy, melting point; reference ensembling; tested on >100,000 experimental solubility values
nodes.key_findings: Fast, general method for predicting solubility of neutral organic molecules across wide range of solvents and temperatures; thermodynamic fusion cycle: combine ML predictions of activity coefficient + fusion enthalpy + melting point; tested on >100,000 experimental values; better or comparable performance vs competing methods on many benchmarks, even at elevated temperatures; "reference ensembling": leverage all available experimental solubilities for a given solute to estimate solubility in new solvent; reference ensembling also enhances robustness of models trained directly on solubility data; addresses hard-to-estimate solubility limits that hinder process design
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the method handle ionic compounds and electrolytes? How does accuracy vary with solvent polarity range? Integration with crystallization process design?

nodes.label: Neutral organic molecules in various solvents
Thermodynamic fusion cycle + ML predictions of act
Elliptical Small-Angle X-Ray Scattering Patterns from Aligned Lamellar Arrays
nodes.full_title: Elliptical Small-Angle X-Ray Scattering Patterns from Aligned Lamellar Arrays
nodes.journal: Journal of Polymer Science Part B: Polymer Physics 2016, 54, 308-318
nodes.authors: David Grubb, N. Sanjeeva Murthy, Oriano Francescangeli
nodes.materials_raw: Semicrystalline polymers (drawn films/fibers), liquid crystalline cybotactic nematics
nodes.methods_raw: SAXS analysis, 2D intensity distribution modeling, equilibrium distribution of molecular orientations and lamellar tilts
nodes.key_findings: Aligned semicrystalline polymers produce 2- or 4-spot SAXS patterns; liquid crystalline cybotactic nematics produce similar patterns; stacked lamellae structure (tilted for 4-point) explains basic features; peak intensity positions lie on ellipse (not layer line or circle arc); elliptical form emerges from statistical distribution of stack properties without directly assignable structural cause; model uses equilibrium distribution of molecular orientations and lamellar tilts; model parameters chosen by fitting entire 2D intensity distribution; some fitted parameters uncertain due to modeling assumptions; practical guidance for interpreting SAXS of oriented polymers
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the model distinguish between different lamellar tilt distributions? How does drawing ratio affect the elliptical pattern parameters? Extending to block copolymer mesophases?

nodes.label: Bio-based polyesters: Recent progress and future prospects
nodes.full_title: Bio-based polyesters: Recent progress and future prospects
nodes.authors: Qinan Zhang, Mengze Song, Yanyan Xu, Wencai Wang, Zhao Wang, Liqun Zhang
nodes.materials_raw: Bio-based polyesters: aliphatic (PLA, PBS, PEF) and aromatic (vanillin, furan, terpenoid-based); bio-based monomers from renewable resources; bio-based polyester elastomers (BEPE)
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review of bio-based polyester synthesis (melt polycondensation, ring-opening polymerization), copolymerization modification
nodes.key_findings: Bio-based polyesters = sustainable alternative to petroleum-based (PET, PTT, PBS); classification: aliphatic vs aromatic bio-based polyesters; key bio-based monomers: 2,5-furandicarboxylic acid (FDCA), vanillin, terpenoids, isosorbide, succinic acid; PLA and PEF as leading bio-based polyesters (industrialized); copolymerization modification creates diverse property combinations; bio-based polyester elastomers (BEPE) emerging as new class; challenges: cost competitiveness, performance matching, scalable monomer production; future: expanding monomer library, green catalysis, closed-loop recycling
nodes.scientific_questions: Can bio-based polyesters match PET barrier properties for packaging? How does bio-based monomer purity affect polymer properties? Life cycle assessment of bio-based vs petroleum polyesters?

A review: Polyacrylonitrile as high-performance piezoelectric materials
nodes.full_title: A review: Polyacrylonitrile as high-performance piezoelectric materials
nodes.authors: Junzhu Tao, Yifan Wang, Xuekai Zheng, Chao Zhao, Xin Jin, Wenyu Wang, Tong Lin
nodes.materials_raw: Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) piezoelectric polymers, PAN composites (with inorganic materials, high-temp polymers), NH₄NO₃/NaNO₃-doped PAN
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review of PAN piezoelectric design, structure, fabrication, and applications
nodes.key_findings: PAN rediscovered as piezoelectric polymer in 2019 (Wang et al.) via electrospinning → high zigzag conformation; PAN advantages: -CN dipole moment, H-bonding between cyano groups, ladder-like structure formation after thermal treatment → high-temp piezoelectricity; PAN works at extremely high temperatures (up to 500°C!) vs PVDF loses piezoelectricity above ~75°C; structure-property: zigzag conformation → higher piezoelectric response than helical; applications: nanogenerators, acoustoelectric conversion, sensors, high-temperature thermoacoustic conversion; methods to improve: changing molecular structure, blending with inorganic/high-temp polymer materials; PAN = carbon fiber precursor → dual-use potential
nodes.scientific_questions: Quantitative relationship between zigzag content and d₃₃? Can PAN piezoelectric fibers be directly integrated into carbon fiber composites? Long-term stability of PAN piezoelectricity under thermal cycling?
