Hydrogen Bonding−Assisted Sequential Mineralization of Polymer−Stabilized Ionic Clusters
nodes.full_title: Hydrogen Bonding−Assisted Sequential Mineralization of Polymer−Stabilized Ionic Clusters
nodes.authors: Angxuan Wu, Rui Sun, Xiaolin Qian, Baosheng Guo, Zhiwei Li, Daqi Li, Wenwen Yu, Yilan Ye, Zhenzhong Yang
nodes.materials_raw: Calcium phosphate (CaP) clusters, poly(2-(dimethylamino)ethyl methacrylate) (PDMAEMA), hydroxyapatite (HAp) nanorods
nodes.methods_raw: H-bonding stabilization in ethanol, sequential destabilization (H₂O addition, competitive capping agents, mild heating), mineralization control
nodes.key_findings: Biomimetic mineralization using polymer-stabilized ionic clusters; PDMAEMA as polymeric capping agent for CaP clusters via H-bonding in ethanol (low dielectric constant); three metastable states: (1) concentration-dependent reversible aggregation → hybrid networks, (2) thermally triggered irreversible aggregation → enhanced networks, (3) H₂O-induced coassembly → hybrid nanochains + phase separation → metastable interfaces; sequential mineralization by progressively destabilizing H-bonding; controlled transformation of CaP clusters → hydroxyapatite nanorods with tunable aspect ratios under mild conditions; inspired by PILP (polymer-induced liquid phase) mechanism in biomineralization; new avenues for nanomaterial design
nodes.scientific_questions: Can this approach be extended to other mineral systems (CaCO₃, SiO₂)? How does PDMAEMA molecular weight affect cluster stability? In vivo applications for bone regeneration?

nodes.label: Bio-Based Elastomers: Design, Properties, and Biomedical Applications
nodes.full_title: Bio-Based Elastomers: Design, Properties, and Biomedical Applications
nodes.authors: Qingsheng Liu, Pengfei Lou, Zhentao Sun, Daochang Li, Haijun Ji, Zhengzhuo Xu, Lei Li, Jiajia Xue, Runguo Wang, Zhao Wang, Liqun Zhang
nodes.materials_raw: Bio-based elastomers: polyester elastomers, polyamide elastomers, polyurethane elastomers; bio-based monomers (diacid, diol, diamine, diisocyanate); biopolymer-derived elastomers (natural rubber, protein-based, polysaccharide-based)
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review of bio-based elastomer design, synthesis, and biomedical applications
nodes.key_findings: Global elastomer consumption ~30 million metric tons (2022), majority from fossil feedstocks; bio-based elastomers from molecular building blocks and biopolymers; Goodyear targeting 100% renewable tire within decade; categories: bio-based polyester/polyamide/polyurethane elastomers; tunable Tg, rheology, mechanical strength, degradation rate via monomer selection; performance-advantaged properties: immune modulation, biocompatibility, biodegradability; biomedical applications: wound dressing, cardiovascular, nerve repair, bone repair, biosensors; challenges: cost, performance matching, scalable bio-monomer production
nodes.scientific_questions: Can bio-based elastomers achieve the fatigue resistance needed for automotive applications? How does bio-based content percentage affect biocompatibility? Standardized testing protocols for bio-based elastomers?

nodes.label: Comprehensive review of bio-based elastomer design
Polymer Translocation Time
nodes.full_title: Polymer Translocation Time
nodes.authors: Yuyuan Lu, Zhenhua Wang, Lijia An, An-Chang Shi
nodes.materials_raw: Linear and ring polymers in nanopores/nanochannels
nodes.methods_raw: Multiparticle collision dynamics + molecular dynamics simulations, scaling analysis, theoretical derivation
nodes.key_findings: Force- and flow-induced translocation of linear and ring polymers studied; translocation time scaling exponents δ, δ', α are not constants (contrary to many theoretical predictions); α = 1.0 for long chains in both force- and flow-induced translocations; difference between force- and flow-induced translocations stems from different monomer crowding effects due to distinct flow patterns outside channel; general relations for translocation time derived and validated against simulations; provides clear molecular pictures for translocation processes; implications for DNA sequencing and ultrafiltration
nodes.scientific_questions: How do ring polymer topology effects manifest in translocation? Can the scaling relations predict experimental DNA translocation times? Effect of channel charge on translocation dynamics?

nodes.label: Linear and ring polymers in nanopores/nanochannels
Role of Functionality in Cross-Stream Migration, Structures, and Dynamics of Star Polymers in Poiseu
nodes.full_title: Role of Functionality in Cross-Stream Migration, Structures, and Dynamics of Star Polymers in Poiseuille Flow
nodes.authors: Aiqing Liu, Zhenyue Yang, Lijun Liu, Jizhong Chen, Lijia An
nodes.materials_raw: Star polymers with varying functionality (f = 2 to >10)
nodes.methods_raw: Multiparticle collision dynamics + molecular dynamics simulations
nodes.key_findings: Star polymer migrates toward centerline of cylindrical tube with increasing flow strength; degree of inward migration amplified with increasing functionality or monomer number; low-functionality stars undergo large conformational changes (tumbling motion) at high flow; high-functionality stars maintain steady stretched state (trumpet shape); tumbling suppressed with increasing functionality; trumpet structure amplifies inward migration; high-functionality stars perturb surrounding solvent more strongly → higher hydrodynamic drag forces → balanced by forces stabilizing near centerline; crossover from tumbling to tank-treading motion at f = 2-10
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the migration behavior be exploited for polymer separation by functionality? How do arm polydispersity effects manifest? Experimental validation of trumpet-shaped conformation?

nodes.label: Piezoelectric polymers: theory, challenges and opportunities
nodes.full_title: Piezoelectric polymers: theory, challenges and opportunities
nodes.authors: Michael Smith, Sohini Kar-Narayan
nodes.materials_raw: Piezoelectric polymers: PVDF, PVDF-TrFE, PVDF-HFP, PLLA, odd-numbered nylons, cellulose, silk, collagen, polyurea, polylactic acid
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review of piezoelectric polymer theory, symmetry requirements, mechanisms, processing, and applications
nodes.key_findings: Piezoelectric polymers: flexibility, ease of processing, biocompatibility vs lower d₃₃ than ceramics; symmetry requirements for piezoelectricity (non-centrosymmetric crystal structure); mechanisms: dipole alignment (poling), molecular chirality (PLLA), hydrogen bonding; PVDF: β-phase with all-trans conformation → largest piezoelectric response among polymers; poling methods: corona, contact, stretching; challenges: understanding piezoelectric mechanism in polymers, conflicting theories, improving performance; open questions: poling stability, long-term degradation, environmental effects; biological piezoelectricity: bone, tendon, collagen, wood; applications: sensors, actuators, energy harvesting, biomedical
nodes.scientific_questions: Can polymer piezoelectric coefficients approach ceramic levels? How does crystallinity affect piezoelectric response? Non-toxic alternatives to PVDF for biomedical applications?

nodes.label: Comprehensive review of piezoelectric polymer theo
Application of the Tube Model to Explain the Unexpected Decrease in Polymer Bending Energy Induced b
nodes.full_title: Application of the Tube Model to Explain the Unexpected Decrease in Polymer Bending Energy Induced by Knot Formation
nodes.authors: Luwei Lu, Haoqi Zhu, Yuyuan Lu, Lijia An, Liang Dai
nodes.materials_raw: Knotted polymers (theoretical model)
nodes.methods_raw: Tube model analysis, molecular dynamics simulations, phase diagram construction
nodes.key_findings: Knotting generally expected to increase polymer bending energy, but simulations show knotting can decrease bending energy ("negative extra bending"); tube model explains: polymer segments in knot core confined in virtual tube; tube affects bending in two opposite ways: (1) tube curvature increases bending, (2) tube confinement suppresses bending; second effect overwhelms under certain conditions because: (i) excluded volume interactions reduce accessible tube diameter, (ii) polymer segments tend to escape tube → entropic force straightens conformation; phase diagram quantifies competition between these effects; demonstrates usefulness of tube model for polymer knots
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the tube model predict knot probability in long polymers? How does knot type (trefoil, figure-8) affect the bending energy? Experimental validation with DNA knots?

nodes.label: Protic Ionic Liquids: Properties and Applications
nodes.full_title: Protic Ionic Liquids: Properties and Applications
nodes.authors: Tamar L. Greaves, Calum J. Drummond
nodes.materials_raw: Protic ionic liquids (PILs): ethylammonium nitrate (EAN), various ammonium/phosphonium-based PILs
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review of PIL formation, physicochemical properties, and applications
nodes.key_findings: PILs formed by proton transfer from Brønsted acid to base; distillation possible for some PILs (unique for ionic liquids); extensive physicochemical properties: thermal phase behavior, glass transition, melting point, thermal stability, density, refractive index, surface tension, viscosity, ionic conductivity, polarity; EAN as prototypical PIL (comparison with water); amphiphile self-assembly in PILs (lyotropic and thermotropic phases); applications: organic synthesis (Knoevenagel, Diels-Alder, aldol, etc.), chromatography, MALDI-MS, biological applications (protein solvents, biocatalysis, antimicrobial), nonaqueous electrolytes, polymer membrane fuel cells, lubricants, explosive formulations; industrial example: BASIL process (BASF)
nodes.scientific_questions: Can PILs achieve ionic conductivity comparable to liquid electrolytes? How does PIL nanostructure affect solvent properties? Long-term stability of PILs under electrochemical cycling?

nodes.label: Effects of Chain Flexibility on Polymer Conformation in Dilute Solution Studied by Lattice Monte Car
nodes.full_title: Effects of Chain Flexibility on Polymer Conformation in Dilute Solution Studied by Lattice Monte Carlo Simulation
nodes.authors: Yunqi Li, Qingrong Huang, Tongfei Shi, Lijia An
nodes.institution: Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry CAS
nodes.materials_raw: Homopolymers in good solvents (simulation)
nodes.methods_raw: Modified eight-site bond fluctuation Monte Carlo simulation, bond angle constraint, persistence length analysis, equivalent ellipsoid model
nodes.key_findings: Chain flexibility effects on polymer conformation studied via lattice Monte Carlo; orientation of polymer chains under confinement driven by loss of conformational entropy; polymer conformation changes gradually from spherical iso-diametric ellipsoid to rodlike iso-diametric ellipsoid with decreasing flexibility; freely jointed chain (FJC) vs wormlike chain (WLC) model comparison; quasi-quantitative boundary for model suitability established; persistence length as key parameter for chain flexibility; simulation model incorporates bond angle constraint coupled with persistence length
nodes.scientific_questions: How do solvent quality effects interact with chain flexibility? Can the simulation predict real polymer behavior quantitatively? Extension to branched and ring polymers?

nodes.label: Modified eight-site bond fluctuation Monte Carlo s
Shear Banding in Entangled Polymers: Stress Plateau, Banding Location, and Lever Rule
nodes.full_title: Shear Banding in Entangled Polymers: Stress Plateau, Banding Location, and Lever Rule
nodes.authors: Yongjin Ruan, Yuyuan Lu, Lijia An, Zhen-Gang Wang
nodes.materials_raw: Entangled polymer melts (simulation)
nodes.methods_raw: Molecular dynamics simulation, steady shear analysis, stress plateau characterization
nodes.key_findings: Shear banding in entangled polymer melts under steady shear; stress vs shear rate curve exhibits plateau spanning nearly two decades of shear rates; steady shear stress unchanged after switching shear rates within plateau region; strong correlation in shear band location between different shear rates from same microstate → importance of inherent structural heterogeneity in entangled network; for persistent steady bands: shear rate in slow band and band proportion don't change much with imposed rate, but fast band shear rate increases proportionally → contradicts lever rule; challenges the continuum theory view of shear banding as equilibrium-like phase separation
nodes.scientific_questions: What determines the inherent structural heterogeneity that nucleates shear bands? Can shear banding be controlled by polymer architecture? Experimental validation of the lever rule violation?

nodes.label: Mechanisms of Flow-Induced Polymer Translocation
nodes.full_title: Mechanisms of Flow-Induced Polymer Translocation
nodes.authors: Zhenhua Wang, Ruishu Wang, Yuyuan Lu, Lijia An, An-Chang Shi, Zhen-Gang Wang
nodes.materials_raw: Linear and ring polymers in nanochannels
nodes.methods_raw: Multiparticle collision dynamics + molecular dynamics simulations
nodes.key_findings: Both end-capture and fold-capture present in capture of linear chains in weak flows; fold-capture becomes dominant in strong flows → similar behavior for linear and ring chains in strong flow regime; for narrow channels: critical flux decreases with channel size (consistent with Wu et al. prediction); for large channels: critical flux independent of channel size (consistent with de Gennes prediction); two scaling regimes indicate confined blob crossover from free-draining to non-draining as channel size increases; polymer conformation exhibits flow-induced coil-compact-stretch transition (not first order); monomers far from channel and in channel exhibit independent dynamics
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the capture mechanism be controlled by polymer functionalization? How does polymer stiffness affect translocation? Practical implications for DNA sequencing optimization?

nodes.label: Linear and ring polymers in nanochannels
Stress-Structure Relationship of the Reversible Associating Polymer Network under Start-up Shear Flo
nodes.full_title: Stress-Structure Relationship of the Reversible Associating Polymer Network under Start-up Shear Flow
nodes.authors: Ai-Qing Liu, Li-Jun Liu, Wen-Sheng Xu, Xiao-Lei Xu, Ji-Zhong Chen, Li-Jia An
nodes.materials_raw: Telechelic reversible associating polymer gels (physical gels with noncovalent crosslinks)
nodes.methods_raw: Langevin dynamics simulation, start-up shear flow analysis, stress-structure relationship
nodes.key_findings: Three shear regimes: (1) weak shear (W=12.6): stress proportional to strain → strain hardening → maximum → steady state; gel network slightly broken, maintains framework; hardening from excessive chain stretching; (2) intermediate shear (W=505.6): no strain hardening, dramatic stress increase at large strains; network rupture at small strains, recovery at large strains; (3) strong shear (W=6319.7): network immediately broken, stress-strain resembles classical polymeric liquids; bridge chains responsible for mechanical strength; dangling and free chains cannot withstand stress; telechelic polymers form clusters as physical crosslinks; four chain types: bridge, loop, dangling, free
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the shear regimes be predicted from association strength alone? How does association kinetics affect stress relaxation? Designing associating networks with tunable shear response?

nodes.label: All-polymer piezo-ionic-electric electronics
nodes.full_title: All-polymer piezo-ionic-electric electronics
nodes.authors: Tianpei Xu, Long Jin, Yong Ao, Jieling Zhang, Yue Sun, Shenglong Wang, Yuanxiao Qu, Longchao Huang, Tao Yang, Weili Deng, Weiqing Yang
nodes.materials_raw: PVDF/Nafion/PVDF (PNP) sandwich structure, all-polymer piezo-ionic-electric electronics
nodes.methods_raw: Sandwich structure fabrication, piezoelectric/piezoionic coupling characterization, d₃₃ measurement, pressure sensitivity testing, power output measurement
nodes.key_findings: All-polymer piezo-ionic-electric electronics with PVDF/Nafion/PVDF sandwich; regularized ion-electron interfaces for remarkable electromechanical performance; piezoelectric effect and piezoionic effect mutually couple at ion-electron interfaces → unique piezo-ionic-electric working mechanism; massive interfacial accumulation of ion and electron charges → force-electric coupling enhancement; d₃₃ ~80.70 pC/N (much higher than PVDF alone ~-33 pC/N); pressure sensitivity 51.50 mV/kPa; maximum peak power 34.66 mW/m²; self-polarization by bending (no poling needed); transducer lights LEDs; sensor detects weak physiological signals and mechanical vibration; paradigm for highly-optimized all-polymer piezo-generators; overcomes limitations of disordered ion-electron interfaces in previous systems
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the PNP concept be extended to other ionomer/piezoelectric polymer combinations? How does the sandwich thickness ratio affect performance? Long-term stability of the ion-electron interfaces?

nodes.label: Viscoelastic Dynamics of Photothermal-Responsive Liquid Crystal Elastomer Fibers
nodes.full_title: Viscoelastic Dynamics of Photothermal-Responsive Liquid Crystal Elastomer Fibers
nodes.authors: Shihao Xu, Zhixiu Hao, Xindong Chen, Chao Wan, Li Yu, Xi-Qiao Feng
nodes.materials_raw: Liquid crystal elastomer (LCE) fibers with photothermal agents (CNTs, Au nanoparticles)
nodes.methods_raw: 1D string model, order parameter for active deformation, temperature-dependent linear viscoelasticity for passive deformation, independent parameter measurement, numerical algorithm, PID control simulation
nodes.key_findings: Fundamental framework for modeling photothermal-responsive LCE (PTR-LCE) fiber dynamics; fiber decomposed into active (order parameter-driven) and passive (viscoelastic) deformation parts; temperature-dependent linear viscoelasticity captures passive response; independent experimental measurements for model parameters; validated for convergence, time efficiency, and accuracy; simulates both open-loop and PID-controlled actuators; reveals physical mechanisms underlying PTR-LCE fiber dynamics; LCE fibers as "artificial muscles" for soft robotics; photochemical (azobenzene) vs photothermal (CNTs, Au NPs) mechanisms; applications: contraction, bending, twisting actuators
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the model predict fatigue life of LCE actuators? How does fiber diameter affect photothermal response time? Multi-physics coupling (optical-thermal-mechanical) optimization?

nodes.label: Phase Behavior and Interfacial Properties of Diblock Copolymer-Homopolymer Ternary Mixtures: Influen
nodes.full_title: Phase Behavior and Interfacial Properties of Diblock Copolymer-Homopolymer Ternary Mixtures: Influence of Volume Fraction of Copolymers and Interaction Energy
nodes.authors: Jian Wang, Jian-hui Song, Yu-yuan Lu, Yong-jin Ruan, Li-jia An
nodes.institution: Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry CAS
nodes.materials_raw: A-b-B diblock copolymer in A/B homopolymer blend (ternary mixture)
nodes.methods_raw: Monte Carlo simulation, confined between asymmetric hard walls, phase behavior and interfacial property analysis
nodes.key_findings: Block copolymers as polymeric surfactants at A/B interface; weak interaction: copolymers uniformly distributed; strong interaction: distribution changes from smooth interface to curved/multi-layer interface; increasing copolymer volume fraction (φ_C): profiles broaden, lamellar phase forms at φ_C ≥ 0.4, thinner layers at higher φ_C; interface transforms from plane to curved surface with increasing φ_C; copolymer chains stretch and orient perpendicular to interface with increasing ε_AB; difference between symmetric and asymmetric copolymers negligible when block lengths comparable; SCFT and simulation compared for understanding fluctuation effects
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the simulation predict experimental blend morphologies quantitatively? How does polydispersity affect phase behavior? Extending to triblock and gradient copolymer compatibilizers?

nodes.label: phase behavior and interfacial property analysis
Anti-Creep Adhesive Tapes via Trapped-Entanglement-Regulated Topological Networks
nodes.full_title: Anti-Creep Adhesive Tapes via Trapped-Entanglement-Regulated Topological Networks
nodes.authors: Le Yao, Yi Huang, Guoqing Chen, Ziqian Zhou, He Zhu, Qi Zhang, Shiping Zhu
nodes.institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
nodes.materials_raw: Ionogel-based adhesives with trapped entanglement topology, ionic liquids (ILs), polymer networks with varying architectures (linear, covalent-crosslink, trapped-entanglement)
nodes.methods_raw: Systematic comparison of three network architectures, creep testing, adhesion strength measurement, topological network design
nodes.key_findings: Anti-creep adhesive tapes via trapped entanglements as non-local movable crosslinks; entanglement-stabilized networks achieve 3-orders-of-magnitude reduction in creep rate while maintaining strong interfacial adhesion; long-chain entanglements + sparse crosslinks enable efficient elastic energy storage without sacrificing tackiness; resolves trade-off between dynamic dissipation and permanent mechanical integrity; ionic interactions (via ILs) enable rapid energy dissipation and interfacial wetting under dynamic conditions; entanglement-dominated network ensures elastic energy storage for long-term stability; topology-driven approach addresses fundamental limitation of transient non-covalent interactions (dynamic reversibility → creep under sustained stress)
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the trapped entanglement concept be applied to other adhesive formats (tapes, glues, patches)? How does entanglement density affect the creep-adhesion balance? Long-term performance under cyclic loading?

nodes.label: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
Design of a Recyclable Thiol−ene Network with Dynamic Ether Linkages: Regulations of Creep and Dynam
nodes.full_title: Design of a Recyclable Thiol−ene Network with Dynamic Ether Linkages: Regulations of Creep and Dynamicity
nodes.authors: Pawan Kumar, Rahul Patwal, Ramkrishna Sarkar
nodes.materials_raw: Benzylic ether-based trifunctional alkenes, dithiols (1,2-ethanedithiol, 1,6-hexanedithiol, 1,10-decanedithiol), thiol-ene networks with dynamic ether linkages
nodes.methods_raw: Thiol-ene click polymerization, cross-link density variation, creep testing, stress relaxation, shape memory testing, chemical recycling demonstration
nodes.key_findings: Dynamic ether linkages in thiol-ene networks → robustness + malleability + reprocessability; cross-link density regulates creep and dynamicity; increasing cross-link density → increased creep (counterintuitive, explained by network architecture); dithiol chain length regulates dynamicity; noteworthy thermal robustness and chemical stability; shape memory behavior demonstrated; degradation and chemical recycling of polymers achieved; upcycling strategy: degraded monomer repurposed to construct new dynamic network with improved mechanical properties; broadens scope of thiol-ene chemistry for recyclable cross-linked polymers
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the ether exchange be catalyzed for faster reprocessing? How does the ether linkage density affect network topology? Extending to other click chemistry dynamic networks?

nodes.label: thiol-ene networks with dynamic ether linkages
A New Approach to the Theory of Relaxing Polymeric Media
nodes.full_title: A New Approach to the Theory of Relaxing Polymeric Media
nodes.authors: M. S. Green, A. V. Tobolsky
nodes.materials_raw: Polymeric networks (rubber), relaxing media
nodes.methods_raw: Molecular theory development, kinetic theory of elasticity extension to non-isotropic networks, thermodynamic analysis
nodes.key_findings: Foundational paper for molecular theory of viscoelastic polymers; stress expressed in terms of strain history via distribution of internal strain parameters; second law of thermodynamics defines dissipation at constant temperature; explicit expressions for energy dissipation for any strain history; kinetic theory of elasticity extended to non-isotropic polymeric networks; tensor expression for stress-strain-time relations; model: equal rates of breaking and reforming of bonds constituting network chains; no permanent changes occurring with time (reversible relaxation); precursor to modern viscoelastic models (reptation, sticky Rouse, tube models)
nodes.scientific_questions: (Historical - answered by subsequent developments in reptation theory, tube models, etc.)

nodes.label: kinetic theory of elasticity extension to non-isot
嵌段共聚物增容剂对不相容均聚物共混体系相行为和界面性质的影响 (Effect of block copolymer compatibilizers on phase behavior and inte
nodes.full_title: 嵌段共聚物增容剂对不相容均聚物共混体系相行为和界面性质的影响 (Effect of block copolymer compatibilizers on phase behavior and interfacial properties of immiscible homopolymer blends)
nodes.journal: 高分子学报 (Acta Polymerica Sinica) 2016, No. 3, 271-286
nodes.authors: 王健, 卢宇源, 徐玉赐, 阮永金, 李良一, 安立佳
nodes.institution: 中国科学院长春应用化学研究所
nodes.materials_raw: A/A-b-B/B ternary blends (homopolymer/block copolymer/homopolymer), block copolymer compatibilizers
nodes.methods_raw: Review of experimental, theoretical (SCFT), and simulation studies on copolymer compatibilization
nodes.key_findings: Block copolymers as polymeric surfactants for immiscible blends; selective localization at interface → reduced interfacial tension, improved adhesion, prevented coalescence; chain entanglement across interface necessary for mechanical strength; homopolymer/copolymer/homopolymer as classic ternary system; history: Molau (1960s) discovered emulsification effect; Leibler and Noolandi (1980s) theoretical foundations; Chinese researchers contributed significantly (Feng Zhiliu, An Lijia, Tang Tao, Zhao Hanying); key factors: copolymer architecture, volume fraction, interaction energy, chain length
nodes.scientific_questions: Can copolymer compatibilizers be designed for specific blend morphologies? How does copolymer polydispersity affect compatibilization efficiency? Extending to nanocomposite-filled blends?

nodes.label: and simulation studies on copolymer compatibilizat
中国科学院长春应用化学研究所
Unified Memory Kernel for Polymer Dynamics: Coupling Hydrodynamic and Excluded Volume Effects in Gen
nodes.full_title: Unified Memory Kernel for Polymer Dynamics: Coupling Hydrodynamic and Excluded Volume Effects in Generalized Langevin Equations
nodes.authors: Xiaofei Tian, Qinhang Liu, Zhi-Chao Yan, Tongfei Shi, Jizhong Chen
nodes.materials_raw: Polymer chains in various solvent conditions
nodes.methods_raw: Generalized Langevin equation (GLE), memory kernel function (MKF) derivation, Langevin dynamics simulation validation
nodes.key_findings: End-to-end vector memory kernel function K(t) analytically determined from autocorrelation C_rr(t) and force-position cross-correlation C_fr(t); under preaveraged approximation, C_fr(t)/C_rr(t) becomes time-independent → K(t) depends only on C_rr(t); universally applicable analytical expression for K(t) derived; explicitly links hydrodynamic interactions (HI), excluded volume (EV), and their interplay through two parameters (α, β); α, β govern subdiffusive scaling and are linear functions of Flory exponent; validates against Langevin dynamics simulations; decodes multiscale mean-squaredisplacement behavior by linking memory effects to Flory exponent; advances beyond Rouse chain MKF to include HI and EV coupling
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the MKF framework handle ring polymers and branched architectures? How does the preaveraged approximation break down for stiff chains? Applications to polymer translocation and cyclization dynamics?

nodes.label: Polymer chains in various solvent conditions
Slip-Link Theory Predicts Entangled, Star-Branched Relaxation Measurably Different from Tube Model P
nodes.full_title: Slip-Link Theory Predicts Entangled, Star-Branched Relaxation Measurably Different from Tube Model Predictions
nodes.authors: Maria Katzarova, Diego Becerra, Andrés Córdoba, Konstantin Taletskiy, Jay D. Schieber
nodes.materials_raw: Entangled star-branched polymer melts (up to 12 entanglements per arm)
nodes.methods_raw: Slip-link model calculations, corrected algorithm for star-branched relaxation, relaxation spectrum and zero-shear-rate viscosity analysis, comparison with tube models
nodes.key_findings: Star-branched polymers: relaxation time scales as exp(γM_a/M_e) (much stronger than linear M^3.4); tube models with ad hoc corrections give poor and inconsistent predictions for stars; slip-link model: self-consistent constraint release dynamics without additional physics; only change needed for different architectures is chain free energy (not model parameters); corrected algorithm orders of magnitude faster than previous slip-link calculations; slip-link correctly describes observed molecular-weight dependence for star-branched melts; tube model fails to model constraint release self-consistently; slip-link includes fluctuations absent in tube models
nodes.scientific_questions: Can slip-link handle multi-arm stars (more than one branch point)? How does slip-link predict star-linear blend dynamics? Computational efficiency for industrial polymer design?

nodes.label: Viscoelastic properties of physically crosslinked networks, Part 2. Dynamic mechanical moduli
nodes.full_title: Viscoelastic properties of physically crosslinked networks, Part 2. Dynamic mechanical moduli
nodes.authors: F. Tanaka, S. F. Edwards
nodes.materials_raw: Transient polymer networks with reversible crosslinks (associating end groups)
nodes.methods_raw: Linear response theory for oscillatory deformations, transient network model, frequency-temperature superposition
nodes.key_findings: Foundational theory for physically crosslinked (associating) polymer networks; three network types: covalent, thermo-reversible, entangled; storage modulus G'(ω) increases with temperature (opposite to uncrosslinked melts!); plateau extends to lower ω with increasing Tτ (temperature × lifetime); temperature shift factor a_T proportional to Tτ → exponential temperature dependence; frequency-dependent viscosity η(ω) generally smaller than stationary viscosity η_st(γ̇) at ω = γ̇ → breakdown of Cox-Merz rule in physically crosslinked networks; chain breakage rate related to temperature, molecular weight, and bond lifetime; precursor to modern associating polymer theories
nodes.scientific_questions: (Historical - answered by subsequent developments in sticky Rouse, transient network models)

nodes.label: Transient polymer networks with reversible crosslinks
Linear response theory for oscillatory deformation
管子模型 (Tube Model Review)
nodes.full_title: 管子模型 (Tube Model Review)
nodes.journal: 高分子学报 (Acta Polymerica Sinica) 2018, No. 12, 1493-1512
nodes.authors: 阮永金, 卢宇源, 安立佳
nodes.institution: 中国科学院长春应用化学研究所
nodes.materials_raw: Entangled polymer melts and concentrated solutions
nodes.methods_raw: Review of tube model (Doi-Edwards model) and its modifications: contour length fluctuation (CLF), constraint release (CR), convective constraint release (CCR), GLaMM theory
nodes.key_findings: Comprehensive Chinese-language review of tube model for entangled polymer rheology; de Gennes (1971) reptation concept → Doi-Edwards tube model (1978-1986); three basic assumptions of DE model; molecular weight scaling: relaxation time ~ N^3.4, viscosity ~ N^3.4, diffusion ~ N^-2.4; DE model too simplified → deviations from experiments; modifications: CLF (contour length fluctuations), CR (constraint release from surrounding chains), CCR (convective constraint release under flow); GLaMM theory as most complete modification; tube model marks beginning of molecular rheology era; challenges: multi-chain interactions, fast large-deformation rheology
nodes.scientific_questions: Can beyond-tube models (slip-link, slip-spring) replace tube model for industrial applications? Unified framework for linear and nonlinear rheology? Molecular design of processing aids based on tube model predictions?

nodes.label: Entangled polymer melts and concentrated solutions
Review of tube model  and its modifications: conto
Steady-State Extensional Viscosity of Wormlike Micellar Solutions via Dissipative Particle Dynamics
nodes.full_title: Steady-State Extensional Viscosity of Wormlike Micellar Solutions via Dissipative Particle Dynamics Simulations
nodes.authors: Yusuke Koide, Takato Ishida, Takashi Uneyama, Yuichi Masubuchi
nodes.materials_raw: Wormlike micellar solutions (surfactant self-assembled aggregates: CTAB/NaSal, CPyCl/NaSal systems)
nodes.methods_raw: Dissipative particle dynamics (DPD) simulations, steady-state extensional viscosity measurement, micellar structure analysis (radius of gyration R_g), scission kinetics analysis
nodes.key_findings: Steady-state extensional viscosity η_E(ε̇) shows nonmonotonic behavior: plateau at low ε̇, increase to maximum, then decrease at high ε̇; nonmonotonicity arises from competition between micellar stretching and scission under uniaxial extensional flow; proposed relation connecting extensional viscosity to micellar structures and kinetics provides unified description for unentangled wormlike micellar solutions across various temperatures, concentrations, and extension rates; DPD simulations capture the three-regime behavior observed experimentally in opposed jet and filament-stretching rheometer studies; micellar scission at high extension rates reduces extensional viscosity; challenges in measuring extensional rheology of wormlike micelles addressed by computational approach
nodes.scientific_questions: Can DPD simulations predict transient extensional behavior (strain hardening before rupture)? How do entanglements between wormlike micelles modify the nonmonotonic behavior? Scaling laws for extensional viscosity in different concentration regimes?

nodes.label: Influence of Chain Entanglement on Rheological and Mechanical Behaviors of Polymerized Ionic Liquids
nodes.full_title: Influence of Chain Entanglement on Rheological and Mechanical Behaviors of Polymerized Ionic Liquids
nodes.authors: Gang Liu, Ronald G. Larson, Lei Li, Huan Luo, Xi He, Yanhua Niu, Guangxian Li
nodes.materials_raw: Polymerized ionic liquid (PIL) poly[1-(4-vinylbenzyl)-3-methylimidazolium bis(trifluoromethylsulfonyl)imide] (P[VBMIM][TFSI]), wide range of molecular weights
nodes.methods_raw: RAFT polymerization, SEC molecular weight characterization, steady-shear rheology, LAOS (large-amplitude oscillatory shear), extensional rheology, cyclic stretch testing
nodes.key_findings: Zero-shear viscosity scaling η₀ ∼ M_w^α shows two regimes: α = 1.1 ± 0.1 (disentangled) and 3.6 ± 0.4 (entangled), typical for neutral entangled polymers; entanglement molecular weight M_e = 1.6×10⁵ g/mol (experimental, rheological) and 1.8×10⁵ g/mol (calculated from packing length p = 8.6 Å and Kuhn length b = 20 Å) — much higher than conventional uncharged polymers due to monomer bulkiness; reversible strain-induced disentanglement observed under LAOS; strong strain hardening in extensional flow for entangled samples; chain entanglement in high-MW PIL brings significant improvement in mechanical strength and robust recoverability under cyclic stretch vs unentangled PILs; high-MW PILs processable for high-performance electrolytes
nodes.scientific_questions: How do electrostatic interactions between charged chains and counterions modify entanglement dynamics compared to neutral polymers? What determines the unusually high M_e in PILs? Can PIL entanglement be tuned by counterion design?

nodes.label: 聚合物弹性体材料应力-应变关系的理论研究 (Theoretical Study on Stress-Strain Relationship of Polymer Elastomers)
nodes.full_title: 聚合物弹性体材料应力-应变关系的理论研究 (Theoretical Study on Stress-Strain Relationship of Polymer Elastomers)
nodes.journal: 高分子学报 (Acta Polymerica Sinica) 2019, Vol. 50, No. 12
nodes.authors: 丁芳, 张欢, 丁明明, 石彤非, 李云琦, 安立佳
nodes.institution: 中国科学院长春应用化学研究所
nodes.materials_raw: Polymer elastomers (rubber, thermoplastic elastomers - TPE), general elastomeric materials
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive comparison of 30+ constitutive models (phenomenological and statistical mechanics models), nonlinear curve fitting, Fréchet distance calculation for model similarity, mathematical equivalence analysis
nodes.key_findings: 30+ constitutive models compared systematically; five pairs of models found mathematically equivalent: Gent ↔ Warner, Three-Chain ↔ Eight-Chain (and others); some complex multi-parameter models can be unidirectionally replaced by simpler models in curve characteristics; phenomenological models (Mooney-Rivlin, Ogden, Yeoh, Gent) vs statistical mechanics models (Treloar, Arruda-Boyce, Three/Eight-Chain); stress-strain curve types: hard-brittle, hard-tough, hard-strong, soft-tough (typical elastomers, E = 1-10 MPa), soft-weak; crosslinked rubbers show no obvious yielding; elastoplastic materials show clear yield; constitutive models bridge macroscopic mechanics to microscopic structure
nodes.scientific_questions: Can machine learning replace analytical constitutive models for complex elastomers? How to incorporate time/temperature dependence into constitutive models for viscoelastic elastomers? Unified framework for filled vs unfilled elastomers?

nodes.label: Fréchet distance calculation for model similarity
近平衡态与快速启动剪切下缠结高分子流体流变行为研究 (Rheological Behavior of Entangled Polymer Fluids Under Near-Equilibrium a
nodes.full_title: 近平衡态与快速启动剪切下缠结高分子流体流变行为研究 (Rheological Behavior of Entangled Polymer Fluids Under Near-Equilibrium and Fast Startup Shear)
nodes.journal: 高分子学报 (Acta Polymerica Sinica) 2015, No. 7, 858-866
nodes.authors: 刘冬梅, 刘茗竹, 卢宇源, 安立佳, 张汉壮
nodes.materials_raw: Entangled polymer melts and solutions
nodes.methods_raw: Brownian dynamics simulation, GLaMM theory calculations, startup shear experiments, comparison of simulation vs theory vs experiment
nodes.key_findings: In linear regime, GLaMM theory agrees with experiments except small deviations (e.g., stress relaxation faster than experimental at Rouse time scale); under fast startup shear, significant discrepancies remain between theory and simulation even when time scaled by τ_d; confirms stretched entanglement network suppresses disentanglement; stress overshoot mechanism is chain retraction (not excessive orientation as tube model suggests); stress overshoot strain increases with shear rate (consistent with experiments, contradicts tube model prediction); questions tube model's molecular picture of chains freely retracting in smooth, barrier-free tubes; initial slope of stress-strain curve is 1 (affine deformation) not 4/5 (tube model prediction), indicating orientation and stretching cannot be decoupled
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the correct molecular picture for chain retraction in entangled systems? How to modify tube model for fast large-deformation rheology? Can slip-link/slip-spring models resolve these discrepancies?

nodes.label: comparison of simulation vs theory vs experiment
高分子/棒状纳米粒子复合物的分子动力学模拟 (MD Simulation of Polymer/Rod Nanoparticle Composites)
nodes.full_title: 高分子/棒状纳米粒子复合物的分子动力学模拟 (MD Simulation of Polymer/Rod Nanoparticle Composites)
nodes.journal: 高等学校化学学报 (Chemical Journal of Chinese Universities) 2021, Vol. 42, No. 3, 875-883
nodes.authors: 刘爱清, 徐文生, 徐晓雷, 陈继忠, 安立佳
nodes.materials_raw: Polymer nanocomposites (PNC) with rod-shaped nanoparticles in polymer matrix; volume fractions 0.8%-10%
nodes.methods_raw: Non-equilibrium molecular dynamics (NEMD) simulation, shear flow analysis, Weissenberg number (Wi) characterization, chain structure analysis (normalized R_g, gyration tensor, orientation suppression parameter), tumbling dynamics analysis
nodes.key_findings: Rod nanoparticles suppress polymer chain diffusion and relaxation with increasing volume fraction, while chain dimensions remain nearly unchanged; under shear flow, structural properties (normalized R_g, gyration tensor, orientation suppression) are nearly independent of nanoparticle volume fraction; polymer chain tumbling motion is suppressed by nanoparticles; shear viscosity curves for nanocomposites follow same trend as pure melt — Wi = 1 divides plateau region from shear-thinning region; nanorods quantitatively change fluid shear viscosity but don't alter the qualitative flow behavior; non-spherical nanoparticles (CNTs, nanorods, nanowires) more effective than spherical ones at modifying polymer properties
nodes.scientific_questions: How does nanoparticle aspect ratio affect the critical Wi for shear thinning? Can MD predict long-term aging effects in PNCs? Role of nanoparticle-polymer interactions (attractive vs repulsive) on rheological properties?

nodes.label: Polymer nanocomposites  with rod-shaped nanoparticles in pol
高分子黏弹性的经典唯象模型 (Classical Phenomenological Models for Polymer Viscoelasticity)
nodes.full_title: 高分子黏弹性的经典唯象模型 (Classical Phenomenological Models for Polymer Viscoelasticity)
nodes.journal: 高分子学报 (Acta Polymerica Sinica) 2016, No. 6, 688-698
nodes.authors: 卢宇源, 安立佳, 王健
nodes.institution: 中国科学院长春应用化学研究所高分子物理与化学国家重点实验室
nodes.materials_raw: Entangled polymer melts and concentrated solutions (general)
nodes.methods_raw: Review of three classical phenomenological models: Maxwell model (spring + dashpot in series), Voigt-Kelvin model (spring + dashpot in parallel), transient network model; Boltzmann superposition principle derivation; linear viscoelasticity fundamentals
nodes.key_findings: Comprehensive Chinese-language review of classical viscoelastic models; Maxwell model: describes stress relaxation (σ = G·γ·exp(-t/τ)), relaxation time τ = η/G; Voigt-Kelvin model: describes creep (delayed elasticity); transient network model: entanglement points as transient crosslinks, chains as bead-spring chains; Boltzmann superposition principle: stress at time t is integral of all past strain increments weighted by relaxation modulus; these models remain practically useful for polymer processing despite being simplified; tube model (microscopic theory) covered separately; three model types: (1) continuum mechanics phenomenological, (2) transient network, (3) microscopic molecular theories
nodes.scientific_questions: How to bridge phenomenological models with molecular theories for quantitative predictions? Can transient network models capture nonlinear rheology better than Maxwell models? Extension to branched/copolymer architectures?

nodes.label: Review of three classical phenomenological models:
中国科学院长春应用化学研究所高分子物理与化学国家重点实验室
高分子动力学的单链模型 (Single-Chain Models for Polymer Dynamics)
nodes.full_title: 高分子动力学的单链模型 (Single-Chain Models for Polymer Dynamics)
nodes.journal: 高分子学报 (Acta Polymerica Sinica) 2017, No. 5, 727-737
nodes.authors: 阮永金, 王振华, 卢宇源, 安立佳
nodes.institution: 中国科学院长春应用化学研究所高分子物理与化学国家重点实验室
nodes.materials_raw: Polymer dilute solutions (general); linear and branched polymers
nodes.methods_raw: Review of three single-chain models: Rouse model (no hydrodynamic interactions), Zimm model (two-body HI), partially permeable sphere model (multi-body HI); derivation of dynamic equations, diffusion coefficients, intrinsic viscosity
nodes.key_findings: Rouse model: bead-spring chain, no excluded volume, no HI, point friction; Zimm model adds two-body HI via Oseen tensor; partially permeable sphere model (proposed by authors' group) handles multi-body HI and long-range cumulative effects; Rouse model extended to short-chain polymer melts/concentrated solutions; de la Torre et al. added volume correction factor to Kirkwood-Riseman theory; Wiegel's permeability function considers local density but treats chain segments as point particles; authors' partially permeable sphere model effectively handles multi-body HI and long-range cumulative effects, establishing intrinsic viscosity theory for arbitrary topologies; Einstein's colloidal sphere viscosity cannot directly apply to polymer coils because solvent penetrates the coil
nodes.scientific_questions: Can partially permeable sphere model handle ring polymers quantitatively? How to extend to semidilute solutions where inter-chain HI matters? Connection to modern computational methods (Brownian dynamics, Stokesian dynamics)?

nodes.label: Review of three single-chain models: Rouse model
Dynamic reaction-induced phase separation in tunable, adaptive covalent networks
nodes.full_title: Dynamic reaction-induced phase separation in tunable, adaptive covalent networks
nodes.authors: Katie M. Herbert, Patrick T. Getty, Neil D. Dolinski, Jerald E. Hertzog, Derek de Jong, James H. Lettow, Joy Romulus, Jonathan W. Onorato, Elizabeth M. Foster, Stuart J. Rowan
nodes.institution: University of Chicago (Pritzker School of Molecular Engineering)
nodes.materials_raw: Dynamic covalent networks (DCNs/CANs) based on thia-Michael (tM) reaction; benzalcyanoacetate-based Michael-acceptors; tetrathiol crosslinker PTMP; catalyst-free, room-temperature dynamic bonds
nodes.methods_raw: Thia-Michael reaction kinetics control via electron-donating/withdrawing groups; mechanical testing (tensile, DMA); thermal analysis (DSC, TGA); morphology characterization (AFM, optical microscopy); shape memory testing; compression molding reprocessing
nodes.key_findings: Catalyst-free, room-temperature dynamic thia-Michael bonds from benzalcyanoacetate-based acceptors; equilibrium directly tunable by electron-donating/withdrawing nature of Michael-acceptor; wide range of mechanical properties and thermal responses by modulating composition; dynamic reaction-induced phase separation (DRIPS) — a new concept where dynamic bond exchange itself drives phase separation, creating reconfigurable phase morphologies; reprogrammable shape-memory behavior demonstrated by heat-induced folding of predetermined structures; bonds active at ambient conditions represent attractive avenue for tuning properties; DRIPS provides reinforcement without traditional filler; at least 10 reprocessing cycles without significant property loss
nodes.scientific_questions: How does DRIPS morphology evolve over multiple reprocessing cycles? Can DRIPS be controlled spatially (e.g., by light or patterned catalysts)? Generalizability to other dynamic chemistries beyond thia-Michael?

University of Chicago (Pritzker School of Molecular Engineering)
Multipotent Elastomers via Tempering of Phase-Separated Dynamic Covalent Networks
nodes.full_title: Multipotent Elastomers via Tempering of Phase-Separated Dynamic Covalent Networks
nodes.authors: Nicholas R. Boynton, Camaryn M. Bennett, Trevor D. Hagan, Gabrielle R. Solymosy, Charlie A. Lindberg, Naomi A. Schaller, Stephanie L. Vivod, Shrayesh N. Patel, Stuart J. Rowan
nodes.institution: University of Chicago (Pritzker School of Molecular Engineering)
nodes.materials_raw: Dynamic covalent networks (DCNs) with thia-Michael (tM) bonds; benzalcyanoacetate (BCA)-based Michael acceptors; thiol-functionalized PEG derivatives (varying MW and architectures); tetrathiol crosslinkers
nodes.methods_raw: Tempering (isothermal heating at different temperatures followed by quenching), Raman spectroscopy for tM adduct quantification, AFM for DRIPS morphology, uniaxial tensile testing, cyclic loading-unloading, DSC thermal analysis
nodes.key_findings: Inspired by multipotent stem cells — single feedstock elastomer can be differentiated into different mechanical properties via tempering; increasing tempering temperature reduces Young's modulus and maximum stress while maintaining high elastic recovery and low energy dissipation; tempering is completely reversible — retempering at different temperature retunes properties; ~40% decrease in tM adducts from 45°C to 120°C tempering window; DRIPS morphology (continuous soft matrix + globular hard domains) reconfigures with tempering; lower T_g and upper T_UT transitions in DSC; lower activation energy for bond formation (45 kJ/mol) than bond cleavage (122 kJ/mol) stabilizes tempered properties at room temperature; extends pluripotent polymer concept (Science 2024) to multipotent elastomers within specific material class
nodes.scientific_questions: How does PEG MW affect tempering response range? Can spatial tempering patterns create gradient elastomers? Long-term stability of tempered states under mechanical cycling?

nodes.label: Raman spectroscopy for tM adduct quantification
Accessing pluripotent materials through tempering of dynamic covalent polymer networks
nodes.full_title: Accessing pluripotent materials through tempering of dynamic covalent polymer networks
nodes.authors: Nicholas R. Boynton, Joseph M. Dennis, Neil D. Dolinski, Charlie A. Lindberg, Anthony P. Kotula, Garrett L. Grocke, Stephanie L. Vivod, Joseph L. Lenhart, Shrayesh N. Patel, Stuart J. Rowan
nodes.materials_raw: Dynamic covalent networks with thia-Michael (tM) bonds; ditopic BCA Michael acceptor (pentaethylene glycol core); pentaerythritol tetrakis(3-mercaptopropionate) tetrathiol crosslinker; dithiol chain extender
nodes.methods_raw: Tempering protocol (isothermal heating → quenching), solution-state ¹H NMR for adduct quantification, DSC (two thermal transitions: T_g and T_UT), AFM for DRIPS morphology characterization, mechanical testing, shape memory testing, spatial patterning
nodes.key_findings: "Pluripotent" polymer concept — single feedstock differentiated into hard-brittle to soft-extensible via tempering (inspired by stem cells and metallurgy); three design criteria: (i) reconfigurable bonds, (ii) sensitivity to accessible/tunable stimuli, (iii) ability to retain properties within operational temperature window; BCA tM bonds: catalyst-free, room-temperature exchange, K_eq tunable from 10 M⁻¹ (electron-donating) to 500 M⁻¹ (electron-withdrawing); ~40% decrease in tM adducts between T_g and T_UT; lower E_a for bond formation (45 kJ/mol) than cleavage (122 kJ/mol) → tempered state stabilized at ambient; DRIPS morphology with continuous soft matrix and globular hard domains; shape memory behavior tailored through tempering; spatial patterning to control mechanical properties locally
nodes.scientific_questions: Can tempering approach extend to other dynamic chemistries beyond tM? What determines the ultimate property range accessible from a single feedstock? Industrial scalability of tempering-based property tuning?

nodes.label: Flagellar dynamics and entanglement of E. coli bacteria in polymeric hydrogel
nodes.full_title: Flagellar dynamics and entanglement of E. coli bacteria in polymeric hydrogel
nodes.authors: Diksha Shrestha, Deborah Okyere, Sam Mortenson, Jingyi Chen, Yong Wang
nodes.materials_raw: Polyethylene glycol (PEG)-based hydrogel; E. coli bacteria with fluorescently labeled flagella
nodes.methods_raw: Site-directed mutagenesis for flagellar labeling, specific fluorescence labeling, fluorescence microscopy for direct flagellar visualization, shape quantifiers and descriptors for flagella analysis, motility tracking
nodes.key_findings: Three distinct types of flagellar motions classified in hydrogels: swimming (SWIM), trapped (TRAP), stalled (STALL); shape descriptors reliably report bacterial flagellar behaviors; flagella-hydrogel polymer/mesh interactions reduce correlation between motility and flagellar dynamics; flagellar filament abnormalities identified due to hydrogel confinement and entanglement with hydrogel polymers; methods support development of hydrogels for trapping motile bacteria (contamination control in biomedical applications); extends previous cell-body motility studies to flagellar filament dynamics in complex environments
nodes.scientific_questions: How does hydrogel mesh size affect the SWIM/TRAP/STALL transition? Can hydrogel design selectively trap pathogenic bacteria? Role of flagellar bundling/unbundling in gel penetration?

Entropy-Driven Intramolecular Cross-Linking of Polymers near the Solubility Limit
nodes.full_title: Entropy-Driven Intramolecular Cross-Linking of Polymers near the Solubility Limit
nodes.authors: Xueqing Liu, Yunhui Wen, Shuailong Li, Zhiqin Xia, Xin Li, Peifan Li, Shaowei Shi
nodes.materials_raw: Single-chain nanoparticles (SCNPs); poly(4-vinylpyridine) (P4VP, 60 kDa); phosphotungstic acid (PWA) as noncovalent crosslinker; homopolymers, block copolymers, random copolymers
nodes.methods_raw: Dynamic light scattering (DLS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), concentration-dependent cross-linking, entropy-driven intramolecular collapse
nodes.key_findings: Entropy-driven strategy enables one-pot SCNP preparation at concentrations approaching polymer solubility limit (g/mL scale, orders of magnitude higher than previous <1 mg/mL); works for homopolymers, block copolymers, and random copolymers with high MW; PWA crosslinker acts as staining agent for TEM visualization; P4VP hydrodynamic diameter decreases from 10 nm to 7.5 nm upon intramolecular cross-linking; above overlap concentration, viscosity increase impedes intermolecular cross-linking → intramolecular reaction favored entropically; breaks conventional concentration barrier in SCNP synthesis; provides general framework for next-generation single-chain nanostructures
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the minimum polymer concentration needed for the entropy-driven mechanism to work? Can covalent crosslinkers replace noncovalent PWA for permanent SCNPs? Applications in catalysis and nanoreactors at practical concentrations?

nodes.label: Ionic polymers for bioelectronics
nodes.full_title: Ionic polymers for bioelectronics
nodes.authors: Ilaria Abdel Aziz, David Mecerreyes
nodes.materials_raw: Ionic polymers: poly(ionic liquid)s (PILs), poly(eutectic solvents), hydrogels, ionogels, eutectogels, conjugated ionic polymers/conjugated polyelectrolytes (CPEs), mixed ionic-electronic conducting polymers (PEDOT:PSS-based), conducting polymers (PPy, PANi, PEDOT)
nodes.methods_raw: Review of polymer design, synthesis, and characterization for bioelectronic devices; ion transport mechanisms; structure-ion conductivity relationships; device fabrication (organic electrochemical transistors - OECTs, amperometric detectors, controlled-release devices, neuromorphic computing)
nodes.key_findings: Comprehensive review of ionic polymers for bioelectronics; two main categories: (1) purely ionic conducting polymers (ionic polymers, hydrogels, ionogels, eutectogels) and (2) mixed ionic-electronic conducting polymers (conjugated polyelectrolytes, conjugated polymer gels); applications: controlled drug release (ion pumps, electroactive polymers), sensors (pressure, ion-selective, OECTs), electrophysiology recording (ECG, EEG, EMG), neuromorphic devices; biological signals based on ion and electron transport → materials with mixed transport highly desirable; tunable mechanical properties + scalable processing make ionic polymers ideal for bioelectronics; structure-property relationships for ion conduction in polymer matrices
nodes.scientific_questions: How to achieve simultaneous high ionic and electronic conductivity? Long-term stability of ionic polymers in biological environments? Biocompatibility and biodegradability of ionic polymer devices?

nodes.label: Quaternary tannic acid with improved leachability and biocompatibility for antibacterial medical the
nodes.full_title: Quaternary tannic acid with improved leachability and biocompatibility for antibacterial medical thermoplastic polyurethane catheters
nodes.authors: Yue Wang, Shuaizhen Liu, Kaidi Ding, Yaocheng Zhang, Xuejia Ding, Jianguo Mi
nodes.materials_raw: Thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU); quaternary tannic acid (QTA) — tannic acid (TA) grafted with quaternary ammonium salt (dimethyl dodecyl 6-bromohexyl ammoniumbromide); TPU/QTA blends
nodes.methods_raw: Melt extrusion blending, antibacterial testing (in vitro, flow state, in vivo), biocompatibility testing, mechanical property characterization, dissolution rate measurement
nodes.key_findings: QTA combines strong antibacterial effect of quaternary ammonium salt (QAS) with high biocompatibility of tannic acid; QTA blended with TPU via melt extrusion (convenient, industrially scalable); dissolution rate and biocompatibility of QTA significantly improved vs pure QAS; TPU/QTA maintains satisfactory antibacterial activities in vitro, under flow, and in vivo; QTA content of 4% is effective, durable, stable, and non-toxic; only slight effect on original TPU mechanical properties; addresses leaching problem of physically blended antibacterial agents; catheter infections account for 45% of hospital device-related infections
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the long-term leaching kinetics of QTA from TPU matrix? How does QTA particle size affect mechanical properties at higher loading? Can QTA approach be extended to other medical-grade polymers?

Effect of stereochemical constraints on the structural properties of folded proteins
nodes.full_title: Effect of stereochemical constraints on the structural properties of folded proteins
nodes.authors: Jack A. Logan, Jacob Sumner, Alex T. Grigas, Mark D. Shattuck, Corey S. O'Hern
nodes.materials_raw: Folded proteins (>2500 x-ray crystal structures); coarse-grained protein models
nodes.methods_raw: Collapse simulations of coarse-grained models with increasing complexity; structural analysis (R_g, fraction of core amino acids f_core, packing fraction φ, structure factor S(q)); comparison with x-ray crystal structures
nodes.key_findings: Folded proteins are compact with R_g(N) ~ N^ν where ν ≈ 1/3; f_core ≈ 0.09; packing fraction φ ≈ 0.55; internal scaling R_g(n) does not obey simple power law — instead R_g(n) ~ n^ν₁,₂ with ν₁ > 1/3 for small n and ν₂ < 1/3 for large n; minimal coarse-grained model: single spherical backbone bead + variable-sized side-chain beads with backbone bend- and dihedral-angle constraints recapitulates R_g(n), f_core, φ, and S(q) for >2500 protein structures; stereochemical constraints (bond angles, dihedral angles) essential for capturing protein structural properties; connects polymer physics (scaling laws) to protein structure
nodes.scientific_questions: Can this minimal model predict intrinsically disordered protein (IDP) structures? How do stereochemical constraints affect protein folding kinetics? Extension to membrane proteins with different packing constraints?

Geometry of disordered porous environments regulates cell migration
nodes.full_title: Geometry of disordered porous environments regulates cell migration
nodes.authors: Laeschkir Würthner, Frederik Graw
nodes.materials_raw: Extracellular matrix (ECM) porous environments; computational cell migration models
nodes.methods_raw: 3D cellular Potts model with amoeboid-like cell shape dynamics; computational modeling and theory; motility regime analysis; transport property calculations
nodes.key_findings: Cell migration in disordered porous environments characterized by distinct transient motility regimes that deviate from persistent motion — best described by "hopping" between "traps"; motility regimes and large-scale transport properties linked to geometrical properties of microstructure; spatial heterogeneities in porosity lead to nonhomogeneous cell distributions; cells effectively guided toward regions of low porosity — termed "porotaxis" (analogous to galvanotaxis/chemotaxis); ECM porosity identified as important control parameter shaping cell migration and cellular distribution; conceptual framework to relate experimentally observed cell motility modes to tissue structures; implications for chronic inflammation, immunity, and cancer
nodes.scientific_questions: How does porotaxis interact with chemotaxis in vivo? Can porosity gradients be designed to direct cell migration in tissue engineering? Role of nuclear mechanics in porosity sensing?

Tough hydrogels enabled by transient entanglements
nodes.full_title: Tough hydrogels enabled by transient entanglements
nodes.authors: Zhaoyang Yuan, Zhenxing Cao, Hao Wang, Haitao Wu, Jing Zheng, Rongchun Zhang, Jinrong Wu
nodes.institution: Sichuan University (State Key Laboratory of Advanced Polymer Materials)
nodes.materials_raw: Polyacrylamide (PAAm) hydrogels with abundant dangling chains forming transient entanglements
nodes.methods_raw: Network design with dangling chains for transient entanglements, mechanical testing (tensile, toughness), comparison with permanently trapped entanglements
nodes.key_findings: Simple yet effective strategy to resolve strength-toughness conflict in single-covalent-network hydrogels; PAAm networks with abundant dangling chains form transient entanglements (unlike permanently trapped entanglements); transient entanglements provide energy dissipation while maintaining network integrity; achieves simultaneous high toughness and strength; dangling chain length and density control entanglement dynamics; transient nature allows reconfiguration under stress → improved fatigue resistance
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the optimal dangling chain length for maximum toughness enhancement? How do transient entanglements behave under cyclic loading? Can this approach be extended to other hydrogel chemistries?

nodes.full_title: In Situ Visualization of Microphase Separation in High-Performance Hyperbranched Polyurethane
nodes.authors: Jingyuan Wei, Yufei Zhang, Huan Ma, Jia Li, Junzhuo Cheng, Haotian Ma, Shenggui Du, Kai Cheng, Hefeng Zhang, Tianqi Zhou, Yu Jiang, Daohong Zhang, Nikos Hadjichristidis
nodes.materials_raw: Hyperbranched polyurethane (PU-HPAE_x) elastomers; hyperbranched poly(amino ester) (HPAE) as dual-function macromonomer (chain extender + fluorescent probe)
nodes.methods_raw: Fluorescence spectroscopy (cluster-triggered emission CTE, through-space conjugation TSC), mechanical testing, SAXS/WAXD, quantum yield measurement, in situ fluorescence visualization of phase separation
nodes.key_findings: HPAE serves as both chain extender and nonconventional fluorescent probe for in situ visualization of microphase separation; hyperbranched architecture creates 3D network with high-density sacrificial H-bonds and well-defined microphase separation; exceptional mechanical properties: strength 65.80 MPa, elongation 1031.70%, toughness 185.3 MJ/m³ — overcomes classical strength-toughness conflict; CTE via TSC gives exceptionally strong fluorescence (quantum yield 11.16%); in situ visualization of micrometer-scale phase separation and its dynamic evolution; stimuli-responsive fluorescence under mechanical strain and humidity for smart sensing
nodes.scientific_questions: How does HPAE branching degree affect microphase separation kinetics? Can the fluorescent probe approach work for other PU chemistries? Correlation between CTE intensity and crosslink density?

Recent Advances in Dielectric Elastomer Actuators: Materials, Structures, and Applications
nodes.full_title: Recent Advances in Dielectric Elastomer Actuators: Materials, Structures, and Applications
nodes.authors: Junbo Peng, Luohui Zhou, Ofoq Normahmedov, Xudong Liang, Ye Shi
nodes.materials_raw: Dielectric elastomer actuators (DEAs); elastomer films (acrylic, silicone, polyurethane-based); compliant electrodes (carbon grease, CNTs, graphene, hydrogels)
nodes.methods_raw: Review of materials, structures, and applications; Maxwell stress analysis (σ = ε₀εᵣ(V/t)²); polymer network engineering; functional component incorporation
nodes.key_findings: DEAs are promising artificial muscles with large actuation strains, high energy/power densities, fast response; Maxwell stress compresses elastomer film thickness → in-plane expansion; key advances: polymer network engineering for improved performance, long-term stability, multifunctionality; functional component incorporation for enhanced dielectric properties; diverse structural designs for specific applications; remaining challenges: high driving voltage, electromechanical instability, lifetime, manufacturing scalability
nodes.scientific_questions: How to reduce driving voltage below 100 V while maintaining performance? Can self-healing DEAs be developed for long-term applications? Integration with sensing for closed-loop control?

nodes.label: Dynamic Covalent Oleogel with Mechanical Force-Induced Reversible Phase Transition for Self-Adaptive
nodes.full_title: Dynamic Covalent Oleogel with Mechanical Force-Induced Reversible Phase Transition for Self-Adaptive Lubrication
nodes.authors: Ao Xie, Jiaying Zhang, Xin-Gang Wang, Xiaopeng Ruan, Luyao Bao, Hao-Zhe Xu, Changmin Qi, Yanyan Bai, Ming Zhang, Yijing Liang, Qiangliang Yu, Meirong Cai, Feng Zhou, Weimin Liu
nodes.materials_raw: Poly(thioctic acid) (poly(TA)) oleogels; thioctic acid (TA) — natural small molecule with disulfide bonds; dynamic disulfide covalent networks; various common oils as gelation media
nodes.methods_raw: Ring-opening polymerization (ROP) of thioctic acid, thermally stimulated polymerization, tribological testing, mechanical force-induced phase transition characterization, H-bonding and Al(III)-carboxylate coordination crosslinking
nodes.key_findings: Mechanical force breaks disulfide bonds in poly(TA) backbone → gel-to-sol transition + sulfur radical generation; upon force removal, sulfur radicals rapidly rebind → sol-to-gel transition (reversible); poly(TA) oleogels gelate over a dozen common oils; self-adaptive lubrication: oleogel exists in gel state when machine stopped, sol state when running (adapts to friction conditions); excellent lubricating properties and outstanding performance stability under changing friction conditions; natural thioctic acid as building block — simple, versatile, efficient; disulfide bonds participate directly in polymer backbone (not just crosslinks); dynamic covalent network with mechanical responsiveness
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the critical force threshold for gel-to-sol transition? How does oil viscosity affect the phase transition kinetics? Can poly(TA) oleogels work under extreme temperatures?

nodes.full_title: Unexpected strong paramagnetism of hydrogels containing carbon-oxygen double bonds induced by calcium cations
nodes.authors: Ruoyang Chen, Yue-Yu Zhang, Xing Huang, Liping Wang, Lei Zhang, Chao Song, Lixiong Dai, Min Zhang, Jun Wang, Yong Jian, Weiyuan Xu, Hui Dong, Bingquan Peng, Shuqiang He, Shanshan Liang, Fangfang Dai, Qihui Fan, Fangfu Ye, Xin Zhang, Feng Zhang, Haiping Fang
nodes.institution: Multiple Chinese institutions (Shanghai Institute of Applied Physics CAS
nodes.materials_raw: Hydrogels rich in C=O groups: alginate, carboxymethyl chitosan, polyacrylamide (PAAm), N-isopropyl acrylamide (NIPAM), sodium hyaluronate; calcium cations (Ca²⁺)
nodes.methods_raw: SQUID magnetometry, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), computational modeling, MRI contrast agent testing, magnetic drug delivery demonstration
nodes.key_findings: Hydrogels conventionally thought to be diamagnetic can show strong paramagnetism with Ca²⁺ cations; paramagnetism arises from coupling of single Ca²⁺ with single C=O group → net magnetic moments; ubiquitous across multiple hydrogel types (alginate, chitosan, PAAm, NIPAM, hyaluronate); Curie law behavior (χ ∝ 1/T) from 2-60 K confirms paramagnetism; trace ferromagnetic contamination ruled out by ICP-MS (impurity moment 3 orders of magnitude lower); applications demonstrated: MRI contrast agent (alginate-calcium hydrogel), magnetic drug delivery carrier; eliminates need for potentially harmful magnetic additives in biomedical applications; provides insights into origin of magnetism in organic materials
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the critical Ca²⁺ concentration threshold for paramagnetism? Does the paramagnetism persist at physiological temperatures? Can other divalent cations (Mg²⁺, Zn²⁺) induce similar effects?

nodes.label: Multiple Chinese institutions (Shanghai Institute of Applied Physics CAS
Synergy between dynamic covalent boronic ester and boron-nitrogen coordination: strategy for self-he
nodes.full_title: Synergy between dynamic covalent boronic ester and boron-nitrogen coordination: strategy for self-healing polyurethane elastomers at room temperature with unprecedented mechanical properties
nodes.authors: Kai Song, Wujin Ye, Xingchen Gao, Huagao Fang, Yaqiong Zhang, Qi Zhang, Xueliang Li, Shanzhong Yang, Haibing Wei, Yunsheng Ding
nodes.institution: Hefei University of Technology (Department of Polymer Science and Engineering)
nodes.materials_raw: Supramolecular polyurethane elastomers (SPUEs); dynamic covalent boronic ester bonds; boron-nitrogen (B-N) coordination; chain extenders containing boron and nitrogen
nodes.methods_raw: Boronic ester transesterification, B-N coordination characterization, mechanical testing, self-healing efficiency measurement, surface wetting for accelerated healing
nodes.key_findings: Synergistic quadruple dynamic mechanism combining boronic ester and B-N coordination; highest tensile toughness (~182.2 MJ/m³) for room-temperature self-healable polymers; excellent ultimate tensile strength (~10.5 MPa) and ultra-high fracture energy (~72100 J/m²); B-N coordination facilitates boronic ester formation/dissociation at room temperature AND dramatically enhances mechanical properties via intermolecular coordinated crosslinking and intramolecular coordinated chain folding; B-N coordination and urethane H-bonding serve as sacrificial bonds → energy dissipation during stretching, recovery after release; superior notch insensitiveness and recoverability; self-healing efficiency dramatically accelerated by surface wetting
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the relative contribution of boronic ester vs B-N coordination to healing vs mechanical strength? Can the synergistic mechanism be generalized to other dynamic chemistries? Water-assisted healing mechanism details?

nodes.label: chain extenders containing boron and nitrogen
Hefei University of Technology (Department of Polymer Science and Engineering)
Dynamic Boronate Ester Chemistry Facilitating 3D Printing Interlayer Adhesion and Modular 4D Printin
nodes.full_title: Dynamic Boronate Ester Chemistry Facilitating 3D Printing Interlayer Adhesion and Modular 4D Printing of Polylactic Acid
nodes.authors: Wenjun Peng, Hanxin Xia, Jingjun Wu, Zizheng Fang, Xianming Zhang
nodes.materials_raw: Polylactic acid (PLA); dynamic boronate ester bonds; PLA/dynamic system blends for FDM 3D printing
nodes.methods_raw: Fused deposition modeling (FDM) 3D printing, boronate bond exchange for interlayer adhesion, Z-axis tensile testing, shape memory effect for 4D printing, modular assembly
nodes.key_findings: Boronate bond exchange enhances interlayer mechanical strength and enables modular 4D printing of PLA; 150% enhancement in Z-axis interlayer strength, nearly unchanged X-axis; excellent printability maintained; enhanced interlayer bonding facilitates modular assembly of intricate structures without traditional 3D-printed supports; combined with shape memory effects for diverse modular 4D printing possibilities; addresses fundamental weakness of FDM: poor interlayer adhesion due to rapid cooling; dynamic covalent bonds as solution to 3D printing limitations
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the optimal concentration of dynamic bonds for printability vs mechanical performance? Can this approach work with other FDM materials (ABS, PETG)? Long-term stability of boronate ester bonds in humid environments?

nodes.label: boronate bond exchange for interlayer adhesion
Programmable Liquid Crystal Elastomers Via Magnetic Field Assisted Oligomerization
nodes.full_title: Programmable Liquid Crystal Elastomers Via Magnetic Field Assisted Oligomerization
nodes.authors: Rakine Mouhoubi, Vincent Lapinte, Sébastien Blanquer
nodes.materials_raw: Liquid crystal elastomers (LCEs); liquid crystal oligomers (LCOs); mesogens with positive diamagnetic anisotropy
nodes.methods_raw: Magnetic field alignment during thio-Michael polyaddition, photo-crosslinking, nematic phase characterization, actuation testing (radial contraction/expansion, thickness contraction, bending)
nodes.key_findings: Magnetic field assisted thio-Michael polyaddition followed by photo-crosslinking enables volumetric mesogen alignment in LCEs; synthesizes magnetically aligned high-MW LCOs maintaining nematic phase before crosslinking; three distinct LCE networks by different magnetic field orientations: radial contraction/expansion, thickness contraction, bending; LCE four-leaf clovers demonstrate selective and reversible large-angle bending despite high thickness; addresses limitations of mechanical stretching (restricted to pre-shaped samples), surface-enforced alignment (limited to thin films), and rheological alignment (restricted by extrusion pathway); practical and versatile method for robust LCE actuators
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the maximum achievable alignment degree with magnetic fields? Can magnetic alignment combine with other alignment methods for hierarchical structures? Scalability of magnetic field alignment for large-area LCE actuators?

nodes.label: Magnetic field alignment during thio-Michael polya
基于π-π堆积作用的磁性(聚)离子液体的合成与性能 (Synthesis and Properties of Magnetic (Poly)Ionic Liquids based on π-π Sta
nodes.full_title: 基于π-π堆积作用的磁性(聚)离子液体的合成与性能 (Synthesis and Properties of Magnetic (Poly)Ionic Liquids based on π-π Stacking Interaction)
nodes.authors: 于晓亮 (Yu Xiaoliang)
nodes.institution: 天津大学材料科学与工程学院 (Tianjin University)
nodes.materials_raw: Magnetic (poly)ionic liquids; FeCl₄⁻ anion-based magnetic ionic liquids; ROMP-derived magnetic poly(ionic liquid)s; biphenyl and pyrene π-π stacking units; azobenzene photoresponsive units; perylene diimide derivatives
nodes.methods_raw: Ring-opening metathesis polymerization (ROMP), post-modification, SQUID magnetometry, ZFC-FC measurements, UV-vis photoresponse testing
nodes.key_findings: FeCl₄⁻ affects ROMP controllability → use ROMP + post-modification strategy; π-π stacking reduces Fe-Fe distance from >6 Å to ~4 Å → enhanced magnetic coupling; biphenyl π-π stacking → superparamagnetic behavior (T_c = 29 K for small molecule, 25 K for polymer); pyrene π-π stacking → soft ferromagnetism (hysteresis at 100 K, ZFC-FC bifurcation at 5.5 K); azobenzene incorporation → photoresponsive magnetism (UV light changes FeCl₄⁻ distance → magnetic property modulation); perylene diimide cation → additional magnetic control; demonstrates molecular design strategy for organic magnets using π-π stacking to control magnetic center distances
nodes.scientific_questions: Can room-temperature ferromagnetism be achieved in magnetic PILs? How does polymer chain flexibility affect π-π stacking-driven magnetic ordering? Practical applications of photoresponsive magnetic polymers?
