Gel-Based Ionic Circuits
nodes.full_title: Gel-Based Ionic Circuits
nodes.authors: Hyunjae Yoo, Yun Hyeok Lee, Min-Gyu Lee, Jeong-Yun Sun
nodes.materials_raw: Gel-based ionic circuits: hydrogels, ionogels, eutectogels; ionic conductors; ionic resistors, capacitors, memristors, transistors, bipolar junction transistors; TENGs, thermoelectric generators, concentration-driven power generators, piezoionic power generators, moisture electricity generators; electro-osmosis systems; ion-polymer metal composites (IPMCs)
nodes.methods_raw: Review of gel-based ionic circuit fundamentals, materials strategies, and device architectures; ionic conduction mechanisms; electrochemical processes; device integration
nodes.key_findings: Comprehensive categorization: passive elements (resistors, capacitors, memristors), active elements (transistors, BJTs), power sources (TENGs, TEGs, concentration-driven, piezoionic, moisture), non-circuit elements (electro-osmosis, IPMCs); gels as mechanically compliant ionic conductors bridge biological and artificial systems; exploit intrinsic ion characteristics (mass, diversity, local accumulation) for selectivity, hysteresis, chemical-electric signal transduction; dynamic and nonlinear behaviors emulate AND extend beyond electronic paradigms; addresses stability challenges (solvent leakage, ion leakage) through polyelectrolyte elastomers and other strategies
nodes.scientific_questions: Can gel-based ionic circuits achieve computational complexity comparable to electronic circuits? Long-term stability of ionic circuits under continuous operation? Integration of ionic and electronic circuits for hybrid systems?

nodes.label: Review of gel-based ionic circuit fundamentals
Self-healing and hyperelastic magneto-iono-elastomers through molecular confinement of magnetic anio
nodes.full_title: Self-healing and hyperelastic magneto-iono-elastomers through molecular confinement of magnetic anions
nodes.authors: Xuan Zhang, Lishu Zhang, Meilin Liu, Chin Boon Chng, Eddy Pang Yi Ler, Jinrun Zhou, Naoji Matsuhisa, Yu Jun Tan
nodes.materials_raw: Magneto-iono-elastomers (MINEs); magnetic ionic liquid (MIL) [Emim][FeCl₄]; urethane group-based polymer matrix; potential hydrogen bonds (PHBs) and metal-coordination bonds (MCBs)
nodes.methods_raw: MIL loading optimization, ionic conductivity measurement, mechanical testing (hyperelasticity, self-healing), magnetization characterization, sensor fabrication
nodes.key_findings: MINEs combine high magnetization (2.6 emu/g) with hyperelasticity (99% elastic recovery) and self-healing; urethane groups confine magnetic FeCl₄⁻ anions through PHBs and MCBs; high MIL loading (80 wt%) while maintaining structural integrity; ionic conductivity >10⁻³ S/cm; transparent; applications: wearable strain sensors, contactless magneto-responsive electronics, see-through touch panels, soft magnetic carriers; addresses traditional trade-off between magnetization and mechanical properties; inspired by migratory bird magnetoreception
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the maximum MIL loading before structural integrity is lost? How does MIL confinement affect ion transport kinetics? Biocompatibility of MINEs for implantable applications?

nodes.label: potential hydrogen bonds  and metal-coordination bonds
Polyelectrolyte elastomer-based ionotronic sensors with multi-mode sensing capabilities via multi-ma
nodes.full_title: Polyelectrolyte elastomer-based ionotronic sensors with multi-mode sensing capabilities via multi-material 3D printing
nodes.authors: Caicong Li, Jianxiang Cheng, Yunfeng He, Xiangnan He, Ziyu Xu, Qi Ge, Canhui Yang
nodes.institution: Southern University of Science and Technology (SUSTech)
nodes.materials_raw: Polyelectrolyte elastomer (photo-polymerizable ionic monomer); dielectric elastomers; DLP-based 3D printing materials
nodes.methods_raw: DLP-based multi-material 3D printing, photo-polymerizable ionic monomer synthesis, capacitive sensor design, drone remote control demonstration
nodes.key_findings: Leakage-free polyelectrolyte elastomer: stretchable, transparent, ionically conductive, thermally stable, leakage-resistant (unlike hydrogels/ionogels with solvent leakage, and LiTFSI-doped elastomers with ion leakage); multi-material 3D printing enables architected sensor designs with multi-mode sensing (tension, compression, shear, torsion); robust interfaces between polyelectrolyte and dielectric elastomers; extraordinary long-term stability; integrated sensors perceive different mechanical stimuli simultaneously without mutual signal interference; sensing kit (4 shear + 1 compressive sensor) wirelessly controls drone flight; polyelectrolyte elastomers fix ions to backbone → eliminates ion leakage problem
nodes.scientific_questions: Can polyelectrolyte elastomers achieve higher ionic conductivity? How does ionic monomer structure affect printability and mechanical properties? Scaling up DLP printing for larger ionotronic devices?

nodes.label: Southern University of Science and Technology (SUSTech)
Enhanced Mechanical Properties of Polyurethane Ionogels by Synergistic Interaction of Phase-Locking
nodes.full_title: Enhanced Mechanical Properties of Polyurethane Ionogels by Synergistic Interaction of Phase-Locking Ion and Hydrogen Bonds
nodes.authors: Wanjiang Li, Chuhao Ma, Shaoji Wu, Xulian Hu, Zhao Liu, Yurong Yan
nodes.institution: South China University of Technology (School of Materials Science and Engineerin
nodes.materials_raw: Polyurethane cationomer (PUC) ionogels; ionic liquid (IL) with ureido group; PUCD/IL (swelling-prepared) vs PUCB/IL (solution-blending-prepared)
nodes.methods_raw: Swelling strategy for IL incorporation, mechanical testing, capacitive sensor fabrication, human movement monitoring
nodes.key_findings: Phase-locked ions + H-bonding synergy between PUC chain and IL enhances mechanical properties; PUCD/IL: strength ~10.50 MPa, elongation ~1638.74% (enhanced ~162.50% and ~80.74% vs pure PUCD); traditional solution-blending PUCB/IL: only 0.71 MPa and 423.93% (6.76% and 25.87% of PUCD/IL); swelling strategy far superior to solution blending; wide sensing range (strain ~1000%, stress ~1000 kPa); fast response time (104/154 ms); stable repeatable response to human movement for intelligent posture monitoring; IL with ureido provides both ionic conduction and H-bonding sites; phase-locking mechanism prevents IL from disrupting chain packing
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the optimal IL loading for maximum synergy? How does ureido group density on IL affect phase-locking? Long-term stability of phase-locked structure under cyclic deformation?

nodes.label: South China University of Technology (School of Materials Science and Engineerin
Transparent, flexible, and paramagnetic ionogels based on PMMA and the iron-based ionic liquid [Bmim
nodes.full_title: Transparent, flexible, and paramagnetic ionogels based on PMMA and the iron-based ionic liquid [Bmim][FeCl₄]
nodes.authors: Zai-Lai Xie, Aleksandra Jeličić, Fei-Peng Wang, Pierre Rabu, Alwin Friedrich, Sabine Beuermann, Andreas Taubert
nodes.materials_raw: PMMA-based ionogels; iron-containing ionic liquid 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrachloroferrate(III) [Bmim][FeCl₄]; various other ILs for comparison
nodes.methods_raw: UV/Vis spectroscopy, ionic conductivity measurement, magnetic property characterization, thermal stability analysis (TGA)
nodes.key_findings: Pioneering work: first transparent magnetic ionogel (2010); Fe(III) coordination slightly changes upon IL incorporation into PMMA; thermal stability of PMMA significantly increases with IL (onset weight loss at 265°C completely suppressed); ionic conductivity increases with IL weight fraction (strong temperature dependence); magnetic properties similar to pure IL — not affected by PMMA incorporation; prototype for soft, flexible, transparent materials combining polymer mechanics with metal-containing IL functionality (magnetism); IL immobilization prevents leakage while maintaining magnetic response
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the magnetic response be enhanced with higher FeCl₄⁻ loading? How does PMMA chain length affect ionogel transparency and magnetic properties? Biocompatibility assessment for biomedical applications?

nodes.label: Energy Applications of Ionic Liquids: Recent Developments and Future Prospects
nodes.full_title: Energy Applications of Ionic Liquids: Recent Developments and Future Prospects
nodes.journal: Chemical Reviews 2023, 123, 12170-12253 (virtual special issue "Ionic Liquids for Diverse Applications")
nodes.authors: Teng Zhou, Chengmin Gui, Longgang Sun, Yongxin Hu, Hao Lyu, Zihao Wang, Zhen Song, Gangqiang Yu
nodes.materials_raw: Ionic liquids (ILs) for energy applications: batteries (rocking-chair, dual-ion, redox flow), supercapacitors, thermoelectric cells, CO₂ electroreduction, H₂ production, heat transfer fluids, phase-change materials, CO₂ capture/conversion, biomass treatment, biofuel extraction, high-energy propellants
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review of IL structures/properties, electrolyte design (liquid, quasi-solid, solid-state), electrochemical characterization, thermal analysis
nodes.key_findings: Extensive overview of IL energy applications; ILs as electrolytes: batteries (Li-ion, Na-ion, K-ion, Mg-ion, Al-ion, dual-ion, redox flow), supercapacitors (EDLC, pseudocapacitors), thermoelectric cells; ILs for thermal energy: heat transfer fluids, phase-change materials, solar power; ILs for CO₂: capture, conversion, electroreduction; ILs for biomass: pretreatment, biofuel extraction; ILs as high-energy propellants; tunable properties (nonvolatility, large electrochemical window, variety); challenges: cost, viscosity, moisture sensitivity; selection criteria for different applications
nodes.scientific_questions: Can ILs achieve cost parity with conventional electrolytes? How to design task-specific ILs for each energy application? Integration of multiple IL functions in single devices?

nodes.label: Theory and Simulations of Ionic Liquids in Nanoconfinement
nodes.full_title: Theory and Simulations of Ionic Liquids in Nanoconfinement
nodes.authors: Svyatoslav Kondrat, Guang Feng, Fernando Bresme, Michael Urbakh, Alexei A. Kornyshev
nodes.materials_raw: Room-temperature ionic liquids (RTILs) under nanoconfinement; nanoporous electrodes (supercapacitors, batteries); confined IL lubricants
nodes.methods_raw: Theoretical models (lattice models, off-lattice models, Schmickler's model, mean-field continuum), classical density functional theory (cDFT), molecular simulations (MD), equivalent circuit models, statistical mechanics
nodes.key_findings: Nanoconfined ILs exhibit fascinating phenomena: layering, overscreening, crowding, nanoscale capillary freezing, quantized and electrotunable friction, superionic state; superionic state in conducting confinement: enhanced ion mobility; image-charge attraction between ions and pore walls; capacitance oscillations with pore size; ionophobic pores show anomalous behavior; systematic theoretical framework from simple analytical models to full atomistic simulations; cDFT as reliable, computationally inexpensive tool for microscopic details; single-file cylindrical pores vs slit-shaped pores have different physics; practical implications for supercapacitor/battery electrode design and tribological lubrication
nodes.scientific_questions: Can theory predict optimal pore size for maximum energy storage? How do quantum effects affect IL behavior in sub-nanometer confinement? Unified theory connecting capacitance, friction, and ion transport in nanoconfined ILs?

nodes.label: Lyotropic Microphase Separation in Aqueous Solutions of Double Sulfobetaine Diblock Copolymers Induc
nodes.full_title: Lyotropic Microphase Separation in Aqueous Solutions of Double Sulfobetaine Diblock Copolymers Induced by Charge Separation Length Mismatch
nodes.authors: Yuji Higaki, Yasuhiro Eguchi, Takumi Masuda, Yuri Mitsunobu
nodes.materials_raw: Double hydrophilic diblock copolymer PSB2-b-PSB4 (two polysulfobetaine chains with different charge separation lengths); poly(2-(N-2-methacryloyloxyethyl-N,N-dimethyl)ammonioethanesulfonate) (PSB2); poly(4-(N-2-methacryloyloxyethyl-N,N-dimethyl)ammoniobutanesulfonate) (PSB4)
nodes.methods_raw: RAFT polymerization, fractional precipitation, phase behavior characterization as function of polymer concentration and block composition
nodes.key_findings: Lyotropic microphase separation driven by charge separation length (CSL) mismatch between chemically similar sulfobetaines; specific interactions between identical sulfobetaines trigger phase separation; structural similarity induces increased miscibility, lattice distortion, morphological change; low Flory-Huggins χ for PSB2/PSB4; poor hydrophilicity of PSB2 and weak PSB2-PSB4 interactions; unique mesoscale aqueous molecular assemblies from double-hydrophilic block copolymers with high chemical symmetry; promising as novel aqueous compartments for molecular reservoirs; inspired by biological liquid-liquid phase separation (membraneless organelles)
nodes.scientific_questions: Can CSL mismatch be used as a general design principle for aqueous phase separation? How does the CSL affect the morphology of phase-separated domains? Applications in drug delivery and molecular encapsulation?

nodes.label: phase behavior characterization as function of pol
Polymer Network Diffusion in Charged Gels
nodes.full_title: Polymer Network Diffusion in Charged Gels
nodes.authors: Shoei Sano, Takashi Yasuda, Takeshi Fujiyabu, Naoyuki Sakumichi, Takamasa Sakai
nodes.institution: The University of Tokyo (Department of Bioengineering
nodes.materials_raw: Charged polymer gels with immobilized ionic species; model gels with controlled network structure
nodes.methods_raw: Dynamic light scattering (DLS) for collective diffusion coefficient measurement; tunable model polymer network synthesis; decomposition of diffusion into mixing (D_mix) and elastic (D_el) contributions; Donnan equilibrium analysis
nodes.key_findings: Collective diffusion coefficient D of charged gels stems from sum of elastic, mixing, and ionic contributions without evident cross-correlations; significant ionic contribution conforms to Donnan equilibrium; explains equilibrium electrical potential gradients in biological systems; charged gels swell up to 1000× dry weight due to Donnan excess osmotic pressure; D = (K + 4G/3)/f extended to charged gels by including ionic contribution to K; quantitative validation of Tanaka's swelling kinetics model for neutral gels extended to charged case; tunable model networks enable systematic study of gel dynamics
nodes.scientific_questions: How does salt concentration affect the relative contributions of elastic, mixing, and ionic terms? Can this framework predict swelling kinetics of polyelectrolyte gels? Connection to biological osmotic regulation mechanisms?

Polymerized and Colloidal Ionic Liquids — Syntheses and Applications
nodes.full_title: Polymerized and Colloidal Ionic Liquids — Syntheses and Applications
nodes.authors: Qi Li, Feng Yan, John Texter
nodes.materials_raw: Polymerized ionic liquids (PILs); colloidal ionic liquids (CILs — 0D, 1D, 2D, 3D topologies); ionic liquid monomers (ILMs); nonimidazolium nuclei; step-growth PILs; cross-linked CILs
nodes.methods_raw: Review of synthesis methods: radical chain polymerization, controlled polymerization (ATRP, RAFT), step-growth chemistries, click chemistry; characterization of MW, thermal properties, rheology, ion transport, self-healing, stimuli-responsiveness
nodes.key_findings: Comprehensive review updating PIL field over past 5-6 years; CILs bridge IL monomers and macro-scale PIL materials; increasing use of nonimidazolium nuclei and step-growth chemistries; radical chain polymerization remains primary, with increasing controlled polymerization; key properties: glass transitions, critical solution temperatures, liquidity, ion transport, self-healing; applications: adhesion (ionogel properties), antimicrobial/antifouling, emulsion/dispersion, catalysis, devices (sensors, actuators), 3D printing; energy applications: supercapacitors, batteries, fuel cells, solar cells; stimuli-responsiveness distinguishes PILs from other polymer classes; solvation tuning is critical for function
nodes.scientific_questions: Can PILs achieve ionic conductivity rivaling liquid ILs? How to design PILs with simultaneous high ionic conductivity and mechanical strength? Next decade: PIL integration in wearable and implantable devices?

nodes.label: Review of synthesis methods: radical chain polymer
Advanced Interfacial Design for Electronic Skins with Customizable Functionalities and Wearability
nodes.full_title: Advanced Interfacial Design for Electronic Skins with Customizable Functionalities and Wearability
nodes.authors: Di Tan, Bingang Xu
nodes.materials_raw: Electronic skins (E-skins); functional components (sensing, power, therapy); interface components (adhesion, energy/mass transfer); skin adhesion materials
nodes.methods_raw: Review of skin adhesion mechanisms, interface design strategies, wearability and functionality optimization
nodes.key_findings: E-skin systems composed of functional and interface components; interface serves as foundation, conduit, and link between skin and functional components; wearability and functionality significantly impacted by interfacial adhesion and intermediate effect; only 1.4% of E-skin reviews focused on interfacial design despite its critical importance; skin adhesion mechanisms affect both wearability and function; advanced interface design enables diverse functions and different wear forms; >2500 reports on E-skins in past year alone; next-generation E-skin systems need integrated interface-function design
nodes.scientific_questions: How to achieve long-term adhesion without skin irritation? Can interfaces be designed for on-demand detachment and reattachment? Integration of sensing within the interface layer itself?

nodes.label: State-independent ionic conductivity
nodes.full_title: State-independent ionic conductivity
nodes.authors: J. Barclay, J. M. Williamson, H. Litt, S. J. Cowling, K. Shimizu, A. A. Freitas, S. Poppe, J. Sturala, Y. Sun, M. Kohout, A.-J. Avestro, J. N. Canongia Lopes, C. Groves, J. C. Jones, P. R. McGonigal
nodes.materials_raw: Organic salts (tris(trialkoxyphenyl)cyclopropenium salts, SIEn·X) with nine equivalent linear alkyl chains; discotic mesogens with cyclopropenium core
nodes.methods_raw: DFT modeling of electrostatic surface potential, MD simulations, X-ray crystallography, ionic conductivity measurement across phase transitions, Arrhenius analysis
nodes.key_findings: State-independent ionic conductivity — same Arrhenius behavior across three states (isotropic liquid, liquid crystal, crystalline solid); achieved by minimizing ion-pairing interactions between mobile ions and highly diffuse counterions; counterions assemble in stepwise manner preserving conformational flexibility across phase transitions; triskelion molecular design with rigid aromatic core and flexible alkyl periphery; extremely weak ion-pairing (ESP ~230 kJ/mol at cyclopropenium, ~254 kJ/mol at periphery — half of imidazolium/ammonium); counterions form halo around aromatic core in all three phases; liquid-like ionic conductivity in organic solids; opens opportunities for solid-state electrolytes without conductivity loss at phase transitions
nodes.scientific_questions: Can SIE design be applied to lithium salts for battery electrolytes? What determines the upper limit of ionic conductivity in SIEs? How does alkyl chain length affect the LC phase stability and conductivity?

nodes.label: SIEn·X) with nine equivalent linear alkyl chains
Perspectives for Polymer Electrolytes: A View from Fundamentals of Ionic Conductivity
nodes.full_title: Perspectives for Polymer Electrolytes: A View from Fundamentals of Ionic Conductivity
nodes.authors: V. Bocharova, A. P. Sokolov
nodes.materials_raw: Polymer electrolytes: PEO-based, single-ion conducting polymers, gel electrolytes, block copolymer electrolytes, composite electrolytes; lithium salts (LiTFSI, LiPF₆)
nodes.methods_raw: Review of ionic conductivity mechanisms, fundamental limitations, ion-ion correlations, polymer electrolyte design strategies
nodes.key_findings: PEO benchmark: high salt solubility but crystallization below ~60°C, low Li⁺ transport number (t_Li⁺ ~0.2-0.3); strong Li coordination by PEO oxygen atoms provides good solubility but restricts mobility; single-ion conducting polymers have significant advantages over dual-ion for batteries; ion-ion correlations not negligible in concentrated/semidilute systems; gel, block copolymer, and composite electrolytes already employed or have good potential for batteries; fundamental limitations: viscosity-controlled ion diffusion, decoupling of ion and segmental dynamics; dry polymer electrolytes still have relatively low room-temperature conductivity; design strategies for achieving required RT conductivity discussed
nodes.scientific_questions: Can polymer electrolytes achieve 10⁻³ S/cm at room temperature? How to decouple ion transport from segmental dynamics? Role of ion clustering in conductivity?

nodes.label: Developing Design Rules for Polyelectrolyte Complex Materials: Role of Polyelectrolyte Length, Charg
nodes.full_title: Developing Design Rules for Polyelectrolyte Complex Materials: Role of Polyelectrolyte Length, Charged Group, and Backbone
nodes.authors: Isaac A. Ramírez Marrero, Ria Ghosh, Louisa M. Coughlin, Wen-Wei Wong, Emily Ng, Elijah Kellner, Madyson Redder, Nadine Kaiser, Bernhard von Vacano, Rupert Konradi, E. Bryan Coughlin, Sarah L. Perry
nodes.materials_raw: Polyelectrolyte complexes (PECs); homopolymer PECs with varying chain length, side chain chemistry, backbone chemistry
nodes.methods_raw: Compositional studies of PEC phase behavior, dynamics and mechanics analysis, glass transition humidity measurement, water affinity characterization
nodes.key_findings: Three design parameters examined: chain length, charged group identity, backbone chemistry; ionizable group identity heavily affects ion solvation — PECs with lower water affinities have higher glass transition humidities and are more brittle; backbone chemistry affects chain mobility — acryloyl chemistries have lower glass transition humidities than methacryloyl; chain length effects depend on match/mismatch — matched PEC systems have higher glass transition humidities than mismatched; side chain and backbone chemistry effects are universal across different mediums, while length effects are medium specific; establishes fundamental structure-property relationships for rational PEC design; applications: underwater adhesives, flame retardants, antimicrobial materials, drug delivery, water remediation
nodes.scientific_questions: Can these design rules predict PEC properties for new polymer chemistries? How do these rules apply to block copolymer PECs vs homopolymer PECs? Role of counterion identity in PEC phase behavior?

nodes.label: homopolymer PECs with varying chain length
Compositional studies of PEC phase behavior
Molecular Mobility, Ion Mobility, and Mobile Ion Concentration in Poly(ethylene oxide)-Based Polyure
nodes.full_title: Molecular Mobility, Ion Mobility, and Mobile Ion Concentration in Poly(ethylene oxide)-Based Polyurethane Ionomers
nodes.authors: Daniel Fragiadakis, Shichen Dou, Ralph H. Colby, James Runt
nodes.institution: The Pennsylvania State University (Department of Materials Science and Engineeri
nodes.materials_raw: PEO-based polyurethane single-ion conductors (Li⁺ or Na⁺); anions covalently bound to polymer chains; amorphous single-phase ionomers
nodes.methods_raw: Dielectric relaxation spectroscopy, electrode polarization model for separating mobile ion concentration and ion mobility, ion association model (unpaired ions, separated ion pairs, contact ion pairs)
nodes.key_findings: Physical model of electrode polarization separately determines mobile ion concentration and ion mobility in single-ion conductors; very small fraction of free ions reconciled with previous studies using model including unpaired ions, separated ion pairs, and contact ion pairs; amorphous single-phase materials with no ion clustering (unlike typical ionomers); conduction due exclusively to cation motion (anions fixed to chains); ion mobility strongly coupled to segmental mobility and controlled by T_g; cations complexed with 4-6 ether oxygen atoms; fundamental understanding of ion transport in polymer electrolytes
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the fraction of free ions vary with polymer architecture? Can electrode polarization model be extended to concentrated electrolytes? Role of ion pair dynamics in conductivity?

The Pennsylvania State University (Department of Materials Science and Engineeri
Solvent-free, supersoft and superelastic bottlebrush melts and networks
nodes.full_title: Solvent-free, supersoft and superelastic bottlebrush melts and networks
nodes.authors: William F. M. Daniel, Joanna Burdyńska, Mohammad Vatankhah-Varnoosfaderani, Krzysztof Matyjaszewski, Jarosław Paturej, Michael Rubinstein, Andrey V. Dobrynin, Sergei S. Sheiko
nodes.institution: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
nodes.materials_raw: Bottlebrush macromolecules (comb-like architecture with dense side chains); bottlebrush melts and elastomers (covalently crosslinked networks)
nodes.methods_raw: Molecular dynamics simulations, mechanical testing, scaling analysis (Kavassalis-Noolandi conjecture), side chain length/diameter control
nodes.key_findings: Solvent-free, supersoft and superelastic polymer melts and networks from bottlebrush architecture; brush-like architecture expands chain diameter, diluting entanglements without markedly increasing stiffness; unprecedented combination: low modulus (~100 Pa), high strain at break (~1000%), extraordinary elasticity — on par with designer gels but without solvent; adjustable interplay between chain diameter and stiffness enables tailoring modulus and extensibility; liquid-like filaments that neither coalesce nor interpenetrate, remaining flexible with growing diameter (l_p ~ D, unlike solid filaments where l_p ~ D⁴); entanglement modulus G_e ~ M_e⁻¹ ~ D⁻³ ~ n_sc⁻³/²; eliminates complications of swollen gels (phase separation, evaporation, leakage, inflammatory responses); Kavassalis-Noolandi conjecture: M_e ~ D⁶/l_p³
nodes.scientific_questions: Can bottlebrush networks achieve self-healing? How does side chain polydispersity affect mechanical properties? Biocompatibility and biodegradability for biomedical applications?

nodes.label: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
High-Performance Polyethylene-Ionomer-Based Thermoplastic Elastomers Exhibiting Counteranion-Mediate
nodes.full_title: High-Performance Polyethylene-Ionomer-Based Thermoplastic Elastomers Exhibiting Counteranion-Mediated Mechanical Properties
nodes.authors: Jun Zhang, Xiaohui Mao, Zhe Ma, Li Pan, Bin Wang, Yuesheng Li
nodes.materials_raw: Ethylene/5-iodomethyl-2-norbornene/1-hexene terpolymers; imidazolium-based ionomers with CH₃SO₃⁻, CF₃SO₃⁻, or Tf₂N⁻ counteranions
nodes.methods_raw: Living polymerization, ionization/ion exchange, mechanical testing, elastic recovery measurement
nodes.key_findings: Dual-crosslinking strategy: physical crosslinks from PE crystallinity + dynamic ionic crosslinks; counteranion type controls interaction intensity with imidazolium ions; CH₃SO₃⁻ ionomers: highest tensile strength (13.7 MPa); Tf₂N⁻ ionomers: favorable strain-at-break (624%); tensile properties modulated over broad range by counteranion type and content; excellent elastic recovery up to 96%; resolves tradeoff between tensile strength and elastic recovery in random polyolefin TPEs; advantages over block copolymer TPEs: simpler synthesis, lower cost, higher abundance, potential biorenewability
nodes.scientific_questions: How does counteranion size affect ionic crosslink dynamics under cyclic loading? Can this approach be extended to other polyolefin architectures? Long-term stability of ionic crosslinks at elevated temperatures?

nodes.label: Effect of Monomer Polarity on Polymer Dynamics, Glass Transition, and Ionic Conductivity of Polyethe
nodes.full_title: Effect of Monomer Polarity on Polymer Dynamics, Glass Transition, and Ionic Conductivity of Polyether Electrolytes
nodes.authors: Soma Ahmadi, Danielle DeJonge, Niloofar Safaie, Shaylynn Crum-Dacon, Robert C. Ferrier Jr., Shiwang Cheng
nodes.materials_raw: Poly(butylene oxide) (PBO, ε_s=4)/LiTFSI; poly(propylene oxide) (PPO, ε_s=7)/LiTFSI; poly(epichlorohydrin) (PECH, ε_s=15)/LiTFSI
nodes.methods_raw: X-ray scattering, FT-IR, broadband dielectric spectroscopy (BDS), rheology, DSC, Walden rule analysis
nodes.key_findings: Molar conductivity follows Walden rule across different polyethers; intriguing glass transition and viscoelastic features: clear slowing of τ_α with large T_g elevation at high salt concentration; DSC shows strong broadening of T_g step or phase separation; T_g elevation depends linearly on salt concentration with slope NOT correlating with static dielectric constants; apparent "disentanglement" with salt concentration; two types of salt-induced structures: (i) intrachain polymer-ion complex (shortens effective chain length) and (ii) interchain polymer-ion complex (bridges chains); ion solvation structure plays crucial role in local chain packing → influences polymer dynamics, T_g, and ionic conductivity; challenges conventional wisdom that higher ε_s always improves ionic conductivity
nodes.scientific_questions: Can monomer polarity be tuned independently of T_g to optimize conductivity? How do intrachain vs interchain complexes affect mechanical properties? Design principles for polyether electrolytes with decoupled ion and segmental dynamics?

nodes.label: Unraveling the pathway towards superionic transport in polymer electrolytes
nodes.full_title: Unraveling the pathway towards superionic transport in polymer electrolytes
nodes.authors: Ain Uddin, Michelle Lehmann, Harmandeep Singh, Vera Bocharova, Logan T. Kearney, Joshua T. Damron, Yifan Liu, Valentino R. Cooper, Rajeev Kumar, Kenneth S. Schweizer, Alexei P. Sokolov, Tomonori Saito, Catalin Gainaru
nodes.materials_raw: LiTFSI-doped polymer matrix functionalized with zwitterionic (ZI) groups; poly(zwitterion) electrolytes
nodes.methods_raw: Calorimetric T_g measurement, ionic conductivity measurement, dielectric spectroscopy, DFT calculations, ion dynamics analysis
nodes.key_findings: Self-assembled percolation conductivity regime emerges above specific ZI concentration; ion hopping decouples from segmental dynamics by up to ten orders of magnitude; in highly concentrated ZI regime, glassy-state energy barriers for ion hopping similar to or smaller than superionic ceramics; poly(zwitterion) with all monomers carrying ZI groups shows significantly faster ion dynamics than monomeric ZI compound (despite slower structural relaxation) — highlights crucial role of local morphology; overcomes tradeoff between polarity (good for ion dissociation) and segmental dynamics (slowed by attractive interactions); opens new pathway for superionic polymer design; significantly expands limited portfolio of solid-state electrolytes
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the optimal ZI concentration for maximum conductivity? How does ZI group chemistry affect percolation threshold? Can superionic polymer electrolytes be processed into thin films for batteries?

nodes.label: LiTFSI-doped polymer matrix functionalized with zwitterionic
Directionally Aligned "Mechanical Balance" Design Enables Near-Frictionless Li⁺ Transport in Polymer
nodes.full_title: Directionally Aligned "Mechanical Balance" Design Enables Near-Frictionless Li⁺ Transport in Polymer Electrolytes
nodes.authors: Song Duan, Zongtao Lu, Yun Zheng, Xiang Liu, Lifen Zhang, Zhenghao Li, Zewen Liu, Yiyuan Luo, Bingsen Qin, Yao Liu, Maojun Pei, Jiaming Xu, Xiuzhou Lin, Wei Yan, Jiujun Zhang
nodes.materials_raw: Fluorinated graphene/zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (FG/ZIF-8) scaffold; in situ-polymerized 1,3-dioxolane (PDOL); anchored anion clusters; lithium metal anode; LiFePO₄ cathode; LiNi₀.₅Co₀.₂Mn₀.₃O₂ high-voltage cathode
nodes.methods_raw: Mechanical balance (MB) zone construction, scaffold design, in situ polymerization, ionic conductivity measurement, battery cycling tests, pouch cell fabrication
nodes.key_findings: Paradigm-shifting "mechanical balance" (MB) strategy: opposing force from anchored anion clusters counteracts strong Li⁺-polymer interaction, dramatically reducing Li⁺ decoupling energy barriers; MB zones directionally aligned via FG/ZIF-8 scaffold enabling near-frictionless, short-distance Li⁺ transport; exceptional ionic conductivity of 1.2 mS/cm at 25°C; impressive Li⁺ transference number of 0.71; Li|LiFePO₄ cells: unprecedented rate capability (97.7 mAh/g at 8C), ultralong cyclability (81% capacity retention after 3500 cycles at 4C); excellent compatibility with high-voltage NMC cathode; outstanding safety/cycling in practical pouch cells; pioneers universal MB paradigm for polymer electrolyte design
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the MB concept scale to larger-format batteries? Can MB zones be designed for Na⁺ or K⁺ transport? Long-term stability of anchored anion clusters under cycling?

nodes.label: Ion-size-dependent electroconvective transport in confined channels
nodes.full_title: Ion-size-dependent electroconvective transport in confined channels
nodes.authors: Qing Chen, Peng Wang, Yeye Wen, Wen Li, Zhengwu Jiang, Zhiguo Yan, Jiann-Wen Woody Ju, Hehua Zhu, Wei Liu
nodes.materials_raw: Ion-selective membranes in charged confined channels; electrolyte solutions with finite-size ions
nodes.methods_raw: Direct numerical simulations, modified Poisson-Nernst-Planck equations with ionic steric effects, electrohydrodynamic coupling analysis, Debye number and steric parameter variation
nodes.key_findings: Electroconvective transport across weakly to strongly confined channels; under strong confinement (overlapping EDLs): transport primarily governed by steric effects, average ion concentration inversely related to steric parameter; under weak confinement (non-overlapping EDLs): electroconvective flow dominates, decreased dimensionless Debye number increases vortex velocity; above critical Debye number: transition from electroconvective flow to steric effects captured by asymptotic scaling law; framework connecting behavior in weakly and strongly confined channels; implications for membrane science, drug delivery, ion transport
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the scaling law predict transport in real membrane systems? How do polyvalent ions affect the electroconvective-steric transition? Optimization of channel geometry for maximum ion selectivity?

nodes.label: Ion-selective membranes in charged confined channels
Debye number and steric parameter variation
Analytical solution for diffusiophoretic mobility of soft particles considering finite ion size effe
nodes.full_title: Analytical solution for diffusiophoretic mobility of soft particles considering finite ion size effect
nodes.authors: Rahul Kumar Singh, Partha Sarathi Majee, Hiroyuki Ohshima
nodes.materials_raw: Core-shell soft particles (hard inner core + polyelectrolyte layer PEL); electrolyte solutions with finite-size ions
nodes.methods_raw: Analytical solution derivation, flat-plate geometry approximation, ion steric exclusion effect, ion partitioning effect, hydrophobic core consideration
nodes.key_findings: Closed-form analytical solution for diffusiophoretic mobility of soft particles with finite ion size effect; applies to arbitrarily charged particles; ion steric effect significant for cooperative chemiphoretic and electrophoretic components at higher particle charge; core hydrophobicity and ion partitioning effect elevate ion steric effect by enhancing effective particle charge; for low-permeable PEL, influence of ion steric, partitioning, and hydrophobicity effects becomes negligible; different asymptotic limits derived; diffusiophoresis advantage: no external electric field needed (no cell damage), zero Joule heating; applications: targeted drug delivery, nanoparticle separation, DNA translocation
nodes.scientific_questions: How does PEL charge density affect the steric contribution? Can the model predict diffusiophoresis of biological cells? Extension to non-spherical soft particles?

nodes.label: Piezoionic elastomers by phase and interface engineering for high-performance energy-harvesting iono
nodes.full_title: Piezoionic elastomers by phase and interface engineering for high-performance energy-harvesting ionotronics
nodes.journal: (Supporting Information — main paper likely published separately)
nodes.authors: Weiyan Zhu, Baohu Wu, Zhouyue Lei, Peiyi Wu
nodes.institution: Donghua University (State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and
nodes.materials_raw: PVDF-HFP/EMIM TFSI ionogel; ionic plastic crystal (IPC) C₂mpyr TFSI; piezoionic elastomers with varying IPC content (30-67 wt%)
nodes.methods_raw: AFM phase imaging, in situ XRD, ¹⁹F NMR, Raman spectroscopy, isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC), piezoionic testing
nodes.key_findings: Phase and interface engineering for high-performance piezoionic elastomers; optimized IPC mass concentration of 55 wt%; PVDF-HFP/EMIM TFSI/IPC composite system; phase-separated morphology with IPC domains providing ionic plastic crystal phase; interface between PVDF-HFP matrix and IPC domains enables stress-driven ion transport; piezoionic effect from differential cation/anion mobility under mechanical stress
nodes.scientific_questions: How does IPC crystallinity affect piezoionic response? What is the mechanism of stress transfer at the PVDF-HFP/IPC interface? Can the piezoionic coefficient be further enhanced by interface engineering?

nodes.label: Donghua University (State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and
Phase-Pinned Bicontinuous Structures Reconciling with the Superior Fracture Toughness and Reusabilit
nodes.full_title: Phase-Pinned Bicontinuous Structures Reconciling with the Superior Fracture Toughness and Reusability of Ionic Elastomers
nodes.authors: Zibi Wang, Xiaoxu Liu, Fahu Yang, Xinxin Li, Haipeng Li, Dong Liu, Fei Chen
nodes.materials_raw: Supramolecular ionic elastomers with phase-pinned bicontinuous structures; liquid-crystal phase with fluorine-chain association; continuous percolating phase with flexible chain entanglement
nodes.methods_raw: Association dynamics regulation, chain entanglement control, fracture toughness testing, fatigue threshold measurement, recyclability/self-healability testing
nodes.key_findings: Phase-pinned bicontinuous structures reconcile high fracture toughness and reusability (normally contradictory in ionic elastomers); continuous liquid-crystal phase with sticky fluorine-chain association: high fracture energy barrier + effective energy dissipation; continuous percolating phase with flexible chain entanglement: good stretchability and elasticity; bicontinuous phases pinned by dynamic intra/interphase noncovalent interactions → load distribution and network reconfiguration; brilliant toughness (60.3 MJ/m³), fracture energy (63.9 kJ/m²), fatigue threshold (1.56 kJ/m²), ultrastretchability (~3040%); excellent recyclability and self-healability through reversible network reconstruction; inspired by human tendons/ligaments with high modulus contrast
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the liquid-crystal phase volume fraction affect the toughness-reusability balance? Can this bicontinuous design be applied to other ionic material systems? Role of fluorine-chain association dynamics in fatigue resistance?

nodes.label: continuous percolating phase with flexible chain entanglemen
Iontronics: Neuromorphic Sensing and Energy Harvesting
nodes.full_title: Iontronics: Neuromorphic Sensing and Energy Harvesting
nodes.authors: Yun Goo Ro, Sangyun Na, Jeeyoon Kim, Yoojin Chang, Seungjae Lee, Min Sub Kwak, Seokhee Jung, Hyunhyub Ko
nodes.materials_raw: Iontronic devices: ionic sensors, neuromorphic sensors, artificial synapses, energy harvesters, power generators
nodes.methods_raw: Review of ion transport mechanisms, electrochemical interactions, electron-ion coupling, neuromorphic sensing architectures, energy harvesting mechanisms
nodes.key_findings: Iontronics leverage ion transport, electrochemical interactions, and electron-ion coupling for intelligent sensing and sustainable energy harvesting; neuromorphic sensing: in-sensor computing enables real-time data processing with minimal power consumption; iontronic advantages over conventional electronics: intrinsic softness, biocompatibility, low power, ionic signal transduction; applications: intelligent robotics, wearable AI, biointerfacing technologies; energy harvesting from mechanical, thermal, and chemical sources via ionic mechanisms; challenges: long-term stability, scalability, integration with existing electronics
nodes.scientific_questions: Can iontronic neuromorphic devices achieve learning rates comparable to electronic neuromorphic chips? How to integrate iontronic and electronic systems for hybrid computing? Biodegradable iontronic devices for transient electronics?

nodes.label: Self-compliant ionic skin by leveraging hierarchical hydrogen bond association
nodes.full_title: Self-compliant ionic skin by leveraging hierarchical hydrogen bond association
nodes.authors: Huating Ye, Baohu Wu, Shengtong Sun, Peiyi Wu
nodes.materials_raw: Ionic skin based on P(BA-co-MAA) ionogel; hierarchical hydrogen bond (H-bond) association; topological entanglements as complementary crosslinks
nodes.methods_raw: Gel point state optimization, frequency-dependent rheology, electrophysiological signal recording, self-healing testing, adhesion testing
nodes.key_findings: Self-compliant ionic skin works at critical gel point state (G' = G'') over super-wide frequency range; hierarchical H-bond association: continuous release of polymer strands creates topological entanglements as complementary crosslinks with decreasing frequency; properties: rapid stress relaxation, softness (modulus ~14 kPa), ionic conductivity, self-healability (~97%), flaw-insensitivity (fractocohesive length ~1.2 cm), self-adhesion (~370 J/m² on porcine skin), water-resistance, high transparency (~92%), super-stretchable (2900%); excellent interfacial compliance with cyclically deforming substrates; high-fidelity electrophysiological signals with alleviated motion artifacts; addresses viscosity-elasticity imbalance in existing ionic skins; inspired by natural skin/tissue viscoelasticity (stress relaxation for self-regulated movement)
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the H-bond hierarchy affect the gel point frequency range? Can this approach work for ionic skins on moving joints? Long-term stability of hierarchical H-bonds under sweat and environmental exposure?

nodes.label: Sustainable electronic textiles towards scalable commercialization
nodes.full_title: Sustainable electronic textiles towards scalable commercialization
nodes.authors: HaoTian Harvey Shi, Yifei Pan, Lin Xu, Xueming Feng, Wenyu Wang, Prasad Potluri, Liangbing Hu, Tawfique Hasan, Yan Yan Shery Huang
nodes.materials_raw: Electronic textiles (e-textiles); conductive fibers; energy harvesting/storage textiles; sensing textiles; display textiles
nodes.methods_raw: Review of 4R paradigm (Repair, Recycle, Replacement, Reduction); material selection framework; biofabrication concepts; supply chain analysis; market viability assessment
nodes.key_findings: 4R circular e-textile paradigm: Repair, Recycle, Replacement, Reduction; e-textile market projected at US$1.4 billion by 2030; challenges: high cost, limited cross-compatibility, short product life, inadequate supply chains, environmental costs; 2019: 53.6 million tonnes e-waste generated; textile production >20% clean water pollution, 35% primary microplastics; systematic design framework unifying environmental friendliness, market viability, supply-chain resilience, user experience; biofabrication-inspired processing for sustainable growth; actionable principles for industrialization and commercialization
nodes.scientific_questions: Can biofabrication achieve cost parity with conventional textile manufacturing? How to design e-textiles for easy disassembly and recycling? Role of standardization in e-textile commercialization?

nodes.label: Diffusion and migration in polymer electrolytes
nodes.full_title: Diffusion and migration in polymer electrolytes
nodes.authors: Youngwoo Choo, David M. Halata, Irune Villaluenga, Ksenia Timachova, Nitash P. Balsara
nodes.materials_raw: PEO/LiTFSI polymer electrolyte system (as model system); neutral polymers with lithium salts
nodes.methods_raw: Review of six diffusion coefficients: D_seg (Rouse motion), D_self,+ and D_self,- (self-diffusion), D₀⁺, D₀⁻, and D₊₋ (Stefan-Maxwell); activity coefficient analysis; limiting current prediction; pulsed-field gradient NMR
nodes.key_findings: Complete characterization of interplay between polymer-salt interactions and macroscopic ion transport (completed only in PEO/LiTFSI); six diffusion coefficients describe different aspects of ion transport; surprising result: D₀⁻ is negative in certain concentration windows → both cations and anions predicted to migrate toward positive electrode at early times; limiting current is most important electrolyte characteristic; excellent agreement between theoretical and experimental limiting current in PEO/LiTFSI; Stefan-Maxwell diffusion coefficients predict ion velocities at very early times after electric field applied; activity coefficient discussion; what monomer sequence leads to highest limiting current remains unanswered question
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the Stefan-Maxwell framework predict limiting current for non-PEO polymer electrolytes? Why is D₀⁻ negative and what are the physical implications? Design rules for maximizing limiting current?

nodes.label: Review of six diffusion coefficients: D_seg
Decoupling of Ionic Transport from Segmental Relaxation in Polymer Electrolytes
nodes.full_title: Decoupling of Ionic Transport from Segmental Relaxation in Polymer Electrolytes
nodes.authors: Yangyang Wang, Alexander L. Agapov, Fei Fan, Kunlun Hong, Xiang Yu, Jimmy Mays, Alexei P. Sokolov
nodes.materials_raw: Comblike polymers with PEO side chains and different backbone rigidities: PPEOSt-b-PSt, PPEOMA, PPEOCL, PNBCEM, PPEOSt; LiClO₄ salt
nodes.methods_raw: DSC, ionic conductivity measurement, segmental relaxation time measurement, fragility analysis, decoupling exponent calculation
nodes.key_findings: Classical picture σ·τ_S = const breaks down in rigid polymers; ionic conductivity can be decoupled from segmental dynamics; strength of decoupling correlates with fragility (steepness of T-dependence of segmental dynamics) but NOT with T_g; loose local structure in rigid polymers (packing frustration) responsible for both increased fragility and decoupling; five comblike polymers with varying backbone rigidity demonstrate systematic trend; decoupling opens way to design polymers with high ambient-temperature ionic conductivity (similar to "superionic glasses"); challenges conventional view that flexible polymers always give better ionic conductivity
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the microscopic mechanism connecting fragility to ionic decoupling? Can decoupling be engineered without increasing fragility? Practical polymers that exploit decoupling for battery applications?

nodes.label: Comblike polymers with PEO side chains and different backbon
A healable, mechanically robust and ultrastretchable ionic conductive elastomer for durably wearable
nodes.full_title: A healable, mechanically robust and ultrastretchable ionic conductive elastomer for durably wearable sensor
nodes.authors: Guoxian Zhang, Chunmei Li, JiaoJun Tan, Mingqi Wang, Yafeng Ren, Feijie Ge, Qiuyu Zhang
nodes.materials_raw: Ionic liquid-multigrafted poly(urethane-urea) (PUU) elastomer networks; free ionic liquids (ILs) impregnated; imidazolium cations; centipede-like structures
nodes.methods_raw: Double-modification of PUU for imidazolium cation grafting, IL impregnation, mechanical testing, self-healing testing, conductivity measurement, cyclic fatigue testing
nodes.key_findings: Centipede-like structures from double-modification of PUU lock impregnated ILs through strong ionic interactions; PUU matrix contributes outstanding mechanical properties; H-bonds and ionic interactions endow self-healing, conductivity, non-volatility, transparency; conductivity 3.8×10⁻⁶ S/cm; tensile stress 4.64 MPa, elongation 1470%; excellent healing ability (90% efficiency at room temperature for 12 h); excellent antifatigue: highly reproducible response after 1000 uninterrupted extension-release cycles; integrates non-volatility, mechanical robustness, ionic conductivity, and ultra-stretchability; addresses IL leakage problem in conventional approaches
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the optimal IL loading for maximum conductivity without sacrificing mechanical properties? How does centipede-like architecture affect IL ion mobility? Long-term stability of ionic interactions under repeated deformation?

nodes.label: Ion Transport in Solvent-Free Polymers
nodes.full_title: Ion Transport in Solvent-Free Polymers
nodes.authors: M. A. Ratner, D. F. Shriver
nodes.materials_raw: Polymer-salt complexes: PEO-based, comb-like polymers, network polymers, polyelectrolytes, proton-conducting polymers, mixed ionic-electronic conductors; solvent-free polymer electrolytes
nodes.methods_raw: Review of solid electrolyte classes, metal salt complex formation, structure characterization, ion transport measurement and interpretation, frequency-dependent conductivity, dynamic percolation theory
nodes.key_findings: Pioneering comprehensive review of ion transport in solvent-free polymers; ions move only if polymer segments undergo large-amplitude excursions; below T_g, chain motion stops and ionic conductivity drops sharply (contrast with framework inorganic electrolytes); Wright and Armand's pioneering 1970s studies on PEO/alkali metal salt complexes; three classes of solid electrolytes: ceramic framework (high Debye T), soft ionic crystals, polymeric (fast-ion behavior only above T_g); dynamic percolation and relaxation models for frequency-dependent conductivity; polymer electrolytes fundamentally different from inorganic framework electrolytes
nodes.scientific_questions: (Historical — many answered by subsequent research, but fundamental questions about ion-polymer coupling remain relevant)

nodes.label: Mechanisms Controlling the Energy Barrier for Ion Hopping in Polymer Electrolytes
nodes.full_title: Mechanisms Controlling the Energy Barrier for Ion Hopping in Polymer Electrolytes
nodes.authors: Catalin Gainaru, Rajeev Kumar, Ivan Popov, Md Anisur Rahman, Michelle Lehmann, Eric Stacy, Vera Bocharova, Bobby G. Sumpter, Tomonori Saito, Kenneth S. Schweizer, Alexei P. Sokolov
nodes.materials_raw: Single-ion conducting polymers (SICPs); polymerized ionic liquids (PolyILs) with t₊ ~ 0.8-1; glassy polymer electrolytes
nodes.methods_raw: Arrhenius analysis below T_g, dielectric permittivity measurement, instantaneous shear modulus measurement, Anderson and Stuart model, temperature-dependent energy barrier analysis
nodes.key_findings: Traditional Arrhenius fit below T_g leads to unphysically small prefactors (τ₀ ≪ 10⁻¹³ s); imposing τ₀ = 10⁻¹³ s reveals strong temperature dependence of energy barrier even below T_g; significant temperature variations of dielectric permittivity and instantaneous shear modulus in glassy state; Anderson and Stuart model demonstrates these variations justify temperature-dependent energy barrier; most importantly: energy barrier for ion hopping is significantly (~30-40%) lower than traditional Arrhenius estimate; calls for revision of many earlier results based on apparent Arrhenius fits; new approach provides more accurate guidance for solid-state electrolyte design; SICPs show conductivity decoupled from segmental dynamics (10⁵-10⁸ times faster than segmental motions near T_g)
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the revised energy barrier values explain conductivity in zwitterionic polymer electrolytes? How does the Anderson-Stuart model need to be modified for concentrated electrolytes? Implications for designing polymers with lower ion hopping barriers?

nodes.label: Polymer Chain Conformation Triggers Electro-osmotic Flow in Uncharged Nanochannels
nodes.full_title: Polymer Chain Conformation Triggers Electro-osmotic Flow in Uncharged Nanochannels
nodes.authors: Xinxi Liu, Zexuan Li, Andriy Yaroshchuk, Wenyao Zhang, Yuqing Zhao, Qiuwang Wang, Alaa S. Abd-El-Aziz, Cunlu Zhao
nodes.materials_raw: Polyelectrolyte (PE) solutions confined in uncharged nanochannels
nodes.methods_raw: Dissipative particle dynamics (DPD) simulations, parametric studies on chain topology/stiffness, EDL analysis, confinement effects
nodes.key_findings: New EOF mechanism: PE chain conformation-induced spanwise charge separation in EDL drives flow in uncharged nanochannels; wall-dissipation-free transport (vanishing velocity gradients on channel wall); chain topology and stiffness critically regulate conformation-induced EOF; increasing rigidity suppresses charge separation and reduces flow velocity; linear chains most affected, star-like chains least; long rigid chains adopt U-shaped or claw-like conformations under electric fields + confinement; channel confinement modulates flow — PE distributions evolve from single to double to triple layers; nonmonotonic EOF velocity variation with decaying nanoconfinement (oscillations of charge separation); added salt gradually suppresses EOF via screening; contradicts classical EOF theory (predicts no EOF in uncharged channels)
nodes.scientific_questions: Can conformation-induced EOF be used for nanofluidic pumping? How does PE molecular weight affect the EOF magnitude? Experimental validation of DPD predictions?

nodes.label: Ion Transport in Polymerized Ionic Liquid Block Copolymers: Insights for High Polymerized Ionic Liqu
nodes.full_title: Ion Transport in Polymerized Ionic Liquid Block Copolymers: Insights for High Polymerized Ionic Liquid Content
nodes.authors: Yingying Chen, Christopher M. Evans
nodes.materials_raw: PNIPAM-b-PIL block copolymers; PIL content ranging 3.2-70.4 mol%; poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) neutral block
nodes.methods_raw: DSC, SAXS, ionic conductivity measurement, phase behavior analysis
nodes.key_findings: Previous studies focused on BCPs with low PIL content (<25 mol%); this study explores broad PIL content range up to 70.4 mol%; weak microphase separation confirmed by PIL composition-dependent T_g and absence of long-range periodic structures in SAXS; at fixed PNIPAM block length, ionic conductivity increases with PIL content; BCPs with >50 PIL mol% exhibit larger conductivity than homopolymer at high temperature (enhanced phase separation + nanoconfinement effects); longer PNIPAM blocks show 5-fold conductivity enhancement at similar PIL compositions; ionic conductivity trends consistent with previously reported systems despite monomer structure variations; higher PIL content improves conductivity via stronger phase separation and ionic domain ordering
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the optimal PIL content for maximum conductivity at room temperature? How does PIL block length affect domain connectivity? Can these BCPs be processed into thin films for battery applications?

nodes.label: Counterion Effects of Protic Ionic Liquids on the Conductivity and Stretchability of Free-Standing P
nodes.full_title: Counterion Effects of Protic Ionic Liquids on the Conductivity and Stretchability of Free-Standing PEDOT:PSS Films
nodes.authors: Thuy T. Le, Fatima Abbas, Shawn Bourdo, Fumiya Watanabe, Noureen Siraj
nodes.materials_raw: PEDOT:PSS films; protic ILs: [p-MIM][DCA], [p-IM][DCA]; aprotic IL: [EMIM][DCA]; six ions ranging hard to soft: cations [p-IM]⁺, [p-MIM]⁺, [EMIM]⁺; anions [BF₄]⁻, [TCB]⁻, [DCA]⁻
nodes.methods_raw: HSAB (hard-soft acid-base) theory-guided IL design, electrical conductivity measurement, mechanical testing (strain at break), IL synthesis and characterization
nodes.key_findings: HSAB theory applied to IL selection for PEDOT:PSS enhancement; [p-MIM][DCA] and [p-IM][DCA] synthesized for first time; tremendous conductivity increase: 2730 S/cm ([p-MIM][DCA]) and 3547 S/cm ([p-IM][DCA]); improved external strain up to 11% (vs ~7% for aprotic [EMIM][DCA] and <4% for pristine PEDOT:PSS); hard cation + soft anion combination exhibited superior conductivity and flexibility; protic ILs superior to aprotic ILs for simultaneous conductivity and stretchability enhancement; HSAB theory provides rational design principle for IL selection in conductive polymer composites
nodes.scientific_questions: Why do protic ILs outperform aprotic ILs in PEDOT:PSS? How does the proton transfer in protic ILs affect PEDOT:PSS morphology? Can HSAB-guided design achieve >5000 S/cm in PEDOT:PSS?

nodes.label: six ions ranging hard to soft: cations [p-IM]⁺
Emergent Nanostructure and Ion Transport in Polyzwitterion/Polyanion Blends
nodes.full_title: Emergent Nanostructure and Ion Transport in Polyzwitterion/Polyanion Blends
nodes.authors: Hongwei Li, Qinyu Zhu, Yuya Shinohara, Yangyang Wang, Panagiotis Christakopoulos, Autumn F. Kudlack, Zitan Huang, Peter V. Bonnesen, Changwoo Do, Md Anisur Rahman, Michelle L. Lehmann, Tomonori Saito, Ralph H. Colby, Rajeev Kumar, Jodie L. Lutkenhaus
nodes.materials_raw: Poly(1-(3-sulfonatopropyl)-2-vinylpyridinium) (P2VPPS, polyzwitterion); poly(lithium (trifluoromethane)sulfonimide methacrylate) (P(MTFSI)Li, polyanion); miscible polyzwitterion/polyanion blends at varying molar ratios
nodes.methods_raw: SAXS/SANS for nanostructure characterization, broadband dielectric spectroscopy, Gordon-Taylor equation for T_g analysis, coarse-grained MD simulations, effective activation energy analysis
nodes.key_findings: Polyanions as single-ion conductors but prohibitively low conductivity due to decreased segmental mobility and ion aggregation; introduction of polyzwitterion competitively interacts with polyanion charge groups → improved conductivity; attractive interactions confirmed by increased T_g (Gordon-Taylor equation); ordered local nanostructure (~24 Å) emerges at certain compositions (SAXS/SANS); concurrent with nanostructure emergence, ionic conductivity improves; highest conductivity at P2VPPS:P(MTFSI)Li = 0.2:1 (glassy state) and 0.3:1 (rubbery state) corresponding to lowest effective activation energy E*; coarse-grained MD correlates complexation with new SAXS/SANS peak; fresh perspective on local structural design for SPEs
nodes.scientific_questions: What drives the formation of the 24 Å nanostructure? Can the polyzwitterion approach be generalized to other polyanion systems? Optimal polyzwitterion chain length for maximum conductivity enhancement?

nodes.label: miscible polyzwitterion/polyanion blends at varying molar ra
Role of stress relaxation in stress-induced polymer crystallization
nodes.full_title: Role of stress relaxation in stress-induced polymer crystallization
nodes.authors: Wen Luo, Jiping Wang, Yaqian Guo, Wenbing Hu
nodes.institution: Nanjing University (School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
nodes.materials_raw: Polymers under stretching (general polymer crystallization); lattice polymer models
nodes.methods_raw: Dynamic Monte Carlo simulations with Maxwell model of stress relaxation; comparison of stress-induced vs strain-induced crystallization; varying strain rates and temperatures
nodes.key_findings: Stress relaxation reduces deformation of polymer coils against strain-induced crystallization; competitions among stress relaxation, strain rates, and temperatures determine crystallization behaviors; fast stretching → limited stress relaxation plays dominantly kinetic role (not thermodynamic) in retarding crystallization; enhanced stress relaxation during post-growth process affects final crystallinity and crystal morphology; transient jamming phenomenon reveals entropic barrier for polymer stress relaxation in bulk amorphous phase; kinetics rather than thermodynamics of repeating-unit interactions dominate enthalpic barrier; nonlinear viscoelasticity of stress relaxation at low temperature → early-stage physical crosslinking for rubbery state
nodes.scientific_questions: Can stress relaxation be used to control crystal morphology in industrial processing? How does stress relaxation affect the shish-kebab transition? Role of stress relaxation in polymer nanocomposite crystallization?
