comparison of stress-induced vs strain-induced cry
Nanjing University (School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Michell's-Instability-Mediated Fast Reconfiguration of Hydrogel-Based Ring Actuators
nodes.full_title: Michell's-Instability-Mediated Fast Reconfiguration of Hydrogel-Based Ring Actuators
nodes.authors: Qing Li Zhu, Zhijie Li, Hanlei Cheng, Weixuan Liu, Zhi Jian Wang, Olena Khoruzhenko, Josef Breu, Wei Hong, Qiang Zheng, Zi Liang Wu
nodes.materials_raw: Hydrogel-based ring actuators; anisotropic hydrogel strings; stimulus-responsive (heat, light) hydrogels
nodes.methods_raw: Michell's instability exploitation, mechanical simulation, stimulus-triggered shape morphing, task demonstration (screwing bottle cap, kicking ball, synergetic deformations)
nodes.key_findings: Stimulus-triggered Michell's instability enables fast shape morphing in hydrogel ring actuators; saddle-to-figure-of-eight transformation upon heating or light irradiation; mechanism: stimuli-induced variations in bending and torsional stiffnesses of anisotropic gel string reduce critical twist for Michell's instability; effects of light intensity, environmental temperature, ring size, and pre-torsion on reconfiguration investigated; fast action demonstrated through various tasks in aqueous conditions; design principle of soft actuators by harnessing Michell's instability; Michell's instability: classic mechanical instability where pre-torsion in elastic ring surpasses critical value → spontaneous transition from flat/warped to figure-of-eight; inspired by DNA coiling and plant organs
nodes.scientific_questions: Can Michell's instability be combined with other instabilities for multi-modal actuation? How to precisely control the critical twist for programmable actuation? Scaling laws for Michell's instability in hydrogels vs elastomers?

nodes.label: Thermodynamics of hydrogen bonding in polycomplexes of poly(4-vinylpyridine) with maleic acid-alt-et
nodes.full_title: Thermodynamics of hydrogen bonding in polycomplexes of poly(4-vinylpyridine) with maleic acid-alt-ethylene copolymer
nodes.authors: Viviana Villar, Lourdes Irusta, María José Fernández-Berridi, Juan José Iruin, Marian Iriarte, Ligia Gargallo, Deodato Radić
nodes.materials_raw: Poly(4-vinylpyridine) (P4VPy, Mw=50000); maleic acid-alt-ethylene copolymer (MA-alt-E, from hydrolysis of MAH-alt-E); interpolymer complexes via acid-base H-bonding
nodes.methods_raw: FT-IR (Nicolet Magna 560, temperature-dependent 298–363 K), flow calorimetry (direct ΔH_M measurement), Painter association model (IR-based stoichiometric parameters), viscometry, TGA
nodes.key_findings: Carboxylic groups of MA-alt-E interact with pyridine ring of P4VPy via H-bonding (new FT-IR bands at 1640 and 1503 cm⁻¹, broadening of C=C pyridinic band); Painter association model enables calculation of temperature-composition phase diagram and ΔH_M; high negative ΔH_M consistent with blend miscibility; ΔH_M is dominant contribution to ΔG_M (entropic contribution negligible for high-Mw systems); flow calorimetry provides direct experimental verification of association model predictions; quantitative thermodynamic framework for interpolymer complex formation
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the strength of acid-base H-bonding compare to other specific interactions (ionic, charge-transfer) in polycomplexes? Can the Painter model be extended to predict phase behavior in ternary systems?

nodes.label: Multiscale and Multistep Ordering of Flow-Induced Nucleation of Polymers
nodes.full_title: Multiscale and Multistep Ordering of Flow-Induced Nucleation of Polymers
nodes.authors: Kunpeng Cui, Zhe Ma, Nan Tian, Fengmei Su, Dong Liu, Liangbin Li
nodes.institution: University of Science and Technology of China (National Synchrotron Radiation La
nodes.materials_raw: Semicrystalline polymers (PE, iPP, PEO, PVDF, nylon, etc.); flow-induced crystallization (FIC) systems
nodes.methods_raw: Synchrotron radiation X-ray scattering (SAXS/WAXD), ultrafast X-ray detectors (ms time resolution), rheo-optical devices, in situ rheo-SAXS/WAXD, polarized optical microscopy, birefringence, small-angle neutron scattering, MD simulations
nodes.key_findings: FIC is a typical nonequilibrium phase transition; flow accelerates crystallization kinetics by orders of magnitude and induces oriented crystallites (shish-kebab); multiscale structures include: segmental conformation → packing of conformational ordering → whole-chain deformation → macroscopic crystallite aggregation; multistep process involves: conformation transition → isotropic-nematic transition → density fluctuation/phase separation → precursor formation → shish-kebab crystallites; classical interpretations from quiescent/mild-flow conditions challenged by new high-time-resolution experiments; flow-induced precursors may be crystalline or amorphous (debated); molecular parameters (M, M_e, polydispersity) and flow parameters (rate, duration, temperature) critically influence precursor formation; shish formation correlates with whole-chain deformation (coil-stretch transition); kebab growth from precursors follows heterogeneous nucleation
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the true nature of flow-induced precursors (crystalline vs amorphous)? How to reconcile thermodynamic and kinetic theories of FIC? Can FIC be precisely controlled at industrial processing scales?

nodes.label: University of Science and Technology of China (National Synchrotron Radiation La
Effect of Solvent Viscoelasticity on the Stress Induced Demixing
nodes.full_title: Effect of Solvent Viscoelasticity on the Stress Induced Demixing
nodes.authors: Mario Minale
nodes.institution: Seconda Università di Napoli (Dipartimento di Ingegneria Aerospaziale e Meccanic
nodes.materials_raw: Polymer blends in viscoelastic solvents; polymer solutions undergoing stress-induced demixing
nodes.methods_raw: Two-fluid theory (reformulated by Minale), thermodynamic analysis (free energy of mixing modified for chain stretch entropy), constitutive equations for polymer (reptation/Rouse) and solvent (Rouse dynamics), linear viscoelastic regime analysis
nodes.key_findings: Stress-induced demixing studied within two-fluid theory framework; free energy of mixing modified to account for mixing entropy variation due to chain stretch; both blend constituents are non-Newtonian; solvent viscoelasticity tends to stabilize the solution (suppresses demixing); reconciles thermodynamic theories (actual phase separation at steady state) with kinetic theories (flow-induced concentration fluctuation enhancement); new formulation enables appropriate boundary conditions at demixing interface; individual momentum balances for polymer and solvent provide framework for predicting stratified solution composition
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the two-fluid theory perform for highly entangled systems? Can this framework predict the kinetics (not just onset) of stress-induced demixing? What is the role of solvent molecular weight on demixing stabilization?

nodes.label: constitutive equations for polymer  and solvent
Seconda Università di Napoli (Dipartimento di Ingegneria Aerospaziale e Meccanic
Strain-induced multiscale structural evolutions of crystallized polymers: From fundamental studies t
nodes.full_title: Strain-induced multiscale structural evolutions of crystallized polymers: From fundamental studies to recent progresses
nodes.authors: Shanshan Xu, Jian Zhou, Pengju Pan
nodes.institution: Zhejiang University (State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering)
nodes.materials_raw: Semicrystalline polymers (HDPE, iPP, LLDPE, LDPE, PA, PB-1, P4M1P, PVDF, PBA, PBAT, PBN, PBO, PBS, PLA, PHA, etc.)
nodes.methods_raw: In situ synchrotron SAXS/WAXD, AFM, FTIR, CT (computed tomography), MD/IMC (interphase Monte Carlo) simulations, true stress-strain analysis, crystal phase transition tracking, cavitation detection
nodes.key_findings: Crystallized polymers undergo multiscale and multistage structural evolutions during deformation; typical stress-strain curve shows multiple critical strains (yield, hardening, etc.) corresponding to different deformation mechanisms; important structural changes include: crystal phase transition (e.g., α→γ in iPP, Form II→Form I in PB-1), crystallographic slip, lamellar fragmentation, cavitation, strain-induced melting and recrystallization, chain orientation; deformation mechanisms strongly depend on stretching temperature (T_s), strain rate, and molecular parameters; crystal morphology evolution from spherulitic to fibrillar; relationships between microstructures and end-use properties (mechanical, barrier, optical) discussed; multi-scale vision from plasticity initiation to failure
nodes.scientific_questions: Can crystal phase transitions be deliberately triggered to design materials with specific properties? How does cavitation interact with crystallographic slip in determining failure mechanisms? What controls the transition from homogeneous to heterogeneous deformation?

nodes.label: Zhejiang University (State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering)
Nonmonotonic Variation of the Preferential Orientation of Lamellar Crystals in Films of Cold-Crystal
nodes.full_title: Nonmonotonic Variation of the Preferential Orientation of Lamellar Crystals in Films of Cold-Crystallized Poly(L-lactide) of Increasing Thickness and Molecular Weight
nodes.authors: Decai Lin, Zebin Su, Yuxiang Chen, Jianquan Xu, Günter Reiter, Cuiyun Zhang, Xinping Wang
nodes.materials_raw: Poly(L-lactide) (PLLA); cold-crystallized thin films on silicon substrates (with/without gold coating, SAM modification)
nodes.methods_raw: Infrared reflection-absorption spectroscopy (IR-RAS), AFM, film thickness variation (10–1000+ nm), molecular weight variation, substrate modification (11-mercapto-1-undecanol, 1-dodecanethiol SAMs on Au)
nodes.key_findings: Nonmonotonic change in lamellar crystal orientation with increasing film thickness: edge-on → flat-on → edge-on (unprecedented); in thinnest films: size confinement + adsorption-induced chain alignment favor edge-on nucleation; enhanced polymer-substrate interactions promote edge-on in thinnest films at higher M_w; with increasing thickness: cross-nucleation of flat-on lamellae at fold surfaces of first-nucleated edge-on crystals, enhanced by transport difference; in thickest films: high chain mobility at free surface causes chains to align parallel to surface → increased edge-on density; with increasing M_w, the thickness window for flat-on orientation narrows; three-layer model (surface, bulk, substrate interface) explains competing nucleation mechanisms
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the nonmonotonic orientation be exploited for device applications (e.g., organic photovoltaics)? How does crystallization temperature interact with the thickness-dependent orientation transition? What is the critical role of chain entanglement density?

nodes.label: cold-crystallized thin films on silicon substrates (with/wit
Polymorphic Self-Poisoning in the Isothermal Crystallization of Thermoplastic Polyurethanes
nodes.full_title: Polymorphic Self-Poisoning in the Isothermal Crystallization of Thermoplastic Polyurethanes
nodes.authors: Zakarya Baouch, Irene Guardincerri, Katalee Jariyavidyanont, Leire Sangroniz, Yunxiang Shi, Elmar Pöselt, Alejandro J. Müller, René Androsch, Dario Cavallo
nodes.materials_raw: Thermoplastic polyurethanes (TPUs) with 29–80 wt% hard segment (HS); MDI/BDO hard segments; PTMO soft segments; two polymorphs: triclinic Form II (thermodynamically stable) and paracrystalline Form I (kinetically favored)
nodes.methods_raw: DSC, WAXD, polarized light optical microscopy (PLOM), fast scanning calorimetry (FSC), isothermal crystallization kinetics
nodes.key_findings: TPUs with low HS content (29, 33 wt%) crystallize exclusively in Form I; overall crystallization rate decreases monotonically with temperature at low supercooling; TPUs with ≥50 wt% HS display nonmonotonic temperature dependence of crystallization rate: rate first increases with supercooling → passes through relative minimum → rises again at larger supercooling; rate minimum attributed to polymorphic self-poisoning: Form I temporarily hinders crystallization of Form II; direct link established between polymorphic competition and crystallization kinetics; self-nucleation can alter polymorphic outcome (lower self-nucleation temperatures enable more Form II); HS content is critical determinant of accessible polymorphs
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the molecular mechanism of polymorphic self-poisoning (surface blocking? growth front poisoning?)? Can polymorphic composition be precisely tuned for targeted mechanical properties? How does self-poisoning affect long-term aging of TPU products?

nodes.label: two polymorphs: triclinic Form II  and paracrystalline Form
Crystal Structures of Sustainable Long-Spaced Aliphatic Polyesters
nodes.full_title: Crystal Structures of Sustainable Long-Spaced Aliphatic Polyesters
nodes.authors: Kohji Tashiro, Colby-Ann Donohoe, Marcel Eck, Stefan Mecking, Rufina G. Alamo
nodes.materials_raw: Long-spaced aliphatic polyesters (LSAPE): PE-X,18 series (X = 2, 3, 4, 6; −[−(CH₂)ₓ−OCO−(CH₂)₁₆−COO−]ₙ−) and PE-2,13; biodegradable/recyclable polyethylene mimics
nodes.methods_raw: 2D wide-angle X-ray fiber diffraction, IR spectral analysis (correlation splitting, progression bands), crystal structure determination, subcell analysis, comparison with n-alkane dispersion curves
nodes.key_findings: Chain conformation differs for odd vs even X: even-X takes vertically oriented almost straight chain form with local gauche kinks at CH₂−O bonds; odd-X packs into large zigzag arms of extended 16-methylene segments with gauche bonds at CH₂−O junction; methylene segments pack closely in sublattice similar to herringbone-type orthorhombic polyethylene; a_sub and b_sub values very close to orthorhombic PE crystal; ester groups form crystalline layers inside core crystalline regions; crystal thickness transition (one repeating unit increase) associated with minima in isothermal crystallization rate vs temperature; IR correlation splitting confirms subcell structure; LSAPE are biodegradable + closed-loop recyclable (solvolysis to monomer) alternatives to classical PE
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the odd/even effect on chain conformation affect mechanical properties? Can LSAPE achieve the same processing window as commodity PE? What controls the polymorphic transitions (thermally induced) in PE-2,18 and PE-3,18?

nodes.label: Role of Stress Relaxation in Biaxially Stretching-Induced Crystallization of Hard Segments in Thermo
nodes.full_title: Role of Stress Relaxation in Biaxially Stretching-Induced Crystallization of Hard Segments in Thermoplastic Elastomers
nodes.authors: Rongsheng Sun, Yaqian Guo, Wenbing Hu
nodes.institution: Nanjing University (School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
nodes.materials_raw: Thermoplastic elastomers (multiblock copolymers): olefin block copolymers, polyurethanes, polyester-polyethers, polyamide-polyethers; concentrate and dilute cases of bulk diblock copolymers
nodes.methods_raw: Dynamic Monte Carlo simulations comparing crystallization with and without stress relaxation; biaxial stretching simulation; concentrate (parallel stack of hard segments) vs dilute (alternating stack) microdomain cases
nodes.key_findings: Stress relaxation accelerates intramolecular crystal nucleation but significantly suppresses crystallinity → more and smaller crystallites in both concentrate and dilute hard-segment microdomains; dilute microdomains with suppressed crystallinity contribute less to physical cross-links → lower Young's modulus and toughness; smaller crystallites weaken yielding strength of physical cross-links; stress relaxation plays a negative role in production of strong yet tough TPE films/foams; high-speed biaxial stretching/foaming reduces stress relaxation extent → better industrial solidification for high-quality production; switch from intramolecular chain-folding nucleation to intermolecular fringed-micelle nucleation upon uniaxial strain (earlier finding by same group)
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the optimal stretching rate that balances stress relaxation minimization with processing feasibility? How does stress relaxation interact with soft segment crystallization? Can molecular design (e.g., block length, rigidity) mitigate the negative effects of stress relaxation?

nodes.label: Dynamic Monte Carlo simulations comparing crystall
Melt Memory Effect on Biaxially Oriented Random Copolyamides: Direct Evidence for Stretch-Induced Cr
nodes.full_title: Melt Memory Effect on Biaxially Oriented Random Copolyamides: Direct Evidence for Stretch-Induced Crystal Retention above the Equilibrium Melting Point
nodes.authors: Yunqi Xu, Kejian Yang, Wanding Chen, Minghui Wu, Yueshuai Xu, Zhongke Yuan, Zhikun Zheng, Cheng Wang, Xudong Chen
nodes.materials_raw: Biaxially oriented PA56/512 random copolyamides (BOPA56/512) with varying copolymerized compositions; biobased long-chain polyamides
nodes.methods_raw: DSC, XRD, FTIR, in situ POM, CP/MAS NMR, crystallization Gibbs free energy (ΔG_c) calculations
nodes.key_findings: BOPA56/512 with relatively high PA512 mol% develops distinct long-range shish-kebab structures that persist even above the equilibrium melting point (T_m^0); thermal history from biaxial stretching elevates crystallization temperature and persists upon heating to molten state; increasing heat treatment temperature induces γ→α form transition in high PA512 samples; with decreasing PA512 mol%, only irregular shorter secondary crystals form and temperature changes only d-spacing of γ form (no phase transition); CP/MAS NMR and ΔG_c calculations show shish-kebab formation and γ→α transition result from better chain mobility and lower ΔG_c of PA512; shish-kebab structures partially preserved even 90°C above T_m^0; upon cooling from molten state, preserved structures serve as nucleation sites for recrystallization; biaxially stretch-induced oriented structures do not completely dissolve into relaxed random coils but partially relax into crystalline precursors maintaining structural order
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the molecular nature of the "crystalline precursors" that survive above T_m^0? How does hydrogen bonding network reconstruction interact with melt memory? Can melt memory be deliberately enhanced for processing advantage?

nodes.label: Polymorphic Self-Poisoning in Poly(Lactic Acid): A New Phenomenon in Polymer Crystallization
nodes.full_title: Polymorphic Self-Poisoning in Poly(Lactic Acid): A New Phenomenon in Polymer Crystallization
nodes.authors: Shu-Gui Yang, Xiang-bing Zeng, Feng Liu, Goran Ungar
nodes.institution: Xi'an Jiaotong University (State Key Laboratory for Mechanical Behavior of Mater
nodes.materials_raw: Poly(L-lactic acid) (PLLA) of various molecular weights (9k, 36k, 110k); with/without plasticizer; two crystal forms: α (stable, requires strict up-down order of polar chains) and α' (kinetically favored, no up-down order requirement)
nodes.methods_raw: Spherulite growth rate measurement vs temperature, lamellar thickness determination, growth rate equation model development, Monte Carlo simulation comparison
nodes.key_findings: New type of self-poisoning: less stable α' form disturbs growth of stable α form (different from previously known self-poisoning where thinner crystals poison thicker ones); below growth rate minimum temperature, lamellar thickness increases rather than drops (opposite to all other reported polymer crystallization cases); growth rate equation model gives good match with experiments but reveals unexpectedly low fold surface free energy of α' form; delayed crystallization due to α self-poisoning in fast-cooling processing gives low-modulus α' form AND increased glassy amorphous fraction → embrittlement of PLA through physical aging; self-poisoning is ubiquitous in polymer crystallization but previously manifested only as growth rate minima in monodisperse or periodically segmented chains; implications for amyloid formation (SP can block amyloid growth)
nodes.scientific_questions: Can self-poisoning be exploited to control polymorph selection in industrial PLA processing? What determines the α/α' growth front competition at the molecular level? How general is polymorphic self-poisoning across other polymer systems?

Tacticity-independent crystallization of polymers
nodes.full_title: Tacticity-independent crystallization of polymers
nodes.authors: Leire Sangroniz, Ainara Sangroniz, Changxia Shi, Miriam Scoti, Marta Ximenis, Claudio De Rosa, Eugene Y.-X. Chen, Haritz Sardon, Alejandro J. Müller
nodes.materials_raw: Atactic/stereoirregular polymers that can crystallize; precision polymers with regularly spaced substituents; polymers with specific ordering features enabling crystallization despite tacticity irregularity
nodes.methods_raw: Review of crystal structure analysis, X-ray diffraction, thermal analysis, molecular modeling
nodes.key_findings: 75% of commercial polymers have crystallinity; usually requires stereo- and regioregularity; unusual cases exist where stereoirregular atactic polymers can crystallize when structural features provide specific ordering for irregular chains to pack into crystals; review highlights relationship between structures required in atactic polymers to induce crystallization; provides tools to design polymer systems with crystallinity without requiring exquisite stereocontrol; examples include precision polymers (halogen/ester groups at regular intervals on PE chain), polymers with bulky groups that enforce specific packing, and polymers with strong directional interactions (H-bonding) that overcome stereochemical irregularity
nodes.scientific_questions: What are the minimum structural requirements for atactic polymer crystallization? Can machine learning predict which atactic polymers will crystallize? How do the properties of atactic crystalline polymers compare with their stereoregular counterparts?

nodes.label: precision polymers with regularly spaced substituents
polymers with specific ordering features enabling crystalliz
Polythiourethanes: Synthesis, Applications, and Opportunities
nodes.full_title: Polythiourethanes: Synthesis, Applications, and Opportunities
nodes.authors: Xabier Lopez de Pariza, Paula Fanlo Pujol, Lucas Polo Fonseca, Alaitz Ruiz de Luzuriaga, Haritz Sardon
nodes.materials_raw: Polythiourethanes (PSUs); thiourethane bonds; sulfur-containing polyurethane analogs
nodes.methods_raw: Review covering synthesis (isocyanate and non-isocyanate routes), catalysis, DMTA, DSC, FTIR, dynamic bond exchange characterization, chemical recycling/reprocessing
nodes.key_findings: Polythiourethanes are relatively unknown polymer family with similar properties to conventional polyurethanes but enhanced dynamic behavior; enhanced dynamic character of thiourethane bond enables improved recyclability and reprocessing at milder conditions without diminishing physical/mechanical properties; review covers: main synthetic routes (focus on catalysis and non-isocyanate routes), thermomechanical and optical properties (similarities/differences vs polyurethanes), opportunities from enhanced dynamic character (chemical recycling, reprocessing of thermosets), advanced applications and manufacturing processes; thiourethane bond has faster exchange kinetics than urethane bond → better vitrimer behavior; non-isocyanate routes avoid toxic isocyanate precursors; sulfur atom in thiourethane provides higher refractive index and UV absorption
nodes.scientific_questions: How do thiourethane exchange kinetics compare quantitatively with other dynamic bonds (transesterification, disulfide, boronic ester)? Can non-isocyanate polythiourethanes match the mechanical performance of isocyanate-based ones? What are the long-term stability and degradation pathways of thiourethane bonds?

nodes.label: Hydrogen Bonding in Carboxyl/Amido-Functionalized PEDOT:PSS Copolymers: Breaking the Conductivity-In
nodes.full_title: Hydrogen Bonding in Carboxyl/Amido-Functionalized PEDOT:PSS Copolymers: Breaking the Conductivity-Interfacial Property Trade-Off for Low-Impedance, High-Fidelity Epidermal Bioelectrical Monitoring
nodes.authors: Qiao Fan, Kai Zhang, Yufei Liu, Lanlan Wei, Yucheng Yin, Tingting Luo, Min He, Chong Chen, Shigui Peng, Jie Yu
nodes.materials_raw: PEDOT:PSS doped with PSS-co-poly(acrylic acid)/poly(acrylamide) binary copolymers (PSA/PSM); carboxyl (−COOH) and amide (−CONH₂) functional groups
nodes.methods_raw: Multiscale characterization, molecular dynamics simulations, in vivo ECG/EMG testing, contact impedance measurement, cyclic tensile testing, FTIR, conductivity measurement
nodes.key_findings: −COOH groups enhance π-π stacking and charge doping in PEDOT → highest conductivity (PSA 3:1 surpasses pure PEDOT:PSS) but severe swelling and poor adhesion; −CONH₂ groups form H-bond network with static structural features (uniform bond length/angle, homogeneous network) → optimizes interfacial performance with ≤10% conductivity loss; PSM 3:1 electrode: strong adhesion, low contact impedance (62.8 kΩ at 10 Hz), swelling resistance, high electromechanical stability; MD simulations confirm static structural advantages of PSM H-bond network (stable bonding sites, moderate bond length/angle fluctuations) are critical for wet performance; ECG RMS noise 10.3 μV; EMG peak-to-peak 0.32 mV under 25 kg grip force, outperforming commercial Ag/AgCl; "static structure of H-bond network" introduced as key design parameter for synergistic optimization
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the H-bond network evolve under repeated mechanical cycling? Can the PSM approach be generalized to other conducting polymer systems? What is the long-term biocompatibility of the PSA/PSM dopants?

nodes.label: Cellulose sulfate lithium as a conductive binder for LiFePO4 cathode with long cycle life
nodes.full_title: Cellulose sulfate lithium as a conductive binder for LiFePO4 cathode with long cycle life
nodes.authors: Xu Su, Han Fang, Hao Yang, Fangfang Zou, Gang Li, Lian Wang, Hongxin Liao, Wei Guan, Xuebu Hu
nodes.materials_raw: Cellulose sulfate lithium (CSL) - first-time prepared; LiFePO4 cathode; lithium-ion battery
nodes.methods_raw: Binder synthesis, electrode fabrication, electrochemical testing (cycling stability, capacity), conductivity measurement
nodes.key_findings: CSL successfully prepared for the first time; used as both conductive and water-soluble binder for LiFePO4 cathode; replaces conventional PVDF binder (which requires toxic NMP solvent); CSL provides ionic conductivity through lithium sulfonate groups on cellulose backbone; long cycle life demonstrated; water-soluble → environmentally friendly processing; cellulose-based → sustainable/renewable
nodes.scientific_questions: How does CSL ionic conductivity compare with liquid electrolyte contribution? Can CSL approach be extended to other cathode materials (NMC, NCA)? What is the long-term stability of the cellulose sulfate backbone under electrochemical cycling?

nodes.label: Supramolecular Adhesives with Extended Tolerance to Extreme Conditions via Water-Modulated Noncovale
nodes.full_title: Supramolecular Adhesives with Extended Tolerance to Extreme Conditions via Water-Modulated Noncovalent Interactions
nodes.authors: Wang Guan, Wenhe Jiang, Xinling Deng, Wansheng Tao, Jiaqi Tang, Yuangang Li, Jianhong Peng, Cheng-Lung Chen, Kaiqiang Liu, Yu Fang
nodes.institution: Shaanxi Normal University (Key Laboratory of Applied Surface and Colloid Chemist
nodes.materials_raw: Two-component supramolecular adhesives from small organic molecules; imidazole-based ionic liquids + phenols; water as modulator/cross-linker
nodes.methods_raw: Adhesion strength testing (lap shear), temperature-dependent adhesion (liquid nitrogen to elevated temperatures), various substrate testing, supramolecular interaction balance (H-bonding, electrostatic, π-π stacking, cation-π)
nodes.key_findings: Maximum adhesion strength ~10.0 MPa on various surfaces in air, organic solvents, or liquid nitrogen; balanced supramolecular interactions (H-bonding, electrostatic, π-π, cation-π) enable extreme-condition tolerance; water participation prolonged and increased tolerance in extreme environments; water acts as physical cross-linker modulating noncovalent interactions; imidazole IL + phenol combination applicable for various interfacial adhesion; design principle: balance multiple noncovalent interactions rather than maximize single interaction type
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the molecular mechanism of water modulation of supramolecular interactions at the interface? How does the adhesive perform under continuous humidity cycling? Can the IL/phenol ratio be optimized for specific substrates?

Shaanxi Normal University (Key Laboratory of Applied Surface and Colloid Chemist
Dynamic cross-linked topological network reconciles the longstanding contradictory properties of pol
nodes.full_title: Dynamic cross-linked topological network reconciles the longstanding contradictory properties of polymers
nodes.authors: Zekai Wu, Chengzhen Chu, Yuhui Jin, Lei Yang, Bo Qian, Yuepeng Wang, Yihan Wang, Jiani Wu, Yujie Jia, Wenwen Zhang, Zhengwei You
nodes.institution: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Fiber Materials
nodes.materials_raw: Diaminoglyoxime (DAG) tetrafunctional cross-linker; polyurethane elastomers (PTMEG + IPDI + DAG); triple dynamic bonds: oxime-carbamate + amidine-urea + hydrogen bonds
nodes.methods_raw: One-pot polycondensation, rheology (G'/G'' crossover temperature), DMA, tensile testing, stress relaxation, reprocessing tests
nodes.key_findings: Chemically coupled four-arm dynamic polymer cross-link site designed; concurrently increasing cross-link sites AND dynamic bond contents → high cross-link density yet low processing temperature (subverts conventional structure-property relationship); DAG cross-linker has two oxime + two amino groups reacting with isocyanates → dynamic oxime-carbamate + dynamic amidine-urea bonds + H-bonds; G'_max/G'_min ratio at ΔT=120°C reaches 153.3 (higher than all reported cross-linked polyurethanes); at room temperature: higher cross-link density → better mechanical performance; at elevated temperature: amidine-urea catalyzes oxime-carbamate dissociation → rapid relaxation → modulus decrease rate outperforms all reported dynamic PUs; James & Guth's classic principle (cross-linking restricts plasticity) overcome by electronic effect + topology network design
nodes.scientific_questions: How general is the "chemically coupled cross-link site" concept beyond polyurethanes? What is the fatigue life under repeated thermal cycling? Can the catalytic effect between dynamic bonds be tuned independently?

nodes.label: triple dynamic bonds: oxime-carbamate + amidine-urea + hydro
State Key Laboratory of Advanced Fiber Materials
Multiresponsive and Mechanically Robust Self-Healing Waterborne Polyurethane with Photoluminescent a
nodes.full_title: Multiresponsive and Mechanically Robust Self-Healing Waterborne Polyurethane with Photoluminescent and Acid-Base Adaptive Properties
nodes.authors: Tian-Yu Su, Jing-Ying Yang, Hui-Tao Yu, Rui-Guang Xing, Xin Ge, Li-Na Jia, Yu-Fei Wang, Jin-Xiao Bao, Gao-Fei Pan, Wei Feng
nodes.materials_raw: Waterborne polyurethane (WPU) with [2,2'-bipyridine]-4,4'-dicarbohydrazide (BD); intramolecular rotatable conjugated structures; H-bonding sites
nodes.methods_raw: Tensile testing, UV excitation (260–400 nm) photoluminescence, self-healing tests (40°C, 24 h), acid-base response testing, thermal/mechanical/poor solvent experiments
nodes.key_findings: WPU-BD elastomer: 72.6 MPa tensile strength + 1249.7% stretchability; stable photoluminescence under 260–400 nm UV excitation; fluorescence mechanism: synergistic AIE + proton-coupled energy transfer (PCEnT); reversible responses to mechanical stretching and acidic/alkaline environments (fluorescence intensity changes but recovers upon stimulus removal); 100% self-healing efficiency within 24 h at 40°C via dynamic H-bonds and ionic bonds; bipyridine unit provides both luminescence and metal coordination sites
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the PCEnT mechanism vary with different proton donor-acceptor pairs? Can the self-healing temperature be reduced to room temperature? What is the biocompatibility of the bipyridine-hydrazide unit?

nodes.label: Unprecedented and Readily Tunable Photoluminescence from Aliphatic Quaternary Ammonium Salts
nodes.full_title: Unprecedented and Readily Tunable Photoluminescence from Aliphatic Quaternary Ammonium Salts
nodes.journal: Angewandte Chemie International Edition 2022, 61, e202117368 (Hot Paper)
nodes.authors: Saixing Tang, Zihao Zhao, Jinquan Chen, Tianjia Yang, Yunzhong Wang, Xiaohong Chen, Meng Lv, Wang Zhang Yuan
nodes.materials_raw: Aliphatic quaternary ammonium salts (QASs); various alkyl chain lengths and counterions
nodes.methods_raw: Photoluminescence spectroscopy, excitation wavelength variation, alkyl chain length variation, counterion effects, mechanical stimuli response
nodes.key_findings: QASs (widely used as phase-transfer catalysts, ionic liquids, disinfectants, surfactants) previously known as luminescence quenchers and considered non-emissive; unprecedented intrinsic fluorescence/phosphorescence dual emissions discovered; tunable by excitation wavelength, alkyl chain length, counterion, and mechanical stimuli; bright photoluminescence with distinct afterglow and tunable multicolor emissions; clustering-triggered emission (CTE) mechanism: clustering of electron-rich moieties promotes spin-orbit coupling and narrows singlet-triplet energy gaps → facilitates intersystem crossing → phosphorescence; challenges previous assumption that oxidation is required for aliphatic amine emission; applications in advanced multimode anticounterfeiting
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the exact role of the quaternary nitrogen in the emission mechanism? How does counterion identity affect the clustering geometry? Can QAS luminescence be used for real-time monitoring of quaternization reactions?

nodes.label: various alkyl chain lengths and counterions
A Dynamic Supramolecular H-bonding Network with Orthogonally Tunable Clusteroluminescence
nodes.full_title: A Dynamic Supramolecular H-bonding Network with Orthogonally Tunable Clusteroluminescence
nodes.journal: Angewandte Chemie International Edition 2023, 62, e202214422 (Hot Paper)
nodes.authors: Chen-Yu Shi, Dan-Dan He, Bang-Sen Wang, Qi Zhang, He Tian, Da-Hui Qu
nodes.institution: East China University of Science and Technology (Tian and Qu groups)
nodes.materials_raw: Supramolecular poly(disulfides) network from thioctic acid (TA) derivatives; oligopeptide-modified 1,2-dithiolane building blocks; electron-rich groups (hydroxyl, ester, carbonyl, amide)
nodes.methods_raw: Clusteroluminescence spectroscopy, 77K phosphorescence measurement, disulfide exchange kinetics, metal coordination kinetics, reprocessability testing, chemical closed-loop recycling
nodes.key_findings: Supramolecular poly(disulfides) network with tunable clusteroluminescence; reticular H-bonds synergize rigidity and mobility of dynamic networks → mechanical adaptivity + robustness + efficient clusteroluminescence and phosphorescence at 77K; orthogonally tunable luminescence via two mechanisms: (1) slow backbone disulfide exchange and (2) fast side-chain metal coordination; reprocessability and chemical closed-loop recycling of intrinsic dynamic networks demonstrated; synergistic strategy of dynamic chemistry offers novel pathway for smart emissive materials; through-space conjugation (TSC) mechanism for clusteroluminescence
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the orthogonal tuning of luminescence be extended to other dynamic chemistries? What is the quantum yield limit for clusteroluminescent polymers? How does the H-bond network topology affect emission wavelength?

nodes.label: Supramolecular poly network from thioctic acid  derivatives
East China University of Science and Technology (Tian and Qu groups)
Enabling nonconjugated polyesters emit full-spectrum fluorescence from blue to near-infrared
nodes.full_title: Enabling nonconjugated polyesters emit full-spectrum fluorescence from blue to near-infrared
nodes.authors: Bo Chu, Xiong Liu, Zuping Xiong, Ziteng Zhang, Bin Liu, Chengjian Zhang, Jing Zhi Sun, Qing Yang, Haoke Zhang, Ben Zhong Tang, Xing-Hong Zhang
nodes.materials_raw: Nonconjugated polyesters from amine-initiated copolymerization of epoxides and cyclic anhydrides; amine initiators (triethylamine, TEA) as chain-end groups; amine-ester complexes
nodes.methods_raw: PL spectroscopy, quantum yield measurement, concentration-dependent PL, excitation-dependent emission analysis, lifetime measurement, UV-vis, molecular simulation
nodes.key_findings: Full-spectrum clusteroluminescence from 470 to 780 nm (blue to NIR) achieved in nonconjugated polyesters; amines act as initiators attached to polymer chain ends; formation of various amine-ester complexes induces red-to-NIR light via intra/inter-chain charge transfer; emission colors easily tuned by: amine content and type, polyester microstructures, and concentration; P1-5.0TEA shows highest solid-state QY of 15.8%; excitation-dependent emission weakens as amine content increases (stronger TSIs dominate); amine-ester complexed clusteroluminescence is new mechanism beyond conventional CTE; low-cost, scalable platform for high-efficiency multicolor luminescent materials
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the molecular structure of the amine-ester complex that enables NIR emission? Can the quantum yield be further improved by molecular design? How does the copolymer sequence distribution affect clustering geometry?

nodes.label: Clusterization-triggered emission: Uncommon luminescence from common materials
nodes.full_title: Clusterization-triggered emission: Uncommon luminescence from common materials
nodes.authors: Haoke Zhang, Zheng Zhao, Paul R. McGonigal, Ruquan Ye, Shunjie Liu, Jacky W.Y. Lam, Ryan T.K. Kwok, Wang Zhang Yuan, Jianping Xie, Andrey L. Rogach, Ben Zhong Tang
nodes.materials_raw: Clusteroluminogens: polymers (natural: starch, cellulose, proteins; synthetic: PEG, PAM, polyurea), small molecules (with/without aromatic rings), metal clusters
nodes.methods_raw: Review of photoluminescence mechanisms, through-space conjugation (TSC) theory development, application demonstrations
nodes.key_findings: CTE: materials with entirely nonconjugated structures (saturated C-C, C-O, C-N bonds) showing bright visible emission in aggregates/solid state; three categories: polymers, small molecules, metal clusters; through-space conjugation (TSC) theory proposed as comprehensive explanation: electron-rich groups cluster → extend electron delocalization via through-space interactions → rigidification inhibits nonradiative relaxation → emission; advantages over traditional π-conjugated chromophores: facile synthesis, environmental friendliness, biocompatibility, no quenching in aggregate state; applications: processing monitoring, structural visualization, sensors, probes; natural products (starch, cellulose, proteins) as clusteroluminogens → abundant, low-cost, biocompatible; CTE effect is general phenomenon applicable to diverse nonconjugated systems
nodes.scientific_questions: Can TSC theory quantitatively predict emission wavelengths from molecular structure? What is the minimum cluster size for efficient CTE? How does water (biological environment) affect CTE in natural polymers?

nodes.label: Dissipative Particle Dynamics Simulation of Multiscale Phase-Separated Structures in Graft-Type Poly
nodes.full_title: Dissipative Particle Dynamics Simulation of Multiscale Phase-Separated Structures in Graft-Type Polymer Electrolyte Membrane by Optimization Compared to Scattering Analysis
nodes.authors: Toshinori Motegi, Masataka Abe, Yue Zhao, Kimio Yoshimura, Akihiro Hiroki, Toshihiro Kawakatsu, Yasunari Maekawa
nodes.materials_raw: Graft-type polymer electrolyte membranes (PEMs); ion channel structures; hydrophilic-hydrophobic phase-separated systems
nodes.methods_raw: Dissipative particle dynamics (DPD) simulations, SANS (small-angle neutron scattering) comparison, Teubner-Strey model analysis, χ-parameter optimization, coarse-grained particle assignment
nodes.key_findings: DPD simulations reproduce multiscale phase-separated structures in graft-type PEMs by optimizing conservation parameters and coupling force terms vs SANS profiles; most decisive χ-parameters evaluated by appropriate terminal group and charge assignments of coarse-grained particles; simulated scattering profiles of ion channels and hydrophilic-hydrophobic phase structures adjusted by tuning spring constants; Teubner-Strey model analysis: average ion channel diameter d = 1.9–2.0 nm with deviation ε = ±0.5 nm; optimized structural simulation model enables expression of ion channel structures as digital values affecting ion conductivity; approach reduces experiments needed for high-performance PEM development
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the DPD-SANS optimization approach be automated with machine learning? How sensitive is the ion conductivity prediction to the ion channel diameter precision? Can this method predict PEM degradation under operational conditions?

nodes.label: Highly Tough, Notch-Insensitive, and Fast Self-Healing Thermoplastic Polyurethane Elastomers by Tail
nodes.full_title: Highly Tough, Notch-Insensitive, and Fast Self-Healing Thermoplastic Polyurethane Elastomers by Tailored Soft Segment Design
nodes.authors: Changhoon Yu, Hongdeok Kim, Jinho Choi, Sunwu Song, Joonmyung Choi, Min Sang Kwon
nodes.materials_raw: Acrylic TPU elastomers; α,ω-hydroxyl end-functionalized polyacrylates as soft segments; 2-ethylhexyl acrylate (branched alkyl); PTMO-based conventional TPU for comparison
nodes.methods_raw: MD simulation of phase separation, tensile testing, fracture energy measurement, self-healing tests (45°C, 3 h), notch sensitivity testing, DMA
nodes.key_findings: Soft-segment modification strategy incorporating α,ω-hydroxyl end-functionalized polyacrylates into conventional TPU; branched-alkyl acrylic soft segments (2-ethylhexyl acrylate) generate side-chain architectures with segmental dynamics sensitive to temperature; enhanced thermal responsiveness → thermally activated chain mobility + more uniform stress distribution without compromising hard domain mechanical contribution; acrylic TPUs: 186 MJ/m³ toughness, fracture energy >197 kJ/m² at >900% elongation (notch-insensitive), ~90% self-healing recovery at 45°C within 3 h; branched polyacrylate soft-segment engineering offers balanced pathway to mechanically robust and adaptable TPU; addresses long-standing trade-off between mechanical robustness and dynamic recovery
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the branch length/density of the acrylate soft segment affect the toughness-healing balance? Can the self-healing temperature be further reduced? What is the fatigue life under cyclic loading with notch?

nodes.label: Morphological Changes in PEDOT:PSS under Electrolytes, Dopamine, and PEG-400 Exposure: A Molecular S
nodes.full_title: Morphological Changes in PEDOT:PSS under Electrolytes, Dopamine, and PEG-400 Exposure: A Molecular Simulation Perspective
nodes.authors: Amali G. Guruge, Hesam Makki, Alessandro Troisi
nodes.materials_raw: PEDOT:PSS conducting polymer; electrolytes (CuCl₂, NaCl); dopamine; PEG-400
nodes.methods_raw: Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, morphological analysis (lamella crystallite size, π-π stacking, chain orientation, interlamellar connectivity)
nodes.key_findings: All chemical components screen electrostatic interactions between PEDOT and PSS → potentially affecting conductivity; dopamine reduces conductivity by intercalating between PEDOT and PSS → disrupting interdomain connectivity; PEG-400 enhances conductivity by improving interlamellar connectivity without altering PEDOT chain conformation → challenges conventional explanation (alternative mechanism); CuCl₂ enhances conductivity via PEDOT conformational changes with partial PSS loss; NaCl shows minimal morphological changes; MD simulations confirm experimental trends, provide alternative insights, and challenge commonly accepted explanations; demonstrates utility of MD in validating, refining, and reinterpreting molecular mechanisms in complex polymer systems
nodes.scientific_questions: What determines whether an additive improves or degrades PEDOT:PSS conductivity at the molecular level? Can MD-guided additive selection replace trial-and-error experimental screening? How does water diffusion kinetics compare with electrolyte diffusion in PEDOT:PSS?

nodes.label: Multiscale Simulation of Multicomponent Polyurethane Elastomers: Unraveling Composition-Dependent Mi
nodes.full_title: Multiscale Simulation of Multicomponent Polyurethane Elastomers: Unraveling Composition-Dependent Microphase Separation Dynamics and Mechanical Properties
nodes.authors: Yujiang Meng, Xionghui Wu, Anqiang Zhang, Yaling Lin
nodes.materials_raw: Multicomponent polyurethane elastomers; PDMS + PCDL (bi-incompatible soft segments); hard segments (urea/urethane)
nodes.methods_raw: Bottom-up multiscale simulation: DFT → all-atom MD → reactive coarse-grained (CG) simulation; RESP charge assignment; iterative Boltzmann inversion (IBI); chemical bond formation algorithm in CG
nodes.key_findings: DFT quantifies superior H-bonding capability of urea/urethane groups and pronounced incompatibility between soft components (PDMS/PCDL); quantum-derived insights inform AA simulations via RESP method; structural correlations transferred to mesoscopic scale through IBI; reactive CG system captures various interactions → aggregation of hard segments + thermodynamic incompatibility of soft segments dictate evolving microphase morphology; systematic variation of component ratio enables precise control of structural transitions: dispersed → bicontinuous → island morphologies; morphologies directly correlate with mechanical properties; predictive platform for customized design of high-performance PU elastomers
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the multiscale framework predict PU properties for entirely new compositions without experimental calibration? How sensitive are the morphological transitions to processing conditions (cooling rate, solvent)? What is the role of interfacial regions between PDMS and PCDL domains?

nodes.label: chemical bond formation algorithm in CG
Coarse-Graining of Slit-Confined Star Polymers in Solvents of Varying Quality
nodes.full_title: Coarse-Graining of Slit-Confined Star Polymers in Solvents of Varying Quality
nodes.authors: Reyhaneh A. Farimani, Christos N. Likos
nodes.materials_raw: Star polymers with varying functionalities; confined within slit geometry; solvents of varying quality (athermal, good, poor)
nodes.methods_raw: Effective interaction computation (pair force calculation), monomer-resolved MD simulations, Monte Carlo simulations with effective interactions, radial distribution function comparison, morphological model for interpenetration analysis
nodes.key_findings: Significant sensitivity of radial distribution function to subtle variations in tail of interaction potential, particularly in dilute regimes; solvent quality has minimal impact on degree of interpenetration between star polymers; star functionality affects interpenetration markedly → enhanced faceting and reduced interpenetration as number of arms grows; effective interaction in 2D has logarithmic core + Gaussian tail; results relevant for understanding rheological and mechanical properties of polymeric materials under confinement
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the confinement transition from 3D to quasi-2D affect the glassy dynamics of star polymer systems? Can the coarse-grained model predict the phase behavior of confined star polymers? What is the role of solvent quality in the adsorption transition?

nodes.label: Monte Carlo simulations with effective interaction
Electrically Switchable Molecular Adhesion via Self-Assembled Monolayer-Mediated Hydration and Ion S
nodes.full_title: Electrically Switchable Molecular Adhesion via Self-Assembled Monolayer-Mediated Hydration and Ion Structuring
nodes.authors: Valentina Wieser, Yoyo Cheng-Ting Yu, Andrea Valencia Ramirez, David T. Wu, Frank Uwe Renner, Hsiu-Wei Cheng
nodes.materials_raw: Mica and metal surfaces modified with self-assembled monolayers (SAMs): mercaptobenzimidazole and cysteamine films; electrolyte solutions
nodes.methods_raw: Electrochemical surface forces apparatus (EC-SFA), molecular dynamics simulations, force-distance measurements, electrochemical potential modulation
nodes.key_findings: Electromechanical adhesion switch mechanism demonstrated; molecular surface modification and potential modulation impact adhesive/repulsive forces between surfaces; ion and hydration structuring at interface susceptible to surface potential, structural modifications, charge of functional groups, and electrolyte composition; SAM-mediated hydration and ion ordering under electrochemical modulation control film-mica interactions; beyond classical DLVO theory: specific ion adsorption and near-surface hydration are key features; flexible route for electromechanical adhesion switches demonstrated
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the adhesion switching be made reversible over many cycles? What is the switching speed limited by (ion transport, SAM reorganization)? Can this mechanism be scaled to macroscopic adhesive applications?

nodes.label: Mica and metal surfaces modified with self-assembled monolay
Investigating the Water-Induced Stiffening Mechanism in a Novel Polyurethane through Simulation and
nodes.full_title: Investigating the Water-Induced Stiffening Mechanism in a Novel Polyurethane through Simulation and Experimental Analysis
nodes.authors: Ao Wang, Wenkai Liu, Xiaohan Jin, Hecheng Wu, Dongfei Zhang, Xianglong Han, Yang Liu, Zhen Li, Mingming Ding, Jiehua Li, Hong Tan
nodes.materials_raw: Side-chain PEG polyurethane (SPPU) with hard backbone and dangling PEG soft segments; implantable medical device application (intravascular stents)
nodes.methods_raw: Coarse-grained (CG) and all-atom (AA) MD simulations, gradient boosting regression (GBR) model, experimental validation, H-bond analysis
nodes.key_findings: SPPU has unique self-stiffening property activated by water (compensates for mechanical property decrease after shape recovery and water-induced plasticization); water induces higher degree of phase separation in SPPU; under stretching, hard domains of aqueous SPPU orient more strongly in tensile stress direction → effective stress transfer; water-stiffening enhancement requires sufficient side chain density; H-bond ratio in SPPU shows higher degree of enhancement with water content (matches experiments); SPPU unique topology identified as most important factor for Young's modulus by GBR model; promising for implantable medical devices
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the critical side chain density threshold for water-stiffening? How does the water-stiffening mechanism compete with plasticization at different hydration levels? Can the water-stiffening concept be generalized to other polymer architectures?

nodes.label: Coarse-Grained Modeling of P3HT:SBS Blends for Stretchable Organic Semiconductors
nodes.full_title: Coarse-Grained Modeling of P3HT:SBS Blends for Stretchable Organic Semiconductors
nodes.authors: Joohee Choi, Gibeom Song, Hae-Jin Kim, Joonmyung Choi, Seunghwa Ryu
nodes.materials_raw: P3HT (conjugated polymer) + SBS (styrene-butadiene-styrene thermoplastic elastomer) blends for stretchable electronics
nodes.methods_raw: CG MD framework, force matching + Boltzmann inversion for CG parameters, three-site mapping for P3HT (backbone rigidity + side chain), one-site-per-monomer for SBS, water-assisted tensile experiments, STEM analysis
nodes.key_findings: Chemistry-specific CG potential parameters derived through force matching and Boltzmann inversion; increasing P3HT fraction → enhanced strength/stiffness but reduced ductility → brittle fracture; behavior originates from rigid π-conjugated backbone + dense chain packing restricting chain mobility and limiting energy dissipation; SBS-rich blends retain rubber-like toughness (elastomeric network of flexible PB midblocks + PS junctions delocalizes stress, prevents localized fracture); water-assisted tensile experiments + STEM validate CG model (composition-dependent stiffening, uniform-to-localized deformation transition); direct link between blend morphology and mechanical response confirmed; practical design guidelines for stretchable organic semiconductors
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the CG model predict the electronic properties (charge mobility) alongside mechanical properties? What is the optimal P3HT:SBS ratio for balanced conductivity and stretchability? How does phase domain size affect crack propagation?

nodes.label: P3HT  + SBS  blends for stretchable electronics
force matching + Boltzmann inversion for CG parame
In Situ Visualization of Microphase Separation in High-Performance Hyperbranched Polyurethane
nodes.full_title: In Situ Visualization of Microphase Separation in High-Performance Hyperbranched Polyurethane
nodes.authors: Jingyuan Wei, Yufei Zhang, Huan Ma, Jia Li, Junzhuo Cheng, Haotian Ma, Shenggui Du, Kai Cheng, Hefeng Zhang, Tianqi Zhou, Yu Jiang, Daohong Zhang, Nikos Hadjichristidis
nodes.materials_raw: Hyperbranched PU elastomer (PU-HPAE_x) using hyperbranched poly(amino ester) (HPAE) as dual-function macromonomer (chain extender + fluorescent probe)
nodes.methods_raw: Fluorescence spectroscopy, quantum yield measurement, mechanical testing, in situ fluorescence microscopy for phase separation visualization, SAXS/WAXD
nodes.key_findings: HPAE acts simultaneously as chain extender and nonconventional fluorescent probe; hyperbranched architecture creates 3D network with high-density sacrificial H-bonds + well-defined microphase separation → 65.80 MPa strength, 1031.70% elongation, 185.3 MJ/m³ toughness (overcomes strength-toughness conflict); hyperbranched topology promotes efficient cluster-triggered emission (CTE) via through-space conjugation (TSC) → quantum yield 11.16%; HPAE as intrinsic fluorescent probe enables in situ visualization of micrometer-scale phase separation and its dynamic evolution → key insights into morphology-performance relationship; stimuli-responsive fluorescence under mechanical strain and humidity → smart sensing applications; novel strategy for fluorescent PU elastomers integrating high performance with nondestructive visualization
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the hyperbranched HPAE architecture quantitatively affect the H-bond density and sacrificial bond mechanics? Can the fluorescence-based visualization be extended to other PU systems? What is the relationship between CTE intensity and microphase separation degree?

nodes.label: in situ fluorescence microscopy for phase separati
A Computational Analysis of Crystallite Shape under Quiescent and Stretch-Induced Polyethylene Cryst
nodes.full_title: A Computational Analysis of Crystallite Shape under Quiescent and Stretch-Induced Polyethylene Crystallization
nodes.authors: Fotis Venetsanos, Stefanos D. Anogiannakis, Doros N. Theodorou
nodes.institution: National Technical University of Athens (Theodorou group)
nodes.materials_raw: Linear polyethylene melts (uniform molecular weight distribution)
nodes.methods_raw: Monte Carlo equilibration → isothermal MD simulations under quiescent and stretching conditions; home-built algorithms for crystallinity evolution; radius of gyration tensor + Q-tensor analysis for crystallite shape/orientation; mean first passage time analysis for induction time distributions
nodes.key_findings: Flow field strongly impacts nucleation and growth → accelerates crystalline phase emergence; methodology established for quantifying shape and orientation of crystallites via radius of gyration tensor and Q-tensor analysis; stretched crystallites strongly oriented along drawing direction → cylindrical symmetry; quiescent crystallites are quasi-spherical with random orientation; growth rates quantified along principal axes of crystallites and volumetric growth rate; quiescent specimens at long times show bridging between crystallites and curving of lamellar planes; 3D visualizations illuminate mechanisms and stretching influence on chain orientation
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the crystallite shape transition from quasi-spherical to cylindrical depend on strain rate? Can the methodology predict the shish-kebab morphology? What is the critical strain rate for the onset of oriented crystallization?

mean first passage time analysis for induction tim
National Technical University of Athens (Theodorou group)
A New Autocatalytic Thioacetate-Enal Addition Reaction: A Michael Addition or Not?
nodes.full_title: A New Autocatalytic Thioacetate-Enal Addition Reaction: A Michael Addition or Not?
nodes.authors: Gennadiy Ilyashenko, Andrew Whiting, Allen Wright
nodes.materials_raw: Thioacetic acid + trans-2-hexenal (unsaturated aldehyde); thio-Michael addition products
nodes.methods_raw: Adiabatic calorimetry (Dewar flask), reaction calorimetry, NMR studies, kinetic fitting
nodes.key_findings: Rather than proceeding through simple Michael-type 1,4-addition, thioacetic acid adds across unsaturated aldehydes in an autocatalytic manner involving double exotherm; NMR shows intermediate acylthio-hemiacetal involved; product continues to react competitively with thioacetic acid; two-stage adiabatic temperature rise observed (not simple bimolecular addition); multi-stage reaction with at least two intermediates; suggests reinterpretation of hetero-Michael additions may be required; implications for asymmetric catalysis of thio-Michael reactions
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the generality of the autocatalytic mechanism across different thiol-ene systems? Can the autocatalysis be exploited for asymmetric amplification? How does the autocatalytic pathway affect polymer network formation kinetics?

nodes.label: Amino acid catalyzed thio-Michael addition reactions
nodes.full_title: Amino acid catalyzed thio-Michael addition reactions
nodes.authors: Atul Kumar, Akanksha
nodes.materials_raw: Enones + alkyl/aryl thiols; amino acid catalysts (especially L-proline)
nodes.methods_raw: Amino acid catalysis, various solvent/screening conditions, yield optimization
nodes.key_findings: Amino acid (especially L-proline) catalyzed thio-Michael addition under mild, neutral conditions; inexpensive, nontoxic, environmentally friendly, metal-free protocol; excellent yields; good alternative to existing metal catalyst methodology; broad substrate scope with various enones and thiols; L-proline effective via enamine-based catalysis (as in aldol, Mannich reactions)
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the enantioselectivity potential with chiral amino acid catalysts? Can this approach be used for polymer network formation (thiol-ene polymerization)? How does the amino acid catalyst interact with the thiol nucleophile?

nodes.label: Facile, modular transformations of RAFT block copolymers via sequential isocyanate and thiol-ene rea
nodes.full_title: Facile, modular transformations of RAFT block copolymers via sequential isocyanate and thiol-ene reactions
nodes.authors: Joel D. Flores, Nicolas J. Treat, Adam W. York, Charles L. McCormick
nodes.materials_raw: PDMA-b-PHEA block copolymers (RAFT-synthesized); 2-(acryloyloxy)ethyl isocyanate (AOI); allyl isocyanate (AI); various thiols (alkyl, aryl, hydroxyl, carboxylic acid, amine, amino acid)
nodes.methods_raw: RAFT polymerization, isocyanate-hydroxyl coupling, Michael and radical-mediated thiol-ene addition, GPC, NMR
nodes.key_findings: Robust strategy using RAFT + sequential carbamate formation + thiol-ene click for well-defined functional block copolymers; hydroxyl-functional scaffold PDMA-b-PHEA prepared via RAFT (no protecting groups); hydroxyl groups reacted with isocyanates → acrylate- or allyl-functionalized precursors; both Michael and radical-mediated thiol-ene additions efficient with diverse model thiols; mild conditions throughout; facile, modular route to functional copolymers from single polymeric precursor
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the sequential approach be extended to more complex architectures (stars, brushes)? What is the orthogonality of isocyanate and thiol-ene reactions in one-pot? How does the thiol-ene efficiency depend on the polymer architecture?

nodes.label: The Thiol-Michael Addition Click Reaction: A Powerful and Widely Used Tool in Materials Chemistry
nodes.full_title: The Thiol-Michael Addition Click Reaction: A Powerful and Widely Used Tool in Materials Chemistry
nodes.authors: Devatha P. Nair, Maciej Podgórski, Shunsuke Chatani, Tao Gong, Weixian Xi, Christopher R. Fenoli, Christopher N. Bowman
nodes.materials_raw: Thiol-Michael addition systems; acrylate, methacrylate, vinyl sulfone, maleimide, acrylamide acceptors; various thiol donors
nodes.methods_raw: Review of reaction mechanisms, substrate/catalyst scope, materials applications
nodes.key_findings: Thiol-Michael addition is modular "click" reaction: highly efficient, "green", applicable from small molecule synthesis to in situ polymer modification in biological systems to surface functionalization; key attributes: stereospecific, regiospecific products, simple, robust; comprehensive toolbox of different Michael addition chemistries; mechanisms: base-catalyzed, nucleophilic, radical-mediated; recent developments in: step-growth polymer networks, surface modifications, hydrogel formation, biological conjugation, coatings; catalysts include: base (tertiary amines, DBU, phosphines), nucleophilic (DMAP, NHC), photobase generators
nodes.scientific_questions: How can the thiol-Michael reaction be made truly orthogonal to other click chemieties in complex systems? What are the long-term stability and degradation products of thioether linkages? Can photobase generators enable spatiotemporal control of network formation?

nodes.label: 基于迈克尔加成构筑聚丁二烯三嵌段共聚物 (Synthesis of polybutadiene based triblock copolymer via Michael addition reacti
nodes.full_title: 基于迈克尔加成构筑聚丁二烯三嵌段共聚物 (Synthesis of polybutadiene based triblock copolymer via Michael addition reaction)
nodes.journal: Journal of Shaanxi University of Science & Technology 38(4) (2020) 70–74
nodes.authors: Zhang Wanbin, Luo Jie, Zhang Guanghua, Wang Yanmeng, Ni Meile, Yang Dongdong, Liu Jing
nodes.materials_raw: Hydroxyl-terminated polybutadiene (HTPB); methoxy polyethylene glycol amine (mPEG-NH₂); mPEG-b-PB-b-mPEG triblock copolymer
nodes.methods_raw: Acylation + Michael addition reaction; FTIR, ¹H-NMR, ¹³C-NMR characterization; TGA; DLS; TEM
nodes.key_findings: mPEG-b-PB-b-mPEG triblock copolymer synthesized via acylation + Michael addition; structure confirmed by FTIR/NMR; good thermal stability; forms stable dot micelles in aqueous solution (DLS/TEM); amphiphilic block copolymer from hydrophobic PB + hydrophilic PEG segments
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the PB block length affect micelle size and stability? Can the Michael addition approach be extended to other hydrophobic polymer backbones? What are the drug loading capabilities of these micelles?

nodes.label: Michael addition reactions in macromolecular design for emerging technologies
nodes.full_title: Michael addition reactions in macromolecular design for emerging technologies
nodes.authors: Brian D. Mather, Kalpana Viswanathan, Kevin M. Miller, Timothy E. Long
nodes.materials_raw: Michael addition systems: acetoacetates, acrylates, maleimides, cysteine; polymer architectures from linear to hyperbranched to networks
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review: mechanism (base strength, solvent, substrate effects), polymer synthesis (step-growth, chain-growth), dendrimers, hydrogels, bioconjugates
nodes.key_findings: Michael addition is versatile for coupling electron-poor olefins with nucleophiles; polymer architectures from linear thermoplastics to hyperbranched to networks achievable; applications: biomedical (gene delivery, drug conjugates, tissue scaffolds), pharmaceutical, optoelectronic, composites, adhesives, coatings; heteroatomic donors (thiols, amines) expand scope; poly(amidoamine)s, poly(imidosulfide)s, poly(aspartamide)s via step-growth; anionic polymerization of methacrylates and cyanoacrylates; dendrimers and hyperbranched polymers; hydrogel networks for biomedical applications; versatility in monomer selection, solvent, temperature enables sophisticated structures under mild conditions
nodes.scientific_questions: How to achieve sequence control in Michael addition step-growth polymerization? Can Michael addition networks be made recyclable? What are the degradation pathways of Michael addition linkages in biological environments?

nodes.label: polymer architectures from linear to hyperbranched to networ
Thia-Michael addition: An emerging strategy in organic synthesis
nodes.full_title: Thia-Michael addition: An emerging strategy in organic synthesis
nodes.journal: Asian Journal of Organic Chemistry (2017, DOI: 10.1002/ajoc.201700609)
nodes.authors: Anuj Sharma, Preeti Wadhwa, Anupreet Kharbanda
nodes.materials_raw: Thia-Michael addition systems: thiols + electron-deficient alkenes; organosulfur compounds
nodes.methods_raw: Review of catalyzed and catalyst-free thia-Michael addition reactions
nodes.key_findings: Thia-Michael addition is powerful tool for organosulfur compound synthesis; applications in medicinal chemistry, catalysis, drug discovery, material science; robust, highly efficient, high functional group interconversion, high reaction rates, orthogonality to other reactions under mild conditions; applications in drug delivery, polymer chemistry, surfactant modifications, dendrimer synthesis, pharmaceuticals; categorized by catalyzed vs catalyst-free approaches
nodes.scientific_questions: Can thia-Michael addition be rendered enantioselective with chiral catalysts? What is the reversibility of thia-Michael linkages under physiological conditions? How does the thia-Michael reaction compare kinetically with aza-Michael and oxa-Michael?

nodes.label: Thia-Michael addition systems: thiols + electron-deficient a
Review of catalyzed and catalyst-free thia-Michael
Biphasic synergistic enhancement hydrogels via modulus-matched rubbery and entangled phases
nodes.full_title: Biphasic synergistic enhancement hydrogels via modulus-matched rubbery and entangled phases
nodes.authors: Jing Duan, Yuyi Zhang, Shixiang Zhao, Yuri V. Petrov, Vladimir E. Baulin, Aslan Yu Tsivadze, Dechang Jia, Yu Zhou, Baoqiang Li
nodes.materials_raw: Biphasic hydrogels; rubbery phase + entangled phase with nano crystalline domains; PVP/PAAm system; modulus-matched biphasic design
nodes.methods_raw: Continuous phase separation (solvent polarity reduction, H-bond breaking), mechanical testing, cyclic testing, conductivity measurement, EMG signal monitoring
nodes.key_findings: Modulus-matched rubbery and entangled phases synergistic enhancement strategy via continuous phase separation; molecular chain aggregates of rubbery and entangled phases with nano crystalline domains induced by reducing solvent polarity and breaking polymer-solvent H-bonds; key: modulus matching between phases; excellent mechanical properties: 38.76 MPa tensile strength, 137.03 MJ/m³ toughness, 58.36 MPa modulus, 130.21 kJ/m² fracture energy; low strength decay (2.73%) over 10 cycles at strain 3; transcends stiffness-toughness trade-off; heat resistance + stable electrical conductivity; high-performance epidermal bioelectrode for EMG monitoring
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the optimal modulus ratio between the two phases for maximum synergy? Can the modulus-matching principle be generalized to other biphasic systems? How does the crystalline domain size affect the toughness enhancement?

nodes.label: rubbery phase + entangled phase with nano crystalline domain
Non-faradaic junction sensing
nodes.full_title: Non-faradaic junction sensing
nodes.authors: Yecheng Wang, Kun Jia, Zhigang Suo
nodes.materials_raw: Non-faradaic junction (NFJ) sensors: ionic conductor + electronic conductor interfaces; various ionic conductors (hydrogels, ionogels, electrolytes) and electronic conductors (PEDOT:PSS, metals, carbon)
nodes.methods_raw: Review of NFJ sensing principles, voltage step characterization, device design for pressure/acoustic/temperature/chemical sensing
nodes.key_findings: NFJ is connection between ionic and electronic conductor with no electrochemical reaction; behaves as capacitor coupling ionic and electronic currents through chemistry, electricity, and entropy; charge-voltage curve sensitive to environmental signals → sensor function; no reaction → non-destructive, long-lasting sensing; high sensitivity, rapid response, small size, self-powered; applications: electrophysiology (ECG, EEG, EMG), wearable/implantable devices, soft robotics, pressure/sound/temperature/chemical sensing; flexibility in material choice enables softness, stretchability, transparency, degradability; transitioning from wired/bulky devices to wireless/wearable/implantable
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the fundamental sensitivity limit of NFJ sensors? How does the NFJ response degrade over millions of cycles? Can NFJ sensors be integrated into fully autonomous sensing systems?

nodes.label: Non-faradaic junction  sensors: ionic conductor + electronic
Impact of Chain Length on PCL Dielectric β-Relaxation: Decoupling the Roles of Chain Ends, Main Chai
nodes.full_title: Impact of Chain Length on PCL Dielectric β-Relaxation: Decoupling the Roles of Chain Ends, Main Chains, Entanglements, and Crystallinity
nodes.authors: Leire Sangroniz, Angel Alegría, Alejandro J. Müller
nodes.materials_raw: Polycaprolactone (PCL) with number-average molar mass range 2–173 kg/mol
nodes.methods_raw: Broadband dielectric spectroscopy, systematic chain length variation, modeling of end group/main chain/crystallinity contributions
nodes.key_findings: First decoupling of chain length, chain ends, entanglements, and crystallinity effects on β relaxation in PCL; as chain length increases, amorphous main chain contribution altered due to entanglements affecting chain dynamics; strong correlation between local dynamics changes and rigid amorphous fraction in semicrystalline polymers; entanglements arise above ~3 kg/mol (M_e estimated 1.6–3.9 kg/mol); M_c for η₀ ∼ M^3.4 scaling falls in 3.1–7.8 kg/mol range; tools provided for using broadband dielectric spectroscopy to isolate complex chain parameters
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the rigid amorphous fraction evolve with crystallization conditions? Can the decoupling approach be applied to other semicrystalline polymers? What is the relationship between entanglement density and β-relaxation time?

nodes.label: modeling of end group/main chain/crystallinity con
Synergistic Effects of Maxwell Stress and Electrostriction in Electromechanical Properties of Poly(v
nodes.full_title: Synergistic Effects of Maxwell Stress and Electrostriction in Electromechanical Properties of Poly(vinylidene fluoride)-Based Ferroelectric Polymers
nodes.authors: Baobao Qiao, Xiao Wang, Shaobo Tan, Weiwei Zhu, Zhicheng Zhang
nodes.materials_raw: P(VDF-TrFE-CTFE) relaxor ferroelectric copolymers; β-PVDF; P(VDF-TrFE)
nodes.methods_raw: Synthesis of varying CTFE content copolymers, dielectric/ferroelectric/electromechanical characterization, crystalline and ferroelectric phase transition analysis
nodes.key_findings: New model combining Maxwell stress and electrostrictive effect for electrostrain mechanism; P(VDF-TrFE-CTFE) copolymers show high thickness strain under external field; contributions of each effect to overall strain depend on electric field, material composition, and film fabrication process; perfect synergism of Maxwell stress + electrostriction responsible for large strain under low field; strategy to design electroactive polymers with excellent electrostrain; Maxwell stress ∝ ε₀ε_r E²; electrostriction ∝ M·E²; combined effect enables large electromechanical coupling
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the Maxwell stress + electrostriction synergy be optimized independently? What determines the crossover field where one mechanism dominates? How does the relaxor state affect the electromechanical coupling coefficient?

crystalline and ferroelectric phase transition ana
Tough, Multifunctional, and Biodegradable Polyurethanes Enabled by Structure-Property Tuning for Ver
nodes.full_title: Tough, Multifunctional, and Biodegradable Polyurethanes Enabled by Structure-Property Tuning for Versatile Applications
nodes.authors: Jiaxiong Liu, Kenji Yamaoka, Ryohei Ikura, Akihide Sugawara, Yuya Takahashi, Bunsho Kure, Naomi Takenaka, Hiroshi Uyama, Yoshinori Takashima
nodes.materials_raw: Polyurethanes with triacetylated γ-cyclodextrin (TAcγCD)-based movable cross-links (γCD-PUs); biodegradable PUs
nodes.methods_raw: Structure-property tuning, enzymatic degradation, enzymatic reinforcement strategy, mechanical testing, shape-memory testing, adhesion testing, strain sensor fabrication
nodes.key_findings: γCD-PUs with low TAcγCD content exhibit enhanced mechanical properties + excellent recyclability; selective enzymatic degradation with specific enzymes/reaction conditions; biodegradable γCD-PUs function as: hydrophobic materials, shape-memory materials, adhesives, strain sensors; enzymatic reinforcement strategy → reinforced γCD-PUs: (a) 45.3 MPa fracture stress + 450 MJ/m³ toughness OR (b) 2943% fracture strain + 381 MJ/m³ toughness; cyclodextrin movable cross-links provide: dynamic nature, easy modification, bulky structure → energy dissipation + stress dispersion; fundamental insight for tough biodegradable polymers with tailored functionalities → circular plastics economy
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the movable cross-link dynamics compare with conventional vitrimer chemistry? Can the enzymatic reinforcement be applied to other polymer systems? What is the environmental degradation rate under real composting conditions?

nodes.label: Conformation and dynamics of active polymers with one end fixed
nodes.full_title: Conformation and dynamics of active polymers with one end fixed
nodes.authors: Song Wu, Jia-Xiang Li, Yu-Qiang Ma
nodes.institution: Nanjing University (National Laboratory of Solid State Microstructures)
nodes.materials_raw: Polar active polymers (chains of active Brownian particles); one-end-fixed boundary conditions
nodes.methods_raw: Comparative simulations (tail vs head fixation), scaling analysis, conformational relaxation dynamics
nodes.key_findings: Tail fixation → nonuniform stretching + nonmonotonic conformational relaxation dependence on chain activity; head fixation → uniform compression + yarn-ball-like structure + distinct relaxation dynamics; new scaling relations: R_g ∼ N^{-0.38}, τ* ∼ N^{0.6}, τ* ∼ Pe^{-1.35}; choice of fixed point (tail vs head) leads to dramatically distinct conformational and dynamical behaviors; fundamental insights into interplay between activity and boundary conditions; implications for bioinspired active materials design
nodes.scientific_questions: How do these scaling relations change in crowded/confinement environments? Can the active polymer model predict chromatin dynamics in the nucleus? What happens when both ends are fixed (loop configuration)?

nodes.label: Nanjing University (National Laboratory of Solid State Microstructures)
Size Effect of the End-Attached Particle on the Adsorption-Responsive Polymer Switches
nodes.full_title: Size Effect of the End-Attached Particle on the Adsorption-Responsive Polymer Switches
nodes.authors: Zengju Lian, Shuanhu Qi
nodes.materials_raw: Adsorption-active polymer chain with free end attached to hard-core nanoparticle in inert brush; two-component brush system
nodes.methods_raw: Analytical theory, self-consistent field (SCF) calculations, scaling analysis
nodes.key_findings: Particle-polymer system exhibits different phases and transition features vs particle-free system only when particle is large enough; two critical particle radii R* and R_c defined: R > R* → particle stuck at brush surface (partially exposed state, not possible in particle-free system); small particles → only two states (exposed and adsorbed); R > R_c → transition properties show different dependence on grafting density, solvent quality, chain length; analytical expressions for transition point, transition width, transition barrier derived; large particle mainly broadens transition width while keeping transition barrier small; strong osmotic repulsion on finite volume particle responsible for particular phase/transition characteristics
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the polymer switch be experimentally realized with current nanofabrication techniques? How does the particle shape (non-spherical) affect the switching behavior? What is the response time of the particle-polymer switch?

nodes.label: Adsorption-active polymer chain with free end attached to ha
A Scaling Theory for Polyelectrolyte Brushes: Insights from Intermomomer Electrostatic Interactions
nodes.full_title: A Scaling Theory for Polyelectrolyte Brushes: Insights from Intermomomer Electrostatic Interactions
nodes.authors: Jadong Chen, Mingyu Duan, Runqi Zhang, Guang Chen
nodes.materials_raw: Polyelectrolyte (PE) brushes; charged polymers grafted to solid-liquid interface; monovalent salt solutions
nodes.methods_raw: Cell model with intermonomer electrostatic interactions, scaling theory, molecular dynamics simulations
nodes.key_findings: New scaling regime identified: H ∝ n_s^{-1/5} (vs classical salted brush H ∝ n_s^{-1/3}); arises from non-overlapping electric double layers (EDLs) between charged monomers at high salt concentrations; critical charge fraction φ_c identified; classical H ∝ n_s^{-1/3} only for weakly charged (φ < φ_c) and loosely grafted brushes; weaker salt dependence (H ∝ n_s^{-1/5} or transition to neutral brush) more commonly observed; explains experimental anomalies where H ∝ n_s^{-0.15} or n_s^{-0.17} reported instead of n_s^{-1/3}; MD simulations confirm predictions; guides design of PE systems with tailored swelling-shrinking behavior
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the new scaling regime extend to multivalent salt solutions? Can the theory predict the response dynamics (not just equilibrium) of PE brushes? What is the role of chain stiffness in the scaling behavior?

nodes.label: charged polymers grafted to solid-liquid interface
Cell model with intermonomer electrostatic interac
Facile mechanochemical cycloreversion of polymer cross-linkers enhances tear resistance
nodes.full_title: Facile mechanochemical cycloreversion of polymer cross-linkers enhances tear resistance
nodes.authors: Shu Wang, Yixin Hu, Tatiana B. Kouznetsova, Liel Sapir, Danyang Chen, Abraham Herzog-Arbeitman, Jeremiah A. Johnson, Michael Rubinstein, Stephen L. Craig
nodes.materials_raw: Cyclobutane-based mechanophore cross-linkers (C1, cis-diaryl substituted cyclobutane); polyacrylate elastomers (2-methoxyethyl acrylate); side-chain cross-linked networks via RAFT polymerization
nodes.methods_raw: RAFT polymerization, single-molecule force spectroscopy, mechanical testing (constant displacement rate + fatigue), simulation
