nodes.key_findings: Cyclobutane mechanophore cross-linkers break through force-triggered [2+2] cycloreversion → networks up to 9× tougher than conventional analogs; side-chain cross-linking approach (mechanophore not in middle of network strand); C1 cycloreversion requires ~5× less force than C-C bond scission at tearing-relevant timescales; enhanced toughness without hysteresis from noncovalent cross-linking; observed in two different acrylate elastomers, in fatigue and constant displacement rate, in gel and elastomers; force-coupled reactivity of mechanophore determines material properties; long, strong primary polymer strands + low cross-linker cission force → synergistic toughening
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the mechanophore approach be combined with other toughening strategies (e.g., double network)? What is the fatigue life improvement quantitatively? How does the mechanophore density affect the toughness-crosslinker strength trade-off?

nodes.label: side-chain cross-linked networks via RAFT polymerization
Controlling the Equilibrium Swelling of Polyampholyte Gels Using Charge Sequence
nodes.full_title: Controlling the Equilibrium Swelling of Polyampholyte Gels Using Charge Sequence
nodes.authors: D. Wijesekera, Y. Ge
nodes.materials_raw: Polyampholyte gels; alternating, random, and diblock charge sequences; charged polymer networks with both positive and negative charges
nodes.methods_raw: Molecular simulations, electrostatic tension analysis, elastic stress modeling, osmotic pressure decomposition, Bjerrum length dependence study
nodes.key_findings: Charge sequence dramatically affects equilibrium swelling of polyampholyte gels; alternating sequences show highest swelling due to strong intrachain electrostatic pairing that reduces effective cross-link density; random sequences show intermediate behavior; diblock sequences show lowest swelling due to microphase separation and charge neutralization; three competing contributions: electrostatic tension, elastic stress, and osmotic pressure; Bjerrum length (λ_B) controls the balance between electrostatic and entropic effects; at high λ_B, alternating gels swell more than diblock; at low λ_B, sequence effect diminishes; design principle: charge sequence is a powerful tuning parameter for gel swelling independent of composition
nodes.scientific_questions: How does charge sequence affect the mechanical properties (not just swelling) of polyampholyte gels? Can sequence-controlled polyampholyte gels be experimentally synthesized? What is the role of counterion condensation in sequence-dependent swelling?

nodes.label: charged polymer networks with both positive and negative cha
Recyclable Thiol-Ene Networks with Dynamic Ether Linkages
nodes.full_title: Recyclable Thiol-Ene Networks with Dynamic Ether Linkages
nodes.authors: Kumar et al.
nodes.materials_raw: Trifunctional alkene monomer with benzylic ether groups + dithiol cross-linkers; thiol-ene networks with dynamic ether linkages
nodes.methods_raw: Thiol-ene polymerization, creep testing, dynamic mechanical analysis, shape memory testing, degradation and chemical recycling experiments
nodes.key_findings: Benzylic ether-based trifunctional alkene reacted with dithiols to form recyclable thiol-ene networks; dynamic ether linkages enable network reprocessing and recycling; creep behavior and dynamicity tunable by cross-link density; shape memory properties demonstrated; degradation under specific conditions allows monomer recovery and chemical recycling; dynamic ether exchange provides vitrimer-like behavior without traditional transesterification catalysts; mild recycling conditions compared to conventional thermosets
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the activation energy for ether exchange in these networks? How does the ether exchange rate compare with other dynamic bonds (disulfide, transesterification)? Can the recycled monomers achieve the same network properties upon repolymerization?

nodes.label: Trifunctional alkene monomer with benzylic ether groups + di
Viscoelastic Dynamics of Photothermal-Responsive Liquid Crystal Elastomer Fibers
nodes.full_title: Viscoelastic Dynamics of Photothermal-Responsive Liquid Crystal Elastomer Fibers
nodes.authors: Xu et al.
nodes.materials_raw: Liquid crystal elastomer (LCE) fibers; photothermal-responsive LCEs
nodes.methods_raw: 1D string model; order parameter + temperature-dependent linear viscoelasticity; PID control simulation; dynamic analysis of LCE fiber actuation
nodes.key_findings: 1D string model captures the viscoelastic dynamics of LCE fibers under photothermal stimulation; order parameter evolution coupled with temperature-dependent viscoelastic response; PID control enables precise actuation control; model predicts fiber contraction/expansion dynamics under varying illumination; viscoelastic relaxation times strongly temperature-dependent near nematic-isotropic transition; framework applicable to design of LCE-based soft actuators with programmable response
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the model extend to 2D/3D LCE geometries (films, tubes)? What is the fatigue behavior under cyclic photothermal stimulation? Can the PID framework be adapted for multi-input (light + thermal) control?

nodes.label: dynamic analysis of LCE fiber actuation
Triply Periodic Minimal Surface (TPMS) Porous Structures: Design, Fabrication, and Applications
nodes.full_title: Triply Periodic Minimal Surface (TPMS) Porous Structures: Design, Fabrication, and Applications
nodes.authors: Feng et al.
nodes.materials_raw: Triply periodic minimal surface (TPMS) structures; various materials (metals, ceramics, polymers) fabricated via additive manufacturing
nodes.methods_raw: Review covering TPMS mathematical design (Schwarz P, Schwarz D, Gyroid, Diamond, I-WP, F-RD, etc.), additive manufacturing (SLM, SLA, FDM, inkjet 3D printing), multi-scale characterization, mechanical/fluid/thermal/ biological testing
nodes.key_findings: TPMS structures are smooth, non-self-intersecting periodic surfaces with zero mean curvature at every point; advantages over traditional lattice/cellular structures: smooth stress distribution, no stress concentrations, high surface-to-volume ratio, tortuous interconnected channels; mathematical representation: level-set method f(r) = c for parametric control of porosity; major types: Primitive (P), Diamond (D), Gyroid (G), I-WP, F-RD, Neovius, Lidinoid; applications span: lightweight structures (energy absorption, aerospace), heat transfer (heat exchangers, thermal management), filtration/membranes, biomedical implants (bone scaffolds with tunable stiffness matching), acoustic metamaterials; multi-material TPMS for graded/functional structures; additive manufacturing enables complex TPMS geometries impossible with conventional methods; mechanical properties highly sensitive to relative density, cell size, and wall thickness; Gibson-Ashby model applies but TPMS shows unique deformation mechanisms
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the optimal TPMS topology for specific applications (e.g., bone scaffold vs heat exchanger)? How do manufacturing defects (surface roughness, dimensional accuracy) affect TPMS performance? Can TPMS structures be designed with spatially graded properties using machine learning?

nodes.label: Spontaneous Chiral Symmetry Breaking in Polydisperse Achiral Near-Rigid Nematogens
nodes.full_title: Spontaneous Chiral Symmetry Breaking in Polydisperse Achiral Near-Rigid Nematogens
nodes.authors: W. S. Fall, H. H. Wensink
nodes.materials_raw: Achiral bead-spring rods with marginal backbone flexibility; continuous spread in contour length (polydispersity σ = 0.2–0.5); log-normal rod length distributions
nodes.methods_raw: Molecular dynamics simulations (LAMMPS), NPT ensemble, chirality correlation function analysis, Onsager theory comparison, phase diagram mapping
nodes.key_findings: Spontaneous entropy-driven chiral symmetry breaking observed in cholesteric liquid crystals formed from strictly achiral rods; symmetry breaking caused by long-lived chiral conformations of long rods undergoing chiral synchronization → homochiral twisted nematic; at intermediate polydispersity (σ = 0.38), spontaneous cholesteric phase emerges with helical twisting of nematic director; pitch Pc/L̄ ≈ 16–2 for cholesteric phase; shape-persistent configurational fluctuations of nonchiral objects with inherent size dispersity can bootstrap chiral synchronization; polydispersity widens isotropic-nematic biphasic region; simple theory shows even without chiral synchronization, size dispersity alone can drive chirality transfer; marginal flexibility (persistence length LP ≈ 240d0) is key — rods are neither rigid nor semiflexible
nodes.scientific_questions: Can this entropy-driven chirality be controlled by tuning polydispersity precisely? How does the cholesteric pitch depend on the exact polydispersity distribution shape? Does the phenomenon persist in 2D confinement or on curved surfaces?

nodes.label: Achiral bead-spring rods with marginal backbone flexibility
Shear Response of Ionizable Polymer Melts at the Crossover from Ionomers to Polyelectrolytes
nodes.full_title: Shear Response of Ionizable Polymer Melts at the Crossover from Ionomers to Polyelectrolytes
nodes.authors: Shalika Meedin, Gary S. Grest, Dvora Perahia
nodes.materials_raw: Polystyrene randomly sulfonated with SO₃⁻ fractions f = 0.20 and 0.35; pristine and THF-swollen polyelectrolyte melts; ionomer-to-polyelectrolyte transition regime
nodes.methods_raw: Fully atomistic molecular dynamics simulations, nonequilibrium MD under shear, viscosity analysis, ionic cluster morphology tracking
nodes.key_findings: Nonlinear shear response reflects ionic domain evolution; at f = 0.20 (ionomer regime): ionic clusters first fracture into smaller clusters → split into individual ionic groups → eventually reform; at f = 0.35 (polyelectrolyte regime): clusters morph in shape but do not break under high shear; at very high shear rates, all chains stretch and recoil rapidly; at reduced shear rates, some chains stretch while others remain unaffected (heterogeneous response); macroscopically: elastic response → nonlinear shear stress overshoot → steady state; THF swelling increases distance between ionomeric microstructures; ionic cluster shape, size, and continuity directly affect nonlinear shear response
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the critical shear rate for cluster fracture vs morphing transition? How does the shear-induced cluster reformation kinetics compare with quiescent relaxation? Can the ionomer-to-polyelectrolyte crossover be tuned in real-time during processing?

nodes.label: Polystyrene randomly sulfonated with SO₃⁻ fractions f = 0.20
Heterogeneous Local Dynamics in Mussel-Inspired Elastomers
nodes.full_title: Heterogeneous Local Dynamics in Mussel-Inspired Elastomers
nodes.authors: Marianna Spyridakou, Elpida Iliopoulou, Katerina Peponaki, Stelios Alexandris, Emmanouela Filippidi, George Floudas
nodes.materials_raw: Mussel-inspired elastomers with iron-catechol complexes; PEG-based networks with covalent + coordination cross-links; PPO380-DAB-CAT-Fe and PPO640-DAB-CAT-Fe networks; PEG2000-DAB-CAT-Fe networks
nodes.methods_raw: DSC, temperature-modulated DSC (TM-DSC), broadband dielectric spectroscopy (BDS, temperature + pressure dependent), SAXS, Havriliak-Negami analysis
nodes.key_findings: Heterogeneous segmental dynamics within single broad Tg; two distinct dynamics reflect relaxation of segments in vicinity vs further from cross-linked DAB units; iron-catechol coordination bonds increase Tg and broaden Tg range; SAXS reveals ionomer-like peaks with iron-rich domain correlation distances of 4–12 nm depending on network topology; different topologies have different fragilities → tend to form stronger glasses with increasing network complexity; ionic conductivity influenced by segmental dynamics and increased Tg; features (reduced Δcp at Tg, "strong" dynamic behavior) also found in vitrimers — similarities between mussel-inspired networks and vitrimer networks discussed; pressure-dependent BDS reveals distinct apparent activation volumes for the two segmental relaxations
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the exact nature of the iron-catechol coordination geometry and its dynamics under stress? Can the heterogeneous dynamics be tuned by varying Fe³⁺ concentration? How do these networks perform under cyclic loading (fatigue)?

nodes.label: Theory of Inhomogeneous Multicomponent Polymer Systems
nodes.full_title: Theory of Inhomogeneous Multicomponent Polymer Systems
nodes.authors: K. M. Hong, J. Noolandi
nodes.materials_raw: Multicomponent polymer systems: homopolymers, block copolymers, monomers, solvents
nodes.methods_raw: Functional integral representation of partition function, saddle-function method optimization, gradient expansion for interaction energy, Cahn-Hilliard theory comparison, numerical binodal curve calculation
nodes.key_findings: New theory for inhomogeneous multicomponent systems starting from functional integral representation of partition function; determines interfacial properties and microdomain structures of homopolymer/block copolymer/monomer/solvent combinations; reduces to Cahn-Hilliard theory when no polymers present; saddle-function method optimization with constraints of no volume change on mixing and constant particle numbers → new mean-field equations; "kinetic terms" (involving inverse degree of polymerization) explicitly absent from mean-field equations when Flory-Huggins free energy used as reference; avoids ad hoc vanishing compressibility term used in Helfand's approach; detailed numerical results for homopolymer-solvent system: binodal curves, interfacial profiles, interfacial tensions as function of molecular weight and interaction parameter; framework extendable to block copolymer systems
nodes.scientific_questions: How does this theory compare quantitatively with self-consistent field theory (SCFT) for block copolymer morphologies? Can the framework handle polydisperse polymer mixtures? How do the predictions compare with modern computational polymer field theories?

nodes.label: Functional integral representation of partition fu
Generalized Entropy Theory of Glass Formation in Graft Polymer Melts Having Variable Grafting Densit
nodes.full_title: Generalized Entropy Theory of Glass Formation in Graft Polymer Melts Having Variable Grafting Density
nodes.authors: Qi-Lu Yuan, Jack F. Douglas, Wen-Sheng Xu
nodes.materials_raw: Graft polymer melts (comb and bottlebrush polymers); variable grafting density; flexible backbone + rigid side chains vs rigid backbone + flexible side chains
nodes.methods_raw: Generalized entropy theory (GET), coarse-grained molecular dynamics simulations, packing frustration analysis, fragility analysis
nodes.key_findings: GET extended to graft polymer melts with variable grafting density; polymers with flexible backbone + rigid side chains exhibit opposite trends in glass formation characteristics vs other polymer classes when grafting density varies; relative rigidities of backbone and side chains are key factor governing glass formation; GET predictions consistent with MD simulations of coarse-grained graft polymer melts; GET offers insights into grafting density dependence of Tg and fragility (m) from packing frustration perspective; universal relations predicted between change in extent of collective motion at Tg (relative to Arrhenius regime) and both Tg and m; bottlebrush polymers show large effective persistence length and reduced entanglement density → lower entanglement plateau modulus and lower zero-shear viscosity than linear analogues
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the GET predictions be experimentally validated with well-controlled graft polymer synthesis? How does grafting density affect crystallization in graft polymers? What is the optimal grafting density for specific applications (e.g., supersoft elastomers, pressure sensors)?

nodes.label: flexible backbone + rigid side chains vs rigid backbone + fl
Anti-Creep Adhesive Tapes via Trapped-Entanglement-Regulated Topological Networks
nodes.full_title: Anti-Creep Adhesive Tapes via Trapped-Entanglement-Regulated Topological Networks
nodes.authors: Le Yao, Yi Huang, Guoqing Chen, Ziqian Zhou, He Zhu, Qi Zhang, Shiping Zhu
nodes.institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
nodes.materials_raw: Ionogel-based adhesive tapes; trapped-entanglement-dominated polymer networks; ionic liquid (IL) mediated ionic interactions; three architectures: linear, covalent-crosslinked, trapped-entanglement-dominated
nodes.methods_raw: Lap joint shear testing, creep rate measurement, polymer network topology design, ionic liquid selection, rheological characterization
nodes.key_findings: Trapped-entanglement-dominated networks achieve three-orders-of-magnitude reduction in creep rate while maintaining strong interfacial adhesion; long-chain entanglements + sparse crosslinks enable efficient elastic energy storage without sacrificing tackiness; resolves trade-off between dynamic dissipation (needed for adhesion) and permanent mechanical integrity (needed for creep resistance); linear/dangling-dominated networks: prone to excessive deformation → cohesive failure via chain reptation/disentanglement; covalent-crosslink-dominated networks: improved creep resistance but compromised microscopic interfacial contact → adhesion failure; entanglement-stabilized networks: entanglements act as sliding anchors during deformation while preserving chain mobility for surface wetting; topology-driven approach decouples cohesion and adhesion requirements; scalable synthesis demonstrated
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the optimal entanglement-to-crosslink ratio for maximum creep resistance without losing adhesion? How does the creep rate scale with entanglement density? Can this approach be extended to underwater adhesion or extreme temperature applications?

nodes.label: The Process-Directed Self-Assembly of Block Copolymer Particles
nodes.full_title: The Process-Directed Self-Assembly of Block Copolymer Particles
nodes.authors: Yanyan Zhu, Changhang Huang, Liangshun Zhang, David Andelman, Xingkun Man
nodes.materials_raw: Block copolymer (BCP) particles in poor solvent; AB diblock copolymers
nodes.methods_raw: Dynamic self-consistent field theory (DSCFT), temperature regulation (Flory-Huggins parameter χAB), solvent selectivity variation, kinetic path analysis
nodes.key_findings: Process-directed self-assembly of BCPs in poor solvent leads to striped ellipsoids, onion-like particles, and double-spiral lamellar particles; reversible shape transition between onion-like and striped ellipsoidal particles by regulating temperature (χAB) and solvent selectivity; kinetic path: onion-like → double-spiral lamellar → onion-like demonstrated; formation of striped ellipsoids requires changing intermediate bi-continuous structure into layered one; onion-like particle formation characterized by two-stage microphase separation: first induced by solvent preference, second controlled by thermodynamics; effective way of tailoring BCP nanoparticle structure for sensors, smart coatings, drug delivery
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the process-directed approach produce BCP particles with controlled chirality? How do the shape transitions depend on the BCP molecular weight and composition? What is the kinetics of the two-stage microphase separation?

nodes.label: Torsional Polymer Chains: Flexibility Models, Conformational Entropy, and Uniaxial−Biaxial Nematic P
nodes.full_title: Torsional Polymer Chains: Flexibility Models, Conformational Entropy, and Uniaxial−Biaxial Nematic Phase Transition
nodes.authors: Jiayi Huang, Artem M. Rumyantsev
nodes.materials_raw: Conjugated and ladder-like polymers with ribbon-like shape; torsional polymer chains; poly(3-hexylthiophene) and similar conjugated polymers
nodes.methods_raw: Two models: (i) torsional persistent chain (continuous twistability) and (ii) torsional freely jointed chain (twistability at hinges); Lifshitz conformational entropy derivation; 2D Maier-Saupe theory for uniaxial-biaxial nematic transition; analytical solutions in weak/strong ordering limits
nodes.key_findings: Ribbon-like conjugated polymers have additional complexity beyond standard bending models — backbone torsion (twisting) enables biaxial nematic phases; torsional persistent chain: analytical expression for Lifshitz conformational entropy derived; torsional freely jointed chain: rigid board-like segments connected by point-like hinges; both models show biaxial ordering via continuous second-order phase transition (consistent with symmetry considerations); biaxial order parameter derived as function of interaction strength; torsional persistent chains require π²/3 times stronger interactions than freely jointed counterparts for transition; torsional persistent chains exhibit weaker biaxial ordering; torsional persistent chain = limiting case of ribbon-like chain model with infinite bending rigidity
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the torsional models predict the exact biaxial nematic transition temperature for specific conjugated polymers? How does backbone torsion affect charge transport in conjugated polymers? Can biaxial nematic order be exploited for anisotropic optoelectronic properties?

nodes.label: Conjugated and ladder-like polymers with ribbon-like shape
Two models:  torsional persistent chain  and  tors
analytical solutions in weak/strong ordering limit
Curvature-Corrected Surface Tension Governs Nucleation in Molecular Systems
nodes.full_title: Curvature-Corrected Surface Tension Governs Nucleation in Molecular Systems
nodes.journal: Journal of the American Chemical Society (2025, DOI: 10.1021/jacs.5c14775)
nodes.authors: Bin Chen, Ngoc My Nhi Nguyen, Kirsten K. Johnson
nodes.materials_raw: Water models (SPC, SPC/E, TIP4P, TIP4P/2005); molecular nucleation systems
nodes.methods_raw: Aggregation-volume-bias Monte Carlo (AVBMC) simulations, finite-difference free energy analyses (Δ²G and Δ³G), Tolman length extraction, classical nucleation theory (CNT) comparison
nodes.key_findings: Simulation-based framework combining AVBMC with finite-difference free energy analyses to extract planar surface tension and Tolman length from cluster-size-dependent nucleation free energies; consistently negative Tolman length (≈ −0.48 Å) found across all four water models; negative Tolman length increases nucleation barriers by 5–6 kBT relative to CNT predictions; excellent agreement with experimental measurements; resolves long-standing discrepancy between theory and experiment; curvature-dependent surface tension provides unifying description of nucleation barriers; general, predictive, and quantitative framework for nucleation in molecular systems; implications for atmospheric processes, biomolecular assembly, materials design
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the framework be extended to multicomponent nucleation (e.g., salt aerosols)? How does the Tolman length depend on temperature and molecular structure? Can the approach predict nucleation rates quantitatively for industrial crystallization processes?

nodes.label: Interfacial Failure in Graft Block Copolymer-Reinforced Polymer Blends
nodes.full_title: Interfacial Failure in Graft Block Copolymer-Reinforced Polymer Blends
nodes.authors: Ashutosh K. Nehete, Frank S. Bates, Kevin D. Dorfman
nodes.institution: University of Minnesota (Bates and Dorfman groups)
nodes.materials_raw: AB graft copolymers (A_M(B_N)_n) adsorbed at immiscible glassy A/B homopolymer interfaces; ternary systems
nodes.methods_raw: Coarse-grained molecular dynamics simulations (Hsu-Kremer model for glassy polymers), uniaxial elongation, toughness analysis, entanglement counting
nodes.key_findings: Graft copolymers serve as universal compatibilizers for immiscible polymer blends; enhanced interfacial adhesion depends on molecular architecture; toughness data explained by model for removal of interfacial crossings reflecting more facile reptation of grafts relative to trapped entanglements constraining backbone; toughness approximately linear with geometric mean of total backbone and graft entanglements prior to elongation; direct connection established between macroscopic toughness and microscopic entanglement network; graft architectures different from linear copolymers: combination of free-end entanglements on graft side + constrained backbone segments → distinctly different interfacial failure mechanism; graft copolymers have lower tendency to micellize than linear counterparts → superior performance at comparable loading
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the optimal graft density and graft length for maximum toughness? How does the graft copolymer architecture affect the craze formation at the interface? Can the model predict the critical graft copolymer loading for percolation of entanglements?

nodes.label: AB graft copolymers _n) adsorbed at immiscible glassy A/B ho
University of Minnesota (Bates and Dorfman groups)
Revealing Hidden Length by Force: Decoupling Modulus and Toughness in Network Gels
nodes.full_title: Revealing Hidden Length by Force: Decoupling Modulus and Toughness in Network Gels
nodes.authors: Shakkeeb Thazhathethil, Xiaoran Hu
nodes.materials_raw: Bicyclic cyclobutane mechanophore-containing polymer networks; double-network (DN) gels; reactive strand extension (RSE) systems; P5 and P12 copolymers (5 and 12 atoms in fused ring)
nodes.methods_raw: Single-molecule force spectroscopy (SMFS), AFM, radical copolymerization, hierarchical study from single molecules to networks
nodes.key_findings: Reactive strand extension (RSE) decouples toughness from modulus in polymer networks; force-coupled [2+2] cycloelimination in bicyclic cyclobutane mechanophores releases "hidden" stored length (~5.9 Å per cycloelimination for P5, ~15.2 Å for P12); single-chain stretching at ~1.5–2.0 nN reveals reproducible plateaus where stored length is released (~44% of total contour length); RSE enables more strands to engage synergistically at crack tip to resist fracture; by time RSE strand breaks, it absorbs and dissipates more energy through force-coupled cycloeliminations than analogous strand without RSE; improves toughness at nominal breaking point without affecting low-strain modulus; hierarchical study: molecular SMFS → network mechanics
nodes.scientific_questions: Can RSE be combined with other toughening strategies (e.g., sacrificial bonds) for synergistic enhancement? What is the fatigue life of RSE-containing networks under cyclic loading? How does the mechanophore density affect the toughness-modulus decoupling?

nodes.label: hierarchical study from single molecules to networ
Coherent State Field Theory for Reversible Gelation of Star Polymers
nodes.full_title: Coherent State Field Theory for Reversible Gelation of Star Polymers
nodes.authors: Daniel L. Vigil, Glenn H. Fredrickson, Jian Qin
nodes.materials_raw: End-functional f-arm star polymers; reversible gel systems; associative polymer stars in implicit solvent
nodes.methods_raw: Coherent state field theory (CST), mean-field analysis, Gaussian fluctuation analysis, loop density operator, Flory-Stockmayer (FS) theory comparison
nodes.key_findings: CST framework for reversible gelation of end-functional f-arm stars; implicitly enumerates all cross-linking patterns with correct statistical weight for each cluster type; at mean-field level: CST produces only tree-like clusters → recovers all essential predictions of Flory-Stockmayer (FS) theory; saddle-point (mean-field) condition reproduces FS condition for gelation; mean-field theory predicts two-phase region where sol and gel phases of different composition coexist; beyond mean-field: both tree and loop-like clusters present; loop density operator developed by considering spatial translation of finite clusters; field theory fully developed at Gaussian level of fluctuations; excluded volume interactions do not affect density of loops at Gaussian level but do affect bonding probability
nodes.scientific_questions: How do loop corrections change the gel point prediction quantitatively? Can the CST framework handle polydisperse star mixtures? What is the role of solvent quality in the gelation phase diagram?

nodes.label: associative polymer stars in implicit solvent
Effective Entanglement and Constraint Release in Deformed Polymer Melts
nodes.full_title: Effective Entanglement and Constraint Release in Deformed Polymer Melts
nodes.authors: Lin-Feng Wu, Long-Fei Mao, Zhe Wang
nodes.materials_raw: Entangled polymer melts; polystyrene melt (experimental validation)
nodes.methods_raw: Molecular dynamics simulations, primitive path analysis, small-angle neutron scattering (SANS) experimental validation, tube model framework, constraint release analysis
nodes.key_findings: Effective entanglement identified that governs chain conformation and rheological response of deformed entangled polymers; distribution of effective entanglements gives natural estimation of mean survival tube; constraint release (CR) caused by motion of effective entanglements well describes relaxation process after step elongation within tube model framework; relation between dynamics of effective entanglements and orientation/stretch/retraction of chain segments examined; SANS experiments on elongated polystyrene melt confirm: "Rouse ramp" observed in decay of effective entanglements → key feature of tube relaxation induced by constraint release; effective entanglement concept bridges gap between microscopic entanglement picture and macroscopic rheological response
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the effective entanglement concept extend to bidisperse blends? Can the CR mechanism explain the extensional stress overshoot quantitatively? What is the relationship between effective entanglement dynamics and convective constraint release in the generalized tube model?

nodes.label: A New Approach to the Theory of Relaxing Polymeric Media
nodes.full_title: A New Approach to the Theory of Relaxing Polymeric Media
nodes.authors: M. S. Green, A. V. Tobolsky
nodes.materials_raw: Polymeric networks (rubber-like materials); relaxing polymeric media
nodes.methods_raw: Molecular theory of relaxing media; kinetic theory of elasticity extended to non-isotropic networks; thermodynamic analysis (second law for dissipation); tensor stress-strain-time relations
nodes.key_findings: Molecular theory gives expression for stress in terms of strain history; strain history produces distribution of internal strains characterized by limited number of internal strain parameters; second law of thermodynamics used to define dissipation at constant temperature; explicit expressions for dissipation for any strain history obtained; relaxation during straining causes essential reorganization of structure → cause of dissipation; kinetic theory of elasticity extended to non-isotropic polymeric networks; tensor expression for stress-strain-time relations developed; materials characterized by equal rates of breaking and reforming of bonds constituting network chains; foundational work for understanding viscoelastic behavior of polymer networks
nodes.scientific_questions: How does this classical framework compare with modern reptation/tube models? Can the internal strain parameters be directly related to molecular entanglement density? How has this theory influenced subsequent developments in polymer rheology?

nodes.label: kinetic theory of elasticity extended to non-isotr
Stretch-Induced Structure Evolution of Poly(vinyl alcohol)−Glycerol Gel Films: An In Situ Synchrotro
nodes.full_title: Stretch-Induced Structure Evolution of Poly(vinyl alcohol)−Glycerol Gel Films: An In Situ Synchrotron Radiation X-ray Scattering Study
nodes.authors: Zheng Huang, Wenyi Li, Caixia Wan, Liguang Xu, Zhengli Xu, Dongmin Jia, Mengyu Xie, Yifan Jia, Jungen Chen, Liangbin Li
nodes.institution: University of Science and Technology of China (USTC
nodes.materials_raw: Poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) gel films containing glycerol; PVA nanofibril films
nodes.methods_raw: In situ synchrotron radiation SAXS/WAXS during uniaxial stretching, temperature-dependent stretching, biaxial stretching + solvent extraction + drying
nodes.key_findings: Uniaxial stretching triggers melting-recrystallization of PVA lamellar crystals and formation of nanofibrils; key factors for structural evolution change with stretching temperature: at low temperature — crystal destruction dominated by stress, reconstructed nanofibrils withstand stress and cause feedback slowing crystal destruction; at high temperature — crystal destruction dominated by melting, crystallinity decreases rapidly under stress, while stretch-induced recrystallization and nanofibril reconstruction more readily achieved; long period of lamellar crystals and nanofibrils increases with stretching temperature; PVA porous nanofibril films with favorable nanofiber networks and porous structures obtained by biaxial stretching + solvent extraction + drying; high-temperature stretching is efficient, industrially available method for nanofibril film preparation (vs electrospinning which is costly and low-efficiency)
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the optimal stretching temperature for maximum nanofibril alignment? How does glycerol content affect the stretching-induced crystallization kinetics? Can the approach be scaled to continuous roll-to-roll manufacturing?

nodes.label: Effect of Hydrogen Bonding on the Enthalpy of Mixing and the Composition Dependence of the Glass Tra
nodes.full_title: Effect of Hydrogen Bonding on the Enthalpy of Mixing and the Composition Dependence of the Glass Transition Temperature in Polymer Blends
nodes.authors: Paul C. Painter, John F. Graf, Michael M. Coleman
nodes.institution: The Pennsylvania State University (Department of Materials Science and Engineeri
nodes.materials_raw: Miscible polymer blends with strong specific interactions (hydrogen bonds); novolac resins with isotactic/syndiotactic poly(methyl methacrylate) (i-PMMA/s-PMMA); electron donor-acceptor complex blends
nodes.methods_raw: Classical thermodynamic theory, self-association/interassociation modeling, Couchman-Karasz equation analysis, enthalpy of mixing calculations
nodes.key_findings: Classical thermodynamic theory for composition dependence of Tg in miscible polymer blends with strong specific interactions; Tg equation separated into three terms: nonspecific interaction component, heat of mixing in liquid-state term, and term accounting for temperature dependence of specific heat due to self-association; third term assumed to dominate overall temperature dependence of specific heat; heat of mixing in systems with strong specific interactions can be positive (not necessarily negative) depending on balance between self-association and interassociation (association between different components); positive ΔHm arises from self-association of at least one pure component → mixing occurs because of entropy gain; explains unusual Tg variations with composition observed for blends with strong specific interactions (Kwei's S-shaped curves, large positive deviations); Couchman-Karasz equations successfully applied to some blends but fail for systems with strong specific interactions
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the self-association/interassociation model predict phase behavior (LCST/UCST) in hydrogen-bonded blends? How does the theory extend to blends with multiple types of specific interactions? What is the role of chain connectivity in modifying the free energy of association?

nodes.label: Miscible polymer blends with strong specific interactions
Speed-Induced Extensibility Elastomers with Good Resilience and High Toughness
nodes.full_title: Speed-Induced Extensibility Elastomers with Good Resilience and High Toughness
nodes.authors: Haiming Chen, J. Justin Koh, Chuanjiang Long, Siqi Liu, Huihui Shi, Jiakang Min, Lili Zhou, Chaobin He
nodes.materials_raw: Thermoplastic elastomers from immiscible PDMS + PPG; reversible hydrogen bonds for self-healing
nodes.methods_raw: Mechanical testing at varying stretching speeds, phase morphology characterization, self-healing testing
nodes.key_findings: Speed-induced extensibility (SIE): elongation at break increases with stretching speed (counter to normal trend); >9000% elongation at break at 70 min⁻¹ stretching speed; notch resistance: strain ~2000% and fracture energy ~53,600 J/m² for notched specimen (surpasses most PDMS-based elastomers); toughness enhanced 6.4× by increasing stretching speed from 2 to 70 min⁻¹; microphase re-separation of PDMS and PPG + entropy elasticity → good elastic recovery (~98%); reversible hydrogen bonds → autonomous self-healing (efficiency ~95%); SIE mechanism: at higher speed, microphase separation kinetics favor formation of more effective stress-bearing domains before fracture
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the molecular mechanism of SIE — is it related to strain-induced crystallization or phase morphology evolution? How does the SIE effect depend on the PDMS/PPG composition ratio? Can SIE be observed in other immiscible polymer blend systems?

nodes.label: Small Angle X-ray Scattering for Nanoparticle Research
nodes.full_title: Small Angle X-ray Scattering for Nanoparticle Research
nodes.authors: Tao Li, Andrew J. Senesi, Byeongdu Lee
nodes.materials_raw: Nanoparticle systems; colloidal nanoparticles; hierarchical nanostructures
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review: SAXS theory (form factor, structure factor, correlation functions), data measurement/processing, transmission and grazing incidence (GISAXS) geometries, combination with simulations and other characterization tools
nodes.key_findings: SAXS unique in examining materials in real time under realistic sample environments at nanometer and angstrom length scales; form factor analysis: particle size, size distribution, shape determination; structure factor analysis: pair distribution function, crystalline assemblies, isothermal compressibility; correlation functions for arbitrary shaped and fractal particles; GISAXS for surface and thin film nanostructures; Distorted Wave Born Approximation (DWBA) for 2D/3D structure analysis; applications: in situ nanoparticle synthesis monitoring, nanoparticle assembly, operando studies of catalysts and energy storage materials; combination with atomistic and total scattering simulations
nodes.scientific_questions: Can machine learning accelerate SAXS data analysis and model fitting? How does SAXS compare with cryo-EM for nanoparticle structure determination? What are the time resolution limits for in situ SAXS studies of fast nucleation events?

nodes.label: combination with simulations and other characteriz
Lyotropic Microphase Separation in Aqueous Solutions of Double Sulfobetaine Diblock Copolymers Induc
nodes.full_title: Lyotropic Microphase Separation in Aqueous Solutions of Double Sulfobetaine Diblock Copolymers Induced by Charge Separation Length Mismatch
nodes.authors: Yuji Higaki, Yasuhiro Eguchi, Takumi Masuda, Yuri Mitsunobu
nodes.materials_raw: Double hydrophilic diblock copolymer PSB2-b-PSB4 (two distinct polysulfobetaine chains with different charge separation lengths); poly(2-(N-2-methacryloyloxyethyl-N,N-dimethyl)ammonioethanesulfonate) (PSB2) and poly(4-(...ammoniobutanesulfonate) (PSB4)
nodes.methods_raw: RAFT polymerization, fractional precipitation, phase behavior study (polymer concentration + block composition), microphase separation characterization
nodes.key_findings: Lyotropic microphase separation in aqueous solutions of PSB2-b-PSB4 induced by charge separation length (CSL) mismatch between identical sulfobetaines; structural similarity of zwitterions induces increased miscibility, lattice distortion, and morphological change; low Flory-Huggins χ for PSB2/PSB4, poor hydrophilicity of PSB2, and weak PSB2-PSB4 interactions rationalize behavior; unique mesoscale aqueous molecular assemblies produced by microphase separation of double-hydrophilic BCPs with high chemical symmetry; promising as novel aqueous compartments for molecular reservoirs; analogous to biological liquid-liquid phase separation (membraneless microdroplets in cytoplasm)
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the CSL mismatch strategy be generalized to other zwitterionic block copolymers? What is the kinetics of microcompartment formation and dissolution? Can these aqueous compartments encapsulate and release functional molecules on demand?

nodes.label: Small-Angle X-ray Scattering of Polymer Systems
nodes.full_title: Small-Angle X-ray Scattering of Polymer Systems
nodes.journal: Handbook of Polymer Synthesis, Characterization, and Processing (2013, Wiley)
nodes.authors: Carlos A. Avila-Orta, Francisco J. Medellín-Rodríguez
nodes.materials_raw: Semicrystalline thermoplastic polymers (PE, iPP, PET, polyamides); lamellar structures; single crystals, spherulites, shish kebabs
nodes.methods_raw: Review of SAXS fundamentals for polymer morphology: 1D data analysis in reciprocal space and real space, lamellar structure determination, unit cell analysis
nodes.key_findings: Lamellar structure (alternating crystalline/amorphous layers) dominates semicrystalline polymer morphology at nanoscale; SAXS useful for determining crystalline and amorphous thicknesses, distributions, and crystal perfection; chain-folded lamellae: thickness 5–20 nm, widths in micrometer range; unit cells: 3D crystal formed by hypothetical smaller crystals with sizes a, b, c in angstrom range; polymer morphology hierarchical: unit cells (Å) → lamellae (nm) → spherulites/shish kebabs (μm); single crystals from dilute solutions; spherulites from glassy/molten state; shish kebabs from stressed conditions; chapter provides fundamentals of polymer morphology, SAXS analysis methods, and application examples
nodes.scientific_questions: How can SAXS be combined with in situ rheology for real-time crystallization monitoring? What are the limitations of SAXS for studying amorphous polymers? How does the SAXS analysis change for highly oriented (fiber) samples?

nodes.label: A Review of Small-Angle Scattering Models for Random Segmented Poly(ether-urethane) Copolymers
nodes.full_title: A Review of Small-Angle Scattering Models for Random Segmented Poly(ether-urethane) Copolymers
nodes.authors: P. R. Laity, J. E. Taylor, S. S. Wong, P. Khunkamchoo, K. Norris, M. Cable, G. T. Andrews, A. F. Johnson, R. E. Cameron
nodes.materials_raw: Poly(ether-urethane) copolymers; hard segment + soft segment microphase-separated systems; commercial PU formulations
nodes.methods_raw: SAXS data fitting with multiple scattering models; comparison of globular models (distorted 1D lattice, Percus-Yevick), weak segregation model, ideal lamellar model, Teubner-Strey microemulsion model; AFM/TEM comparison
nodes.key_findings: Best fits with 'globular' scattering models based on distorted 1D lattice or Percus-Yevick model of liquid structure; consistent with AFM/TEM studies showing discrete globular or elongated cylindrical microdomains; weak segregation model rejected based on deviations in Porod region; ideal lamellar model ruled out (unable to reproduce observed peak width); Teubner-Strey microemulsion model unable to fit low-q data (especially strained samples) but more successful at elevated temperatures → morphologies may become more microemulsion-like during heating; reasons discussed why meandering elongated or finite lamellar microdomains may behave more like globular scattering bodies
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the SAXS model selection affect the quantitative determination of domain sizes? Can the globular model be extended to describe strain-dependent morphological changes? What is the physical origin of the apparent microemulsion-like behavior at elevated temperatures?

nodes.label: hard segment + soft segment microphase-separated systems
Microstructure and Segmental Dynamics of Polyurea under Uniaxial Deformation
nodes.full_title: Microstructure and Segmental Dynamics of Polyurea under Uniaxial Deformation
nodes.authors: Taeyi Choi, Daniel Fragiadakis, C. Michael Roland, James Runt
nodes.materials_raw: Polyurea from modified MDI (Isonate 143 L) + poly(tetramethylene oxide-di-p-aminobenzoate) (Versalink P1000); 4:1 weight ratio; PTMO-amine MW ~1238 g/mol
nodes.methods_raw: Wide-angle and small-angle X-ray scattering (WAXS/SAXS), broadband dielectric spectroscopy (BDS), stress-strain measurements (Instron), uniaxial deformation through yield point to failure
nodes.key_findings: Uniaxial deformation significantly perturbs phase-separated microstructure and chain orientation; considerable slowing down and broadening of polyurea soft phase segmental relaxation under strain; WAXS/SAXS reveal amorphous segment and hard domain orientation changes; polyurea used as acoustic material on submarines and explored for impact/ballistic protection; extensive hydrogen bonding → exceptional mechanical "toughness"; rapid reaction (gel times in minutes) → processing independent of ambient conditions; strain-induced changes in structure and dynamics quantified and related to strain history
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the segmental relaxation broadening relate to energy absorption mechanisms? Can the strain-induced orientation be recovered upon unloading? What is the role of hydrogen bonding reorganization during deformation?

nodes.label: uniaxial deformation through yield point to failur
Piezoionic Elastomers by Phase and Interface Engineering for High-Performance Energy-Harvesting Iono
nodes.full_title: Piezoionic Elastomers by Phase and Interface Engineering for High-Performance Energy-Harvesting Ionotronics
nodes.authors: Weiyan Zhu, Baohu Wu, Zhouyue Lei, Peiyi Wu
nodes.materials_raw: PVDF-HFP/EMIM TFSI ionogel + ionic plastic crystal (IPC, C₂mpyr TFSI); piezoionic elastomers with varying IPC content (30–67 wt%)
nodes.methods_raw: AFM, in situ XRD, ¹⁹F NMR, Raman spectroscopy, isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC), piezoionic energy harvesting testing
nodes.key_findings: Piezoionic elastomers fabricated by incorporating IPC into PVDF-HFP/EMIM TFSI ionogel; optimized IPC mass concentration of 55 wt%; phase and interface engineering for high-performance piezoionic energy harvesting; IPC provides intermediate phase between ionogel and electrode; ITC used to characterize thermodynamic interactions between components; ¹⁹F NMR and Raman for ion coordination analysis; in situ XRD for structural changes during operation
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the exact mechanism of the IPC intermediate phase in enhancing piezoionic response? How does the IPC crystal structure affect ion transport under stress? Can the piezoionic elastomer maintain performance under cyclic loading?

nodes.label: SAXS Measurements of the Interface in Polyacrylate and Epoxy Interpenetrating Networks with Fractal
nodes.full_title: SAXS Measurements of the Interface in Polyacrylate and Epoxy Interpenetrating Networks with Fractal Geometry
nodes.authors: Susheng Tan, Donghua Zhang, Enle Zhou
nodes.materials_raw: Polyacrylate/epoxy interpenetrating polymer networks (IPNs); two-component IPN systems
nodes.methods_raw: SAXS with Ruland's theory for interface thickness determination; fractal dimension analysis; Porod's law comparison
nodes.key_findings: Interface thickness in polyacrylate/epoxy IPNs determined by SAXS using Ruland's theory; interface found to be nonexistent (sharp boundary) at various compositions and synthesis conditions; interface roughness described by fractal dimension D, slightly varying with composition and synthesis conditions; surface fractals confirmed over length scales 0.02–0.4 Å⁻¹; fractal treatment of interface reasonably explained differences between Porod's law and experimental data; insight gained into interactions between different segments at the interface; third phase (interphase) in IPN systems due to interpenetration between networks — more interpenetration → thicker interphase
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the fractal dimension D correlate with the mechanical properties of the IPN? Can SAXS distinguish between interpenetration and chemical grafting at the interface? What controls the sharpness of the interface in IPN systems?

nodes.label: A Multiscale Investigation on the Mechanism of Shape Recovery for IPDI to PPDI Hard Segment Substitu
nodes.full_title: A Multiscale Investigation on the Mechanism of Shape Recovery for IPDI to PPDI Hard Segment Substitution in Polyurethane
nodes.authors: Wei Xu, Ruoyu Zhang, Wei Liu, Jin Zhu, Xia Dong, Hongxia Guo, Guo-Hua Hu
nodes.institution: Ningbo Institute of Material Technology and Engineering
nodes.materials_raw: Shape memory thermoplastic polyurethane (SMTPU); IPDI (isophorone diisocyanate) gradually substituted for PPDI (1,4-phenylene diisocyanate) in hard segments
nodes.methods_raw: Normal and dichroic FTIR, SAXS, single-molecule force spectroscopy (SMFS), mechanical testing
nodes.key_findings: Contradictory to traditional conclusion: degree of microphase separation decreases as IPDI content increases, yet macroscopic shape recoverability is largely improved; dichroic FTIR and SAXS reveal morphology change from lamellar-like to fibrillar-like hard phases (more stable under stretching); SMFS discovers IPDI increases polymer chain elasticity and endows hard phases with "elastic" behavior under stress; two factors explain high recoverability: (1) fibrillar morphology stability, (2) enhanced chain elasticity; IPDI-based SMTPU has excellent shape recoverability even after large strain deformation
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the IPDI/PPDI ratio affect the crystallinity of hard segments? Can SMFS be used to predict shape recovery ratio quantitatively? What is the molecular origin of the "elastic" hard phase behavior induced by IPDI?

nodes.label: Ningbo Institute of Material Technology and Engineering
Deformation Mechanism of Poly(3-alkylthiophene) Studied by In Situ X-ray Scattering and Texture Anal
nodes.full_title: Deformation Mechanism of Poly(3-alkylthiophene) Studied by In Situ X-ray Scattering and Texture Analysis
nodes.authors: Haiming Chen, Cui Su, Guangyu Shi, Xiaolu Li, Xiuqin Zhang, Guoming Liu, Dujin Wang
nodes.materials_raw: Poly(3-dodecylthiophene) (P3DDT) and poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT); conjugated polymers with anisotropic interchain interactions
nodes.methods_raw: In situ X-ray scattering during stretching, pole figure analysis, mechanical testing at different temperatures
nodes.key_findings: Modulus and fracture strength of P3DDT decrease with increasing stretching temperature; elongation at break sharply increases at 45°C; general deformation process consistent with traditional flexible crystalline polymer mechanism but with special features; well-defined yielding not detected even below main-chain Tg → corresponds to absence of well-defined lamellar-fibrillar transition (microscopic observation); P3DDT and P3HT show different orientation features at 30°C but similar c-axis orientation at 110°C; crystallographic slip proposed as main deformation mechanism at 30°C; stress-induced melting and recrystallization observed at 110°C; understanding deformation mechanism essential for flexible/stretchable electronic devices
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the alkyl side chain length affect the deformation mechanism transition temperature? Can the crystallographic slip mechanism be controlled to improve charge transport anisotropy? What is the role of π-π stacking in the deformation process?

nodes.label: conjugated polymers with anisotropic interchain interactions
In situ X-ray scattering during stretching
SAXS and USAXS on the High Brilliance Beamline at the ESRF
nodes.full_title: SAXS and USAXS on the High Brilliance Beamline at the ESRF
nodes.journal: Nuclear Instruments and Methods in Physics Research A 467–468 (2001) 1005–1009
nodes.authors: T. Narayanan, O. Diat, P. Boeseck
nodes.materials_raw: Various weakly scattering biological and soft matter samples
nodes.methods_raw: Time-resolved SAXS/USAXS on ID02 beamline; image-intensified CCD detector; Bonse-Hart USAXS setup; pinhole SAXS camera
nodes.key_findings: ID02 beamline at ESRF designed for time-resolved SAXS with highly monochromatic beam, low divergence, small cross-section; photon flux ~10¹³ photons/s at sample; time resolution: good scattering curve of weakly scattering biological sample in 0.1 s acquisition; for intense scatterers, longest camera length (10 m) accesses d-spacings up to 1 μm; high-resolution Bonse-Hart setup extends spatial range up to 5 μm; detector systems: multi-wire proportional gas-filled detector + image-intensified CCD camera (up to 10 frames/sec, 1024×1024 pixels, 14-bit); sequence of up to 125 frames at time resolution; exposure time < 1 ms possible for strongly scattering sample; typical q-range: 10⁻² to 10 nm⁻¹
nodes.scientific_questions: How has the beamline technology evolved since 2001 (e.g., with pixel detectors)? What are the current limits of time resolution for SAXS studies of fast kinetics? Can the USAXS setup be combined with rheology for in situ studies?

nodes.label: Various weakly scattering biological and soft matter samples
Ionogels Reinforced by Ionophobic Coordination
nodes.full_title: Ionogels Reinforced by Ionophobic Coordination
nodes.authors: Hongfei Huang, Lijie Sun, Yalin Zhang, Ruohan Zhang, Chang Chen, Wei Sun
nodes.materials_raw: Ionogels with ionophobic coordination reinforcement (ICR); ionic liquid-phobic microphase separation + lithium-ion coordination crosslinking; dual Tg design
nodes.methods_raw: Mechanical testing, self-healing testing, melt spinning, respiratory monitoring, wireless platform integration
nodes.key_findings: "Ionophobic Coordination Reinforcement" (ICR) strategy integrating ionic liquid-phobic microphase separation with lithium-ion coordination crosslinking; dual Tg: −60.27°C (soft phase mobility) + 55.33°C (hard phase strength); simultaneous high mechanical performance (6.4× tensile strength, 4× toughness, 35.6× Young's modulus increase) and efficient room-temperature self-healing; dynamic supramolecular architecture → exceptional melt-processability (melt spinning demonstrated); ionogel fibers exhibit enhanced humidity sensitivity and rapid response to respiratory moisture; wireless platform enables real-time non-invasive respiratory monitoring; intrinsic self-healing ensures long-term stability; ICR resolves trade-off between strength and self-healing in ionogels
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the lithium-ion coordination density affect the dual Tg behavior? Can the ICR strategy be extended to other ionic liquid systems? What is the long-term stability of the ionophobic microphase separation under cyclic mechanical loading?

nodes.label: Molecular Mobility, Ion Mobility, and Mobile Ion Concentration in Poly(ethylene oxide)-Based Polyure
nodes.full_title: Molecular Mobility, Ion Mobility, and Mobile Ion Concentration in Poly(ethylene oxide)-Based Polyurethane Ionomers
nodes.authors: Daniel Fragiadakis, Shichen Dou, Ralph H. Colby, James Runt
nodes.institution: The Pennsylvania State University (Materials Science and Engineering)
nodes.materials_raw: PEO-based polyurethane single-ion conductors (Li⁺ or Na⁺); amorphous single-phase ionomers with no ion clustering; anions covalently bound to polymer chains
nodes.methods_raw: Dielectric relaxation spectroscopy, electrode polarization model for mobile ion concentration/ion mobility separation, ion association model (unpaired ions + separated ion pairs + contact ion pairs)
nodes.key_findings: Physical model of electrode polarization employed to separately determine mobile ion concentration and ion mobility in single-ion conductors; conduction due exclusively to cation motion (anions fixed); model including unpaired ions, separated ion pairs, and contact ion pairs reconciles very small fraction of free ions with previous ion association studies; ion mobility strongly coupled to segmental mobility and controlled by Tg; conducting cations complexed with 4–6 ether oxygen atoms; amorphous single-phase materials with no ion clustering → more straightforward interpretation of conductivity parameters; fundamental understanding of ion transport in polymer electrolytes
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the ion association equilibrium shift with temperature and salt concentration? Can the electrode polarization model be extended to polymer electrolytes with mobile anions? What is the relationship between ion mobility and segmental relaxation time quantitatively?

nodes.label: electrode polarization model for mobile ion concen
The Pennsylvania State University (Materials Science and Engineering)
A Hyperelastic Hydrogel with an Ultralarge Reversible Biaxial Strain
nodes.full_title: A Hyperelastic Hydrogel with an Ultralarge Reversible Biaxial Strain
nodes.authors: Lili Chen, Zhekai Jin, Wenwen Feng, Lin Sun, Hao Xu, Chao Wang
nodes.materials_raw: Poly[2-(acryloyloxy)ethyl]trimethylammonium chloride (PAETC) hydrogels; pearl-necklace chain (PNC) conformations; polyelectrolyte hydrogels
nodes.methods_raw: AFM, cryo-TEM, 2D SAXS, in situ SAXS under strain, DFT calculations, mechanical testing (biaxial strain)
nodes.key_findings: Hyperelastic hydrogel with reversible pearl-necklace structure (PNC): beads connected by strings; subnanometric beads ( < 2 nm) efficiently unfold and refold under cyclic mechanical strains; hydrogel rapidly recovers after being stretched to > 10,000% areal strain; maximum deformation of PNCs falls between freely jointed chains (l ~ N^{1/2}) and coiled chains (l ~ N^{2/3}); monomer fraction in beads approximately constant ~ 0.8; in situ SAXS witnesses highly reversible unfolding/refolding of PNC structures; quick healing from minor mechanical damages (needle punctures, cuts); ideal for multifunctional pneumatic gripper materials: ultralarge gripping range + self-sensing + fast healing; AFM confirms PNC structure on mica substrate; cryo-TEM shows dense packing of PNC structures; 2D SAXS confirms isotropic network structure with bead distances 1.14–2.09 nm
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the exact chemical nature of the "beads" in the PNC structure? How does the PNC conformation depend on the ionic strength and counterion type? Can the PNC concept be applied to other polyelectrolyte systems?

nodes.label: A Close Look at the Local Structure of Functional Polymers: The Example of Poly(Vinylidene Fluoride)
nodes.full_title: A Close Look at the Local Structure of Functional Polymers: The Example of Poly(Vinylidene Fluoride)
nodes.authors: Henry J. Kantrow, Claire Welton, Neethu Thomas, Vincent Sarou-Kanian, Tomasz Pawlak, Natalie Stingelin, G. N. Manjunatha Reddy
nodes.materials_raw: Poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF); ferroelectric polymer; α, β, γ crystal polymorphs
nodes.methods_raw: Modern solid-state NMR spectroscopy, quantitative analysis of chain segment conformations, spatial distribution analysis
nodes.key_findings: Modern NMR enables quantitative analysis of complex PVDF solid-state structure; precise fractions of chain segments with different conformations revealed; spatial distributions of segments with respect to each other determined; significant population of short-range ordered chain segments identified, large fraction in close proximity to defects and disordered segments; different environments lead to different structural dynamics; simplistic crystalline/amorphous picture inadequate for functional polymers like PVDF; low-quality crystallites, local ordering, and structural/chemical defects at nanometer scale play critical roles; characterizing local order/disorder and their dynamics imperative for accurate description of functional polymer properties
nodes.scientific_questions: How do the short-range ordered segments contribute to the ferroelectric polarization? Can NMR distinguish between the β and γ polymorphs at the local level? What is the role of defects in the switching mechanism of PVDF?

nodes.label: quantitative analysis of chain segment conformatio
How to Characterize Supramolecular Polymers: A User Guide
nodes.full_title: How to Characterize Supramolecular Polymers: A User Guide
nodes.authors: Yuhang Sheng, Menglan Ma, Shijun Li, Li Shao, Bin Hua, Feihe Huang, Zibin Zhang
nodes.materials_raw: Supramolecular polymers (SPs); noncovalent interactions (π-π stacking, H-bonding, metal coordination, van der Waals, hydrophobic, host-guest)
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review: VC-NMR, VT-NMR, COSY, NOESY, ROESY, DOSY NMR; ESI, MALDI, APCI mass spectrometry; UV-vis, FL, CD, IR spectroscopy; DLS, SLS; SC-XRD, SAXS, WAXS; AFM, SEM, TEM, CLSM microscopy; SEC, VPO, TGA, DSC
nodes.key_findings: SPs are polymeric arrays connected by reversible, highly directional secondary interactions; dynamic reversibility, stimuli-responsiveness (temperature, pH, light, chemical environment), self-healing, controllable disassembly/reassembly; no single technique provides complete description — combination of complementary techniques required; NMR (VC, VT, COSY, NOESY, ROESY, DOSY) for thermodynamics, kinetics, and structure; mass spectrometry (ESI, MALDI, APCI) for molecular weight and architecture; spectroscopy (UV-vis, FL, CD, IR) for electronic/optical properties; scattering (DLS, SLS) for size and shape; X-ray (SC-XRD, SAXS, WAXS) for crystalline/amorphous structure; microscopy (AFM, SEM, TEM, CLSM) for morphology; traditional polymer methods (SEC, VPO, TGA, DSC) for molecular weight, thermal properties
nodes.scientific_questions: Can machine learning help integrate data from multiple characterization techniques for SPs? What are the key unmet needs in SP characterization? How can in situ characterization methods capture the dynamic nature of SPs in real-time?

nodes.label: Re-entry with Rate and Temperature of Brittle−Ductile Transition in Glassy Polymers
nodes.full_title: Re-entry with Rate and Temperature of Brittle−Ductile Transition in Glassy Polymers
nodes.authors: Asal Y. Siavoshani, Chaitanya Gupta, Ming-Chi Wang, Junpeng Wang, Shi-Qing Wang
nodes.materials_raw: Bisphenol A polycarbonate (bpA-PC), poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA), poly(ethylene terephthalate) (PET); cross-linked PMMA
nodes.methods_raw: Systematic mechanical testing (temperature, rate, geometry variation), cross-linking, BDT analysis, LDWO hypothesis comparison
nodes.key_findings: Nonmonotonic relationship between temperature/rate and polymer ductility: ductile polymer can turn brittle at higher temperatures; polymer ductile in fast extension becomes brittle in slow extension; specimen ductile when short but brittle when long; adequate cross-linking renders PMMA ductile at room temperature; macroscopic ductility driven by robust chain network facilitating stress transmission through interchain uncrossability; below BDT: chain network undergoes structural failure through chain pullout → preceding macroscopic yielding; above BDT: chain network remains intact → yielding and plastic deformation without catastrophic failure; findings significantly expand theoretical understanding of polymer ductility in glassy state; crazing sign of local structural breakdown but not necessarily precursor to brittle fracture
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the molecular origin of the nonmonotonic rate/temperature dependence? Can the BDT re-entry be predicted from molecular simulations? How does the chain network integrity evolve during the transition?

nodes.label: Impact of Chain Length on PCL Dielectric β-Relaxation: Decoupling the Roles of Chain Ends, Main Chai
nodes.full_title: Impact of Chain Length on PCL Dielectric β-Relaxation: Decoupling the Roles of Chain Ends, Main Chains, Entanglements, and Crystallinity
nodes.authors: Leire Sangroniz, Angel Alegría, Alejandro J. Müller
nodes.materials_raw: Polycaprolactone (PCL) with Mn range 2–173 kg/mol; semicrystalline biodegradable polymer
nodes.methods_raw: Broadband dielectric spectroscopy, systematic chain length variation, modeling of end group/main chain/crystallinity contributions
nodes.key_findings: First decoupling of chain length, chain ends, entanglements, and crystallinity effects on β relaxation in PCL; as chain length increases, amorphous main chain contribution altered due to entanglements affecting chain dynamics; strong correlation between local dynamics changes and rigid amorphous fraction in semicrystalline polymers; entanglements arise above ~3 kg/mol (Me estimated 1.6–3.9 kg/mol); Mc for η₀ ~ M^3.4 scaling falls in 3.1–7.8 kg/mol range; tie molecules form when chains span multiple crystalline lamellae; tools provided for using broadband dielectric spectroscopy to isolate complex chain parameters; PCL degradation primarily through random chain scission from hydrolysis of ester groups (3–4 years)
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the rigid amorphous fraction evolve with crystallization conditions? Can the decoupling approach be applied to other semicrystalline polymers? What is the relationship between entanglement density and β-relaxation time?

nodes.label: Flaw-Tolerant Fracture Enabled by Liquid-like Elasticity in Liquid Crystal Elastomers
nodes.full_title: Flaw-Tolerant Fracture Enabled by Liquid-like Elasticity in Liquid Crystal Elastomers
nodes.authors: Shunsuke Imai, Yuki Tokudome, Thanh-Tam Mai, Kenji Urayama
nodes.materials_raw: Polydomain liquid crystal elastomers (P-LCEs) with randomly oriented directors
nodes.methods_raw: Tensile tearing tests, notched specimen testing, strain homogenization analysis, comparison with conventional elastomers
nodes.key_findings: P-LCEs exhibit extraordinary in-plane liquid-like mechanical properties due to minimal energy cost of local nematic director rotation; during tensile tearing, notched P-LCEs demonstrate exceptional strain homogenization across specimen → effectively mitigates crack-tip strain concentration and suppresses crack-tip opening over broad range of stretching until polydomain-to-monodomain transition threshold; significantly delays macroscopic fracture; reduction in fracture strain caused by notching substantially smaller in P-LCEs vs conventional elastomers; strain homogenization plays critical role in enhancing fracture resistance; foundation for designing polymer-based soft materials with superior mechanical properties and fracture resistance
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the polydomain texture evolve during crack propagation? Can the flaw-tolerance be tuned by controlling the domain size? What is the fatigue life of P-LCEs under cyclic loading?

nodes.label: Anti-Fatigue-Fracture Hydrogels
nodes.full_title: Anti-Fatigue-Fracture Hydrogels
nodes.authors: Shaoting Lin, Xinyue Liu, Ji Liu, Hyunwoo Yuk, Hyun-Chae Loh, German A. Parada, Charles Settens, Jake Song, Admir Masic, Gareth H. McKinley, Xuanhe Zhao
nodes.materials_raw: Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) hydrogels with tunable crystallinity; crystalline domain cross-links
nodes.methods_raw: Freeze-thaw cycling, annealing (varying time for crystallinity control), fatigue threshold measurement, kirigami hydrogel sheet fabrication
nodes.key_findings: Controlled introduction of crystallinity in hydrogels substantially enhances anti-fatigue-fracture properties; PVA hydrogel with 18.9 wt% crystallinity in swollen state achieves fatigue threshold exceeding 1000 J/m²; energy to fracture crystalline domains much higher than single layer of amorphous chains; increasing annealing time → higher crystallinity, larger crystalline domain size, smaller average distance between adjacent domains; biological tissues (cartilage, tendons, muscles) show extraordinary anti-fatigue properties from highly ordered/partially crystalline collagen fibers; general strategy: pattern highly crystalline regions to resist fatigue fracture while maintaining high water content and low moduli; kirigami hydrogel sheets created with patterned cuts + reinforced cut tips → highly stretchable and fatigue-resistant; reveals new anti-fatigue-fracture mechanism in hydrogels
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the optimal crystallinity for balancing fatigue resistance and softness? How does the crystalline domain size affect the fatigue threshold? Can the anti-fatigue strategy be applied to other hydrogel systems beyond PVA?

nodes.label: Time is Length in Self-Similar Logarithmic Aging of Physically Cross-Linked Semiflexible Polymer Net
nodes.full_title: Time is Length in Self-Similar Logarithmic Aging of Physically Cross-Linked Semiflexible Polymer Networks
nodes.authors: Patrick Ilg, Clarisse Luap, Martin Kröger
nodes.materials_raw: Physically cross-linked networks of semiflexible polymers; bead-spring model (1000 chains, 30 beads each); cohesive energy + bending stiffness
nodes.methods_raw: Molecular dynamics simulations, aging dynamics analysis, self-similarity verification, length scale characterization (persistence length, chord lengths)
nodes.key_findings: Previously unexplored class of self-similar aging identified; network undergoes ultraslow coarsening characterized by logarithmically growing mesh size L(t) ~ ln(t); this single time-dependent length scale defines an internal clock → spatiotemporal self-similarity of both structure and dynamics; governs spatial organization, cohesive and bending energies, and aging dynamics; different from power-law coarsening seen in spinodal decomposition; logarithmically slow coarsening reported for few systems (frustrated Ising, crumpled sheets, physically cross-linked semiflexible networks); physical aging in polymers: diverse macromolecular systems exhibit remarkably similar slow dynamics; weak-ergodicity breaking hypothesis tested; perspective on aging in soft and disordered materials
nodes.scientific_questions: What determines the prefactor of the logarithmic growth? Can the self-similar aging be experimentally verified in real semiflexible networks (e.g., actin, fibrin)? How does the aging dynamics change with different cross-link reversibility?

nodes.label: Physically cross-linked networks of semiflexible polymers
Slip-Link Theory Predicts Entangled, Star-Branched Relaxation Measurably Different from Tube Model P
nodes.full_title: Slip-Link Theory Predicts Entangled, Star-Branched Relaxation Measurably Different from Tube Model Predictions
nodes.authors: Maria Katzarova, Diego Becerra, Andrés Córdoba, Konstantin Taletskiy, Jay D. Schieber
nodes.materials_raw: Entangled star-branched polymer melts; star polymers with up to 12 entanglements per arm
nodes.methods_raw: Slip-link model (Schieber's formulation), corrected algorithm for star-branched relaxation (orders of magnitude faster), relaxation spectrum calculation, zero-shear-rate viscosity comparison with experimental data
nodes.key_findings: Slip-link theory correctly describes observed molecular-weight dependence of star-branched polymer relaxation; star polymers show much stronger MW dependence (~exp(γMa/Me)) than linear chains (τ ~ MW^3.4); tube models with ad hoc terms show poor and inconsistent predictions; slip-link model: constraint dynamics generated self-consistently from time evolution of slip links — no additional physics needed for different architectures; only change needed is chain free energy to reflect architecture; corrected algorithm orders of magnitude faster than previous studies; slip-link model includes important fluctuations absent in tube models; successfully predicts relaxation spectrum and zero-shear-rate viscosity for stars with up to 12 entanglements per arm
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the slip-link model be extended to pom-pom or H-polymers? How do the slip-link predictions compare with neutron scattering data on star polymers? What is the computational cost advantage over tube models for complex architectures?

nodes.label: star polymers with up to 12 entanglements per arm
Direct Characterization of Mechanical Properties in Fully Free-Standing Polymeric Electronic Films f
nodes.full_title: Direct Characterization of Mechanical Properties in Fully Free-Standing Polymeric Electronic Films for Stretchable Devices
nodes.authors: Jintao Feng, Saimeng Li, Chunlong Sun, Junjiang Wu, Long Ye
nodes.materials_raw: Crystalline polymer semiconductor P3PT; semicrystalline PDPP3T; semiparacrystalline PM6; nearly amorphous IDTBT; insulating polymer SEBS
nodes.methods_raw: FAST (free-standing thin film testing) method; systematic examination of film thickness, geometric dimensions, and temperature effects; cross-validation with multiple polymers
nodes.key_findings: Novel FAST method for directly probing mechanical response of fully free-standing polymeric electronic films; eliminates substrate-induced effects from film-on-elastomer (FOE) and film-on-water (FOW) methods; systematically examines how film thickness, geometry, and temperature affect mechanical behavior of ultrathin P3PT films; validated across multiple conjugated polymers (P3PT, PDPP3T, PM6, IDTBT) and insulating SEBS; accurate assessment of intrinsic mechanical properties of free-standing ultrathin films; insights into behavior under practical conditions; advances materials characterization for next-generation flexible/wearable electronic devices
nodes.scientific_questions: How do the intrinsic mechanical properties differ from substrate-supported measurements? What is the thickness limit for reliable free-standing film testing? How do the mechanical properties of different conjugated polymer classes compare systematically?

nodes.label: Viscoelastic Properties of Physically Crosslinked Networks. Part 2. Dynamic Mechanical Moduli
nodes.full_title: Viscoelastic Properties of Physically Crosslinked Networks. Part 2. Dynamic Mechanical Moduli
nodes.authors: F. Tanaka, S. F. Edwards
nodes.materials_raw: Transient networks of uniform polymer chains reversibly crosslinked by associating end groups; three network types: covalent, thermo-reversible, entangled
nodes.methods_raw: Linear response theory for oscillatory deformations, dynamic mechanical moduli derivation (G'(ω), G"(ω)), frequency-temperature superposition analysis, Cox-Merz rule test
nodes.key_findings: Dynamic mechanical moduli obtained as functions of frequency ω and chain breakage rate p(τ); in unentangled regime (M < Me): storage modulus G'(ω) increases with temperature (markedly different from uncrosslinked melts); plateau of modulus extended to lower ω region with increasing M·ν; temperature shift factor aT proportional to M·ν → depends exponentially on temperature; frequency-dependent viscosity η(ω) = G"(ω)/ω generally smaller than stationary viscosity ηst(γ̇) when compared at ω = γ̇ → breakdown of Cox-Merz rule in physically crosslinked networks; chain breakage rate related to temperature, molecular weight, and bond lifetime through activation process
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the theory extend to entangled transient networks? Can the Cox-Merz rule breakdown be experimentally verified for specific reversible networks? What is the relationship between the junction lifetime and the terminal relaxation time?

nodes.label: Transient networks of uniform polymer chains reversibly cros
Fatigue Failure in Glasses under Cyclic Shear Deformation
nodes.full_title: Fatigue Failure in Glasses under Cyclic Shear Deformation
nodes.authors: Swarnendu Maity, Himangsu Bhaumik, Shivakumar Athani, Srikanth Sastry
nodes.institution: Jawaharlal Nehru Centre for Advanced Scientific Research (JNCASR)
nodes.materials_raw: Glasses (amorphous solids) under cyclic shear deformation
nodes.methods_raw: Computer simulations, power-law divergence analysis, damage quantification (plastic rearrangements, energy dissipation), percolation transition analysis
nodes.key_findings: Upon approaching fatigue limit, failure times of glasses under cyclic shear display power-law divergence (varies from commonly used functional forms); strong dependence on degree of annealing of glasses; fraction of particles undergoing plastic rearrangements and percolation transition are predictive of failure; robust power law between accumulated damage (quantified by energy dissipated or non-affine displacements) and failure times → permits prediction of failure times based on initial cycle behavior; transition from non-diffusive to diffusive particle position changes with increasing strain amplitude; shear banding above transition; microscopic initiation of fatigue failure occurs before macroscopic signatures are detectable
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the power-law divergence be connected to critical phenomena theory? How does the fatigue limit depend on the glass preparation protocol? Can the damage prediction model be applied to metallic glasses experimentally?

nodes.label: Jawaharlal Nehru Centre for Advanced Scientific Research (JNCASR)
Conformation of Ionomers in Uniaxial Elongation Deformation
nodes.full_title: Conformation of Ionomers in Uniaxial Elongation Deformation
nodes.authors: Nazanin Sadeghi, Fardin Khabaz
nodes.materials_raw: Polyampholyte ionomer melts; varying degrees of polymerization (20 ≤ N ≤ 100); ionic clusters as transient cross-links
nodes.methods_raw: Nonequilibrium molecular dynamics simulations, extensional flow analysis, chain orientation tracking, end-to-end distance and ionic cluster monitoring
nodes.key_findings: Ionomer chains show characteristic strain-hardening behavior with notable deviation from linear viscoelastic predictions; for intermediate chain lengths (N=40, 60): distinct two-step plateau in stress growth coefficient at slow deformation rates → rearrangements between transient ionic contacts and subsequent network reformation occur in consecutive stages; at high extension rates: rapid bond dissociation → stress overshoots; at slower rates: local rearrangements strengthen ionic interactions → formation of new ionic bonds; shorter chains quickly relax and fail to achieve substantial alignment, while longer chains maintain orientation once stretched; partial breakage and reformation of ionic bonds sustain repeated cycles of stretching, recoiling, and tumbling; macroscopic metrics reach steady states but individual chain conformations show cyclic fluctuations throughout extension; chain length and ionic associations govern ionomer response in extensional flow
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the ionic cluster size distribution evolve during extension? Can the two-step plateau be experimentally observed in extensional rheometry? What is the critical chain length for the transition from single-step to two-step relaxation?

nodes.label: end-to-end distance and ionic cluster monitoring
Segmental Dynamics and Vitrification in Associating Copolymer Melts: Role of Cluster Formation, Micr
nodes.full_title: Segmental Dynamics and Vitrification in Associating Copolymer Melts: Role of Cluster Formation, Microdomains, and Cross-Linking
nodes.authors: Gopika Krishnan, Kenneth S. Schweizer
nodes.institution: University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign (Schweizer group)
nodes.materials_raw: Associating copolymer melts with high attractive sticker fractions; local clustering and disordered microphase ordering; PDMS and PPG telechelics (experimental comparison)
nodes.methods_raw: Microscopic statistical mechanical theory (Elastically Collective Nonlinear Langevin Equation, ECNLE), activated segmental relaxation analysis, sticker clustering/microdomain correlation effects
