nodes.key_findings: Re-entrant glass-melting feature predicted as sticker attraction strength initially increased → speed up of segmental relaxation and reduction of Tg relative to cross-linked homopolymer network; three competing effects: (1) purely kinetic slowing down of nonstickers due to cross-linking, (2) disordering of nonsticker local cage and weakening of effective forces due to sticker physical clustering, (3) longer-range impact of microdomain scale correlations with nonmonotonic dynamical effects; at high enough attraction strength: qualitative change emerges → sticker fraction-dependent elevation of Tg eventually surpasses cross-linked homopolymer network; new physics arises from complex evolution of amplitude of collective elastic field dressing large-amplitude mobile segment hopping; second slower α*-relaxation with weaker temperature dependence decoupled from alpha relaxation; results qualitatively consistent with experiments on associating PDMS and PPG telechelics
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the ECNLE theory predict the absolute Tg values quantitatively? How does the re-entrant behavior depend on the sticker spatial range? What is the relationship between the α* relaxation and the sticker bond lifetime?

nodes.label: Associating copolymer melts with high attractive sticker fra
local clustering and disordered microphase ordering
University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign (Schweizer group)
Entanglements and Fracture in Polymers
nodes.full_title: Entanglements and Fracture in Polymers
nodes.authors: Taewon Kang, Jinyue Dai, Yan Huang, Haeji Kim, Sinan Keten, Junsoo Kim
nodes.materials_raw: Gels, elastomers, plastics; various polymer topologies (linear, star, ring, slide-ring, polyrotaxane); trapped and transient entanglements
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review covering: fracture properties (toughness, strength, stretchability, work of fracture, fatigue threshold, endurance limit), theories (Lake-Thomas, Mikos-Peppas, shear lag, Mazich-Samus), computational modeling (nanoscale rheology, molecular dynamics)
nodes.key_findings: Entangled polymer chains cannot pass each other but slip and transmit tension → unique effects on elastic/viscoelastic/fracture properties; review of fracture properties across gels, elastomers, plastics with discussion of entanglement roles; trapped vs transient entanglements; Lake-Thomas theory and modifications; entanglement effects in: highly entangled hydrogels, slide-ring cross-links, double-network hydrogels, nanoconfined entanglements, single/multiple network elastomers, composites, crazing/shear deformation; rational design of mechanically durable polymers enabled by understanding entanglement-fracture relationship; insights into inferring polymer structures from fracture properties
nodes.scientific_questions: Can entanglement density be used as a single predictor for fracture toughness across all polymer classes? How do topological constraints (e.g., in polyrotaxanes) modify the entanglement-fracture relationship? What are the critical entanglement density thresholds for different fracture mechanisms?

nodes.label: Nonlocal Intrinsic Fracture Energy of Polymerlike Networks
nodes.full_title: Nonlocal Intrinsic Fracture Energy of Polymerlike Networks
nodes.authors: Bolei Deng, Shu Wang, Chase Hartquist, Xuanhe Zhao
nodes.materials_raw: Polymerlike networks (2D and 3D) with varying defects, dispersity, topologies, and length scales
nodes.methods_raw: Experiments, simulations, Lake-Thomas model comparison, nonlocal energy dissipation analysis
nodes.key_findings: Intrinsic fracture energy of polymer networks stems from nonlocal energy dissipation by relaxing chains far from crack tip; Lake-Thomas model underestimates experimental intrinsic fracture energy by ~1–2 orders of magnitude; modified models proposed but cannot fully explain discrepancy → implies nonlocal energy release and dissipation even in well-formed elastic gels; nonlocal process zone exists around crack tip; intrinsic fracture energy orders of magnitude greater than energy to rupture single layer of polymer chains; findings provide physical insights into polymer network fracture AND design principles for tough architected materials; studied across multiple length scales with varying defects, dispersity, topologies
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the spatial extent of the nonlocal dissipation zone? Can the nonlocal model predict the thickness dependence of fracture energy? How does the nonlocal mechanism interact with other toughening strategies (double network, crystallization)?

nodes.label: Two-Population Rouse Models for Polymer Segmental Dynamics in Nanocomposites
nodes.full_title: Two-Population Rouse Models for Polymer Segmental Dynamics in Nanocomposites
nodes.authors: Jack R. Rooks, Giovanni Ferraro, Emiliano Fratini, Naresh C. Osawa, Osawa Takahito, Antonio Faraone, Norman J. Wagner
nodes.materials_raw: Polymer nanocomposite: poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO) + silica nanoparticles
nodes.methods_raw: Quasielastic neutron scattering (QENS), two-population Rouse model analysis, suppressed Rouse model for interfacial constraints
nodes.key_findings: Segmental dynamics of PEO in silica nanocomposite accurately described by Rouse model; bulklike polymer far from NP surface behaves as neat polymer; slower polymer in interface close to NP surface described either with second Rouse population (different relaxation time) or suppressed Rouse model; suppressed Rouse model describes topological constraints on average every 12 beads with interfacial thickness up to 13.5 beads; effect of interface extends to layer thickness comparable to end-to-end distance of polymer; two-population models accurately reproduce experimental QENS data; provides explanation for observed reinforcement in PNC even at low loadings, consistent with current understanding of interphase relevance
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the interfacial thickness depend on NP size and polymer molecular weight? Can the suppressed Rouse model be generalized to other polymer-NP systems? What is the relationship between the interfacial dynamics slowing and the mechanical reinforcement?

nodes.label: suppressed Rouse model for interfacial constraints
Chain-Length-Dependent Correlated Molecular Motion in Polymers
nodes.full_title: Chain-Length-Dependent Correlated Molecular Motion in Polymers
nodes.authors: Matthew Reynolds, Daniel L. Baker, Peter D. Olmsted, Johan Mattsson
nodes.materials_raw: PMMA, PS, PPG-DME, PDMS (spanning stiff to flexible polymer chemistries)
nodes.methods_raw: Temperature-modulated DSC (TMDSC) to extract Nc, four-point correlation function G4(r,t) analysis, dynamic susceptibility χ4(t), KWW fitting of relaxation data
nodes.key_findings: Dynamic heterogeneities (DHs) depend on both chain flexibility and chain length; for highly flexible polymers (PDMS, PPG-DME), Nc ~ 500 monomers undergo correlated motion at Tg, independent of molecular weight; for less flexible polymers (PMMA, PS), Nc(M) shows three regimes: (I) short oligomers (<2 Kuhn steps) with intermolecular correlations and Nc ~ 200, transitioning to intramolecular correlations with Nc < 50 near the regime I-II boundary M*; (II) chains with ~2-20 Kuhn steps where Nc increases weakly; (III) long chains where Nc saturates; for PMMA, a remarkable similarity is found between Nc(M) and the M-dependent ratio of activation barriers for α and β relaxations; evidence presented that the relationship between DH length scale and number of β-relaxation events jointly activated to facilitate α-relaxation is a general feature of glass-forming polymers
nodes.scientific_questions: Why is Nc ~ 500 universal for flexible polymers regardless of chemistry? What determines the regime I-II boundary M* and its relationship to chain folding? How do intramolecular correlations for short chains connect to the intermolecular-to-intramolecular crossover? Can the α-β relaxation coupling ratio predict DH length scales for other glass-forming systems?

nodes.label: Impact of Network Architecture and Cross-Link Aggregation on the Crystallization Behavior of a Polyp
nodes.full_title: Impact of Network Architecture and Cross-Link Aggregation on the Crystallization Behavior of a Polypropylene-Based Thermoset Polyurethane
nodes.authors: Ronard Herrera Monegro, Josiah Hanson, Ioannis Mountziaris, Ramanan Krishnamoorti, Megan L. Robertson
nodes.materials_raw: Maleic anhydride-grafted polypropylene (PPgMAH) → hydroxylated PP (PPOH) → thermoset polyurethane (PU) with urethane exchange catalyst
nodes.methods_raw: Rheology, SAXS, DSC, polarized optical microscopy, Avrami crystallization kinetics analysis
nodes.key_findings: PU rheological behavior transitions from solid-like to viscoelastic liquid with increasing catalyst concentration; SAXS shows hard segment aggregation through a correlation peak persisting above PP melting temperature; PP-rich domains crystallize upon cooling with lamellar dimensions (thickness, long period) influenced by crystallization temperature Tc but NOT by network formation; PU exhibits lower amorphous content and higher interlamellar spacing compared to PPgMAH and PPOH precursors; PP forms spherulites of similar size and nuclei density across all materials regardless of Tc; crystallization kinetics (activation energy, nucleation constant, crystal growth rate) are unchanged by network formation; results indicate isolated PP-rich domains that are microphase-separated from urethane cross-links, undergoing crystallization consistent with neat PP
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the length scale of microphase separation between PP and urethane domains evolve with cross-link density? Can the exchange catalyst concentration be used to independently tune network dynamics without affecting crystallization? What happens to crystallization when the PP molecular weight between cross-links approaches the critical entanglement length?

nodes.label: Unified Memory Kernel for Polymer Dynamics: Coupling Hydrodynamic and Excluded Volume Effects in Gen
nodes.full_title: Unified Memory Kernel for Polymer Dynamics: Coupling Hydrodynamic and Excluded Volume Effects in Generalized Langevin Equations
nodes.authors: Xiaofei Tian, Qinhang Liu, Zhi-Chao Yan, Tongfei Shi, Jizhong Chen
nodes.institution: Chinese Academy of Sciences (Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry)
nodes.materials_raw: Model polymer chains in various solvent conditions (theta, good, poor solvents)
nodes.methods_raw: Generalized Langevin equation (GLE) theoretical derivation, Langevin dynamics simulations, autocorrelation function Crr(t) and force-position cross-correlation Cfr(t) analysis, mode expansion
nodes.key_findings: End-to-end vector memory kernel function K(t) can be analytically determined by Crr(t) and Cfr(t); under preaveraged approximation (averaging chain conformational fluctuations), the ratio Cfr(t)/Crr(t) becomes time-independent, simplifying K(t) to depend solely on Crr(t); derived universally applicable analytical expression for K(t) explicitly links hydrodynamic interactions (HI), excluded volume (EV), and their interplay through two parameters (α, β) that govern subdiffusive scaling on intermediate time scales; α and β can be directly expressed as linear functions of the Flory exponent; theory validates against Langevin dynamics simulation trajectories; framework decodes multiscale mean-squaredisplacement behavior by establishing direct links between memory effects and Flory exponent
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the memory kernel change for branched or ring polymers? Can the framework be extended to entangled chains where reptation dominates? What is the physical origin of the time-independence of Cfr(t)/Crr(t) under preaveraging?

nodes.label: Model polymer chains in various solvent conditions (theta
autocorrelation function Crr and force-position cr
Chinese Academy of Sciences (Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry)
Tensile Strain Softening of Associative Polymer Networks: Understanding Nonlinear Mechanics
nodes.full_title: Tensile Strain Softening of Associative Polymer Networks: Understanding Nonlinear Mechanics
nodes.authors: Cameron K. Locke, Ying Yang
nodes.materials_raw: Metallo-supramolecular networks with pendant group cross-linking, different metal ions, low degree of entanglements
nodes.methods_raw: Uniaxial tensile testing, shear rheology, sticky Rouse model analysis, KWW fitting of relaxation times
nodes.key_findings: All networks exhibit shear-thinning; networks with fast metal-ligand cross-links show tensile strain softening with a yield point, while networks with slow cross-links do not; this reveals a fundamental difference in how associative networks respond to force under shear versus tensile deformation; mechanism of yielding explained by comparing instantaneous extension rate at different stages of tensile test with network relaxation times from sticky Rouse model; extreme strain softening (yield point followed by continuous stress decline) attributed to fast cross-link dynamics allowing chains to detach and reorganize before load transfer; moderate strain softening seen in both pendant and end-group cross-linked networks; connection drawn to glassy polymer yielding (disorder-accelerated segmental relaxation) and Mullins effect in filled rubbers
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the critical ratio of cross-link dissociation time to deformation rate that determines whether softening or stiffening occurs? How does entanglement density modulate the softening/stiffening transition? Can the sticky Rouse model quantitatively predict the yield point location?

nodes.label: Metallo-supramolecular networks with pendant group cross-lin
Temperature Dependence of Polymer Network Diffusion
nodes.full_title: Temperature Dependence of Polymer Network Diffusion
nodes.authors: Takeshi Fujiyabu, Ryota Kudo, Yuki Yoshikawa, Takuya Katashima, Ung-il Chung, Naoyuki Sakumichi, Takamasa Sakai
nodes.materials_raw: Tetra-arm polymer gels with controlled homogeneous network structures (AB-type cross-end coupling), varying network connectivity p (0.7-1.0) and polymer concentration c (30-120 g/L)
nodes.methods_raw: Dynamic light scattering (DLS) to measure collective diffusion coefficient D(T), temperature-dependent measurements, extrapolation to gelation point
nodes.key_findings: Collective diffusion coefficient D of polymer networks can be decomposed into Dgel (diffusion at gelation point) and ΔD ≡ D − Dgel; ΔD is a linear function of absolute temperature T with a significantly large negative constant term: ΔD(T;c,p) = aD(c,p)[T − TD(c)], where TD is independent of connectivity p; this is formally identical to the "negative energy elasticity" discovery (G = aT + b with negative b), demonstrating nontrivial similarity between statics and dynamics of polymer networks; the negative constant term indicates internal energy contributions to network diffusion, analogous to energy elasticity in gel mechanics
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the molecular origin of the negative energy contribution to network diffusion? Does the D-T relationship hold for heterogeneous or dynamically crosslinked networks? How does the temperature TD relate to the T0 from negative energy elasticity?

nodes.label: Tetra-arm polymer gels with controlled homogeneous network s
varying network connectivity p  and polymer concentration c
Dynamic light scattering  to measure collective di
Elastic Properties of Polycatenane Chains and Ribbons
nodes.full_title: Elastic Properties of Polycatenane Chains and Ribbons
nodes.authors: James M. Polson, Liam MacNevin, Alaaddin Elobeid, Carlos E. Padilla Robles
nodes.materials_raw: Polycatenane chains (interlocking molecular rings) and catenane ribbons (two or three polycatenane chains connected by side-link rings), modeled with rigid rings
nodes.methods_raw: Monte Carlo computer simulations, force-extension measurements, torsional stiffness characterization via applied torque to end rings
nodes.key_findings: Force-extension behavior of rigid-ring polycatenane comparable to previous flexible/semiflexible chain studies; applied torque twists chains to oppose extensional force and reduce extension; at low torque, twist angle varies linearly with torque (torsional spring constant); torsional spring constant increases monotonically with applied force; ribbons more elongated at low force but less elongated at strong force compared to polycatenane; increasing ribbon width dramatically increases torsional stiffness; decreasing degree of side-linking in ribbons slightly decreases extension at moderate force but increases torsional stiffness for sufficiently large gaps; results bridge the gap between 1D polycatenane and 2D kinetoplast-like structures
nodes.scientific_questions: How do flexible vs rigid rings affect the torsional mechanics? Can the force-torque phase diagram of polycatenane be mapped experimentally using angular optical traps? What is the relationship between catenane topology and the stress-softening regime attributed to "rotational sliding"?

nodes.label: torsional stiffness characterization via applied t
Polymer Chain Conformation Triggers Electro-osmotic Flow in Uncharged Nanochannels
nodes.full_title: Polymer Chain Conformation Triggers Electro-osmotic Flow in Uncharged Nanochannels
nodes.authors: Xinxi Liu, Zexuan Li, Andriy Yaroshchuk, Wenyao Zhang, Yuqing Zhao, Qiuwang Wang, Alaa S. Abd-El-Aziz, Cunlu Zhao
nodes.materials_raw: Polyelectrolyte (PE) solutions confined in uncharged nanochannels
nodes.methods_raw: Dissipative particle dynamics (DPD) simulations, parametric studies varying chain topology, stiffness, confinement, and salt concentration
nodes.key_findings: New EOF mechanism discovered in PE solutions confined in uncharged nanochannels — arises from PE chain conformation-induced spanwise charge separation in the electric double layer; flow exhibits vanishing velocity gradients on channel wall (wall-dissipation-free transport); chain topology and stiffness critically regulate conformation-induced EOF: increasing chain rigidity suppresses charge separation and reduces flow velocity; linear chains most affected, star-like chains least affected; long rigid chains adopt U-shaped or claw-like conformations under electric fields and confinement, reshaping charge separation; PE distributions exhibit different patterns for various topologies (single to double to triple layers); EOF velocity shows nonmonotonic variation with decaying nanoconfinement due to oscillations of charge separation in EDL; charge separation and EOF gradually suppressed by added salt screening
nodes.scientific_questions: Can this conformation-induced EOF be observed experimentally in nanopore membranes? How does the flow scale with PE molecular weight and charge density? What is the efficiency compared to conventional charged-wall EOF for pumping applications?

nodes.label: Rubbery Surface Elasticity of Polymer Glasses
nodes.full_title: Rubbery Surface Elasticity of Polymer Glasses
nodes.authors: Haiyang Zhang, Zhongyang Zhou, Zhiyuan Yao, Jintian Luo, Biao Zuo
nodes.materials_raw: Polystyrene (PS), poly(4-methylstyrene) (P4MS), poly(tert-butylstyrene) (PtBS) — unentangled glassy polymer films
nodes.methods_raw: AFM measurement of wetting ridge formation induced by immiscible liquid droplet (ionic liquid) surface tension, linear elasticity modeling of surface deformation
nodes.key_findings: Soft elastomeric surface layer exists on polymer glasses, even unentangled ones; wetting ridge formation on polymer glass surface induced by liquid surface tension allows quantitative measurement of surface-layer elastic properties; surface-layer thickness typically below 10 nm, greater for PtBS than PS and P4MS; obtained surface elastic modulus is of same order as rubbery plateau modulus of bulk polymers but consistently higher than bulk values, indicating stiffening of rubbery response at polymer-glass surface; elastocapillary length le = γp/Ge characterizes the balance between wetting forces and surface elasticity; the transient rubbery response on unentangled polymer glasses is attributed to mobility gradient at surface
nodes.scientific_questions: What molecular mechanisms cause the surface rubbery stiffening compared to bulk? How does the surface-layer thickness depend on molecular weight and cooling rate? Does the surface rubbery layer affect macroscopic tribological properties (friction, wear)?

nodes.label: linear elasticity modeling of surface deformation
Marginally Stable Phases in Mean-Field Structural Glasses
nodes.full_title: Marginally Stable Phases in Mean-Field Structural Glasses
nodes.authors: Camille Scalliet, Ludovic Berthier, Francesco Zamponi
nodes.materials_raw: Soft repulsive spheres model (tunable from hard spheres to harmonic to Hertzian interactions), covering granular matter, foams, emulsions, hard/soft colloids, molecular glasses
nodes.methods_raw: Mean-field theory of glass transition (random first-order transition theory), replica symmetry breaking analysis, complete phase diagram construction in (ϕ, kBT/ε) space
nodes.key_findings: Complete phase diagrams delimiting marginally stable glass phase for variety of pair interactions and physical conditions; all types of glasses may become marginally stable but extent highly depends on preparation protocol; marginal phases observable for colloidal and non-Brownian particles near jamming and for poorly annealed glasses; well-annealed glasses show two distinct marginal phases: Gardner glass (full replica symmetry breaking, with long-range correlations in vibrational dynamics, excess of low-frequency modes, unusual rheology, system-spanning soft modes) and another marginally stable phase near jamming; Gardner transition predicted at finite temperature; normal glass responds elastically like regular solid, while Gardner glass is marginally stable; framework unifies previous results on marginal stability across different mean-field models
nodes.scientific_questions: Does the Gardner transition survive in finite spatial dimensions? Can marginal stability be detected experimentally in colloidal glasses via diverging correlation lengths? How does the preparation protocol (cooling rate, compression rate) control the extent of the marginally stable phase?

nodes.label: Glass-Forming Liquids and the Glass Transition: The Energy Landscape Approach to Dynamics and Thermo
nodes.full_title: Glass-Forming Liquids and the Glass Transition: The Energy Landscape Approach to Dynamics and Thermodynamics
nodes.journal: Journal of the Indian Institute of Science 86, 731-749 (2006)
nodes.authors: Srikanth Sastry
nodes.institution: Jawaharlal Nehru Centre for Advanced Scientific Research (Bangalore)
nodes.materials_raw: General glass-forming liquids (review), including silica, polymers, metallic glasses
nodes.methods_raw: Review of energy landscape analysis: inherent structure mapping, potential energy minima enumeration, configurational entropy calculation, molecular dynamics simulations
nodes.key_findings: Comprehensive review of the energy landscape approach to understanding glass transition; (i) onset of slow dynamics related to exploration of landscape topography; (ii) crossover to activated dynamics regime; (iii) fragility quantified by landscape features (configurational entropy, shape of basins); (iv) relationship between liquid-gas spinodal and glass transition line; VFT temperature dependence of viscosity predicts divergence at finite T0; falling out of equilibrium at ~10^13 poise corresponds to ~100 s relaxation time; the approach connects thermodynamic and dynamic properties of glass-formers through the topography of their potential energy landscape
nodes.scientific_questions: Is there a true thermodynamic transition underlying the glass transition? How does the energy landscape approach connect to mode-coupling theory predictions? Can configurational entropy vanishing (Kauzmann paradox) be definitively established or refuted?

nodes.label: Relaxation Dynamics in the Energy Landscape of Glass-Forming Liquids
nodes.full_title: Relaxation Dynamics in the Energy Landscape of Glass-Forming Liquids
nodes.authors: Yoshihiko Nishikawa, Misaki Ozawa, Atsushi Ikeda, Pinaki Chaudhuri, Ludovic Berthier
nodes.materials_raw: Mean-field Mari-Kurchan model, finite-dimensional glass-forming models (harmonic spheres in D=2 and 3)
nodes.methods_raw: Zero-temperature relaxation dynamics following quenches from equilibrium configurations at various initial temperatures, steepest descent energy minimization, inherent structure analysis
nodes.key_findings: In mean-field Mari-Kurchan model, relaxation changes from power law to exponential decay below a well-defined temperature TSF, consistent with mean-field p-spin models; for finite-dimensional systems, relaxation is always algebraic with a nontrivial universal exponent at high temperatures crossing over to harmonic value at low temperatures; evolution controlled by temperature-dependent population of localized glassy excitations; TSF reflects change in landscape structure where inherent states have marginal density of states above TSF which becomes gapped below; these temperatures are distinct from equilibrium mode-coupling temperature TMCT; work unifies several lines of studies on potential energy landscape characterization and challenges both mean-field and real-space descriptions of glasses
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the physical nature of the localized glassy excitations that control the relaxation? Does the critical temperature TSF have an experimental observable signature? How do the landscape properties connect to the dynamic heterogeneities observed in simulations?

nodes.label: Emergent Signatures of the Glass Transition in Colloidal Suspensions
nodes.full_title: Emergent Signatures of the Glass Transition in Colloidal Suspensions
nodes.authors: Patrick Laermann, Haim Diamant, Yael Roichman, Ivo Buttinoni, Manuel A. Escobedo-Sánchez, Stefan U. Egelhaaf
nodes.materials_raw: PMMA hard-sphere colloidal suspensions (diameter 3.19 μm, polydispersity ~7%) with small tracer particles (0.50 μm) in index/density-matched cis-decalin/cycloheptylbromide solvent
nodes.methods_raw: Two-channel confocal microscopy, two-point microrheology, fluctuation-dissipation theorem applied to tracer pair correlations, model-free theoretical description
nodes.key_findings: New framework using two-particle correlations and fluctuation-dissipation theorem to investigate glass-forming colloidal suspension dynamics indirectly; correlated motion of tracer pairs at short timescales reveals three distinct signatures of glass transition: (1) changing decay behavior of correlations with relative distance, (2) steeply increasing length scale related to decay, (3) notable sign reversal in specific correlations; establishes connection between colloidal glass transition and breaking of translational symmetry in dispersion medium; length scale related to tracer pair correlations predicted to diverge with square root of viscosity; framework bypasses the dramatic slowdown problem by probing short-timescale tracer dynamics
nodes.scientific_questions: Is the observed symmetry breaking a true critical phenomenon or an apparent effect? Does the divergence of the length scale match predictions from random first-order transition theory? Can the sign reversal in correlations be used as an experimental order parameter for the glass transition?

nodes.label: polydispersity ~7%) with small tracer particles  in index/de
Pathway Complexity in Supramolecular Polymerization
nodes.full_title: Pathway Complexity in Supramolecular Polymerization
nodes.authors: Peter A. Korevaar, Subi J. George, Albert J. Markvoort, Maarten M. J. Smulders, Peter A. J. Hilbers, Albert P. H. J. Schenning, Tom F. A. De Greef, E. W. Meijer
nodes.materials_raw: S-chiral oligo(p-phenylenevinylene) (SOPV) with chiral butoxy side chains, hydrogen-bonded dimers self-assembling into helical stacks
nodes.methods_raw: Stopped-flow CD spectroscopy, kinetic modeling with two competing nucleated assembly pathways, UV-Vis spectroscopy, chirality manipulation via tartaric acid auxiliary
nodes.key_findings: Time-resolved observations reveal kinetically favoured metastable assembly (P-SOPV, right-handed) forms quickly but then transforms into thermodynamically favoured form (M-SOPV, left-handed); quantitative kinetic model reveals two parallel and competing pathways leading to assemblies with opposite helicity; both on-pathway (M-SOPV) and off-pathway (P-SOPV) formed via homogeneously nucleated growth mechanism; inverted dependence of t50 on concentration observed — aggregation slower at higher concentration due to competing pathways; chirality of thermodynamic product controlled by stereochemistry, but kinetic pathway product has opposite chirality; tartaric acid auxiliary can force aggregation completely down kinetically favoured pathway, yielding only metastable assemblies; transition from metastable to thermodynamically stable occurs via depolymerization of P-SOPV and growth of M-SOPV (helix reversal penalty ~8.1 kBT rules out intrastack stereomutation)
nodes.scientific_questions: How general is the pathway complexity mechanism across different supramolecular systems? Can the kinetic trapping strategy be applied to create metastable functional materials with enhanced properties? What determines the relative nucleation rates of the competing pathways?

nodes.label: S-chiral oligo  with chiral butoxy side chains
hydrogen-bonded dimers self-assembling into helical stacks
chirality manipulation via tartaric acid auxiliary
Experimental Evidence of the Gardner Phase in a Granular Glass
nodes.full_title: Experimental Evidence of the Gardner Phase in a Granular Glass
nodes.authors: A. Seguin, O. Dauchot
nodes.materials_raw: Bidimensional bidisperse granular disks, vibrated below jamming
nodes.methods_raw: Compression cycles within same glass realization, tracking particle trajectories across multiple compressions, mean square displacement and mean square distance analysis, cage order parameter characterization
nodes.key_findings: First direct experimental evidence of Gardner phase in granular system; particles select different average vibrational positions at each compression cycle while neighborhood structure remains unchanged; separation between cages obtained for different compression cycles (ΔAB) plateaus with increasing packing fraction while mean square displacement (Δ) steadily decreases — this phenomenology strikingly similar to numerical observations in mean-field model and hard spheres in finite dimensions; despite nonidealities of experimental system, Gardner signature is remarkably strong; no aging on experimental time scales in stable glass phase, but always present in Gardner phase as predicted theoretically; distribution of cage order parameters shows several differences from numerical results, possibly attributable to activated processes and cage heterogeneities
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the Gardner transition be detected in 3D granular systems? How do activated processes in finite dimensions modify the mean-field Gardner transition picture? What is the nature of the aging dynamics in the Gardner phase?

nodes.label: Compression cycles within same glass realization
tracking particle trajectories across multiple com
mean square displacement and mean square distance
Johari-Goldstein Relaxation Far Below Tg: Experimental Evidence for the Gardner Transition in Struct
nodes.full_title: Johari-Goldstein Relaxation Far Below Tg: Experimental Evidence for the Gardner Transition in Structural Glasses?
nodes.authors: K. Geirhos, P. Lunkenheimer, A. Loidl
nodes.materials_raw: Sorbitol and xylitol (canonical structural glass formers with well-pronounced JG β-relaxation)
nodes.methods_raw: Broadband dielectric spectroscopy down to unprecedented low temperatures far below Tg
nodes.key_findings: Unusual broadening of JG β-relaxation below ~110 K for sorbitol and ~100 K for xylitol, in excess of expected broadening from distribution of energy barriers; results well consistent with presence of Gardner transition in canonical structural glass formers; Gardner transition theoretically predicted to arise deep in glassy state where energy landscape becomes more complex with fractal hierarchy of sub-basins within metabasins; JG β-relaxation arises from local structure of energy landscape (jumps between local energy minima), making it ideal probe for Gardner transition; the two features (JG relaxation and Gardner transition) cannot be the same because JG relaxation is commonly observed at elevated temperatures, but Gardner transition leads to further fractal "roughening" of existing β-relaxation sub-basins
nodes.scientific_questions: Is the observed broadening truly due to Gardner transition or another mechanism? Can the Gardner transition temperature TG be independently determined by other experimental probes? How does the fractal hierarchy of sub-basins relate to the distribution of activation energies for JG relaxation?

nodes.label: Energy Landscapes and Functions of Supramolecular Systems
nodes.full_title: Energy Landscapes and Functions of Supramolecular Systems
nodes.authors: Faifan Tantakitti, Job Boekhoven, Xin Wang, Roman V. Kazantsev, Tao Yu, Jiahe Li, Ellen Zhuang, Roya Zandi, Julia H. Ortony, Christina J. Newcomb, Liam C. Palmer, Gajendra S. Shekhawat, Monica Olvera de la Cruz, George C. Schatz, Samuel I. Stupp
nodes.materials_raw: Peptide amphiphile V3A3K3 with 16-carbon alkyl chain (β-sheet forming domain + lysine charge repulsion), anionic perylene-monoimide amphiphile (π-orbital overlap assembly)
nodes.methods_raw: CD spectroscopy, cryo-TEM, DLS, annealing studies, ionic strength control, energy barrier estimation via Arrhenius analysis
nodes.key_findings: Minima in energy landscapes of supramolecular systems defined by competition between electrostatic repulsion and dominant attractive forces that trap molecules in thermodynamically unfavourable configurations; competing interactions can be selectively switched on and off, with order of doing so determining final product position in energy landscape; below critical ionic strength (Ic ~6 mM): thermodynamically favoured product is short monodisperse fibres (random coil, ~150 nm), while kinetically trapped metastable product is long β-sheet fibres; energy barrier for transition is 171 kJ/mol (half-life of months at room temperature); long fibres promote biological cell adhesion and survival while short fibres interfere with adhesion and can lead to cell death; findings suggest that in supramolecular systems, functions and energy landscapes are linked, superseding traditional connection between molecular design and function
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the energy landscape navigation strategy be generalized to other supramolecular systems beyond peptides? How does the kinetic trapping timescale relate to the molecular design of the building blocks? Can energy landscape control be used to create switchable biomaterials with on-demand cell signaling?

nodes.label: Peptide amphiphile V3A3K3 with 16-carbon alkyl chain
energy barrier estimation via Arrhenius analysis
Dynamic Pathways in Energy Landscapes Guiding Supramolecular Janus Dendrimer Self-Assemblies between
nodes.full_title: Dynamic Pathways in Energy Landscapes Guiding Supramolecular Janus Dendrimer Self-Assemblies between Lamellar and Cubic Architectures
nodes.authors: Jiabin Luan, Danni Wang, Niels P. Kok, Neshat Moslehi, Ilja K. Voets, Daniela A. Wilson
nodes.materials_raw: Janus dendrimer molecules with (3,5)-patterned hydrophobic aliphatic chains and (3,4)-patterned hydrophilic oligo(ethyleneglycol) (OEG) on phenolic acid units
nodes.methods_raw: Temperature-triggered self-assembly, cryo-TEM, dynamic light scattering, nanoparticle tracking analysis, FFT analysis of structural periodicity, kinetic annealing studies
nodes.key_findings: Dynamic and minimalistic Janus dendrimer system capable of reversibly transitioning between lamellar vesicles and inverse cubic structures; temperature-triggered non-covalent interactions (OEG interdigitation and hydrogen bonding) control conformational flexibility and molecular packing; rich energy landscape features diverse assembly pathways spanning lamellar vesicles and inverse cubosomes; multivesicular vesicles (MVVs) transform into inverse sponge structures (interlamellar attachments) upon annealing at 37°C, which serve as intermediates for cubosome formation; sponge structures gradually convert to cubosomes over 72 h annealing; packing parameter p > 1 achieved by molecular design (increasing hydrophobic chain volume and decreasing hydrophilic headgroup area); reversible transitions between lamellar and cubic architectures demonstrate pathway-dependent self-assembly
nodes.scientific_questions: What determines the kinetic barriers between lamellar and cubic phases? Can the energy landscape be further expanded to include hexagonal phases? How does the molecular packing parameter evolve during the structural transitions?

nodes.label: 4)-patterned hydrophilic oligo  on phenolic acid units
Pendant Groups in the Majority Domain of Aliphatic Multiblock Copolymers Prohibit the Double Gyroid
nodes.full_title: Pendant Groups in the Majority Domain of Aliphatic Multiblock Copolymers Prohibit the Double Gyroid Morphology
nodes.authors: Margaret K. Brown, Viola A. Burlein, Benjamin T. Ferko, Anne Saumer, Stefan Mecking, Karen I. Winey
nodes.materials_raw: Strictly alternating multiblock copolymers with sulfonated polar block and various alkyl blocks: linear (PESMx), mixed linear/sterically hindered fatty acid dimer (PESNax-20% Pripol), isopropyl-substituted (PESMx-iPr); blocks with ≤20 backbone bonds and large interaction parameter
nodes.methods_raw: Small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS), DSC, self-consistent field theory (SCFT) comparisons, morphological characterization
nodes.key_findings: PESMx polymers form double gyroid morphology above melting temperature over broad range of polar volume fractions; new PESNax-20% Pripol and PESMx-iPr multiblock copolymers exclusively form hexagonal morphologies in melt; 20% Pripol blocks reduce crystallinity relative to linear alkyl block copolymers; 100% iPr groups make polymers fully amorphous; absence of stable double gyroid attributed to prohibitively high degrees of chain stretching of alkyl blocks; highlights importance of packing frustration in majority domain on self-assembly behavior in high-χ, low-N multiblock copolymers; packing frustration in majority domain is rarely discussed but fundamentally affects morphological stability
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the medial packing model quantitatively predict the morphological transitions induced by pendant groups? Is there a threshold pendant group density that destabilizes the double gyroid? Can the packing frustration concept be used to rationally design network morphologies?

nodes.label: mixed linear/sterically hindered fatty acid dimer
blocks with ≤20 backbone bonds and large interaction paramet
Understanding Soft Glassy Materials Using an Energy Landscape Approach
nodes.full_title: Understanding Soft Glassy Materials Using an Energy Landscape Approach
nodes.authors: Hyun Joo Hwang, Robert A. Riggleman, John C. Crocker
nodes.materials_raw: Model soap foam consisting of compressible spherical bubbles with harmonic repulsion, driven by Ostwald ripening
nodes.methods_raw: Energy minimization (FIRE algorithm), steepest-descent dynamics on energy landscape, analysis of fractal dimension of relaxation pathways, avalanche statistics
nodes.key_findings: Steepest-descent paths on energy landscape are self-similar fractal curves with fractal dimension ~1.46; fractal dimension directly related to exponent describing power-law shear modulus and bubble super-diffusive motion; complex behavior of soft glassy materials (power-law rheology, non-diffusive bubble motion, avalanches) stems directly from fractal dimension and energy function of these paths; energy function along relaxation pathway has large domains with no stable minima, giving rise to avalanche behaviour; energy landscape resembles fractal mountainscape with surprisingly few and rather shallow superimposed minima; ubiquitous soft glassy rheology may be a consequence of emergent fractal geometry in energy landscapes of many complex fluids; model reproduces truncated Lévy walk motion and avalanche-like collective bubble rearrangements
nodes.scientific_questions: Why are the energy minimizing paths fractal? Is the fractal dimension universal across different soft glassy materials? Can the fractal energy landscape picture explain the specific power-law exponents observed experimentally in different SGMs?

nodes.label: Model soap foam consisting of compressible spherical bubbles
analysis of fractal dimension of relaxation pathwa
Self-Organization at the Crack Tip of Fatigue-Resistant Thermoplastic Polyurethane Elastomers
nodes.full_title: Self-Organization at the Crack Tip of Fatigue-Resistant Thermoplastic Polyurethane Elastomers
nodes.authors: Giorgia Scetta, Eric Euchler, Jianzhu Ju, Nathan Selles, Patrick Heuillet, Matteo Ciccotti, Costantino Creton
nodes.materials_raw: Commercial soft TPU (E ~8 MPa) with similar modulus but different large strain behavior, including strain-induced crystallizing TPU (TPU_XTAL) and non-crystallizing TPU (TPU_SOFT)
nodes.methods_raw: Cyclic fatigue testing on pure-shear notched samples, crack propagation rate (dc/dn) vs energy release rate (G) analysis, FTIR spectroscopy, GPC molecular weight characterization
nodes.key_findings: First demonstration that strain-induced stiffening mechanism of TPU combined with nonhomogeneous strain in cyclic fatigue produces selective reinforcement in crack tip area; this selective reinforcement is key to explain remarkable cyclic fatigue resistance of TPU; mechanism stems from multiphase nature of TPU and is not necessarily linked to strain-induced crystallization; TPU_SOFT (with bidentate urea interactions at 1640 cm⁻¹) shows strong interactions contributing to fatigue resistance; during cyclic deformation, TPU experience shake down and stabilization of stress-stretch curve; crack propagation in standard notched pure-shear samples can be robustly expressed as dc/dn vs G; results confirm very high resistance of soft TPU to crack propagation under cyclic fatigue conditions even for large applied strains
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the quantitative relationship between strain-induced stiffening ratio and fatigue crack propagation resistance? How does the local crystallization or hard domain reorganization at the crack tip evolve with number of cycles? Can the self-organization mechanism be engineered into other soft materials?

nodes.label: Cyclic fatigue testing on pure-shear notched sampl

Reduction of Kuhn Length upon Chain Extension
nodes.full_title: Reduction of Kuhn Length upon Chain Extension
nodes.authors: Liel Sapir, Danyang Chen, Michael Rubinstein
nodes.materials_raw: General polymer chains in θ-solvents (theoretical framework applicable to all flexible polymers)
nodes.methods_raw: Analytical theory for strain-dependent Kuhn length, molecular dynamics simulations, force-extension curve analysis
nodes.key_findings: Chain size and elasticity depend on long-range bond correlations determining Kuhn length; these correlations are gradually cut off with increasing applied force or polymer confinement, decreasing effective Kuhn length; theory developed for strain-dependent Kuhn length validated with simulations; model explains why Kuhn length from single-molecule force spectroscopy (SMFS) experiments is smaller than Kuhn length from scattering measurements of unperturbed chains; proposed crossover function for Kuhn length as function of applied force for interpretation of force-extension curves; at small deformations (⟨R⟩ < L/3) response is linear (Gaussian chain, entropic elasticity); at intermediate forces (f > kBT/bn) response becomes nonlinear but still primarily entropic; at high forces enthalpic contributions dominate; chain fluctuations δ² = ⟨R²⟩ − ⟨R⟩² quantify the response
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the Kuhn length reduction depend on chain stiffness? Can the crossover function be generalized to good solvents and melts? What is the relationship between force-dependent Kuhn length and the finite extensibility of polymer chains?

nodes.label: Analytical theory for strain-dependent Kuhn length
Chain Ends Excite Polymer Cooperative Motion
nodes.full_title: Chain Ends Excite Polymer Cooperative Motion
nodes.authors: Quanyin Xu, Zhenghao Wu, Katelyn Randazzo, Wen-Sheng Xu, Bokai Zhang, Rodney D. Priestley, Biao Zuo
nodes.materials_raw: Polystyrene variants: cyclic PS (no ends), linear PS (two ends), six-arm star PS (multiple ends), adamantane-doped PS, PS with various end groups
nodes.methods_raw: DSC glass transition measurements, molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, Adam-Gibbs theory analysis, ECNLE (elastically collective nonlinear Langevin equation) model
nodes.key_findings: Strong correlation discovered between number of chain ends within cooperatively rearranging region (CRR) and glassy properties (Tg and m); fast-relaxing chain ends alleviate requirements of cooperativity for structural rearrangement, facilitating cooperative motion that reduces Tg and m as chain length is shortened; identified new quantity NEG (number of chain ends within CRR) as single parameter capable of capturing Tg(Mw) for complete set of PS; adamantane-doped PS shows markedly reduced Tg and m due to plasticization, but when chemically attached to chain interior, plasticization effect vanishes; cyclic PS shows almost invariant Tg with decreasing molecular length (contrast to linear and star PS); proposed "index of rigidity" (R) to categorize effects of disparate end groups on polymer glass formation; results provide unifying picture of polymer glass formation regarding role of chain end, length, and topology
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the index of rigidity quantitatively relate to the cooperative rearrangement dynamics? Can the NEG framework explain the topology dependence of Tg in branched and network polymers? What determines the spatial extent of the CRR and its relationship to chain end mobility?

nodes.label: Dynamics from the Surface to the Bulk in Ultrastable and Liquid-Cooled Oligomeric Glasses
nodes.full_title: Dynamics from the Surface to the Bulk in Ultrastable and Liquid-Cooled Oligomeric Glasses
nodes.authors: Iain McKenzie, Victoria L. Karner, Ruohong Li, W. Andrew MacFarlane, Gerald D. Morris, Michael F. Thees, John O. Ticknor, James A. Forrest
nodes.materials_raw: Highly monodisperse atactic styrene oligomers (11 monomers, PDI 1.0029), ultrastable glasses (USG) prepared by PVD and normal glasses (NG) by liquid cooling
nodes.methods_raw: β-detected NMR of implanted spin-polarized ⁸Li⁺, depth-resolved measurements (surface z̄=2.8 nm, bulk z̄=79 nm), temperature-dependent relaxation time analysis, entropy-enthalpy compensation analysis
nodes.key_findings: Near bulk Tg, USGs show much faster surface dynamics but slower bulk dynamics than NG; cooling below Tg reveals sharp crossover at T* where surface dynamics vitrify on tens to hundreds of nanosecond timescales and become slower than in bulk; NG displays two distinct T* values near surface, pointing to heterogeneous local structure; greater stability correlates with slower bulk dynamics (higher density imposes stronger steric constraints); despite differences between USG and NG, all dynamics obey entropy-enthalpy compensation, suggesting common molecular mechanism; preparation and thermal history lock styrene oligomeric glasses into distinct regions of potential energy landscape, giving rise to fundamentally different glassy dynamics
nodes.scientific_questions: What determines the crossover temperature T* and its relationship to the cooperative relaxation? Why do USGs have faster surface dynamics than NGs near Tg? Can the entropy-enthalpy compensation be used to predict dynamics for other glass-forming systems?

nodes.label: Highly monodisperse atactic styrene oligomers (11 monomers
ultrastable glasses  prepared by PVD and normal glasses  by
Counterion Effects of Protic Ionic Liquids on the Conductivity and Stretchability of Free-Standing P
nodes.full_title: Counterion Effects of Protic Ionic Liquids on the Conductivity and Stretchability of Free-Standing PEDOT:PSS Films
nodes.authors: Thuy T. Le, Fatima Abbas, Shawn Bourdo, Fumiya Watanabe, Noureen Siraj
nodes.institution: University of Arkansas at Little Rock
nodes.materials_raw: PEDOT:PSS with protic ILs [p-MIM][DCA] and [p-IM][DCA] (newly synthesized), aprotic IL [EMIM][DCA], and various cation-anion combinations
nodes.methods_raw: HSAB (hard-soft acid-base) theory-guided IL selection, ion-exchange synthesis, four-point probe conductivity, mechanical testing, FTIR, TGA
nodes.key_findings: PEDOT:PSS with [p-MIM][DCA] and [p-IM][DCA] exhibited tremendous conductivity increase to 2730 S/cm and 3547 S/cm respectively; both protic ILs improved external strain to ~11%, surpassing aprotic IL [EMIM][DCA] (~7%) and pristine PEDOT:PSS (<4%); ILs containing hard cation and soft anion exhibited superior conductivity and flexibility in composite films; six ions ranging from hard to soft studied: cations [p-IM]+ (hardest), [p-MIM]+, [EMIM]+ (softest); anions [BF4]-, [TCB]-, [DCA]-; HSAB theory successfully guides IL selection for optimizing both conductivity and mechanical properties
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the molecular mechanism by which hard cation/soft anion combinations enhance PEDOT:PSS performance? How does the IL affect the PEDOT chain conformation and PSS chain mobility? Can the HSAB-guided approach be generalized to other conductive polymer/IL systems?

nodes.label: University of Arkansas at Little Rock
Theory of Inhomogeneous Polymers: Fundamentals of the Gaussian Random-Walk Model
nodes.full_title: Theory of Inhomogeneous Polymers: Fundamentals of the Gaussian Random-Walk Model
nodes.authors: Eugene Helfand
nodes.materials_raw: General polymer systems (theoretical framework): immiscible polymer interfaces, block copolymer microdomains, concentrated polymer solution surfaces
nodes.methods_raw: Functional integral techniques, Gaussian random-walk model, mean field theory, statistical mechanical derivation
nodes.key_findings: Connects intuitive mean field arguments for inhomogeneous polymer problems to more fundamental statistical mechanics; functional integral techniques employed extensively to derive systematic procedures for difficult problems where intuition might fail; derives formula for free energy (most difficult quantity to obtain correctly from mean field theories); inhomogeneous structure controlled by competition between free energy cost of making sharp boundaries (associated with delocalization of free energy) and opposing cost of locally having states not of minimum free energy density; framework provides foundation for polymer-polymer interface theory and block copolymer microdomain structure predictions; applications include immiscible polymer interfaces and block copolymer self-assembly
nodes.scientific_questions: How do fluctuation corrections modify the mean field predictions for block copolymer phase behavior? Can the functional integral approach be extended to non-Gaussian chain models? What are the limitations of the Gaussian random-walk approximation for semiflexible or branched polymers?

nodes.label: General polymer systems : immiscible polymer interfaces
Configurational Entropy of Random Trees
nodes.full_title: Configurational Entropy of Random Trees
nodes.authors: Pieter H. W. van der Hoek, Angelo Rosa, Ralf Everaers
nodes.materials_raw: Random tree-like objects (randomly branching polymers, ideal trees), models for bacterial DNA, chromosomal DNA during interphase, viral RNA
nodes.methods_raw: Graph theoretical approach using Prüfer labeling, analytical derivation of configurational entropy and partition functions, efficient sampling scheme for tree configurations
nodes.key_findings: Prüfer labeling provides (i) direct access to exact configurational entropy as function of tree composition, (ii) computable exact expressions for partition functions and experimental observables for tree ensembles with controlled branching activity, and (iii) efficient sampling scheme for tree configurations and arbitrary static properties; revisits Prüfer's proof of Cayley's conjecture (NN-2 distinct labeled trees of N nodes); framework applies to freely jointed noninteracting "ideal" trees where bonds can be oriented randomly; total number of endpoints (leaves) satisfies specific relation with node functionalities; conformational entropy becomes independent of spatial embedding for ideal trees
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the configurational entropy change when excluded volume interactions are included? Can the framework be extended to trees with annealed connectivity (chemical equilibrium)? What is the relationship between tree entropy and the gelation transition in branching polymer systems?

analytical derivation of configurational entropy a
efficient sampling scheme for tree configurations
Size Effect of the End-Attached Particle on the Adsorption-Responsive Polymer Switches
nodes.full_title: Size Effect of the End-Attached Particle on the Adsorption-Responsive Polymer Switches
nodes.authors: Zengju Lian, Shuanhu Qi
nodes.materials_raw: Adsorption-active polymer chain with free end attached to hard-core nanoparticle, embedded in inert brush
nodes.methods_raw: Analytical theory, self-consistent field (SCF) calculations
nodes.key_findings: Particle-polymer system exhibits different phases and transition features compared to particle-free system only when particle is large enough; two critical particle radii R* and Rc defined: if R > R*, particle can be stuck at brush surface exhibiting partially exposed state (not observed in particle-free switches); if particle is small, only two states (exposed and adsorbed) are involved; when R > Rc, transition properties show different dependence on system parameters (grafting density, solvent quality, polymer chain length); analytical expressions derived for transition point, transition width, and transition barrier; large particle attached to end of active chain mainly broadens transition width while keeping transition barrier small; strong osmotic repulsion on finite volume particle responsible for particular phase and transition characteristics
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the polymer switch be realized experimentally with gold nanoparticles or quantum dots? How does the particle shape (non-spherical) affect the switching behavior? What is the optimal particle size for maximizing both sensitivity and response speed?

nodes.label: Correlation between Structural Flexibility of H‑Bonding Motifs and Mechanical Properties of Dynamic
nodes.full_title: Correlation between Structural Flexibility of H‑Bonding Motifs and Mechanical Properties of Dynamic Cross-Linked Polymers
nodes.authors: Rena Tajima, Shintaro Nakagawa, Naoko Yoshie
nodes.materials_raw: Random copolymers bearing flexible diol groups or rigid amide groups as model systems for dynamic H-bond cross-linked polymers
nodes.methods_raw: Quantum chemical calculations of H-bonding structures, mechanical testing (stress-strain), self-recoverability assessment
nodes.key_findings: Structural flexibility of H-bonding groups profoundly affects mechanical properties; quantum chemical calculations revealed diverse H-bonding structures of diol group (multiple configurations) vs single H-bonding structure of amide group; polymer bearing diol groups presented greater stress at greater strains and superior self-recoverability compared to amide-bearing polymer; more frequent dissociation and reassociation of diol groups enables better balance between dynamicity and mechanical robustness; conventional complementary multidentate H-bonding groups (rigid) are advantageous for binding strength but compromise dynamic functions (self-healing, recoverability); flexible multidentate H-bonds serve as robust yet dynamic cross-links; inherent trade-off between mechanical robustness and dynamicity in rigid H-bond polymers resolved by using flexible H-bond motifs
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the optimal flexibility range for H-bonding motifs that maximizes both toughness and self-healing? How does the H-bond diversity (number of configurations) relate to the energy dissipation mechanism? Can flexible H-bond motifs be incorporated into other dynamic polymer architectures (vitrimers, gels)?

nodes.label: Random copolymers bearing flexible diol groups or rigid amid
Quantum chemical calculations of H-bonding structu
Decoding Exchange Mechanisms in High-Performance Polyurethane Vitrimers: Rheological Deconvolution o
nodes.full_title: Decoding Exchange Mechanisms in High-Performance Polyurethane Vitrimers: Rheological Deconvolution of Multi-Pathway Dynamics
nodes.authors: Mona Asl Farshbaf, Mir Karim Razavi Aghjeh
nodes.materials_raw: Polyester-based PU vitrimer (V-PU, from PCL/HDI/BDO with Sn(Oct)2 catalyst) and polyether-based vitrimer (V-PUG, from PEG-based, designed to suppress ester-driven transesterification)
nodes.methods_raw: Single- and multi-mode exponential decay models, relaxation spectra analysis, spectroscopic characterization, rheological stress relaxation
nodes.key_findings: V-PU exhibited ~50% higher tensile strength than linear analogue, retained elongation at break, and displayed rapid stress relaxation (<20 s at 190°C); V-PUG relaxes primarily through uniform exchange process dominated by urethane reversion, with secondary contributions from transcarbamoylation and minor segmental motions; V-PU exhibits intrinsically multimodal relaxation: urethane reversion dominates early time response, then regenerated hydroxyl groups trigger transcarbamoylation, and at longer times/higher temperatures ester-mediated transesterification engages both hard and soft segments; urethane reversion and transcarbamoylation occur mainly within hard domains while transesterification redistributes hydroxyls and modulates transcarbamoylation pathway; systematic deconvolution of associative and dissociative exchange mechanisms establishes molecular basis for tailoring relaxation spectra in PU vitrimers
nodes.scientific_questions: How can the relative contributions of different exchange pathways be independently tuned? What is the activation energy for each exchange mechanism? Can the deconvolution approach be applied to other multi-mechanism vitrimer systems?

nodes.label: Single- and multi-mode exponential decay models
50th Anniversary Perspective: Polymer Brushes: Novel Surfaces for Future Materials
nodes.full_title: 50th Anniversary Perspective: Polymer Brushes: Novel Surfaces for Future Materials
nodes.authors: Wei-Liang Chen, Roselynn Cordero, Hai Tran, Christopher K. Ober
nodes.materials_raw: Polymer brushes from ATRP, NMP, RAFT surface-initiated polymerization; uncharged and charged brushes; various architectures (linear, star, bottlebrush)
nodes.methods_raw: Review of controlled radical polymerization from surfaces, new theoretical models, sophisticated characterization tools (neutron reflectivity, AFM, XPS), nanoscale patterning
nodes.key_findings: Comprehensive review of polymer brush field; control of brush density and thickness gives unparalleled control over surface properties; stretched conformations produce both extension and restricted mobility; residual tension in polymer brushes may produce mechanochemical effects; brush behavior distinct from equivalent coated polymer layers; hairy nanoparticles developed from tight curvature permitting entanglement; applications in resistance to nonspecific binding, cell bioadhesion, biosensor platforms, thermoresponsive surfaces, targeted protein binding; De Gennes and Milner seminal understanding of brush physics; brushes can span channels and gaps over surprising distances due to residual stress; removed brush layers form robust thin films even without cross-linking
nodes.scientific_questions: How do brush dynamics change under confinement or in biological environments? Can brush architecture be used to create responsive surfaces with programmable properties? What are the limits of brush thickness and density achievable with current synthetic methods?

nodes.label: Review of controlled radical polymerization from s
Architecture Effect on Network Phase Formation from Controlled Self-Assembly of High‑χ Block Copolym
nodes.full_title: Architecture Effect on Network Phase Formation from Controlled Self-Assembly of High‑χ Block Copolymers
nodes.authors: Cheng-Yen Chang, Gkreti-Maria Manesi, Yun-Hao Chen, Yu-Jie Tsai, Hsing-Yu Su, Apostolos Avgeropoulos, Rong-Ming Ho
nodes.materials_raw: PS-b-PDMS (high-χ) diblock and star BCPs with 3 and 6 arms
nodes.methods_raw: Controlled self-assembly via solvent evaporation rate tuning with PS-selective solvents, SAXS, TEM
nodes.key_findings: Network phases from star BCPs easily acquired via controlled self-assembly (in contrast to diblocks with linear architecture); architecture effect enriches topological features of network phases including double gyroid, Frank-Kasper-like network phases with alternating three- and four-strut nodes, and double diamond phase; network phase formation stabilized by alleviation of packing frustration from network formation; SCFT calculations predict network phases become more stable in self-assembly of star BCPs due to architecture effect; conformational asymmetry created by nonlinear/branched architectures affects phase behaviors and origin of complex phases; selective solvent swelling at specific degrees provides feasibility to acquire aimed network phases via phase transitions from BCP solutions
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the star architecture approach be generalized to other high-χ BCP systems? What is the role of arm number (3 vs 6) in determining which network phase forms? How does the solvent evaporation rate quantitatively control the phase selection?

nodes.label: Design of High-Performance Viscoelastic Polymer Nanocomposites Using Stiff Nanorings
nodes.full_title: Design of High-Performance Viscoelastic Polymer Nanocomposites Using Stiff Nanorings
nodes.authors: Lv-Zhou Guo, Chao-Hao Wu, Holger Merlitz, Jia-Jia Chen, Zhen-Zhong Yang, M. Gregory Forest, Chen-Xu Wu, Xue-Zheng Cao
nodes.materials_raw: Stiff nanorings (SNRs) threaded by polymer chains in polymer melts
nodes.methods_raw: Advanced statistical rheological simulations, viscoelastic property analysis
nodes.key_findings: Threading polymer chains through stiff nanorings transforms melt into gel-like hyperviscous fluid despite absence of permanent or transient cross-links; under applied strain, dynamic sliding of polymer chains along constrained SNRs concentrates stress on nanorings, effectively delaying chain relaxation and minimizing chain breakage; SNRs act as dynamic stress-dissipative units; mechanism enhances both strength and toughness simultaneously while imparting exceptional viscoelastic properties surpassing conventional polymer melts; approach overcomes intrinsic strength-toughness trade-off without relying on traditional cross-linking or filler reinforcement; paradigm shift from cross-link-based to topology-based reinforcement
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the optimal nanoring stiffness and size for maximizing mechanical enhancement? How does the threading ratio (fraction of chains passing through nanorings) affect the viscoelastic response? Can SNRs be synthesized and incorporated into polymer melts experimentally at scale?

nodes.label: Stiff nanorings  threaded by polymer chains in polymer melts
Multiresponsive and Mechanically Robust Self-Healing Waterborne Polyurethane with Photoluminescent a
nodes.full_title: Multiresponsive and Mechanically Robust Self-Healing Waterborne Polyurethane with Photoluminescent and Acid−Base Adaptive Properties
nodes.authors: Tian-Yu Su, Jing-Ying Yang, Hui-Tao Yu, Rui-Guang Xing, Xin Ge, Li-Na Jia, Yu-Fei Wang, Jin-Xiao Bao, Gao-Fei Pan, Wei Feng
nodes.materials_raw: Waterborne polyurethane (WPU) with [2,2′-bipyridine]-4,4′-dicarbohydrazide (BD) as chain extender; intramolecular rotatable conjugated structures with abundant hydrogen bonding sites
nodes.methods_raw: Mechanical testing, UV-Vis fluorescence spectroscopy, acid-base response testing, self-healing efficiency measurement
nodes.key_findings: WPU-BD exhibits high tensile strength of 72.6 MPa and excellent stretchability of 1249.7%; under 260-400 nm UV excitation, material exhibits stable photoluminescence through synergistic mechanism between aggregation-induced emission (AIE) and proton-coupled energy transfer (PCEnT); reversible responses to mechanical stretching and acidic/alkaline environments with temporary fluorescence reduction due to molecular chain rearrangements, recovering upon stimulus removal; dynamic hydrogen and ionic bonds enable 100% self-healing efficiency within 24 h at 40°C; PCEnT narrows energy gap between ground state (S0) and excited state (S1) for highly efficient luminescence
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the PCEnT mechanism quantitatively contribute to the total luminescence compared to AIE alone? Can the self-healing kinetics be accelerated by increasing H-bond density? What is the long-term stability of the photoluminescent properties under repeated mechanical cycling?

nodes.label: Ultra Strong and Tough Bio-Based Polyurethane Elastomers with Rigid-Flexible Hybrid Soft Segment Sup
nodes.full_title: Ultra Strong and Tough Bio-Based Polyurethane Elastomers with Rigid-Flexible Hybrid Soft Segment Supramolecular Cross-linked Networks
nodes.authors: Ke Zheng, Ao Guo, Xueqin Li, Yanyan Lin, Shengting Zhang, Runhuai Yang, Wanqi Jia, Mingye Yu, Xianfeng Wang, Cong Sui
nodes.materials_raw: Bio-based polyurethane (BPU) with rigid polylactic acid polyol (PLA) and flexible poly(1,3-propanepolyol) polyol (PO3G) as hybrid soft segment, supramolecular hydrogen bond dynamic network
nodes.methods_raw: Mechanical testing, 3D printing of artificial ligaments, fatigue resistance testing in liquid environment, biocompatibility assessment
nodes.key_findings: Rigid PLA crystalline regions enhance mechanical strength while flexible PO3G conformational entropy buffering effect reduces phase interface energy, promoting spatial uniformity of microphase separation; supramolecular hydrogen bond dynamic network achieves efficient energy dissipation; excellent mechanical properties: tensile strength 82.29 MPa, elongation at break 2128%, toughness 993.08 MJ/m³; BPUs processed into artificial ligaments through 3D printing with stable mechanical properties and fatigue resistance in liquid environment; precise coordination between hardness and toughness achieved by adjusting PLA/PO3G molar ratio; bio-based materials reduce fossil resource dependence and carbon footprint
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the PLA crystallinity evolve under cyclic loading in the ligament application? What is the degradation rate of BPU in physiological conditions? Can the 3D-printed ligament architecture match the mechanical anisotropy of natural ligaments?

nodes.label: fatigue resistance testing in liquid environment
Molecular Springs in Dynamic Covalent Polymer Networks
nodes.full_title: Molecular Springs in Dynamic Covalent Polymer Networks
nodes.authors: Ibrahim Oladayo Raji, Mary Eisenhart, Roshan Lama, Govinda Prasad Devkota, C. Scott Hartley, Dominik Konkolewicz
nodes.materials_raw: Polymer networks with spring-like aromatic foldamers (ortho-phenylenes, oPs) in backbones, cross-linked with dynamic Diels-Alder (DA) adducts or nondynamic static cross-linkers
nodes.methods_raw: Mechanical testing, self-healing assessment, dampening property characterization, comparison of DA vs static cross-linkers
nodes.key_findings: Improved mechanical, self-healing, and dampening properties found in networks with shorter chains, more aromatic stacked foldamer units, and dynamic DA adduct cross-links; enhanced properties attributed to synergistic effects of reversible elastic response of spring-like aromatic foldamers and DA adducts; aromatic foldamers adopt helical structures through offset aromatic stacking interactions parallel to helical axis; folded structures offer unique synergy between ultimate stability (covalent bonding) and responsiveness (non-covalent aromatic stacking); foldamer-based strategy is promising due to reversibility of unfolding and folding, enhancing energy dissipation without external stimuli
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the optimal foldamer length and stacking density for maximizing energy dissipation? How does the foldamer unfolding force compare to the DA bond dissociation force? Can foldamer springs be integrated into other dynamic chemistries beyond DA networks?
