cross-linked with dynamic Diels-Alder  adducts or nondynamic
comparison of DA vs static cross-linkers
Dynamic Covalent Polymer Networks: A Molecular Platform for Designing Functions beyond Chemical Recy
nodes.full_title: Dynamic Covalent Polymer Networks: A Molecular Platform for Designing Functions beyond Chemical Recycling and Self-Healing
nodes.authors: Ning Zheng, Yang Xu, Qian Zhao, Tao Xie
nodes.materials_raw: Dynamic covalent polymer networks (DCPN) with >30 types of dynamic bonds including Diels-Alder, disulfide, imine, boronic ester, transesterification, etc.
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review covering chemical recycling, self-healing, shape-shifting, topological transformation
nodes.key_findings: Comprehensive review of DCPN beyond traditional recycling and self-healing; dynamic bond exchangeability shows unparalleled design versatility in shape-shifting materials/devices, artificial muscles, and microfabrication; molecular mechanisms to manipulate network topologies (topological transformation) lead to opportunities to program polymers; notable concepts include living networks and topological isomerization; topology manipulation via living chain growth and template-guided switching; DCPN discover/design functional properties not offered by classical thermoplastic and thermoset polymers; remaining issues and perspectives on how DCPN can shape future complementary with classical polymers
nodes.scientific_questions: How can DCPN be designed for applications beyond shape memory and self-healing? What are the scalability challenges for industrial adoption of DCPN? Can multiple dynamic chemistries be combined in a single network for multi-responsive behavior?

nodes.label: Dynamic covalent polymer networks  with >30 types of dynamic
Supramolecular Chemical Recycling of Dynamic Polymers
nodes.full_title: Supramolecular Chemical Recycling of Dynamic Polymers
nodes.authors: Yuanxin Deng, Ling Liu, Hong-Xi Luo, He Tian, Da-Hui Qu, Ben L. Feringa, Qi Zhang
nodes.institution: East China University of Science and Technology
nodes.materials_raw: Thioctic amide (TAA) derivatives — primary amide derivative of thioctic acid forming poly(disulfide)s with H-bond self-assembled sidechain stacking
nodes.methods_raw: Ring-opening polymerization characterization, NMR, DSC, XRD, mechanical testing, thermal recycling tests
nodes.key_findings: Supramolecular chemistry principle for catalyst-free and solvent-free polymer-to-monomer transformation of kinetically stable poly(disulfide)s; coupling of two dynamic equilibria — H-bond self-assembled stacking of sidechains and dynamic covalent polymerization of backbone — reversibly regulates monomer-polymer equilibrium through simple solvation/desolvation cycles; thermodynamically metastable yet kinetically stable poly(disulfide)s with high crystallinity and tunable mechanical properties; upon mild thermal activation at 120°C, plastic readily recycled into crystalline monomers with quantitative yields and monomer purity >90%; monomers regenerate origin-quality polymers; non-covalent self-assembly drives depolymerization enabling solvent- and catalyst-free recycling with low carbon footprint
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the H-bond stacking strength affect the polymerization/depolymerization equilibrium? Can the supramolecular recycling principle be extended to other dynamic covalent chemistries beyond disulfides? What is the energy consumption comparison with conventional chemical recycling methods?

nodes.label: Thioctic amide  derivatives — primary amide derivative of th
Reusable Ultratough Supramolecular Polyurethane-Urea Elastomer via Rigid-Flexible Mismatched Hard Se
nodes.full_title: Reusable Ultratough Supramolecular Polyurethane-Urea Elastomer via Rigid-Flexible Mismatched Hard Segments and a "Train Coupling Mechanism"
nodes.authors: Xucong Chen, Yuting Tian, Min Lin, Dandan Zhu, Xinling Wang
nodes.materials_raw: Supramolecular polyurethane-urea (PUU) with adipic dihydrazide (ADH, flexible H-bonding) and 2-ureido-4[H]-pyrimidinone (UPy, rigid quadruple H-bonding) hard segments
nodes.methods_raw: Mechanical testing, self-healing assessment, ethanol-assisted recycling testing, ionic conductivity measurement
nodes.key_findings: Biomimetic "train coupling mechanism" achieves both ultrahigh strength and facile recyclability; mismatched rigid-flexible supramolecular interactions between ADH and UPy hard segments enhance toughness and energy dissipation; PUU-AD-5UP exhibits outstanding tensile strength (64.4 MPa) and toughness (700 MJ/m³); dynamic UPy "couplers" enable room-temperature self-healing and ethanol-assisted recycling with 95% efficiency of tensile strength; ion-conductive composite (ICPUU-AD-5UP) achieves good combination of high tensile strength (28 MPa), ionic conductivity, and recyclability; AD H-bonding forms dense "spider web" of hydrogen bonding domains while UPy provides dynamic coupling
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the molecular mechanism of the "train coupling" between ADH and UPy domains? How does the ADH/UPy ratio optimize the strength-toughness-recyclability trilemma? Can the strategy be extended to other supramolecular motifs?

nodes.label: Weaving-Inspired Asymmetric Entangled Nodes in Multi-Component Polymer Networks
nodes.full_title: Weaving-Inspired Asymmetric Entangled Nodes in Multi-Component Polymer Networks
nodes.authors: Zejian He, Liya Chen, Wei You, Chenkai Zhu, Xue Yang, Honggang Mei, Ding Xiao, Qi Song, Tianyu Shan, Wei Yu, Guangfeng Li, Feihe Huang
nodes.materials_raw: Polyurethane (flexible) and epoxy resin (rigid) chains integrated through Pd(II) metal coordination entangled nodes with tridentate binding sites
nodes.methods_raw: Metal coordination chemistry, polymer network construction, mechanical and adhesive property testing
nodes.key_findings: Polymer chain entanglement through asymmetric entangled nodes inspired by macroscopic woven materials; achieves integration of polyurethane and epoxy resin chains at molecular level; abundant dynamic entanglements within polymer network reminiscent of woven topologies; flexible polyurethane chains and rigid epoxy chains work synergistically under external stress, imparting excellent mechanical and adhesive properties; demonstrates potential of weaving-inspired molecular-level entanglement in polymer integration and performance modulation; provides reference for design of novel multi-component polymeric materials through weaving-inspired strategies
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the weaving topology be controlled at the single-chain level? How does the entanglement density affect the mechanical synergy between flexible and rigid components? What other metal-ligand combinations can create effective entangled nodes?

nodes.label: Multiscale Structural Evolution Governing Fatigue Resistance in Polyurethane Elastomers
nodes.full_title: Multiscale Structural Evolution Governing Fatigue Resistance in Polyurethane Elastomers
nodes.authors: Guangzhi Jin, Bowen Gong, Tianle Liu, Kunyan Xie, Yu Wang, Runguo Wang, Jiajia Xue, Yonglai Lu
nodes.materials_raw: Polyurethane elastomer with hierarchical network integrating molecular-scale chemical cross-linking, microscale physical cross-linking, and dynamic hydrogen-bonding interactions
nodes.methods_raw: Multiscale structural characterization (SAXS, WAXD, FTIR, DSC), fatigue testing (9.5×10⁵ cycles at >100% strain), bond-level analysis
nodes.key_findings: Exceptional tensile strength (68.3 MPa), outstanding toughness (374.09 MJ/m³), extraordinary load-bearing capability of 4.27×10⁵ times own weight; sustains up to 9.5×10⁵ loading cycles at >100% strain with high fatigue threshold (793.68 J/m²); fatigue degradation governed by progressive evolution and eventual disintegration of spherulitic crystalline structures; soft-segment crystals first disrupted leading to pronounced crystallinity reduction, followed by gradual degradation of hard-segment crystals and fibrillar bundle dissociation; processes accompanied by weakened microphase separation, attenuated hydrogen bonding, reduced long period, and diminished transition layer thickness; bond-level analysis identifies C-N and especially C-O bonds as most vulnerable sites under cyclic loading; excellent biocompatibility and superior resistance to degradation for artificial ligament applications
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the multiscale structural evolution be monitored in real-time during fatigue testing? What is the critical crystallinity threshold below which fatigue failure accelerates? How does the fatigue mechanism differ between crystallizing and non-crystallizing PU elastomers?

nodes.label: Ligament Inspired Ultra-Strong and Tough Bio-Based Polyurethane Elastomers via Dynamic Hydrogen Bond
nodes.full_title: Ligament Inspired Ultra-Strong and Tough Bio-Based Polyurethane Elastomers via Dynamic Hydrogen Bonding Induced Confinement Effect
nodes.authors: Yuting Chu, Wenyan Zhang, Deguang Liu, Peng Chen, Lei Huang, Yezi You, Shifang Luan, Chuang Li, Yao Fu
nodes.institution: University of Science and Technology of China
nodes.materials_raw: Bio-based polyurethane with PO3G (bio-based soft phase), 2,5-furan diformylhydrazide (FDHA, hard phase building unit with dynamic H-bond network)
nodes.methods_raw: Molecular engineering, 3D printing of artificial ligaments, mechanical testing, biocompatibility assessment
nodes.key_findings: Dynamic hydrogen bonding-induced confinement effect (DHBCE) strategy proposed; bio-based PO3G provides super-ductility while FDHA constructs reversible dynamic hydrogen bond network at molecular level; gradient continuous distribution of soft and hard phases driven by dynamic H-bond network; bionic "soft-hard" gradient bi-continuous microstructure achieves substantial increase in strength and toughness (breaking strength 76.54 MPa, toughness 589.75 MJ/m³); biomimetic artificial ligaments with mechanical response characteristics similar to natural ligaments precisely fabricated using melting deposition 3D printing; excellent biocompatibility; advances sustainable development of biomedical elastomers through innovative molecular design and bio-based raw materials
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the FDHA furan ring contribute to the dynamic H-bond network compared to conventional hydrazide extenders? What is the degradation rate of bio-based PU in physiological conditions? Can the DHBCE strategy be applied to other bio-based polymer systems?

nodes.label: hard phase building unit with dynamic H-bond network)
Preparation of a Tough Elastomer by the Introduction of Multiple Flexible Hydrogen Bonds via Simple
nodes.full_title: Preparation of a Tough Elastomer by the Introduction of Multiple Flexible Hydrogen Bonds via Simple Modification
nodes.authors: Dian Zhang, Shintaro Nakagawa, Hirohiko Houjou, Naoko Yoshie
nodes.materials_raw: Vicinal amino alcohol (VAA)-modified polybutadiene (PB), synthesized by epoxidation of PB followed by ring-opening reaction with various amines; also applicable to SBS
nodes.methods_raw: Epoxidation and ring-opening synthesis, mechanical testing, quantum chemical calculations of H-bonded dimer structures, DFT analysis
nodes.key_findings: Simple and versatile method to introduce novel flexible H-bond group (vicinal amino alcohol, VAA) into commercially available diene-based polymers; PB modified with vicinal (ethylamino) alcohol presented approximately 90-fold increase in stress at break and approximately 75-fold increase in toughness; VAA modification endows PB with mechanical strength comparable to covalently cross-linked elastomers while maintaining dynamic properties such as self-recoverability; quantum chemical calculations revealed VAA adopts wide variety of stable H-bonded dimer structures, stabilizing dimer both enthalpically and entropically; method applicable to other diene polymers such as SBS; structurally simple and flexible H-bonding groups act as effective dynamic cross-links conferring mechanical robustness without sacrificing dynamic properties
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the optimal VAA density for balancing mechanical and dynamic properties? How does the amine structure (primary vs secondary, chain length) affect the H-bond diversity and mechanical response? Can VAA modification be applied to other unsaturated polymers beyond diene-based systems?

nodes.label: synthesized by epoxidation of PB followed by ring-opening re
quantum chemical calculations of H-bonded dimer st
Dynamic Synergy Effect between the Hydrogen Bond Network and Coordination Bond Network in Dual-Netwo
nodes.full_title: Dynamic Synergy Effect between the Hydrogen Bond Network and Coordination Bond Network in Dual-Network Polyurethane Elastomers
nodes.authors: Aofei Zhang, Xiaoyu Du, Jianglong Li, Jianlong Wen, Lang Shuai, Yongqiang Ming, Yijing Nie
nodes.materials_raw: PU elastomers with both hydrogen bond network and metal coordination bond network (dual dynamic networks)
nodes.methods_raw: Mechanical testing, dynamic bond characterization, comparison of single vs dual network systems
nodes.key_findings: Introduction of both weak hydrogen bonds and strong metal coordination bonds endows elastomers with high tensile strength without sacrifice of stretchability; dynamic synergistic mechanisms between hydrogen bond network and coordination bond network: strong coordination bonds promote orientation of local segments, while recombination of weak hydrogen bonds causes relaxation of locally oriented segments; strength of metal coordination bonds can be regulated by selecting different metal ions and ligands; construction of two distinct types of dynamic reversible bonds represents effective strategy for enhancing mechanical properties
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the quantitative relationship between coordination bond strength and the synergy effect? Can the synergy mechanism be generalized to other dual-dynamic-bond systems? How does the spatial distribution of H-bonds and coordination bonds affect the synergy?

nodes.label: Small-Molecule-Enhanced Polymeric Hydrogels: From Molecular Design to Functional Applications
nodes.full_title: Small-Molecule-Enhanced Polymeric Hydrogels: From Molecular Design to Functional Applications
nodes.authors: Ziyu Sha, Xinhua Chen, Hao Song, Yusheng Zheng, Wei Cui, Rong Ran
nodes.materials_raw: Hydrogels enhanced by small molecules including ions, solvents, monomers, and biomass-derived compounds
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review organizing toughening strategies by chemical nature of small molecules
nodes.key_findings: Review underscores critical role of small molecules in enhancing mechanical performance of polymeric hydrogels; molecular additives induce substantial structural transformations including ionic crosslinking, salting-out, phase separation, crystallization, and mechanochemical network growth; small-molecule-enhanced hydrogels underpin wide range of next-generation technologies including bioelectronics, soft robotics, water harvesting systems, and adaptive adhesives; molecular strategies enable significant improvements in strength, toughness, and environmental tolerance without relying on conventional composite structures or multi-step processing; solute-polymer interactions as design principle promotes molecular-level understanding for sustainable and multifunctional hydrogel systems
nodes.scientific_questions: What are the universal design principles for selecting small-molecule toughening agents? How do small molecules affect the long-term stability and fatigue resistance of hydrogels? Can machine learning approaches predict optimal small-molecule combinations for specific applications?

nodes.label: Orthogonal yet Synergistic Supramolecular Interactions to Boost Material Properties
nodes.full_title: Orthogonal yet Synergistic Supramolecular Interactions to Boost Material Properties
nodes.authors: Mengling Yang, Wenbin Wang, Kai Xue, Boyue Gao, Shaolei Qu, Zhi Wei Fan, Yongming Wang, Li Yang, Zhitao Zhang, Xuzhou Yan, Zhaoming Zhang
nodes.materials_raw: Supramolecular polymer networks (SPNs) incorporating three distinct supramolecular interactions: host-guest interactions, single hydrogen bonding, and quadruple hydrogen bonding
nodes.methods_raw: Mechanical testing, self-healing assessment, ACEL device fabrication and testing
nodes.key_findings: Three distinct supramolecular interactions exhibit both orthogonality and synergy; synergy demonstrated by (1) sequential dissociation due to strength gradient and (2) quadruple hydrogen bonding serving as structural scaffolds for host-guest-based cross-linking; SPN-2 exhibits exceptional mechanical performance: tensile strength 31.3 MPa and toughness 172.0 MJ/m³, significantly surpassing control networks lacking multiple supramolecular synergies; reversible nature endows SPN-2 with excellent puncture and tear resistance, rapid self-healing, and reprocessability; SPN-2 serves as effective stretchable substrate for ACEL devices, retaining stable luminescence under 75% tensile strain; extends beyond traditional orthogonality to exploit synergistic effects between different noncovalent interactions
nodes.scientific_questions: Can the synergy between orthogonal interactions be quantitatively predicted from individual binding constants? What is the optimal hierarchy of interaction strengths for maximizing both toughness and self-healing? How does the sequential dissociation mechanism differ from simultaneous dissociation in terms of energy dissipation?

nodes.label: Supramolecular polymer networks  incorporating three distinc
Multiphase Structure and Electromechanical Behaviors of Aliphatic Polyurethane Elastomers
nodes.full_title: Multiphase Structure and Electromechanical Behaviors of Aliphatic Polyurethane Elastomers
nodes.authors: Dong Xiang, Jingjing He, Tongtong Cui, Li Liu, Qi Song Shi, Lan Chao Ma, Yongri Liang
nodes.materials_raw: Aliphatic polyurethane elastomers (PUEs) based on hydroxyl-terminated butadiene-acrylonitrile copolymer (HTBN), hexamethylene diisocyanate (HDI), and various lengths of linear aliphatic diols
nodes.methods_raw: FTIR, WAXS, SAXS, viscoelastic AFM, dielectric spectroscopy, electromechanical testing
nodes.key_findings: PUEs exist in crystalline phase, hard domain (HD) and soft domain (SD) phases composed of HD-rich region and SD-rich region by crystallization and microphase separation; crystal morphology and crystallinity strongly influenced by chain extender length due to various conformations by hydrogen bonding; mechanical and electric field induced responses of segment motions at below room temperature related to constrained SS motions from HD-rich and SD-rich regions; electric field induced strain actuated by both Maxwell stress and electrostriction effect, with electrostriction contribution above 64% in total actuation strain; electrostriction plays important role in improving actuation strain at lower electric field; dielectric and electromechanical properties adjustable by controlling crystallization and microphase separation
nodes.scientific_questions: How does the acrylonitrile content in HTBN affect the electromechanical coupling? Can the electrostriction contribution be further enhanced by tuning the hard domain crystallinity? What is the optimal microphase separation morphology for maximizing actuation strain?

nodes.label: and various lengths of linear aliphatic diols
Sustainable Cycloaliphatic Polyurethanes: From Synthesis to Applications
nodes.full_title: Sustainable Cycloaliphatic Polyurethanes: From Synthesis to Applications
nodes.authors: Agathe Mouren, Luc Avérous
nodes.materials_raw: Cycloaliphatic polyurethanes from sustainable feedstocks: biobased terpenes, carbohydrates, fatty acids, cholesterol, and plastic waste
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review of chemical modification, PU synthesis, and property characterization
nodes.key_findings: Cycloaliphatics combine advantages of both linear aliphatic (oxidation/UV resistance, flexibility, transparency) and aromatic (rigidity, hydrophobicity, fire resistance, chemical/thermal stability) compounds; comprehensive overview of synthesis using various renewable products; chemical modification of main sustainable cycloaliphatic feedstocks summarized; major applications in building, transportation, packaging, and biomedicine; relationship between chemical structures of building blocks and final PU properties determined; environmental concerns, legislation, rarefaction of petrol fractions, and price fluctuations drive interest in sustainable feedstocks
nodes.scientific_questions: How do cycloaliphatic structures affect the hydrogen bonding and microphase separation in PUs? What is the lifecycle assessment comparison between cycloaliphatic and aromatic PUs? Can the mechanical performance of bio-based cycloaliphatic PUs match that of petroleum-based aromatic PUs?

nodes.label: Supramolecular Self-Assembly Regulated Double-Network Ionic Gel Electrolyte for Advanced Electrochro
nodes.full_title: Supramolecular Self-Assembly Regulated Double-Network Ionic Gel Electrolyte for Advanced Electrochromic Device
nodes.authors: Jie Gao, Jiayin Chen, Kaijian Zhou, Guoqiang Kuang, Yangchao Zeng, Mo Cao, Yixin He, Dimitri A. Ivanov, Yijie Tao, Rong Tan, Yan Zhang, Jun Zhang, Shiguo Zhang
nodes.materials_raw: Supramolecular-polymer double-network (SP-DN) ionic gel with 1,3:2,4-dibenzylidene sorbitol (DBS) supramolecular network + covalently cross-linked polymer network, ionic liquid electrolyte
nodes.methods_raw: Self-assembly characterization, ionic conductivity measurement, electrochromic device fabrication and testing, bending cycle stability tests
nodes.key_findings: SP-DN ionic gel synergizes superior mechanical strength and robust interfacial adhesion with high ionic conductivity and optical transparency; DBS self-assembly into supramolecular framework provides unique toughening mechanism through reversible physical cross-linking; even low concentrations of LMWGs yield significant mechanical improvements due to high efficiency of noncovalent interactions; electrochromic devices show rapid switching dynamics, high coloration efficiency, and robust stability over 2000 cycles; devices on ITO-PET substrates retain 90.6% of initial optical contrast after 1000 bending cycles; addresses inherent trade-off in conventional DN strategies where high cross-linking density restricts ion migration
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the optimal DBS concentration for balancing mechanical and ionic transport properties? How does the supramolecular network topology affect the ion migration pathways? Can the SP-DN approach be applied to other electrochemical devices beyond electrochromics?

nodes.label: Filler Effects Inspired High Performance Polyurethane Elastomer Design: Segment Arrangement Control
nodes.full_title: Filler Effects Inspired High Performance Polyurethane Elastomer Design: Segment Arrangement Control
nodes.authors: Jiaxin Shi, Tianze Zheng, Zhiqi Wang, Pujin Wang, Hongkun Yang, Jinjing Guo, Dong Wang, Baohua Guo, Jun Xu
nodes.materials_raw: Thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU) elastomers with varied arrangement of soft/hard segments
nodes.methods_raw: Molecular dynamics simulations, SAXS microphase separation analysis, mechanical and ionic conductivity testing
nodes.key_findings: Inspired by general filler effects, strategy proposed to enhance TPU mechanical properties by designing arrangement of hard/soft segments using traditional chemical compositions; suitable sequence length achieves considerably enhanced strength and toughness by maximizing relative surface area of hard domains; weak hard segments can upgrade normal TPU into advanced elastomers through synergistic effect; mixing obtained elastomer with ionic liquid results in durable ionogel sensor with balanced mechanical strength and ionic conductivity; easy-to-implemented strategy offers new dimension for development of high-performance elastomers; concept extrapolated from well-established relative surface area effect observed in polymer fillers
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the optimal sequence length distribution for maximizing hard domain surface area? How does the segment arrangement affect the hydrogen bonding network within hard domains? Can the filler-inspired concept be applied to other block copolymer systems?

nodes.label: A Stretchable, Mechanically Robust Polymer Exhibiting Shape-Memory-Assisted Self-Healing and Cluster
nodes.full_title: A Stretchable, Mechanically Robust Polymer Exhibiting Shape-Memory-Assisted Self-Healing and Clustering-Triggered Emission
nodes.authors: Xiaoyue Wang, Jing Xu, Yaoming Zhang, Tingmei Wang, Qihua Wang, Song Li, Zenghui Yang, Xinrui Zhang
nodes.institution: Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics (Chinese Academy of Sciences)
nodes.materials_raw: Polyurethane-urea (PUU) elastomer with coordination bonds (Zn²⁺-pyridine) and hydrogen bonds (acyl hydrazone, urea), polycarbonate diol soft segment
nodes.methods_raw: Mechanical testing, shape-memory cycle testing, self-healing assessment, fluorescence spectroscopy, ¹³C NMR, FTIR
nodes.key_findings: Optimal elastomer exhibits good mechanical properties: strength 76.37 MPa, elongation at break 839.10%, toughness 308.63 MJ/m³; phased energy dissipation mechanism involving various supramolecular interactions; shape-memory properties enable shape recovery force that brings damaged surfaces closer and facilitates self-healing; all specimens exhibit clustering-triggered emission with cyan fluorescence under UV light; acyl hydrazone bond (C=N) at 1662 cm⁻¹ and Zn²⁺-pyridine coordination bonds provide dynamic cross-linking; shape-memory-assisted self-healing combines mechanical recovery with molecular-level bond reformation
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the molecular mechanism of clustering-triggered emission in this system? How does the Zn²⁺/pyridine ratio affect both mechanical and self-healing properties? Can the fluorescence be used as a damage sensor to trigger self-healing?

nodes.label: Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics (Chinese Academy of Sciences)
Dynamic Bond Chemistry in Soft Materials: Bridging Adaptability and Mechanical Robustness
nodes.full_title: Dynamic Bond Chemistry in Soft Materials: Bridging Adaptability and Mechanical Robustness
nodes.authors: Haeseung Lee, Jiyun Kim, Minwoo Lee, Jiheong Kang
nodes.materials_raw: Soft materials with various dynamic bonds: H-bonds, metal-ligand coordination, host-guest interactions, dynamic covalent bonds (Diels-Alder, imine, boronic ester, disulfide, vinylogous urethane)
nodes.methods_raw: Comprehensive review covering self-healing, 3D printing, biocompatible hydrogels, recyclable thermosets, shape-shifting polymers; analytical methods for quantifying dynamicity (NMR, ITC, stress relaxation, frequency-dependent response, AFM, Raman spectroscopy)
nodes.key_findings: Review highlights design strategies that harness dynamic bonds effectively to achieve both functionality and mechanical robustness; survey of dynamic bond types and characteristic lifetimes; analytical methods to quantify network dynamicity; required degree of dynamicity varies depending on target functionality; synergistic cross-linking strategies for tough polymers; spatial and structural modulation over polymer networks; facilitation of shear thinning, rapid gelation, and strong interlayer adhesion for 3D printing; bioadhesive and biomedical implant applications; dissociative exchange for depolymerization, associative exchange for reprocessing, supramolecular interaction for efficient recycling
nodes.scientific_questions: How can the dynamic bond lifetime be precisely tuned to match specific application requirements? What are the universal design rules for combining multiple dynamic bonds in a single network? Can dynamic bonds be designed to respond to biological stimuli (pH, enzymes)?

nodes.label: Soft materials with various dynamic bonds: H-bonds
Control on Viscoelasticity of Recyclable Elastomer via Orthogonal Dynamic Bonds to Realize Extrusion
nodes.full_title: Control on Viscoelasticity of Recyclable Elastomer via Orthogonal Dynamic Bonds to Realize Extrusion Reprocessing
nodes.authors: Xinyu Li, Zixin Li, Zhiqi Zhang, Jie Wen, Junyang Wang, Zhijie Wang, Bing Yu, Ming Tian, Jing Bai
nodes.materials_raw: Elastomer with orthogonal dynamic bonds: associative (imine) and dissociative (anthracene dimerization) mechanisms in single system
nodes.methods_raw: Rheological characterization, extrusion processing tests, UV-triggered anthracene dimerization, mechanical testing of reprocessed products
nodes.key_findings: Orthogonal dynamic bonds in associative (imine) and dissociative (anthracene dimerization) mechanisms effectively regulate viscoelasticity and fluidity of elastomer; integration results in semisolid state with viscosity ~10³-10⁴ Pa·s, providing controllable fluidity within 160-180°C temperature range for continuous extrusion processing; anthracene dimerization requires UV light to initiate, allowing differentiation of reaction conditions for both mechanisms; orthogonal approach enhances properties of reprocessed products; enables implementation of continuous processing technologies and provides potential for 3D printing; addresses limitation that current dynamic cross-linked elastomers are limited to crude hot-compression processes
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the optimal ratio of imine to anthracene cross-links for balancing processability and mechanical properties? How does the UV exposure time affect the anthracene dimerization kinetics and final properties? Can the orthogonal bond approach be extended to other combinations of associative/dissociative dynamic bonds?

nodes.label: Development of Tough Thermoplastic Elastomers by Leveraging Rigid–Flexible Supramolecular Segment In
nodes.full_title: Development of Tough Thermoplastic Elastomers by Leveraging Rigid–Flexible Supramolecular Segment Interplays
nodes.authors: Luping Wang, Longfei Guo, Kaiqiang Zhang, Yuguo Xia, Jingcheng Hao, Xu Wang
nodes.materials_raw: Thermoplastic elastomers with rigid and flexible supramolecular segments
nodes.methods_raw: Supramolecular chemistry, mechanical testing, structural characterization
nodes.key_findings: Rigid-flexible supramolecular segment interplays leveraged to develop tough thermoplastic elastomers; strategy exploits the synergy between rigid supramolecular domains (providing strength and physical cross-linking) and flexible segments (providing elasticity and processability); supramolecular interactions enable reversible bond formation for self-healing and recyclability
nodes.scientific_questions: What is the optimal rigid/flexible segment ratio for maximizing toughness? How do the supramolecular interaction dynamics affect the fatigue resistance? Can the rigid-flexible interplay concept be extended to other supramolecular motifs?
